- **Real-world example:** **Autonomous vehicle decision-making** — balances safety, speed, fuel efficiency, and passenger comfort using a weighted utility function.

### E. Learning Agents
- **Self-improving** via feedback from a **critic** and **performance element**.
- Use **supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning**.
- **Real-world example:** **AlphaGo** by DeepMind — learned to play Go beyond human ability by playing millions of games against itself.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Agent Theory Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^{\ast} \to \mathcal{A}$ | Maps percept history to a single action. |
| Performance Measure | $PM = \frac{\text{Successful Outcomes}}{\text{Total Outcomes}}$ | Quantifies how good the agent actually is. |
| Rationality Condition | $\text{Agent is rational if } PM(a) \geq PM(a^{\ast})$ for all possible percept sequences. | Defines ideal behaviour. |
| Utility Maximization | $a^{\ast} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\left[U(\text{Result}(s,a))\right]$ | Selects action maximizing expected utility. |
| POMDP Belief Update | $b^{\prime}(s^{\prime}) = \eta \cdot O(s^{\prime}, o, a) \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} T(s, a, s^{\prime}) b(s)$ | Used in partially observable agent design. |
| Discounted Reward (RL Agent) | $G_{t} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \leq \gamma < 1$ | Total reward for a learning agent. |
| Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\left[r + \gamma \max_{a^{\prime}} Q(s^{\prime},a^{\prime}) - Q(s,a)\right]$ | Core learning rule for practical RL agents. |
| Task Environment (PEAS) | $\langle \text{Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors} \rangle$ | Standard design specification for any new agent. |
| Observability Index | $\mathcal{O} \in \{\text{Full}, \text{Partial}, \text{None}\}$ | Determines whether a reflex or model-based agent is needed. |
| Determinism Index | $\mathcal{D} \in \{\text{Deterministic}, \text{Stochastic}\}$ | Determines whether probabilistic reasoning is needed. |

> **Critical Notational Rule:** In all equations above, vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 PEAS Framework — The Design Specification

For any practical agent, KTU expects students to specify its **PEAS** parameters. PEAS stands for:

- **P**erformance Measure — How do we measure success?
- **E**nvironment — Where does the agent operate?
- **A**ctuators — How does it act?
- **S**ensors — How does it perceive?

### Worked PEAS Example: Autonomous Taxi (KTU Favourite Example)

| Component | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Performance Measure** | Safety, speed, legality, passenger comfort, profit. |
| **Environment** | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, other vehicles. |
| **Actuators** | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signal lights, horn, display screen. |
| **Sensors** | LIDAR, RADAR, GPS, cameras, accelerometers, ultrasonic sensors. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Practical agents are the **deployment manifestation of AI research**. They:
> - Power **24/7 industrial automation** (manufacturing robots, CNC controllers).
> - Enable **scalable web intelligence** (Google's PageRank crawler, recommendation engines).
> - Provide **clinical decision support** in hospitals (IBM Watson for Oncology).
> - Underpin **modern transportation** (Tesla Autopilot, Waymo robotaxis).

The economic and societal impact of practical agents is reflected in the global AI market, which surpassed **500 billion USD in 2024** — making agent design a top-tier engineering skill.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Agent Function to Real-World Behaviour

In this section, we mathematically derive how the abstract agent function translates into observable real-world behaviour. We use **rationality, performance measure, and expected utility** as the binding principles.

### Step 1: Definition of the Agent Function

We begin with the formal agent function:

$$f : \mathcal{P}^{\ast} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

This is the **theoretical definition** of an agent as a mapping from the entire history of percepts ($\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$) to a chosen action ($\mathcal{A}$).

> **Logic:** For every possible percept sequence the agent could ever observe, the agent function prescribes exactly one action. This is the **Platonic ideal** of an agent — a complete lookup table.

---

### Step 2: Conversion to a Practical Agent Program

In real engineering, we cannot implement an infinite lookup table. Instead, we approximate $f$ using an **agent program** that runs on physical hardware.

Let:
- $\mathcal{S}_{t}$ = Environment state at time $t$.
- $p_{t}$ = Percept observed at time $t$.
- $\text{Actuator}(a)$ = Hardware action executed at time $t$.

The practical implementation is:

$$\text{Agent}(p_{t}) = \text{Actuator}\Big(\text{Reasoner}\big(\text{StateEstimator}(p_{t}, \text{Memory}_{t-1})\big)\Big)$$

> **Logic:** A real agent receives percept $p_{t}$ via sensors, fuses it with prior memory $\text{Memory}_{t-1}$ to estimate state, reasons about the best action, and finally executes it via actuators.

---

### Step 3: Formal Definition of a Rational Agent

A rational agent is one that selects, for every percept sequence, the action that **maximizes the expected performance measure**.

Let $\text{PerceptSeq} = \langle p_{1}, p_{2}, \dots, p_{t} \rangle$.

The rational action $a^{\ast}$ is:

$$a^{\ast} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s^{\prime} \in \mathcal{S}} P(s^{\prime} \mid s, a) \cdot PM(s^{\prime})$$

Where:
- $P(s^{\prime} \mid s, a)$ = Probability of transitioning to state $s^{\prime}$ after performing action $a$ in state $s$.
- $PM(s^{\prime})$ = Performance measure of the resulting state.

> **Logic:** In a **stochastic** environment, the agent cannot be certain of outcomes. Hence, it picks the action with the **highest expected** (probabilistically weighted) outcome.

---

### Step 4: Derivation of Utility-Based Action Selection

When the goal is to maximize **utility** (rather than achieve a binary goal), the agent computes:

$$a^{\ast} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\left[U\big(\text{Result}(s,a)\big)\right]$$

Expanding the expectation over all possible next states $s^{\prime}$:

$$a^{\ast} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s^{\prime} \in \mathcal{S}} P(s^{\prime} \mid s, a) \cdot U(s^{\prime})$$

> **Logic:** This is the foundation of **decision theory** and is used by every utility-based practical agent — from autonomous vehicles trading off speed vs. safety, to trading bots balancing profit vs. risk.

---

### Step 5: Derivation of the Learning Agent's Performance Element

A **learning agent** improves its action-selection policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ over time using the **Q-Learning** rule:

$$Q(s_{t}, a_{t}) \leftarrow Q(s_{t}, a_{t}) + \alpha \Big[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot \max_{a^{\prime}} Q(s_{t+1}, a^{\prime}) - Q(s_{t}, a_{t}) \Big]$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **Learning rate** ($0 < \alpha \leq 1$).
- $\gamma$ = **Discount factor** ($0 \leq \gamma < 1$).
- $r_{t+1}$ = Reward received after action.
- The bracketed term is the **Temporal Difference (TD) error**.

The optimal action selection is then:

$$a_{t}^{\ast} = \arg\max_{a} Q(s_{t}, a)$$

> **Logic:** After sufficient training, $Q(s,a)$ converges to the true expected reward, making the agent's behaviour increasingly rational with experience.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Real Agents to Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU reference** that examiners love to test.

| Practical Agent | Agent Type | Environment Class | Observable? | Deterministic? | Key Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Thermostat** | Simple Reflex | Indoor home | Fully | Deterministic | Condition-action rules |
| **Roomba Vacuum** | Model-Based Reflex | Indoor house | Partially | Stochastic | SLAM + state estimation |
| **Google Maps Router** | Goal-Based | Road network | Fully | Stochastic | A* / Dijkstra search |
| **Self-Driving Car** | Utility-Based | Real-world traffic | Partially | Stochastic | POMDP + Deep RL |
| **AlphaGo** | Learning Agent | Go board game | Fully | Deterministic | MCTS + Deep RL |
| **Siri / Alexa** | Goal/Learning | Conversational | Partially | Stochastic | NLP + LLMs + RLHF |
| **Stock Trading Bot** | Utility-Based | Stock market | Partially | Stochastic | Markov Decision Process |
| **IBM Watson Oncology** | Goal-Based Expert | Hospital records | Partially | Stochastic | Bayesian Networks |
| **Mars Rover (Curiosity)** | Model-Based | Martian terrain | Partially | Stochastic | SLAM + planning |
| **Spam Filter (Gmail)** | Learning Agent | Email stream | Partially | Stochastic | Naive Bayes / Neural Net |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Numerical Example — Reflex vs. Learning Agent

> **Scenario:** A vacuum robot must decide whether to clean a room ($a_{1}$) or return to dock ($a_{2}$).

**Simple Reflex Agent (Naive):**
- Rule: *IF battery < 20% THEN return_to_dock, ELSE clean.*
- No learning; behaviour is static.

**Learning Agent (Q-Learning):**
- Initial $Q(\text{room},\text{clean}) = 0$, $Q(\text{room},\text{return}) = 0$.
- After 1000 trials: $Q(\text{room},\text{clean}) \approx 8.4$, $Q(\text{room},\text{return}) \approx 9.7$.
- Action chosen: $a^{\ast} = \arg\max\{8.4, 9.7\} = \text{return}$.

> **Logic:** The learning agent discovered, through experience, that returning to dock is more rewarding on average — outperforming the rigid reflex rule.

---

## 3.4 The Rationality vs. Omniscience Distinction (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rationality $\neq$ Omniscience.**

A **rational agent** does not know the actual outcome of its actions — it only knows what has been *perceived* and what its *knowledge* predicts. An **omniscient agent** would know the true result.

Mathematically:

$$\text{Omniscient}(s,a) = PM(\text{Actual Result}(s,a))$$

$$\text{Rational}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[PM(\text{Result}(s,a)) \mid \text{Knowledge at time } t\right]$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Real-world agents (e.g., self-driving cars) are **rational, not omniscient** — they cannot predict every pedestrian's behaviour, so they plan for the *expected* outcome under uncertainty.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Practical Agent Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **canonical architecture** of every practical agent, mapping how percepts flow from environment to action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1[Environment State]
    sens1[Sensors]
    perc1[Percept Processing Unit]
    mem1[Internal Memory / World Model]
    reas1[Reasoning Engine / Agent Core]
    goal1[Goal / Utility Function]
    act1[Actuator Interface]
    act2[Action on Environment]
    env1 --> sens1
    sens1 --> perc1
    perc1 --> reas1
    mem1 --> reas1
    goal1 --> reas1
    reas1 --> act1
    act1 --> act2
    act2 --> env1
```

> **Caption:** This is a **closed-loop perception-action cycle**, identical in structure to Russell & Norvig's canonical agent diagram. Every practical agent — from a thermostat to a self-driving car — instantiates this loop.

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Practical Agents

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root1[Practical Intelligent Agents]
    root1 --> simp1[Simple Reflex Agent]
    root1 --> mod1[Model-Based Reflex Agent]
    root1 --> goal2[Goal-Based Agent]
    root1 --> util1[Utility-Based Agent]
    root1 --> learn1[Learning Agent]
    simp1 --> exS[Example: Thermostat]
    mod1 --> exM[Example: Roomba Vacuum]
    goal2 --> exG[Example: Google Maps Routing]
    util1 --> exU[Example: Self-Driving Car]
    learn1 --> exL[Example: AlphaGo]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Autonomous Self-Driving Car

Since a physical schematic of an autonomous vehicle cannot be drawn in Mermaid, we render its **block-level functional architecture**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Perception_Layer["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        cam1[RGB Camera]
        lid1[LIDAR Sensor]
        rad1[RADAR Sensor]
        gps1[GPS Module]
        imu1[IMU Accelerometer]
    end
    subgraph Fusion_Layer["SENSOR FUSION LAYER"]
        fus1[Kalman Filter / Particle Filter]
        slam1[SLAM World Model]
        obj1[Object Detection CNN]
    end
    subgraph Decision_Layer["DECISION MAKING LAYER"]
        pln1[Path Planner A-Star]
        util2[Utility Function Engine]
        pol1[Reinforcement Learning Policy]
    end
    subgraph Control_Layer["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        str1[Steering Controller]
        thr1[Throttle Controller]
        brk1[Brake Controller]
        dsp1[HUD Display]
    end
    cam1 --> fus1
    lid1 --> fus1
    rad1 --> fus1
    gps1 --> fus1
    imu1 --> fus1
    fus1 --> slam1
    fus1 --> obj1
    slam1 --> pln1
    obj1 --> util2
    pln1 --> util2
    util2 --> pol1
    pol1 --> str1
    pol1 --> thr1
    pol1 --> brk1
    pol1 --> dsp1
```

> **Caption:** This block diagram mirrors the **Apollo / Waymo / Tesla Autopilot** architecture used by industry-grade autonomous vehicles, where each block is a software module processing percepts into actions.

---

## 4.4 Learning Agent Internal Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    perf1[Performance Element]
    crit1[Critic]
    prob1[Problem Generator]
    learn2[Learning Element]
    env2[Environment]
    perf1 --> env2
    env2 --> crit1
    crit1 --> learn2
    learn2 --> perf1
    prob1 --> perf1
```

> **Caption:** Russell & Norvig's canonical **learning agent** model. The *Critic* provides feedback, the *Learning Element* updates the agent, and the *Problem Generator* suggests exploratory actions to improve generalization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define an Intelligent Agent. List the four components that every practical agent must possess.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that perceives its environment via sensors, reasons about those percepts, and acts upon the environment via actuators to maximize a predefined performance measure. Every practical agent possesses four canonical components: **(1) Sensors** for perception, **(2) Percept Processing Unit** to interpret raw data, **(3) Reasoning Engine / Agent Core** to decide actions, and **(4) Actuators** to execute actions on the environment. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Differentiate between a Goal-Based Agent and a Utility-Based Agent with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Goal-Based Agent** reasons about specific end-states and selects actions that achieve a defined goal (success/failure outcome). *Example:* A GPS navigation system finding the shortest route to a destination. A **Utility-Based Agent** extends this by mapping every state to a continuous **utility value**, allowing it to handle trade-offs among conflicting goals. *Example:* An autonomous vehicle that balances safety, speed, and fuel economy via a weighted utility function. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 1 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Module 1** carries a 14-mark Part B question with **internal choice**. We provide two independent alternatives below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a generic intelligent agent**. Discuss how percepts flow from the environment to the actuator interface. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Identify and justify the **PEAS specification** for an **autonomous medical diagnosis system** (e.g., IBM Watson for Oncology). Classify it according to the **Russell & Norvig agent taxonomy** and justify your classification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The generic intelligent agent architecture consists of four functional blocks connected in a **perception–action loop**:

1. **Sensors** capture raw environmental data (e.g., camera frames, microphone signals).
2. **Percept Processing Unit** converts raw data into structured percepts (e.g., feature vectors).
3. **Reasoning Engine / Agent Core** consults internal memory and goals to decide the next action.
4. **Actuators** translate the chosen action into physical or digital changes in the environment.

```
Environment --> Sensors --> Percept Processor --> Agent Core --> Actuator --> Environment
```

A feedback arrow from Actuator back to Environment completes the loop. In practical implementations, the *agent core* may host a simple rule base, a search algorithm, a neural network, or a hybrid neuro-symbolic system depending on the task complexity. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**PEAS for Autonomous Medical Diagnosis System:**

| Component | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Performance Measure** | Diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity), false-negative rate, patient outcome improvement. |
| **Environment** | Hospital records, patient symptoms, lab reports, medical literature database. |
| **Actuators** | Display screen showing diagnosis, alerts to physician, prescription recommendations. |
| **Sensors** | Keyboard/voice input of patient data, EHR database queries, medical imaging inputs. |

**Classification:** This is a **Goal-Based Agent** with **Learning** capabilities — it has an explicit goal (correct diagnosis) and improves over time by learning from physician feedback and updated medical literature. The environment is **partially observable** (full patient history may be unavailable) and **stochastic** (diseases manifest unpredictably), justifying a goal-based design over a simple reflex. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **five types of agents** in the Russell & Norvig taxonomy with one **real-world example** for each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **self-driving car agent** is observed for 1,000 miles of autonomous driving. It encounters 50 pedestrians suddenly crossing the road. Out of these, it successfully stops in 47 cases and fails in 3 cases. Compute its **safety performance measure** as a percentage. Justify whether the agent is **rational** if its rationality threshold requires $\geq 95\%$ safety. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Operates on direct condition-action rules. *Example:* A home **thermostat** that turns the heater ON when temperature falls below a threshold.
2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Maintains an internal world model to handle partial observability. *Example:* **iRobot Roomba** that remembers the room layout via SLAM.
3. **Goal-Based Agent** — Plans actions to achieve explicit goals. *Example:* **Google Maps** routing engine finding the optimal path to a destination.
4. **Utility-Based Agent** — Uses a utility function to handle trade-offs. *Example:* **Autonomous vehicle** balancing speed, safety, and comfort.
5. **Learning Agent** — Improves performance through experience. *Example:* **AlphaGo** by DeepMind, which learned to surpass human Go players via self-play reinforcement learning. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Safety Performance Measure Calculation:**

$$PM_{\text{safety}} = \frac{\text{Successful Stops}}{\text{Total Pedestrian Encounters}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the values:

$$PM_{\text{safety}} = \frac{47}{50} \times 100\% = 94\%$$

**Rationality Decision:**

The rationality threshold requires $\geq 95\%$ safety. Since $94\% < 95\%$, the agent **fails the rationality threshold** by $1\%$. The agent is therefore **not fully rational** under the given performance criterion. To improve, the agent's perception module must be enhanced (e.g., better pedestrian detection CNN) to reduce miss-detection rate from $6\%$ to $\leq 5\%$. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing rationality with omniscience** — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** when students write "a rational agent knows everything about the environment." Correct answer: rationality is based on *perceived information*, not complete knowledge.
> 2. **Forgetting the performance measure** — A definition of an agent without mentioning the *performance measure* is incomplete and costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong agent classification** — Many students incorrectly classify *Roomba* as a Simple Reflex agent instead of a *Model-Based Reflex* agent. Always justify your classification using environment properties (observability, determinism, episodicity).
> 4. **Missing PEAS components** — When asked to design an agent, students often list only sensors and actuators, forgetting *Performance Measure* and *Environment*. This costs **1 mark per missing component**.
> 5. **Not using equations** — For 14-mark Part B questions, including a relevant equation (like the agent function or utility maximization) adds **1–2 marks** for technical depth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- [x] **Agent Definition:** An autonomous entity that perceives via sensors, reasons via an agent core, and acts via actuators to maximize a *performance measure*.
- [x] **Agent Function:** $f : \mathcal{P}^{\ast} \to \mathcal{A}$ — the mathematical mapping from percept history to action.
- [x] **5 Agent Types (R&N Taxonomy):** Simple Reflex, Model-Based Reflex, Goal-Based, Utility-Based, Learning Agent.
- [x] **PEAS Framework:** Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — mandatory for any agent design question.
- [x] **Rational $\neq$ Omniscient:** Rational agents act on expected outcomes given available percepts, not on complete knowledge.
- [x] **Performance Measure Examples:** Disengagement rate (self-driving cars), Elo rating (chess), CTR (search engines), sensitivity/specificity (medical AI).
- [x] **Key Equations to Memorize:**
   - Agent Function: $f : \mathcal{P}^{\ast} \to \mathcal{A}$
   - Utility Maximization: $a^{\ast} = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s^{\prime}} P(s^{\prime} \mid s,a) \cdot U(s^{\prime})$
   - Q-Learning Update: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a^{\prime}} Q(s^{\prime}, a^{\prime}) - Q(s,a)]$
   - Discounted Reward: $G_{t} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$
- [x] **Environment Properties:** Observable / Partially Observable / Unobservable; Deterministic / Stochastic; Episodic / Sequential; Static / Dynamic; Discrete / Continuous; Single-agent / Multi-agent.
- [x] **Real-World Mapping to Remember:**
   - Thermostat → Simple Reflex
   - Roomba → Model-Based Reflex
   - Google Maps → Goal-Based
   - Self-Driving Car → Utility-Based
   - AlphaGo / Siri → Learning Agent
- [x] **CO1 Alignment:** This topic maps directly to **Course Outcome 1** — *Understand the foundational concepts of AI and intelligent agents* — at **Bloom Level: Understand / Analyze**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving Problems by Searching - Problem Solving Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solving Problems by Searching - Problem Solving Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Problem-Solving Agent** is a special class of **goal-based agent** that, when faced with a complex decision-making situation, explicitly **formulates a problem**, performs a **search** through a state space to discover a sequence of actions (a *solution path*) that leads from the current world state to a desired *goal state*, and then **executes** those actions one by one.

In the canonical KTU reference (Russell & Norvig paradigm adopted in the 2024 syllabus), a problem is formally defined as a **5-tuple** of mathematical components:

$$
\text{Problem} \;=\; \langle S, s_0, \text{ACTIONS}, \text{RESULT}, \text{GOAL-TEST} \rangle
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Examiners consistently test whether a student can name and explain the *five* components of a well-defined problem and the *two* additional parameters (*path-cost* and *step-cost*) needed to define optimization. Memorize the tuple $\langle S, s_0, ACTIONS(s), RESULT(s,a), GOAL-TEST(s) \rangle$ verbatim.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Chef"

Imagine you are a chef blindfolded in a strange kitchen. You are told: *"Make me coffee."*

- You do **not** have a complete map of the kitchen.
- You have a **goal** (coffee in the cup).
- You must **formulate the problem**: locate the jar, open it, find a spoon, find the cup, etc.
- You must **search** among possible action sequences ("reach jar" vs "reach cup" first).
- Once you have a plan, you **execute** it.

The agent behaves exactly the same: it does not know the full world in advance; it uses its percepts to *know* its current state, and it uses reasoning to *imagine* future states until it reaches the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Layer Abstraction (Essential for KTU):**
> 1. **Goal Formulation** — *What do I want?* (e.g., "Get a coffee").
> 2. **Problem Formulation** — *What are the states and actions?* (abstract world model).
> 3. **Search** — *Which sequence of actions achieves the goal?* (algorithm).
> 4. **Execution** — *Perform the actions.*

### 1.3 Why Agents Need Explicit Search

Simple **reflex agents** map percepts directly to actions. They fail when:
- The action sequence is **long**.
- **Future consequences** of actions matter.
- The environment is **partially observable** or stochastic.

Search lifts the agent from the "now" into a reasoning horizon across many possible futures.

| Term | Standard KTU Notation | Engineering Units / Type |
|------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **Branching factor** | $b$ | dimensionless (avg. successors per node) |
| **Depth of shallowest goal** | $d$ | dimensionless (number of action steps) |
| **Maximum depth of tree** | $m$ | dimensionless |
| **Path cost** | $g(n)$ | scalar (e.g., km, ₹, seconds) |
| **Step cost** | $c(s, a, s')$ | non-negative real number $\geq 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five (+Two) Components of a Well-Defined Problem

A KTU examination answer is considered **incomplete** if a student defines the problem with fewer than five components. The extended formulation has **seven** elements.

1. **State Space $S$** — The *implicit* set of all world configurations reachable from the initial state by any sequence of valid actions. The space is usually too large to enumerate (e.g., chess has $\approx 10^{47}$ states).

2. **Initial State $s_0$** — The state in which the agent begins. It is the *entry point* of every search algorithm.

3. **Actions $ACTIONS(s)$** — The set of all legal actions an agent can execute in state $s$.
$$
ACTIONS(s) \;=\; \{ a \mid a \text{ is a legal action in state } s \}
$$

4. **Transition Model (Successor Function) $RESULT(s, a)$** — Describes the result of performing action $a$ in state $s$.
$$
RESULT : S \times A \rightarrow S
$$
$$
s' \;=\; RESULT(s, a)
$$

5. **Goal Test $GOAL$-$TEST(s)$** — A boolean function that returns *True* if $s$ satisfies the goal specification. Goals can be **explicit** (a specific state) or **implicit** (a property, e.g., "no queen attacks another").

6. **Path Cost Function $g(n)$** — A non-negative scalar function that assigns a numerical cost to a path. The cost of a path is the **sum of step costs**:
$$
g(\text{path}) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(s_{i-1}, a_i, s_i)
$$

7. **Step Cost $c(s, a, s')$** — The cost of a single transition from $s$ to $s'$ via action $a$. KTU assumes $c \geq 0$ (no negative edges unless explicitly stated for adversarial search).

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Component:**
> - $S$ + $s_0$ + $ACTIONS$ + $RESULT$ define the **graph** of possibilities.
> - $GOAL$-$TEST$ defines **what success looks like**.
> - $c$ + $g$ define **how we measure success** (so we can prefer cheaper paths).

### 2.2 Solution vs. Optimal Solution

- A **solution** is any sequence of actions leading from $s_0$ to a goal state $g$.
- An **optimal solution** is the solution with the **lowest path cost** among all solutions.

$$
\text{OptimalCost} \;=\; \min_{p \in \text{Solutions}} g(p)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Mathematical Form | Constraints |
|--------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| $S$ | State space | Set of all reachable states | $S \ni s_0$ |
| $s_0$ | Initial state | $s_0 \in S$ | Unique start node |
| $ACTIONS(s)$ | Legal actions at $s$ | Subset of $A$ | May be empty |
| $RESULT(s, a)$ | Successor function | $S \times A \rightarrow S$ | Deterministic unless stochastic |
| $GOAL$-$TEST(s)$ | Goal predicate | $S \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ | Boolean output |
| $c(s, a, s')$ | Step cost | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | $c \geq 0$ |
| $g(n)$ | Path cost to node $n$ | $\sum c$ | Additive |
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic (Module 3) | Estimate of remaining cost | $h \geq 0$ |
| $f(n)$ | Evaluation function | $g(n) + h(n)$ | A* search |
| $b$ | Effective branching factor | $N \approx b^d$ | Avg. successors |
| $d$ | Depth of optimal solution | Integer | $\geq 1$ |
| $C^*$ | Cost of optimal solution | Scalar | $\min g(p)$ |
| $\epsilon$ | Redundancy factor | $b^d \cdot \epsilon$ | For graph search |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse $g(n)$ (path cost so far, Module 2) with $h(n)$ (heuristic estimate, Module 3) or $f(n)$ (combined, Module 3). The KTU 2024 paper frequently contains a 3-mark question asking the difference.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics (Navigation):** SLAM agents use problem formulation where states are $(x, y, \theta)$ poses, actions are wheel velocities, and the cost is energy or distance.
- **VLSI Chip Design:** Crossroads of thousands of wires are routed as shortest-path search on a grid graph.
- **Logistics (Amazon/Flipkart Warehouses):** Travelling-salesman-like search for picker robots.
- **Game AI (Chess, Go):** State space = board positions; goal = checkmate; cost = opponent's material.
- **Industrial Assembly Sequencing:** Car factories plan weld sequences via search over assembly DAGs.
- **Air Traffic Control:** Route planning with fuel cost as $c$, conflict-avoidance as the goal test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From State Space to Search Tree

The search **graph** (state space) and the search **tree** are different objects. The graph is the underlying structure; the tree is what the algorithm actually traverses.

A **search node** $n$ is a data structure that represents a *state* plus information about how it was reached:

$$
n \;=\; \langle s, \text{parent}(n), \text{action}(n), \text{pathCost}(n), \text{depth}(n) \rangle
$$

For the **root** node $n_0$:

$$
\text{parent}(n_0) \;=\; \text{null}, \quad \text{pathCost}(n_0) \;=\; 0, \quad \text{depth}(n_0) \;=\; 0
$$

For any **child** node $n'$ generated by applying action $a$ to parent $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(n') &\;=\; RESULT(s(n), a) \\
\text{parent}(n') &\;=\; n \\
\text{pathCost}(n') &\;=\; g(n) + c(s(n), a, s(n')) \\
\text{depth}(n') &\;=\; \text{depth}(n) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This recursive relationship is what the KTU examiner expects to see written for the 7-mark derivation of "How does a search algorithm maintain node information?"

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tree Search vs. Graph Search

The KTU 2024 syllabus makes a sharp distinction between these two strategies. Students must reproduce this matrix verbatim.

| Property | **Tree Search** | **Graph Search** |
|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Repeated-state check | **No** | **Yes** — uses an *explored set* (closed list) |
| Data structure | **Frontier** (open list) | **Frontier** + **Explored set** |
| Memory | Lower | Higher (stores visited states) |
| Completeness in finite spaces | Yes | Yes |
| Completeness in infinite spaces | No (may loop) | Yes (terminates) |
| Optimality | Algorithm-dependent | Algorithm-dependent |
| Used by | DFS variants | BFS, UCS, A* |
| Risk | Infinite loops on cycles | Higher memory footprint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** A common board question asks: *"Will Breadth-First Search (BFS) terminate on an infinite graph with no solution?"* The correct answer is **NO** for tree-BFS but **YES** (it will exhaust all reachable finite states before continuing) for graph-BFS on a graph with only finitely many reachable states from $s_0$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical: Path Cost in the Romania Tour Example

This is the **classic KTU worked example** (Russell & Norvig). Suppose an agent in **Arad** wants to reach **Bucharest**. Consider the partial path:

$$
\text{Arad} \xrightarrow{\text{Sibiu}} \text{Sibiu} \xrightarrow{\text{Rimnicu Vilcea}} \text{Rimnicu Vilcea}
$$

The relevant step costs (in km) are:

$$
c(\text{Arad}, \text{to Sibiu}, \text{Sibiu}) \;=\; 140
$$
$$
c(\text{Sibiu}, \text{to RV}, \text{RV}) \;=\; 80
$$

Compute the path cost $g$ at each node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(\text{Arad}) &\;=\; 0 \quad \text{(start, no action taken)} \\
g(\text{Sibiu}) &\;=\; g(\text{Arad}) + c(\text{Arad},\text{Sibiu}) \;=\; 0 + 140 \;=\; 140 \\
g(\text{RV}) &\;=\; g(\text{Sibiu}) + c(\text{Sibiu},\text{RV}) \;=\; 140 + 80 \;=\; 220
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **What this demonstrates:** Path cost is **additive** and built up **recursively** along the parent pointer. To reconstruct an optimal path, the algorithm follows the parent pointers backwards from the goal node to the root and reverses the list.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Toy-Problem Catalog (Board-Ready)

KTU Module 2 dedicates substantial weight to distinguishing *toy* from *real-world* problems. The complete catalog is:

| Problem | State Space Size | Action Set | Goal Test | Path Cost |
|---------|------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Vacuum World (1-cell) | 8 | {Left, Right, Suck, NoOp} | Both rooms clean | 1 per step |
| Vacuum World (2-cell) | 8 | {Left, Right, Suck} | All squares clean | 1 per step |
| 8-Puzzle | $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ | Slide a tile into blank | Goal configuration | 1 per move |
| 8-Queens (incremental) | $8^8$ | Place a queen in next column | 8 non-attacking queens | 0 (any is solution) |
| 8-Queens (complete-state) | $C(64, 8) \approx 4.4 \times 10^9$ | Move a queen | 8 non-attacking queens | 0 |
| Route Finding (Romania) | $\approx 20$ cities | Drive to neighbour | In Bucharest | km or time |
| TSP (Romania) | $20!$ | Visit next city | Visit all, return | km total |
| VLSI Layout | $\approx 10^{25}$ | Place a cell | All placed, no overlap | Area or wire length |
| Robot Navigation | Continuous in $SE(2)$ | Turn, Move | Reach pose | Energy / distance |
| Assembly Sequencing | Exponential in parts | Add a part | Full assembly | Time, cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write that the 8-queens state space is "$8^8$" without specifying *which* formulation. The state space **drastically changes** with the formulation. State this clearly in the exam.

### 3.5 Formal Definition of "Well-Defined Problem"

A problem is **well-defined** in the KTU sense iff:

- The initial state is **fully known** and the agent has **full observability**.
- The environment is **deterministic** (the next state depends only on current state and action).
- The state space is **discrete** (or discretised).
- The action set and goal test are **finite and known**.

When any of these is violated, the problem is called **ill-defined** and we must escalate to **planning under uncertainty / partial observability** (later modules).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Problem-Solving Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Percept via Sensors] --> B[Update Internal State]
    B --> C{Goal Formulated?}
    C -- No --> D[Formulate New Goal]
    D --> E[Formulate Problem]
    C -- Yes --> E
    E --> F[Search for Solution]
    F --> G{Solution Found?}
    G -- No --> H[Re-Formulate Problem]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Execute Action]
    I --> J[Environment Effect]
    J --> K[Update Perception]
    K --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical KTU "perceive–think–act" loop specialised for problem-solving agents. The four phases (formulate goal, formulate problem, search, execute) map exactly to the syllabus bullets.

### 4.2 State Space Graph vs. Search Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((S0)) --> B((S1))
    A --> C((S2))
    B --> D((S3))
    B --> E((S4))
    C --> F((S5))
    C --> G((S3))
    D --> H((Goal))
    E --> I((Dead))
    F --> J((Dead))
    G --> H2((Goal))
```

The above is the **state space graph**. Notice that node $S_3$ appears **twice** (reachable via two different paths). The corresponding **search tree** would have *two separate copies* of $S_3$ — one for each distinct path. This duplication is the source of redundant work that *graph search* eliminates by maintaining an explored set.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Search Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[Initialize Frontier with Initial Node] --> Loop{Frontier Empty?}
    Loop -- Yes --> Fail[Return Failure]
    Loop -- No --> Pop[Pop Node from Frontier]
    Pop --> Test{Goal Test Passes?}
    Test -- Yes --> Succ[Return Solution]
    Test -- No --> Expand[Generate Successors via RESULT]
    Expand --> Add[Add Successors to Frontier]
    Add --> Loop
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** This single block diagram subsumes **every** search algorithm studied in this module (BFS, UCS, DFS, DLS, IDS, Bidirectional). The *only* difference between algorithms is the **data structure** used for the *Frontier* (FIFO queue for BFS, priority queue keyed on $g$ for UCS, LIFO stack for DFS, etc.) and whether we maintain an *explored set*.

### 4.4 General Tree Search Algorithm (Pseudocode Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Input: Problem Definition] --> Q1[Set Frontier to MAKE-QUEUE MAKE-NODE-Initi]
    Q1 --> Q2{Frontier EMPTY?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Return FAILURE]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Node = REMOVE-FRONT Frontier]
    Q4 --> Q5{GOAL-TEST on Node STATE?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Return Node Solution Pointer]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[For each action in ACTIONS Node.STATE]
    Q7 --> Q8[Child = CHILD-NODE Problem, Node, action]
    Q8 --> Q9[Add Child to Frontier]
    Q9 --> Q2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a problem in AI. List and briefly explain the five essential components required to formulate a well-defined problem.*

**Model Answer (Expected length: 1 page):**

A problem, in the context of AI search, is a precise mathematical description of the world whose states, actions, and goals the agent must reason about. The five essential components of a well-defined problem are:

1. **Initial State $s_0$** — The state in which the agent begins problem-solving. Example: In the Romania route-finding problem, the initial state is *In(Arad)*.
2. **State Space $S$** — The set of all states reachable from $s_0$ by any sequence of actions. The set is defined **implicitly** (never enumerated fully) because it can be enormous.
3. **Actions $ACTIONS(s)$** — The set of legal actions available to the agent in state $s$. Example: From *In(Arad)* the actions are *{Go(Sibiu), Go(Timisoara), Go(Zerind)}*.
4. **Transition Model $RESULT(s, a)$** — A function describing the state that results from doing action $a$ in state $s$. Example: $RESULT(In(Arad), Go(Sibiu)) = In(Sibiu)$.
5. **Goal Test $GOAL$-$TEST(s)$** — A boolean predicate determining whether a given state satisfies the goal. The goal can be an *explicit set of states*, an *abstract property*, or a *path condition*.

> **Examiner's Note:** For a 3-mark question, listing all five with a *single one-line example each* is sufficient. A diagram earns a bonus mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Slot 2]**
*Differentiate between a "toy problem" and a "real-world problem" in AI. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Toy Problem | Real-World Problem |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Purpose | Illustrate, teach, benchmark | Solve genuine engineering problems |
| State space | Concise, exact number | Huge, often continuous, inexact |
| Description | Precise, unambiguous | Approximate, requires modelling |
| Examples | 8-puzzle, vacuum world, 8-queens | Route finding, TSP, VLSI layout, robot navigation |

**Toy Examples:**
- **8-Puzzle:** $181{,}440$ states; goal is the canonical ordered tile arrangement.
- **Vacuum World:** A simplified 2-cell world where the agent must clean both squares.

**Real-World Examples:**
- **Route Finding (Google Maps):** States are (city, fuel-level) tuples; goal is reaching the destination.
- **Robot Navigation:** States are continuous poses $(x, y, \theta)$; goal is reaching a target location while avoiding obstacles.

> **Examiner's Note:** Mere listing without the differentiating column earns at most 2 of 3 marks. The contrast table is the high-yield strategy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question must be answered for a maximum of 14 marks. The typical split is 7 + 7 (two sub-parts). The student is expected to spend ~25 minutes per question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B.*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1, Q. Module 2-A]**

**(a)** *Explain the architecture of a problem-solving agent with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail the four phases: goal formulation, problem formulation, search, and execution.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider the Romania map problem. The agent is in Arad and wants to reach Bucharest. Given the road distances below, formulate the problem by writing down: (i) Initial state, (ii) Actions from Arad, (iii) Transition model, (iv) Goal test, (v) Path cost of going Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest (given distances 140, 80, 97, 101 km respectively). State whether the resulting search space is a graph or a tree, and explain why.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Draw the *Percept → Goal Formulation → Problem Formulation → Search → Action* loop (diagram in §4.1).
- Discuss each phase with one sentence of "why" and one of "how":
  - **Goal formulation:** Defines the success criteria; based on current state and performance measure.
  - **Problem formulation:** Describes the abstract world — states, actions, transition.
  - **Search:** Computes a sequence of actions (a solution).
  - **Execution:** Performs the actions, with continuous monitoring to handle discrepancies.
- Mention **offline vs. online** search: pre-computed plan vs. interleaved plan-and-act.

**Model Solution (b):**

- (i) $s_0 = In(Arad)$.
- (ii) $ACTIONS(In(Arad)) = \{Go(Sibiu), Go(Timisoara), Go(Zerind)\}$.
- (iii) $RESULT(In(Arad), Go(Sibiu)) = In(Sibiu)$; etc.
- (iv) $GOAL$-$TEST(s) = (s = In(Bucharest))$.
- (v) Path cost computation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
g(\text{Sibiu}) &\;=\; 0 + 140 \;=\; 140 \\
g(\text{RV}) &\;=\; 140 + 80 \;=\; 220 \\
g(\text{Pitesti}) &\;=\; 220 + 97 \;=\; 317 \\
g(\text{Bucharest}) &\;=\; 317 + 101 \;=\; 418
\end{aligned}
$$
- The underlying state space is a **graph** (cycles exist; e.g., Arad–Sibiu–RV–Pitesti–Bucharest has multiple routes, and one could return to Arad). The *search tree* generated by the algorithm may contain **duplicated states** because the graph has cycles.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative.*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2, Q. Module 2-B]**

**(a)** *Compare and contrast tree search and graph search algorithms. For each, state the data structure required and discuss completeness, optimality, and memory implications. Which is preferred for finite graphs with cycles, and why?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe any two of the following toy problems in detail, including state representation, action set, goal test, path cost, and size of state space: (i) 8-Puzzle, (ii) 8-Queens, (iii) Vacuum World. For each, state whether the formulation yields a graph or a tree, and one real-world analogy.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Tree Search:**
- Data structure: only a **Frontier** (open list).
- Repeats states: **allowed** — the same state may appear as multiple nodes.
- Completeness: **NOT** complete in graphs with cycles (infinite loops).
- Memory: smaller.

**Graph Search:**
- Data structure: **Frontier** + **Explored set (closed list)**.
- Repeats states: **discarded** upon detection.
- Completeness: **Complete** in finite spaces, optimal in many cases.
- Memory: higher (closed set grows with state space).

**Preferred:** For finite graphs with cycles (e.g., Romania map), **graph search is preferred** because the explored set prunes redundant revisits and prevents infinite loops. BFS-as-graph-search is the standard choice when an unweighted shortest path is required.

> **Examiner's Note:** A common student error is to confuse "frontier" with "explored set." The frontier holds *generated-but-not-yet-expanded* nodes; the explored set holds *already-expanded* nodes. The two are disjoint at any instant in BFS/UCS.

**Model Solution (b):**

**8-Puzzle (5 marks if two are done):**
- **State:** A 3×3 array with 8 numbered tiles and one blank.
- **State space size:** $9! / 2 = 181{,}440$ reachable states.
- **Initial state:** Any reachable configuration.
- **Actions:** {Move blank Up, Down, Left, Right} — at most 4, fewer near edges.
- **Transition model:** Move the tile into the blank; the blank takes the previous tile position.
- **Goal test:** Check whether tiles match the canonical goal arrangement.
- **Path cost:** 1 per move.
- **Graph or tree:** Underlying state space is a **graph** (one state can be reached by multiple paths).
- **Real-world analogy:** Container yard re-ordering; tile-sliding in logistics.

**Vacuum World (2 marks):**
- **State:** Tuple $(L, R, A)$ where $L, R \in \{Dirty, Clean\}$ and $A \in \{Left, Right\}$.
- **State space size:** 8 states.
- **Actions:** {Left, Right, Suck}.
- **Goal test:** $L = Clean \land R = Clean$.
- **Path cost:** 1 per action.
- **Real-world analogy:** A floor-cleaning robot in two adjacent rooms.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing states with nodes:** A *state* is a world configuration; a *node* is a data structure that also records *parent*, *action*, and *path-cost*. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if the answer is silent on this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the path cost:** Many students list only the first five components and forget to mention the path cost function $g(n)$. In a 14-mark question, this omission typically costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing toy and real-world:** If the question asks for a "real-world" problem and the student gives the 8-puzzle as an example, **0 marks** for that sub-part.
> 4. **Tree search vs. graph search confusion:** Both are complete on finite acyclic graphs, but the distinction matters on cyclic/infinite graphs. The KTU key demands the word *"explored set"* to award full credit for graph search.
> 5. **Mis-stating branching factor:** Branching factor $b$ is the **average** number of successors, not the maximum. Writing "$b = 4$" for the 8-puzzle is acceptable as an *upper bound*, but saying "the branching factor is always 4" is technically incorrect.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Five Components (Must Memorize):** $S$, $s_0$, $ACTIONS(s)$, $RESULT(s, a)$, $GOAL$-$TEST(s)$.
- **The Two Cost Functions:** Step cost $c(s, a, s')$ and path cost $g(n) = \sum c_i$.
- **The Three Phases of a Problem-Solving Agent:** Goal Formulation → Problem Formulation → Search → (then Execute).
- **State vs. Node:** State = world snapshot; Node = state + parent + action + path cost + depth.
- **Tree Search vs. Graph Search:** Tree = no explored set; Graph = frontier + explored set.
- **Solution vs. Optimal Solution:** Solution = any path to goal; Optimal = minimum cost path.
- **Toy vs. Real-World Problems:** Toy = small, exact (e.g., 8-puzzle); Real-world = large, continuous, approximate (e.g., robot navigation).
- **Well-Defined Problem Assumptions:** Fully observable, deterministic, discrete state space, known actions and goal.
- **Path Cost is Additive:** $g(n) = g(\text{parent}(n)) + c(s(\text{parent}(n)), a, s(n))$.
- **Reconstructing a Solution:** Follow parent pointers from the goal node back to the root, then reverse the sequence.
- **BFS, UCS, DFS** all share the *same algorithmic skeleton* (Fig. 4.3); the *only* difference is the **data structure** used for the frontier.
- **The Romania Map** is the *standard worked example* for KTU 2024. Memorize: $s_0 = In(Arad)$, Goal $= In(Bucharest)$, road distances, and the fact that the optimal Arad → Bucharest path has cost **418 km** (via Sibiu → RV → Pitesti).
- **Search Tree** is *generated* by an algorithm; the **State Space Graph** is the *underlying mathematical structure*. Multiple paths in the graph correspond to multiple nodes for the same state in the tree.
- **Frontier Discipline:** Always state which data structure backs the frontier in a 14-mark answer — LIFO stack (DFS), FIFO queue (BFS), priority queue keyed on $g$ (UCS), priority queue keyed on $f$ (A*).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL NEFORMERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Example Problems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **OEERT615 – Introduction to Artificial Intelligence (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, the topic **"Example Problems"** refers to the **worked-out illustrative problem instances and numerical/algorithmic exercises** used to demonstrate the behavior of classical AI problem-solving techniques. These examples cover the operational mechanics of: - **Uninformed (Blind) Search Strategies** — Breadth-First Search (BFS), Uniform-Cost Search (UCS), Depth-First Search (DFS), Depth-Limited Search (DLS), Iterative Deepening Search (IDS), Bidirectional Search. - **Informed (Heuristic) Search Strategies** — Greedy Best-First Search, **A* Search**, Iterative Deepening A* (IDA*). - **Heuristic Function Analysis** — Admissibility, Consistency (Monotonicity), Dominance, Manhattan vs. Euclidean vs. Misplaced Tile heuristics. - **Adversarial Search** — **MINIMAX algorithm**, **Alpha-Beta Pruning**, Expectimax. - **Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)** — Backtracking, Forward Checking, AC-3 Arc Consistency. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** "Example problems" in AI are standardized problem instances (graph search, 8-puzzle, route-finding, game trees) used to **analyze, compare, and evaluate** the efficiency, optimality, and completeness of search algorithms against the formal definitions of the **PEAS framework** and **problem formulation $(S, A, \text{Cost}, \text{Goal})$**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **delivery driver in Kochi** trying to find the **shortest route** from **Vytilla Junction** to **Rajaji Nagar**. You have: - A **city map** (the search space / state space). - Multiple possible paths (the **action set $A$**). - A traffic-time estimate for each road segment (the **step cost $g(n)$**). - A "straight-line distance" to destination you glance at on a GPS (the **heuristic $h(n)$**). **"Example Problems"** in AI are the equivalent of sitting down with a tutor who draws this map on a whiteboard and says: *"Let's trace through exactly what BFS does… now let's see how A* would handle this road… now let's see how MINIMAX works if your rival driver tries to block you."* They convert abstract algorithms into **predictable, traceable, step-by-step numerical exercises** so the student can manually verify the algorithm's output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Example Problems" Matter in KTU Exams:** Approximately **40-50% of Module 2 marks** in the KTU 2024 ESE/IA pattern are awarded to **graph/tree search worked problems** (state expansion order, path cost calculation, A* optimality proof trace, and Alpha-Beta pruning tree). Mastering example problems is therefore **non-negotiable** for a passing grade. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in AI Search The following are the **four foundational metrics** evaluated on every AI search example problem: | Metric | Definition | Notation | |---|---|---| | **Completeness** | Does the algorithm always find a solution if one exists? | Binary (Yes/No) | | **Optimality** | Does it find the *lowest-cost* solution? | Binary (Yes/No) | | **Time Complexity** | Number of nodes generated/expanded | $O(b^d)$ typical | | **Space Complexity** | Maximum nodes held in memory simultaneously | $O(b^d)$ typical | Where **$b$** = branching factor and **$d$** = depth of shallowest goal. > [!TIP] > **Key Constants / Standards to Remember:** Branching factor $b$ in tree search, depth of optimal solution $d$, maximum depth $m$, and the heuristic values $h(n)$ must always be reported with **$h(\text{goal}) = 0$** in any consistent heuristic. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PEAS + Problem Formulation Framework Every "example problem" in KTU AI Module 2 begins with formalizing the task as a search problem using the tuple: $$\text{Problem} = \langle S, s_0, A, \text{Cost}, \text{Goal} \rangle$$ Where: - $S$ = set of all states in the state space. - $s_0 \in S$ = the initial state. - $A(s)$ = set of actions available in state $s$. - $\text{Cost}(s, a, s')$ = the step cost of taking action $a$ in state $s$ to reach state $s'$. - $\text{Goal}(s)$ = the goal test (returns True if $s$ is a goal state). ## 2.2 Search Algorithm Evaluation Summary | Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space | Backedge? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **BFS** | Yes (finite $b$) | Yes (uniform $k$) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | No | | **UCS** | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Yes | | **DFS** | No (if $m=\infty$) | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No | | **DLS** | No | No | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b\ell)$ | No | | **IDS** | Yes | Yes (uniform $k$) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | No | | **Bidirectional** | Yes | Yes (uniform $k$) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ | No | | **Greedy Best-First** | No | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^m)$ | Yes | | **A* Search** | Yes (finite, $h$ admissible) | Yes ($h$ admissible) | $O(b^d)$ worst | $O(b^d)$ worst | Yes | ## 2.3 The A* Evaluation Function — The Heart of Module 2 The single most important formula in KTU AI Module 2 is the **A\* evaluation function**: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$ Where: - $g(n)$ = the **actual cost** from the start node $s_0$ to the current node $n$ (path cost so far). - $h(n)$ = the **heuristic estimate** of the cost from $n$ to the nearest goal node. - $f(n)$ = the **total estimated cost** of the cheapest solution passing through $n$. ### Why Does A* Work? The Operational Logic 1. A\* maintains an **Open list (frontier)** ordered by $f(n)$ and a **Closed list (visited)** of expanded nodes. 2. The node with the **lowest $f(n)$** is expanded first. 3. For every successor $n'$ of the expanded node $n$, compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n', a)$ and $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$. 4. If $n'$ is already in Open with a higher $g$, replace it. If $n'$ is in Closed, reopen it (graph search). 5. Terminate when the goal node is popped from Open (guaranteed optimal if $h$ is admissible). ## 2.4 Heuristic Quality Conditions | Property | Definition | Math Statement | |---|---|---| | **Admissibility** | $h(n)$ never overestimates the true cost to goal | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$ | | **Consistency (Monotonicity)** | $h$ satisfies triangle inequality along any edge | $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ | | **Dominance** | $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ if $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$ | $h_2 \geq h_1$ pointwise | ## 2.5 Alpha-Beta Pruning — The MINIMAX Optimization Alpha-Beta Pruning computes the same value as MINIMAX but prunes branches that cannot influence the final decision. The two parameters passed down the tree are: - $\alpha$ = the value of the best (highest) choice we have found so far for the **MAX** player along the path to the root. - $\beta$ = the value of the best (lowest) choice we have found so far for the **MIN** player along the path to the root. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pruning Condition:** A node is pruned (branch cut off) when **$\alpha \geq \beta$**. The value returned by Alpha-Beta is **identical** to standard MINIMAX, but the number of nodes evaluated drops from $O(b^m)$ to $O(b^{m/2})$ in the best case (with perfect move ordering). ## 2.6 Real-World Utility - **GPS Navigation (Google Maps):** Uses variants of A* with road-network heuristics. - **Game AI (Chess, Checkers):** Uses Alpha-Beta pruning to search 8-12 plies deep. - **Robotics Path Planning (Warehouse Robots at Amazon):** UCS + heuristics for shortest-pick-path. - **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Bidirectional search for cross-docking optimization. - **Medical Diagnosis:** Heuristic-driven state-space search for differential diagnosis trees. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A* Search on a Romanian-Style Map ### Problem Statement Consider the following graph where **Arad (A)** is the start and **Bucharest (B)** is the goal. Step costs are shown on edges, and the heuristic $h(n)$ (straight-line estimate to B) is given for each node. | Node | $h(n)$ | Node | $h(n)$ | |---|---|---|---| | A (Arad) | 366 | R (Rimnicu) | 193 | | S (Sibiu) | 253 | P (Pitesti) | 100 | | T (Timisoara) | 329 | B (Bucharest) | 0 | | Z (Zerind) | 374 | F (Fagaras) | 176 | | O (Oradea) | 380 | C (Craiova) | 160 | **Edges (with road distance costs):** A–S = 140, A–T = 118, A–Z = 75, Z–O = 71, S–O = 151, S–F = 99, S–R = 80, T–O = 111, F–B = 211, R–P = 97, R–C = 146, P–B = 101, C–P = 138. ### Goal: Find the optimal path using A* and the order of node expansion. ### Step 1: Initialize Open = [A]; Closed = $\emptyset$. $f(A) = g(A) + h(A) = 0 + 366 = 366$. ### Step 2: Expand A (lowest $f$) Successors: S, T, Z. | Node | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | |---|---|---|---| | S | $0 + 140 = 140$ | 253 | **393** | | T | $0 + 118 = 118$ | 329 | **447** | | Z | $0 + 75 = 75$ | 374 | **449** | Open = [S(393), T(447), Z(449)]; Closed = [A]. ### Step 3: Expand S (lowest $f$ = 393) Successors of S: A (in Closed, skip), O, F, R. | Node | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | |---|---|---|---| | O | $140 + 151 = 291$ | 380 | **671** | | F | $140 + 99 = 239$ | 176 | **415** | | R | $140 + 80 = 220$ | 193 | **413** | Open = [R(413), F(415), T(447), Z(449), O(671)]. ### Step 4: Expand R (lowest $f$ = 413) Successors: S (in Closed, skip), P, C. | Node | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | |---|---|---|---| | P | $220 + 97 = 317$ | 100 | **417** | | C | $220 + 146 = 366$ | 160 | **526** | Open = [F(415), P(417), T(447), Z(449), C(526), O(671)]. ### Step 5: Expand F (lowest $f$ = 415) Successors: S (skip), B. | Node | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | |---|---|---|---| | B | $239 + 211 = 450$ | 0 | **450** | Open = [P(417), T(447), Z(449), B(450), C(526), O(671)]. ### Step 6: Expand P (lowest $f$ = 417) Successors: R (skip), B, C. A new path to B is found: $g(B) = 317 + 101 = 418$, $f(B) = 418$. **Since $418 < 450$**, replace B's $f$-value in Open. | Node | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | |---|---|---|---| | B (updated) | $317 + 101 = 418$ | 0 | **418** | | C (new path) | $317 + 138 = 455$ | 160 | 615 | Open = [B(418), T(447), Z(449), C(526), O(671)]. ### Step 7: Expand B (lowest $f$ = 418) → GOAL REACHED **Optimal path:** A → S → R → P → B **Optimal cost:** $140 + 80 + 97 + 101 = 418$ **Expansion order:** A, S, R, F, P, B. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning on a Game Tree ### Problem Statement A game tree of depth 4 (MAX at root and depth 2, MIN at depth 1 and depth 3) is shown below. Apply Alpha-Beta pruning. Terminal leaves are labeled L1–L8 with the values: 3, 12, 2, 8, 4, 6, 14, 5. ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ ? ? ? ? /|...\| /|...\| L1 L2 L3 L4 L5 L6 L7 L8 ``` Left subtree of left-MIN: leaves L1, L2, L3 → values 3, 12, 2. Right subtree of left-MIN: leaves L4, L5 → values 8, 4. Left subtree of right-MIN: leaves L6, L7 → values 6, 14. Right subtree of right-MIN: leaf L8 → value 5. ### Step-by-Step Alpha-Beta Pruning (Left-to-Right Order) Pass initial values: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. **Step 1 — Left-MIN, evaluate left MAX-subtree:** - MAX evaluates children: L1 = 3, L2 = 12. Since this is a MAX node with 2 children, return **$\max(3, 12) = 12$**. - MAX continues to L3 = 2, but no other children. So left-MAX = **12**. - Left-MIN receives 12. Now $\beta = \min(+\infty, 12) = 12$. Return to root-MAX: left-MIN returns **12**. Update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 12) = 12$. **Step 2 — Root-MAX, evaluate right-MIN subtree:** - Right-MIN explores its first child (left-MAX-subtree of right-MIN). - MAX evaluates L6 = 6, L7 = 14. So this MAX returns **14**. - Right-MIN gets 14. $\beta = \min(+\infty, 14) = 14$. - But $\alpha = 12$ already. Since $\alpha = 12 < \beta = 14$, no pruning yet. Continue. - Right-MIN explores its second child: leaf L8 = 5. - Right-MIN: $\beta = \min(14, 5) = 5$. - **PRUNING CHECK: $\alpha = 12 \geq \beta = 5$** → **PRUNE the rest of this subtree** (but L8 was the last leaf, so no more to prune). Return 5 from this MAX-subtree. - Right-MIN returns $\min(14, 5) = 5$. - Root-MAX returns $\max(12, 5) = 12$. ### Final Answer - **Value at root:** 12 - **Best move for MAX:** the left child (since $12 > 5$). - **Pruning occurred:** Yes, at the right-MIN when the check $\alpha \geq \beta$ triggered. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — UCS vs Greedy Best-First Comparison ### Problem Setup Use the same Arad map. **Start = A, Goal = B.** | Step | UCS path cost so far | Greedy choice ($h$) | |---|---|---| | 1 | Expand A (no path yet) | Expand A (lowest $h$ from frontier: T=329 vs S=253 vs Z=374) → **S** (lowest $h$ = 253) | | 2 | Frontier {S(140), T(118), Z(75)} → **Z(75) lowest g** | From S: expand F ($h$=176) vs R ($h$=193) vs O ($h$=380) → **F** | | 3 | Expand T ($g=118$) vs S ($g=140$) → **T(118)** | F → successor B ($h$=0!) → **B = goal** | > **Greedy's path:** A → S → F → B = 140 + 99 + 211 = **450** > **UCS optimal path (A\* without heuristic):** A → S → R → P → B = **418** > **Conclusion:** Greedy is **faster but not optimal**; UCS is **optimal but slow**; A\* combines both advantages. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Problem Type | Recommended Algorithm | Reason | |---|---|---| | Find shortest route, unit step cost | BFS | Optimal, complete for finite uniform graphs | | Find cheapest route, varying step cost | UCS | Optimal for weighted graphs | | Find *any* path, memory-constrained | DFS / DLS | Linear space, but incomplete | | Find optimal path, good heuristic available | **A\*** | Optimal + uses heuristic to guide | | Two-player zero-sum game tree | **MINIMAX with Alpha-Beta** | Optimal strategy with pruning | | Path planning with many constraints | CSP / Backtracking | Constraint propagation reduces search | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 A* Search Expansion Tree (Romanian Map Example) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["A: f=366
g=0 h=366"]:::start nodeS["S: f=393
g=140 h=253"]:::visited nodeT["T: f=447
g=118 h=329"] nodeZ["Z: f=449
g=75 h=374"] nodeR["R: f=413
g=220 h=193"]:::visited nodeF["F: f=415
g=239 h=176"]:::visited nodeO["O: f=671
g=291 h=380"] nodeP["P: f=417
g=317 h=100"]:::visited nodeC["C: f=526
g=366 h=160"] nodeB["B: f=418
g=418 h=0 GOAL"]:::goal nodeB2["B: f=450
g=450 h=0 discarded"] nodeA --> nodeS nodeA --> nodeT nodeA --> nodeZ nodeS --> nodeR nodeS --> nodeF nodeS --> nodeO nodeR --> nodeP nodeR --> nodeC nodeF --> nodeB2 nodeP --> nodeB classDef start fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px classDef visited fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef goal fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.2 Alpha-Beta Pruning Game Tree Architecture ```mermaid graph TD root["MAX
alpha=-inf beta=+inf"]:::maxNode minL["MIN-LEFT
alpha=12 beta=12"]:::minNode minR["MIN-RIGHT
alpha=12 beta=14"]:::minNode maxL1["MAX
children L1=3, L2=12, L3=2"]:::maxNode maxL2["MAX
children L6=6, L7=14"]:::maxNode maxR2["MAX
child L8=5 PRUNED"]:::pruneNode L1["L1=3"]:::leaf L2["L2=12"]:::leaf L3["L3=2"]:::leaf L6["L6=6"]:::leaf L7["L7=14"]:::leaf L8["L8=5"]:::leaf root --> minL root --> minR minL --> maxL1 minR --> maxL2 minR -.-> maxR2 maxL1 --> L1 maxL1 --> L2 maxL1 --> L3 maxL2 --> L6 maxL2 --> L7 maxR2 --> L8 classDef maxNode fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px classDef minNode fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px classDef leaf fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px classDef pruneNode fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#616161,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an A* Solver ```mermaid graph LR inputA["Input: Graph G,
start s0, goal G,
heuristic h"]:::input initB["Initialize Open = priority queue
Closed = empty set
g(s0) = 0"]:::process loopC{"Open empty?"}:::decision goalD{"Pop node n
is goal?"}:::decision returnE["Return path
from s0 to n"]:::output expandF["Generate
successors of n"]:::process evalG["For each successor n':
g prime = g n + c n n'
f prime = g prime + h n'"]:::process updateH["If n' in Open or Closed
with higher g: replace"]:::process outputI["Failure: no path exists"]:::output inputA --> initB initB --> loopC loopC -->|No| goalD goalD -->|Yes| returnE goalD -->|No| expandF expandF --> evalG evalG --> updateH updateH --> loopC loopC -->|Yes| outputI classDef input fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Heuristic Property Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD startH["Given heuristic h(n)"]:::input checkA{"h n less than or equal to h star n
for all n?"}:::decision checkB{"h n less than or equal to
c n a n prime + h n prime
for all edges?"}:::decision admissibleH["h is ADMISSIBLE"]:::output consistentH["h is CONSISTENT
(also implies admissible)"]:::output notH["h is NOT admissible
(A star not guaranteed optimal)"]:::output startH --> checkA checkA -->|No| notH checkA -->|Yes| checkB checkB -->|Yes| consistentH checkB -->|No| admissibleH classDef input fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Differentiate between informed and uninformed search strategies with one example each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Uninformed (Blind) Search:** Uses only the problem definition; no extra information about goal distance. Explores the state space systematically. Examples: **BFS, DFS, UCS, IDS**. (1½ marks) - **Informed (Heuristic) Search:** Uses a problem-specific heuristic function $h(n)$ to estimate the cost from any state $n$ to the goal, thereby guiding the search toward the goal efficiently. Examples: **Greedy Best-First Search, A\* Search**. (1½ marks) ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"State the admissibility and consistency conditions for a heuristic function. Why is consistency stronger than admissibility?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Admissibility:** A heuristic $h$ is admissible if $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all nodes $n$, i.e., it never overestimates the true cost to the goal. (1 mark) - **Consistency (Monotonicity):** $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for every edge $(n, n')$. (1 mark) - Consistency is **stronger** because every consistent heuristic is automatically admissible (triangle inequality along the optimal path implies $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$), and consistency additionally ensures that the $f$-values along any path are **non-decreasing**, which allows A* graph search to avoid reopening closed nodes. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **Consider the following graph for a route-finding problem. S is the start node, G is the goal. Step costs are on edges, and heuristic values $h(n)$ are given in the table.** | Node | A | B | C | D | E | G | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $h(n)$ | 6 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 0 | **Edges and costs:** S–A = 3, S–B = 2, A–B = 1, A–C = 4, B–D = 5, B–E = 3, C–E = 2, D–G = 4, E–G = 5. #### **(a) Apply A\* Search to find the optimal path from S to G. Show the order of node expansion and the $f$, $g$, $h$ values at each step. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Initialize:** Open = {S(0+6=6)}; Closed = {}. **Step 1: Expand S.** - Successors: A with $g=3$, $f=3+6=9$. B with $g=2$, $f=2+4=6$. - Open = {B(6), A(9)}. **Step 2: Expand B (lowest $f=6$).** - Successors: S (in Closed, skip), D with $g=2+5=7$, $f=7+5=12$. E with $g=2+3=5$, $f=5+2=7$. A: new $g=2+1=3$, $f=3+6=9$ (same as before, no improvement). - Open = {E(7), A(9), D(12)}. **Step 3: Expand E (lowest $f=7$).** - Successor: G with $g=5+5=10$, $f=10+0=10$. - Open = {A(9), G(10), D(12)}. **Step 4: Expand A (lowest $f=9$).** - Successors: B (skip), C with $g=3+4=7$, $f=7+3=10$. E is already in Open with $g=5$; new $g=3+?$ (no direct edge to E, skip). - Open = {G(10), C(10), D(12)}. **Step 5: Expand G (lowest $f=10$) → GOAL REACHED.** **Expansion order:** S → B → E → A → G. **Optimal path:** S → B → E → G. **Optimal cost:** $2 + 3 + 5 = 10$. #### **(b) Verify whether the heuristic $h$ is admissible and consistent. If not, suggest a corrected heuristic. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** - **Admissibility Check:** Compare $h(n)$ against the true shortest cost from each node to G. - $h^*(A) = ?$ Shortest path A→B→E→G = 1+3+5 = 9. But $h(A)=6 \leq 9$ ✓. - $h^*(B) = $ S→B→E→G from B: B→E→G = 3+5 = 8. $h(B) = 4 \leq 8$ ✓. - $h^*(C) = $ C→E→G = 2+5 = 7. $h(C) = 3 \leq 7$ ✓. - $h^*(D) = $ D→G = 4. $h(D) = 5 \not\leq 4$ ✗. - $h^*(E) = $ E→G = 5. $h(E) = 2 \leq 5$ ✓. - $h(G) = 0$ ✓. - **Conclusion:** $h$ is **NOT admissible** because $h(D) = 5 > h^*(D) = 4$. (3 marks) - **Consistency Check:** Test triangle inequality on each edge. - S→A: $h(S) = 6 \leq c(S,A) + h(A) = 3 + 6 = 9$ ✓. - S→B: $h(S) = 6 \leq 2 + 4 = 6$ ✓ (equal, acceptable). - A→B: $h(A) = 6 \leq 1 + 4 = 5$? **NO — $6 \not\leq 5$** ✗. - A→C: $h(A) = 6 \leq 4 + 3 = 7$ ✓. - B→D: $h(B) = 4 \leq 5 + 5 = 10$ ✓. - B→E: $h(B) = 4 \leq 3 + 2 = 5$ ✓. - C→E: $h(C) = 3 \leq 2 + 2 = 4$ ✓. - D→G: $h(D) = 5 \leq 4 + 0 = 4$? **NO — $5 \not\leq 4$** ✗. - E→G: $h(E) = 2 \leq 5 + 0 = 5$ ✓. - **Conclusion:** $h$ is **NOT consistent** (fails on edges A→B and D→G). (2 marks) - **Corrected Heuristic:** Reduce $h(D)$ to **4** (or less). New table: $h(D) = 4$. All other values remain admissible. (2 marks) --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]** **Consider the following game tree for a MAX-MIN game. Apply the Alpha-Beta pruning algorithm to compute the value at the root and identify the pruned branches.** ``` MAX (root) / \ MIN_a MIN_b / \ / \ MAX_c MAX_d MAX_e MAX_f /|....\| /|...\| /|..\| | 3 12 2 8 4 6 14 5 9 1 ``` **Leaf values (left to right):** Leftmost MAX_c subtree → 3, 12, 2. MAX_d subtree → 8, 4. MAX_e subtree → 6, 14. MAX_f subtree → 5, 9, 1. #### **(a) Trace Alpha-Beta Pruning step by step, showing the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ at each node. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Initialization:** $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. **1. Traverse Left-MIN_a subtree (leftmost MIN):** - **MAX_c** (left child of MIN_a): Evaluate leaves 3, 12, 2. - Return $\max(3, 12, 2) = 12$. - **MIN_a** receives 12. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 12) = 12$. - **MAX_d** (right child of MIN_a): Evaluate leaves 8, 4. - First leaf = 8. As we explore, we get 8. Return $\max(8, 4) = 8$. - **MIN_a:** Update value = $\min(12, 8) = 8$. Return 8 to root. - **Root (MAX):** Update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 8) = 8$. **2. Traverse Right-MIN_b subtree:** - **MAX_e** (left child of MIN_b): Evaluate leaves 6, 14. - Return $\max(6, 14) = 14$. - **MIN_b** receives 14. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 14) = 14$. - Check: $\alpha = 8 < \beta = 14$ → continue. - **MAX_f** (right child of MIN_b): Evaluate leaves 5, 9, 1. - First leaf = 5. Check at MIN_b: $\beta = \min(14, 5) = 5$. - Now **$\alpha = 8 \geq \beta = 5$** → **PRUNE the remaining leaves (9, 1) of MAX_f**. - MAX_f returns 5 (only the first leaf was evaluated). - **MIN_b** returns $\min(14, 5) = 5$. **3. Root MAX:** Returns $\max(8, 5) = 8$. **Final Result:** - **Root value = 8** (identically equal to MINIMAX result). - **Pruned leaves:** The leaves 9 and 1 under MAX_f were pruned. - **Best move for MAX:** Left branch (since 8 > 5). #### **(b) Compare BFS, DFS, and A* in terms of completeness, optimality, time and space complexity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Criterion | BFS | DFS | A* | |---|---|---|---| | **Complete?** | Yes (if $b$ finite) | No (fails in infinite trees) | Yes (if $h$ admissible and $b$ finite) | | **Optimal?** | Yes (only if step cost uniform) | No | Yes (if $h$ is admissible) | | **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ worst case, often less with good $h$ | | **Space Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(b^d)$ worst case | | **Uses Heuristic?** | No | No | Yes (via $f = g + h$) | (5 marks for the table + 2 marks for the narrative explanation: BFS expands level-by-level but is memory-intensive; DFS goes deep first using little memory but may miss the optimal solution; A* combines real path cost $g$ and heuristic $h$ to focus search on promising paths while still guaranteeing optimality.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to update $f$ values** when a shorter path to a node is discovered (especially in A* — this costs 1–2 marks). > 2. **Wrong pruning in Alpha-Beta:** Students often prune when $\alpha \leq \beta$ instead of $\alpha \geq \beta$. Memorize: prune at MIN nodes when value drops below $\alpha$, prune at MAX nodes when value exceeds $\beta$. > 3. **Confusing admissibility vs. consistency:** Admissibility is about overestimation; consistency adds the triangle inequality along edges. > 4. **Not reporting the expansion ORDER** (just the final path loses marks — KTU wants the sequence: e.g., "S → B → E → A → G"). > 5. **Skipping units/numerical labels** on the tree diagrams: always write $f$, $g$, $h$ values on the Mermaid-style or hand-drawn tree nodes. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Example Problems" (Module 2):** > - **Problem Formulation:** $S, s_0, A, \text{Cost}, \text{Goal}$ tuple must be explicitly stated in any search problem. > - **Four Evaluation Metrics:** Completeness, Optimality, Time, Space — must be cited for every algorithm. > - **BFS:** Optimal for uniform cost; uses FIFO queue; space-heavy. > - **UCS:** Optimal for varying costs; uses priority queue by $g$-value. > - **DFS:** Not optimal, not complete in infinite trees; space-efficient. > - **IDS:** Combines BFS optimality + DFS space efficiency via iterative depth limits. > - **A\* Core Formula:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — expand lowest $f$ first. > - **A\* Optimality:** Guaranteed only when $h$ is **admissible** (never overestimates). > - **A\* with Graph Search:** Requires $h$ to be **consistent** to avoid reopening closed nodes. > - **Heuristic Dominance:** If $h_2 \geq h_1$ pointwise and both admissible, then $h_2$ is better; it expands fewer nodes. > - **8-Puzzle Heuristics:** $h_1$ = misplaced tiles, $h_2$ = Manhattan distance. $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ and is always better. > - **MINIMAX:** Recursive value computation: MAX chooses max, MIN chooses min of successors. > - **Alpha-Beta Pruning:** Prune when $\alpha \geq \beta$. Best case reduces $O(b^m)$ to $O(b^{m/2})$. > - **Move Ordering:** Better ordering of successors in Alpha-Beta → more pruning. Examine "best" moves first. > - **PEAS:** Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — must define before solving. > - **CSP Triple:** Variables, Domains, Constraints — use backtracking + arc consistency for efficiency. > **Key Takeaway:** In KTU 2024 Module 2 "Example Problems," the algorithm choice depends on **(a)** whether the cost is uniform or weighted, **(b)** whether a heuristic is available, and **(c)** whether memory or optimality is the priority. A\* with an admissible, consistent heuristic remains the **gold standard** for single-agent path-finding, while Alpha-Beta pruning remains the **gold standard** for two-player adversarial search.

topics.title: Searching for Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Searching for Solutions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Searching for Solutions** in Artificial Intelligence refers to the systematic exploration of a *state space* — a formal mathematical representation of every possible configuration of a problem — to find a sequence of actions (a *path*) that transforms an **initial state** into one or more **goal states**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, this is the foundational *problem-solving paradigm* in classical AI, where the agent does not have a pre-stored plan but must *compute* a plan by traversing the problem graph. > [!IMPORTANT] > **State Space:** A directed graph $\langle S, A \rangle$ where $S$ is the set of all reachable world states and $A$ is the set of actions (edges) that connect one state to another. A problem is formally defined by the **PEAS** specification and four components: $$P = \langle S, s_0, A, G \rangle$$ where $S$ = set of states, $s_0$ = initial state, $A$ = actions, $G$ = goal test (a Boolean function over $S$). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Blindfolded Maze Imagine you are dropped into a pitch-dark maze holding a flashlight that reveals **only the room you are currently standing in**. You know the *shape* of the maze (the state space) but cannot see ahead. Your only option is to: 1. **Look at your current room** (examine the current state). 2. **Walk into an adjacent room** (apply an action). 3. **Repeat** until you find the exit (reach a goal state). This is exactly what a *search algorithm* does.
