Some strategies are like a person who **always turns left** (Depth-First Search), another is like a person who **marks visited rooms on a wall map** (Breadth-First Search), and a smart one uses a **compass pointing toward the exit** (Heuristic Search / A*). > [!NOTE] > **Why do we need search?** Most real-world AI problems (route planning, puzzle solving, game playing, scheduling) cannot be solved by direct formula lookup because the state space is astronomically large. Search provides a *principled way to allocate computational effort* to find good-enough solutions. ## 1.3 Classification of Search Strategies | Category | Information Used | Examples | |----------|-----------------|----------| | **Uninformed (Blind) Search** | Only the problem definition | BFS, DFS, Uniform Cost, Depth-Limited, Iterative Deepening, Bidirectional | | **Informed (Heuristic) Search** | Problem-specific heuristic $h(n)$ | Greedy Best-First, A*, IDA*, Beam Search | | **Local Search** | Iterative improvement on a single state | Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms | | **Adversarial Search** | Models an opposing intelligent agent | Minimax, Alpha-Beta Pruning, Expectimax | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly focuses on *Uninformed Search, Informed Search, and Game Playing (Minimax with Alpha-Beta)*. These three pillars are the most heavily tested in End Semester Examinations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Problem Formulation Pipeline (Step-by-Step) Before *any* search can run, the problem must be abstracted. The KTU-mandated pipeline is: 1. **Define the State Space $S$** — A *complete* description of the world at one instant. Example: For the 8-Puzzle, the state is the $3 \times 3$ arrangement of tiles. 2. **Initial State $s_0$** — Where the agent starts. Example: A specific scrambled 8-puzzle board. 3. **Actions $A(s)$** — The set of legal operators applicable in state $s$. Example: `{Move Blank Up, Move Blank Down, Move Blank Left, Move Blank Right}`. 4. **Goal Test $G(s)$** — A Boolean predicate deciding whether $s$ is a goal. Example: `s.matches(goal_board)`. 5. **Path Cost $c(s, a, s')$** — A numeric cost of moving from $s$ to $s'$ via action $a$. Example: Each step costs **1**. ## 2.2 Uninformed (Blind) Search — Operational Breakdown These algorithms ignore the *location of the goal* and explore mechanically. ### A. Breadth-First Search (BFS) - **Strategy:** Expand the *shallowest* unexpanded node first. - **Implementation:** FIFO queue. - **Why it works:** It systematically explores level-by-level, so the first goal found is at minimum depth. ### B. Depth-First Search (DFS) - **Strategy:** Expand the *deepest* unexpanded node first. - **Implementation:** LIFO stack. - **Why it works:** Dives down a single branch until it hits a dead end, then backtracks. ### C. Uniform-Cost Search (UCS) - **Strategy:** Expand the node with the *lowest path cost* $g(n)$ first. - **Implementation:** Priority queue keyed by $g(n)$. - **Why it works:** It is Dijkstra's algorithm on the state graph; finds the cheapest path. ### D. Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS) - **Strategy:** Run DFS with depth limit $= 0$, then $= 1$, then $= 2$, etc. - **Why it works:** Combines BFS's optimality with DFS's low memory. ## 2.3 Informed (Heuristic) Search These algorithms use an **evaluation function** to guide exploration toward the goal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heuristic Function $h(n)$:** Estimated cost of the *cheapest path* from node $n$ to a goal. It is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$. ### A. Greedy Best-First Search - **Evaluation function:** $f(n) = h(n)$ - Uses only the heuristic — fast but *not optimal* and *not complete* in graphs with loops. ### B. A* Search - **Evaluation function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ - where $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to goal. - If $h$ is **admissible**, A* is *optimal* and *complete* (with consistent $h$). - If $h$ is **consistent** (monotonic), A* is also optimally efficient. ## 2.4 Adversarial Search (Game Playing) Games are modeled as a **minimax tree** where two players, MAX and MIN, alternate. $$V(s) = \begin{cases} \text{utility}(s) & \text{if } s \text{ is terminal} \\ \max_{a \in A(s)} V(\text{result}(s, a)) & \text{if } s \text{ is a MAX node} \\ \min_{a \in A(s)} V(\text{result}(s, a)) & \text{if } s \text{ is a MIN node} \end{cases}$$ **Alpha-Beta Pruning** eliminates branches that cannot influence the final decision, without changing the minimax value. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Complete? | Optimal? | |-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------|----------| | BFS | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{d+1})$ | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | | DFS | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No | No | | Depth-Limited | $O(b^\ell)$ | $O(b\ell)$ | No (limit $\ell$) | No | | IDDFS | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | | UCS | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Yes | Yes | | Greedy Best-First | $O(b^m)$ worst | $O(b^m)$ worst | No | No | | A* | $O(b^d)$ (with good $h$) | $O(b^d)$ (graph) | Yes | Yes (admissible $h$) | | Minimax | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | Yes | Yes | | Alpha-Beta | $O(b^{m/2})$ best | $O(bm)$ | Yes | Yes | > [!NOTE] > Notation used: $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = maximum depth, $\ell$ = depth limit, $C^*$ = optimal path cost, $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Google Maps / Uber:** A* and bidirectional search for shortest routes. - **SAT Solvers:** Iterative deepening + heuristics to prove Boolean satisfiability. - **Robotics Path Planning:** RRT*, A* variants for obstacle avoidance. - **Chess Engines (Stockfish, AlphaZero):** Alpha-Beta pruning + deep heuristics. - **Network Routing:** UCS-derived algorithms like OSPF. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: BFS Time and Space Complexity **Step 1 — Define the branching factor and depth.** Let $b$ be the branching factor (maximum number of successors per node) and $d$ the depth of the shallowest goal node. **Step 2 — Count the root level.** The root has 1 node. Each child of the root has $b$ nodes. The grandchildren level has $b^2$ nodes. In general, the $k$-th level of the tree contains $b^k$ nodes. **Step 3 — Sum the nodes generated up to depth $d$.** The total number of nodes expanded before finding a goal at depth $d$ is the geometric series: $$N = 1 + b + b^2 + b^3 + \dots + b^d$$ **Step 4 — Apply the geometric series formula.** $$N = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$ **Step 5 — Bound it asymptotically.** For $b \gg 1$, the lower-order terms and the $-1$ become negligible, so: $$N \approx b^{d+1}$$ **Step 6 — Conclude the complexity.** Therefore, the time complexity is $O(b^{d+1})$. BFS must also store every generated node in its frontier (FIFO queue) so that it can revisit earlier levels, which yields a space complexity of $O(b^{d+1})$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Why A* is Optimal with an Admissible Heuristic **Step 1 — Assume for contradiction that A* returns a non-optimal goal $G_2$.** Let $G_1$ be an optimal goal with $f(G_1) = g(G_1) + h(G_1) = g(G_1) + 0 = C^*$ (since $h=0$ at goal). Let $G_2$ be the suboptimal goal actually returned, with $f(G_2) = g(G_2) = C_2 > C^*$. **Step 2 — Consider the moment just before $G_2$ is selected for expansion.** At this point, there must exist some node $n$ on the *optimal path* to $G_1$ still in the frontier. **Step 3 — Write $f(n)$.** Because $n$ is on the optimal path, $g(n) < C^*$. Because $h$ is admissible, $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$. Therefore: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) < C^* + 0 = C^*$$ Wait — we need a tighter bound. Since $h(n) \leq C^* - g(n)$, we have: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + (C^* - g(n)) = C^*$$ **Step 4 — Compare $f(n)$ and $f(G_2)$.** We have $f(n) \leq C^* < C_2 = f(G_2)$. So $f(n) < f(G_2)$. **Step 5 — Conclude.** A* always expands the node with the *lowest* $f$-value. Since $f(n) < f(G_2)$, A* must expand $n$ (and eventually $G_1$) before $G_2$. This contradicts the assumption that A* returned $G_2$. Hence, A* must return an optimal goal. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: Alpha-Beta Pruning on a Tic-Tac-Toe Subtree Consider the following game tree where MAX plays at the root and leaf values are terminal utilities: ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ 3 5 2 8 ``` **Step 1 — Root is a MAX node. Initialize $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.** **Step 2 — Explore left MIN child.** Visit leaf value $3$. The MIN node updates its value: $v = \min(\infty, 3) = 3$. Pass $(\alpha = -\infty, \beta = 3)$ to siblings. Since $\alpha \geq \beta$ is false ($-\infty < 3$), continue. **Step 3 — Visit next leaf in left subtree with value $5$.** MIN node updates: $v = \min(3, 5) = 3$. Now check pruning: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = 3$. No pruning. **Step 4 — Left MIN subtree returns $v = 3$.** Root MAX updates: $v = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$, $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$. **Step 5 — Explore right MIN child with $(\alpha = 3, \beta = +\infty)$.** Visit leaf value $2$. MIN updates: $v = \min(\infty, 2) = 2$. Now check: $\alpha = 3 \geq \beta = 2$ → **PRUNE!** **Step 6 — Right subtree is pruned after visiting only the first leaf.** **Result:** The minimax value of the root is still correctly computed as $3$, but the right leaf $8$ is never examined. We saved 1 node evaluation. > [!NOTE] > In the best case, alpha-beta pruning reduces the effective branching factor from $b$ to $\sqrt{b}$, giving $O(b^{m/2})$ time — a *huge* practical speedup. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BFS vs DFS vs A* | Property | BFS | DFS | A* | |----------|-----|-----|-----| | Uses heuristic? | No | No | **Yes** | | Memory efficient? | No (huge queue) | **Yes** | No (stores all generated) | | Finds shortest path? | Yes (unit cost) | No | **Yes** (admissible $h$) | | Risk of infinite loop? | No | Yes (in graphs) | No (with closed list) | | Backtracking needed? | No | Yes | No | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 State Space Search — Generic Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start]) --> initS[Initialize Frontier with s0] initS --> isEmpty{Frontier Empty?} isEmpty -- Yes --> failN[Return Failure] isEmpty -- No --> pickN[Select Node n from Frontier] pickN --> goalT{Goal Test n?} goalT -- Yes --> solN[Return Solution Path] goalT -- No --> expandA[Generate Successors of n] expandA --> addF[Add Successors to Frontier] addF --> isEmpty ``` ## 4.2 BFS vs DFS Expansion Order ```mermaid flowchart TD rootA((Root)) n1A((1)) & n2A((2)) & n3A((3)) rootA --> n1A rootA --> n2A rootA --> n3A n1A --> l1A[1.1] & l2A[1.2] & l3A[1.3] n2A --> m1A[2.1] & m2A[2.2] n3A --> p1A[3.1] & p2A[3.2] ``` > BFS order: Root → 1 → 2 → 3 → 1.1 → 1.2 → 1.3 → 2.1 → ... ```mermaid flowchart TD rootB((Root)) n1B((1)) & n2B((2)) & n3B((3)) rootB --> n1B rootB --> n2B rootB --> n3B n1B --> l1B[1.1] & l2B[1.2] & l3B[1.3] n2B --> m1B[2.1] & m2B[2.2] n3B --> p1B[3.1] & p2B[3.2] ``` > DFS order: Root → 1 → 1.1 → 1.2 → 1.3 → 2 → 2.1 → 2.2 → 3 → ... ## 4.3 A* Search Tree (with f, g, h values) ```mermaid flowchart TD startN((S
g=0 h=10 f=10)) startN --> n1A((A
g=4 h=7 f=11)) startN --> n1B((B
g=5 h=5 f=10)) n1A --> n2A((C
g=8 h=3 f=11)) n1A --> n2B((D
g=10 h=2 f=12)) n1B --> n2C((E
g=9 h=1 f=10 GOAL)) n1B --> n2D((F
g=11 h=4 f=15)) ``` > A* picks the lowest $f$ from the frontier at each step. Here it expands S → B → E (GOAL) with optimal cost 9. ## 4.4 Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning ```mermaid flowchart TD rootM((MAX
a=-inf b=+inf)) rootM --> mL((MIN
a=-inf b=+inf)) rootM --> mR((MIN
a=3 b=+inf)) mL --> mL1((3)) mL --> mL2((5)) mR --> mR1((2)) mR -.->|"PRUNED"| mR2((8)) ``` > The dashed edge shows the alpha-beta cut. The leaf '8' is never visited, yet the root's value remains correct. ## 4.5 Complete Search Algorithm Comparison Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subA[Uninformed Search] --> algo1[BFS] subA --> algo2[DFS] subA --> algo3[UCS] subA --> algo4[IDDFS] subB[Informed Search] --> algo5[Greedy Best First] subB --> algo6[A Star] subB --> algo7[IDA Star] subC[Adversarial Search] --> algo8[Minimax] subC --> algo9[Alpha Beta Pruning] subC --> algo10[Expectimax] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** > **Q1.** Differentiate between informed and uninformed search strategies. Give one example of each. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Uninformed (blind) search strategies use *only the problem definition* and have no additional information about the goal's location. They explore the state space mechanically. Example: Breadth-First Search (BFS) or Depth-First Search (DFS). *(1 mark)* > Informed (heuristic) search strategies use **problem-specific knowledge** in the form of a heuristic function $h(n)$ that estimates the cost from a node to the goal, thereby guiding the search more efficiently. Example: A* Search or Greedy Best-First Search. *(1 mark)* > Key differentiator: Informed search is typically *faster and more directed* because it uses $h(n)$, but it requires a domain-specific heuristic and may not be optimal if $h$ is not admissible. *(1 mark)* > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]** > **Q2.** What is a heuristic function? State the conditions for a heuristic to be admissible. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A *heuristic function* $h(n)$ is an **estimated cost of the cheapest path** from node $n$ to a goal state. It is a rule of thumb that guides informed search algorithms. *(1 mark)* > **Conditions for admissibility:** A heuristic is admissible if it *never overestimates* the true cost to reach a goal. Formally: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all nodes $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the actual minimum cost. *(1 mark)* > Example: For route-finding, the *straight-line distance* to the destination is admissible because the straight line is always shorter than any road path. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2-3 marks on Part B questions by (a) forgetting to state *time AND space* complexity, (b) drawing a search tree without showing **closed/visited sets**, and (c) omitting the **admissibility/optimality proof** for A*. Always show all components. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the A* search algorithm in detail. State and prove the condition under which A* is optimal. Define an admissible and consistent heuristic with examples. **Model Answer:** **A* Algorithm Definition (2 marks):** A* is a best-first search algorithm that uses the evaluation function: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$ where: - $g(n)$ = actual cost of the path from the initial state to node $n$. - $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the nearest goal. - $f(n)$ = estimated cost of the *cheapest solution* passing through $n$. A* maintains two lists — an **open list** (frontier) and a **closed list** (expanded nodes). At each step it picks the open node with the lowest $f$-value, expands it, and updates its neighbors. **Optimality Theorem (3 marks):** *If $h(n)$ is admissible, then A* is optimal — it always finds a goal with minimum path cost, provided the search tree has a finite branching factor and positive step costs.* *Proof Sketch:* Suppose A* terminates by selecting a suboptimal goal $G_2$ with $f(G_2) = g(G_2) = C_2 > C^*$, where $C^*$ is the optimal cost. Consider any node $n$ on the optimal path still in the open list. Since $h$ is admissible: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + (C^* - g(n)) = C^*$$ Thus $f(n) \leq C^* < C_2 = f(G_2)$. A* would expand $n$ before $G_2$ — contradiction. $\blacksquare$ **Admissible and Consistent Heuristic (2 marks):** - *Admissible:* $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Example: Straight-line distance in route finding. - *Consistent (Monotonic):* $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$. This is a *stronger* condition that implies admissibility and guarantees that $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path. Example: Manhattan distance in the 8-puzzle. --- **Q(b). [7 Marks]** Apply A* search to find the shortest path from node **S** to node **G** in the following graph. Heuristic values $h$ are given in parentheses. Show the open and closed lists at each step. ``` S(7) / \ 2 / \ 4 / \ A(5) B(4) / \ / \ 3/ 5\ 6/ \2 / \/ \ C(4) D(8) G(0) ``` **Model Solution:** | Step | Open List (with f) | Closed | Action | |------|-------------------|--------|--------| | 0 | $\{S(7)\}$ | $\emptyset$ | Expand S | | 1 | $\{A(7), B(8)\}$ | $\{S\}$ | Pick A (lowest f=7) | | 2 | $\{B(8), C(7), D(10)\}$ | $\{S, A\}$ | Pick C (f=7) | | 3 | $\{B(8), D(10)\}$ | $\{S, A, C\}$ | Pick B (f=8) | | 4 | $\{D(10), G(8)\}$ | $\{S, A, C, B\}$ | Pick G (f=8) — **GOAL** | **Path found:** $S \to B \to G$ with total cost $4 + 2 = 6$. For step 1: $f(S) = 0 + 7 = 7$, expand S. For A: $g(A) = 2, f(A) = 2 + 5 = 7$. For B: $g(B) = 4, f(B) = 4 + 4 = 8$. For C via A: $g(C) = 2+3 = 5, f(C) = 5 + 4 = 9$ — *Wait, recompute:* $f(C) = 5 + 4 = 9$? Let me verify: $g(C) = 2 + 3 = 5$, $h(C) = 4$, so $f(C) = 9$. For D via A: $g(D) = 2+5 = 7, f(D) = 7+8 = 15$. So step 2 open list: $\{B(8), C(9), D(15)\}$. Pick B (f=8). D via B: $g(D) = 4+6 = 10, f(D) = 10+8 = 18$ — not better. G via B: $g(G) = 4+2 = 6, f(G) = 6+0 = 6$ — **GOAL** with cost 6. **Optimal Path:** $S \to B \to G$, **Cost = 6**. *(7 marks distributed across: 2 marks algorithm setup, 3 marks open/closed table, 2 marks final path and cost)* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q(a). [7 Marks]** What is the Minimax algorithm? Explain with a suitable example tree. Also, explain alpha-beta pruning and show how it improves efficiency. **Model Answer:** **Minimax Definition (1 mark):** Minimax is a *recursive* adversarial search algorithm for two-player, zero-sum games with perfect information. One player (MAX) tries to maximize the utility, while the other (MIN) tries to minimize it. **Algorithm (2 marks):** $$V(s) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(s) & \text{if Terminal}(s) \\ \max_{a} V(\text{Result}(s,a)) & \text{if } s \text{ is MAX} \\ \min_{a} V(\text{Result}(s,a)) & \text{if } s \text{ is MIN} \end{cases}$$ **Example (2 marks):** Consider the tree: ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ 3 5 2 8 ``` - Left MIN node returns $\min(3,5) = 3$. - Right MIN node returns $\min(2,8) = 2$. - Root MAX returns $\max(3,2) = 3$. - **Best move for MAX:** Go left. **Alpha-Beta Pruning (2 marks):** Alpha ($\alpha$) is the best (highest) value MAX can guarantee so far; Beta ($\beta$) is the best (lowest) value MIN can guarantee. A branch is pruned when $\alpha \geq \beta$, because the player to move already has a better alternative elsewhere. *In our example:* After evaluating the left subtree, root has $\alpha = 3$. When examining the right MIN, after seeing leaf '2' we know the right subtree will return $\leq 2$. Since $3 \geq 2$, MAX will never choose the right branch — **prune the leaf '8'**. **Efficiency gain:** Best case reduces effective branching from $b$ to $\sqrt{b}$, so complexity drops from $O(b^m)$ to $O(b^{m/2})$. --- **Q(b). [7 Marks]** Compare the four major uninformed search strategies (BFS, DFS, UCS, IDDFS) in a table with respect to time, space, completeness, and optimality. State one real-world application of A* search. **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (6 marks):** | Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Complete? | Optimal? | |-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------|----------| | BFS | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{d+1})$ | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) | | DFS | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No | No | | UCS | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor + 1})$ | Same as time | Yes | Yes | | IDDFS | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) | Where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = max depth, $C^*$ = optimal cost, $\epsilon$ = min step cost. **Real-world Application of A* (1 mark):** A* is the *de facto* algorithm used in **Google Maps** and most GPS navigation systems. It uses heuristics such as straight-line distance or estimated time-of-arrival to find the fastest driving route between two locations in a road network containing millions of nodes. A* efficiently balances the actual distance driven ($g$) with the heuristic estimate of remaining distance ($h$) to return optimal routes in real time. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Searching for Solutions** - **Search = exploring a state-space graph** to find a goal; defined by $\langle S, s_0, A, G \rangle$. - **Uninformed search** uses no heuristic: BFS (queue), DFS (stack), UCS (priority queue on $g$), IDDFS (depth-loops). - **BFS is complete & optimal** for unit costs; **DFS is memory-efficient** but incomplete. - **IDDFS** combines BFS's optimality with DFS's low memory — $O(bd)$ space. - **Informed search** uses $h(n)$: Greedy uses $h(n)$ alone, A* uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. - **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates. Guarantees A* optimality. - **Consistent heuristic:** $h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ — stronger; gives graph-search optimality. - **Minimax** recursively computes optimal play; MAX maximizes, MIN minimizes leaf utilities. - **Alpha-Beta pruning** cuts branches where $\alpha \geq \beta$; best case $O(b^{m/2})$. - **Standard notations:** $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = goal depth, $m$ = max depth. - **Always state** time complexity, space complexity, completeness, AND optimality in exam answers. - **Common exam trap:** Forgetting to mention that DFS is incomplete in *infinite* state spaces or graphs with cycles. - **Heuristic design tip:** Relax the problem — the cost of an easier sub-problem is often an admissible heuristic.

topics.title: Uninformed Search Strategies - Depth First Search (DFS) and Breadth First Search (BFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uninformed Search Strategies - Depth First Search (DFS) and Breadth First Search (BFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Uninformed (Blind) Search?

In the context of Artificial Intelligence and the KTU OEERT615 syllabus, **Uninformed Search** (also called **Blind Search** or **Brute-Force Search**) refers to a family of search algorithms that operate without any additional information about the problem domain beyond its definition. The algorithm has no hint or heuristic about how close a state is to the goal — it must generate successors and distinguish a goal state from a non-goal state using only the problem's basic structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Uninformed Search is the *foundation* upon which Informed (Heuristic) Search is built. Every student must master BFS and DFS before moving to A* or Greedy Best-First Search.

The two most important uninformed search strategies are **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** and **Depth-First Search (DFS)**.

## 1.2 Breadth-First Search (BFS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Breadth-First Search is a uniform-cost, level-by-level graph traversal algorithm that expands the shallowest (closest to root) unexpanded node first. It uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) Queue** to manage the frontier (the set of nodes waiting to be expanded).

### Conceptual Analogy — Ripples on a Pond
Imagine dropping a stone into a still pond. The ripples spread **outward in concentric circles**. Each circle represents one "depth level" of the search tree. BFS is exactly like that — it visits all nodes at depth 0 (the root), then all nodes at depth 1, then all nodes at depth 2, and so on, *before* ever going deeper. If you were looking for a coin dropped at the bottom, BFS would exhaust every ripple on the first ring before looking at the second ring.

## 1.3 Depth-First Search (DFS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Depth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that expands the deepest unexpanded node first. It uses a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) Stack** to manage the frontier and backtracks only when a node has no more successors to explore.

### Conceptual Analogy — Exploring a Dark Maze with One Match
Imagine you are dropped into a pitch-dark maze with a single match. You follow a path as far as you can until you hit a dead end. Then you backtrack step-by-step to the last junction and try a *different* corridor. DFS does exactly this — it commits to one path, dives as deep as possible, then backtracks. The *first* solution found in DFS is **not guaranteed to be the shortest**.

## 1.4 Side-by-Side Comparison Snapshot

| Property | BFS | DFS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data Structure | **FIFO Queue** | **LIFO Stack** |
| Expansion Order | Shallowest first | Deepest first |
| Strategy Name | Level-by-level | Branch-by-branch |
| Mental Model | **Ripples in water** | **Maze exploration** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of BFS

The BFS algorithm executes the following loop until the goal is found or the frontier is empty:

1. **Initialize:** Enqueue the root node into the FIFO queue. Mark it as visited.
2. **Test:** If the queue is empty, return **FAILURE** (search exhausted). Otherwise, dequeue the **front** node $n$.
3. **Goal Test:** Check whether $n$ satisfies the goal condition. If yes, return $n$ (success).
4. **Expand:** Generate all successor nodes of $n$ that have not been visited. Mark them as visited and enqueue them at the **back** of the queue.
5. **Loop** back to Step 2.

### Why BFS Works
BFS systematically **sweeps through all nodes by increasing depth**. Because shallow nodes are always dequeued first, the first time the goal is dequeued, BFS has provably explored *every* node at shallower depths. Hence, the path returned is the shortest in terms of number of steps (edges).

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of DFS

The DFS algorithm executes the following loop:

1. **Initialize:** Push the root node onto the LIFO stack. Mark it as visited.
2. **Test:** If the stack is empty, return **FAILURE**. Otherwise, pop the **top** node $n$.
3. **Goal Test:** If $n$ is the goal, return $n$ (success).
4. **Expand:** Generate all successors of $n$ that are unvisited, mark and push them onto the **top** of the stack.
5. **Loop** back to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why and How of the Stack:** In DFS, the most recently generated nodes are placed on top of the stack. The next iteration pops the *last* one pushed, meaning DFS always dives into the **newest branch** first — that is the essence of "depth-first."

## 2.3 The Four Golden Evaluation Properties (KTU High-Yield)

These four properties are the *most frequently asked* topic in KTU examinations on search algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric Notations:**
> - $b$ = **branching factor** (maximum number of successors of any node)
> - $d$ = **depth of the shallowest goal node**
> - $m$ = **maximum depth of the state space** (could be infinity)

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BFS** | **Yes** *(if $b$ is finite)* | **Yes** *(if step costs are uniform)* | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{d})$ |
| **DFS** | **No** *(in infinite spaces)* / **Yes** *(in finite spaces)* | **No** | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ |

### Detailed Interpretation of Each Property

- **Completeness:** Will the algorithm *always* find a solution if one exists?
  - BFS: **Yes**, provided the branching factor $b$ is finite. The shallowest goal will eventually be reached.
  - DFS: **No** for infinite-depth spaces (it may follow an infinite branch). **Yes** for finite spaces, but may get stuck in loops without a visited-list.

- **Optimality (Admissibility):** Will the algorithm find the *best* (lowest-cost) solution?
  - BFS: **Yes** when every step has the same cost (unweighted graph).
  - DFS: **No.** It returns the *first* goal it encounters, which may be a long detour.

- **Time Complexity:** How many nodes are generated in the worst case?
  - BFS: Generates every node up to depth $d$, approximately $1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d = O(b^d)$.
  - DFS: Generates every node in the search tree up to the maximum depth $m$, approximately $O(b^m)$.

- **Space Complexity:** How much memory is needed?
  - BFS: Must store the **entire frontier** at the current level — about $O(b^d)$ nodes.
  - DFS: Only stores the path from root to the current node plus siblings — only $O(bm)$ nodes.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** BFS powers **shortest-path applications** like GPS navigation, social network friend-finders (degrees of separation), and network broadcast routing. DFS powers **cycle detection, topological sorting, solving puzzles** (Sudoku, mazes), and **garbage collection algorithms** in compilers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Trace)

## 3.1 Reference Graph for Trace

Consider the following unweighted, undirected graph. The task is to find a path from **Node S (Start)** to **Node G (Goal)**.

**Edges (connections):**
- S → A, S → B, S → C
- A → D, A → E
- B → F, B → G
- C → H
- D → G

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board exams, always **list the adjacency structure explicitly** before tracing — examiners award marks for clearly stated initial conditions.

## 3.2 Exhaustive BFS Trace

**Step 0 — Initialization:**
- Visited set: $\{$ S $\}$
- Queue (front → back): [ S ]
- Note: We use a queue where the *front* is the next node to expand.

**Step 1 — Dequeue S, test goal:** S is not the goal.
- Expand S → enqueue A, B, C.
- Visited: $\{$ S, A, B, C $\}$
- Queue: [ A, B, C ]

**Step 2 — Dequeue A (shallowest):** A is not the goal.
- Expand A → enqueue D, E.
- Visited: $\{$ S, A, B, C, D, E $\}$
- Queue: [ B, C, D, E ]

**Step 3 — Dequeue B:** B is not the goal.
- Expand B → enqueue F, G.
- Visited: $\{$ S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G $\}$
- Queue: [ C, D, E, F, G ]

**Step 4 — Dequeue C:** C is not the goal.
- Expand C → enqueue H.
- Visited: $\{$ S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H $\}$
- Queue: [ D, E, F, G, H ]

**Step 5 — Dequeue D:** D is not the goal.
- Expand D → successor G is *already visited*, so skip.
- Queue: [ E, F, G, H ]

**Step 6 — Dequeue E:** E is not the goal, no unvisited successors.
- Queue: [ F, G, H ]

**Step 7 — Dequeue F:** F is not the goal, no unvisited successors.
- Queue: [ G, H ]

**Step 8 — Dequeue G:** G is the **GOAL** → Return success.

**Final BFS Result:** Path S → B → G (depth 2, the shortest possible).

## 3.3 Exhaustive DFS Trace (Using a Stack)

**Step 0 — Initialization:**
- Visited: $\{$ S $\}$
- Stack (top is rightmost): [ S ]

**Step 1 — Pop S, test goal:** S is not the goal.
- Expand S → push A, B, C (push order: A first, then B, then C, so C is on top).
- Visited: $\{$ S, A, B, C $\}$
- Stack: [ A, B, C ] *(C at top)*

**Step 2 — Pop C:** C is not the goal.
- Expand C → push H.
- Stack: [ A, B, H ]

**Step 3 — Pop H:** H is not the goal, no unvisited successors.
- Stack: [ A, B ]

**Step 4 — Pop B:** B is not the goal.
- Expand B → push F, G.
- Stack: [ A, F, G ] *(G on top)*

**Step 5 — Pop G:** G is the **GOAL** → Return success.

**Final DFS Result:** Path S → C → B → G (length 3, *not* the shortest — illustrating non-optimality).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Both algorithms found the goal, but BFS gave the *optimal* 2-step path while DFS gave a 3-step path. This is the classic KTU examiner's favorite example.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side BFS vs DFS Trace

| Step | BFS Queue State | BFS Action | DFS Stack State | DFS Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | [S] | Initialize | [S] | Initialize |
| 1 | [A, B, C] | Dequeue S | [A, B, C] | Pop S |
| 2 | [B, C, D, E] | Dequeue A | [A, B, H] | Pop C |
| 3 | [C, D, E, F, G] | Dequeue B | [A, B] | Pop H |
| 4 | [D, E, F, G, H] | Dequeue C | [A, F, G] | Pop B |
| 5 | [E, F, G, H] | Dequeue D | [A, F] | Pop G → **GOAL** |
| 6 | [F, G, H] | Dequeue E | — | Done |
| 7 | [G, H] | Dequeue F | — | — |
| 8 | [ ] | Dequeue G → **GOAL** | — | — |

## 3.5 Comparative Step-Count Derivation

Suppose the graph has $b = 3$ and the shallowest goal is at $d = 4$. We derive the number of nodes expanded.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BFS nodes} &= 1 + b + b^2 + b^3 + b^4 \\
&= 1 + 3 + 9 + 27 + 81 \\
&= 121 \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **geometric series** $\sum_{i=0}^{d} b^{i} = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFS worst-case nodes} &= 1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $m$ may be vastly larger than $d$. If $m = 20$ and $b = 3$, DFS could explore up to $3^{21} \approx 10$ billion nodes — illustrating why DFS in deep/infinite spaces is dangerous without a depth limit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BFS — Level-by-Level Expansion Flow (Mermaid)

The following flowchart captures how BFS systematically explores the search tree, level by level, using a FIFO queue.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start]) --> initQ[Initialize empty FIFO queue and visited set]
    initQ --> pushStart[Push root node S into queue]
    pushStart --> checkQueue{Is queue empty?}
    checkQueue -- Yes --> failNode([Return FAILURE])
    checkQueue -- No --> dequeueNode[Dequeue front node n from queue]
    dequeueNode --> goalCheck{n is the GOAL?}
    goalCheck -- Yes --> successNode([Return n and path])
    goalCheck -- No --> expandNode[Generate all unvisited successors of n]
    expandNode --> markVisit[Mark each successor as visited]
    markVisit --> enqueueKids[Enqueue each successor at the back of queue]
    enqueueKids --> checkQueue
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** from "Enqueue each successor" back to "Is queue empty?" — this represents the **iterative expansion cycle** of BFS. The Queue enforces breadth-first ordering because successors are appended at the *back*, ensuring older (shallower) nodes are always processed first.

## 4.2 DFS — Deep-Dive and Backtrack Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start]) --> initS[Initialize empty LIFO stack and visited set]
    initS --> pushStart[Push root node S onto stack]
    pushStart --> checkStack{Is stack empty?}
    checkStack -- Yes --> failNode([Return FAILURE])
    checkStack -- No --> popNode[Pop top node n from stack]
    popNode --> goalCheck{n is the GOAL?}
    goalCheck -- Yes --> successNode([Return n and path])
    goalCheck -- No --> expandNode[Generate all unvisited successors of n]
    expandNode --> markVisit[Mark each successor as visited]
    markVisit --> pushKids[Push each successor ONTO the top of stack]
    pushKids --> checkStack
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference from BFS:** In the BFS diagram, the *oldest* node is removed first. In the DFS diagram, the *newest* node is removed first. This single data-structure choice (Queue vs Stack) is what flips the entire search philosophy from breadth-wise to depth-wise.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Memory & Expansion Engine

The following block diagram illustrates how a generic search engine (whether BFS or DFS) is structured internally. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** fallback for visualizing system interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngineBlock[Search Engine Core]
        direction TB
        frontier[(Frontier: Queue or Stack)]
        explored[(Explored / Visited Set)]
        goalTest[Goal Test Module]
        succGen[Successor Generator]
        controller[Controller / Loop Driver]
    end

    input[/Problem Definition/] --> controller
    controller -->|pop node| frontier
    frontier -->|next node| goalTest
    goalTest -->|if goal| output[/Solution Path/]
    goalTest -->|not goal| succGen
    succGen -->|new nodes| explored
    explored -->|filter visited| succGen
    succGen -->|unvisited children| frontier
    controller -->|iterate| frontier
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** Examiners often award 2 marks for clearly showing the **Frontier (Queue/Stack)**, the **Explored Set**, the **Goal Test**, and the **Successor Generator** as distinct blocks. Always label your arrows with what data flows between them.

## 4.4 Search-Tree Visualization (Mermaid Graph)

Below is a sample search tree showing how BFS (level-by-level) and DFS (branch-by-branch) would conceptually differ in coverage order.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S((S)) --> A1((A))
    S --> B1((B))
    S --> C1((C))
    A1 --> A2a((D))
    A1 --> A2b((E))
    B1 --> B2a((F))
    B1 --> B2b((G))
    C1 --> C2a((H))

    style S fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
    style B2b fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The green node **G** is the goal. BFS would visit nodes in the order: S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G (level by level). DFS in the order: S, C, H, B, F, G (or S, A, D, ... depending on stack ordering) — descending one branch fully before backtracking.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) with respect to the data structure used and the order of node expansion.**

**Model Answer:**

Breadth-First Search uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) Queue** to manage the frontier of nodes pending expansion. It always expands the **shallowest** unexpanded node first, thereby exploring the search tree **level by level**.

Depth-First Search uses a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) Stack**. It always expands the **deepest** unexpanded node first, exploring **one branch as deeply as possible** before backtracking to try alternative branches.

**Memory implication:** BFS stores the entire current frontier, which can be as large as $O(b^{d})$, while DFS stores only the path from the root to the current node along with its unexpanded siblings, requiring only $O(b \cdot m)$ memory. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Is BFS complete and optimal? Justify your answer in the context of a finite, unweighted graph.**

**Model Answer:**

**Yes, BFS is both complete and optimal in a finite, unweighted graph.**

- **Completeness:** Because the branching factor $b$ is finite, BFS systematically expands one level of the search tree at a time. It will not skip over a goal node at depth $d$; it is guaranteed to dequeue the goal eventually. Hence it will always find a solution if one exists.

- **Optimality:** In an unweighted graph, all edges have equal cost. BFS expands nodes in non-decreasing order of path cost (i.e., depth). Therefore, the first goal node it encounters is at the **shallowest depth**, which is also the lowest-cost path. Thus BFS is optimal for unweighted graphs. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** KTU Part B questions on this topic *always* carry an internal choice (Or option). You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** Explain the **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** algorithm in detail, including its working principle, the role of the queue, and a step-by-step trace on the graph shown below. The goal is to find a path from **S** to **G**. **(7 marks)**

**Graph edges:** S–A, S–B, S–C, A–D, A–E, B–F, B–G, C–H, D–G

**(b)** Discuss the **completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity** of BFS. Under what conditions might BFS become infeasible for large problem spaces? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle:** BFS explores the search tree level by level. It uses a **FIFO queue** to maintain the frontier of unexpanded nodes. The algorithm repeatedly dequeues the *oldest* node (the shallowest), tests it for the goal, and if not a goal, enqueues all of its unvisited successors at the *back* of the queue.

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Queue (front→back) | Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | [S] | Initialize |
| 1 | [A, B, C] | Dequeue S, expand → A, B, C enqueued |
| 2 | [B, C, D, E] | Dequeue A, expand → D, E enqueued |
| 3 | [C, D, E, F, G] | Dequeue B, expand → F, G enqueued |
| 4 | [D, E, F, G, H] | Dequeue C, expand → H enqueued |
| 5 | [E, F, G, H] | Dequeue D → G already visited, skip |
| 6 | [F, G, H] | Dequeue E, no new nodes |
| 7 | [G, H] | Dequeue F, no new nodes |
| 8 | [H] | Dequeue G → **GOAL FOUND** |

**Final BFS Path:** S → B → G (length 2, optimal). **(7 marks — 2 for principle, 4 for trace, 1 for final path)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The Four Properties of BFS:**

1. **Completeness:** BFS is **complete** for any finite branching factor $b$. Since it explores all nodes at depth $d$ before depth $d+1$, it will eventually find a goal at depth $d$ if one exists.

2. **Optimality:** BFS is **optimal** for unweighted graphs. Because the goal is found at the shallowest depth, the path cost is minimal when all step costs are equal.

3. **Time Complexity:** BFS generates at most $1 + b + b^{2} + \dots + b^{d} = O(b^{d})$ nodes. This is the size of the entire search tree up to depth $d$.

4. **Space Complexity:** BFS must store the **entire frontier**, which at the deepest level can contain $O(b^{d})$ nodes. This is the most serious limitation of BFS.

**When BFS Becomes Infeasible:**

BFS becomes infeasible when the goal is at a **large depth** $d$ in a graph with a **high branching factor** $b$, because both time and memory grow **exponentially** as $O(b^{d})$. For example, if $b = 10$ and $d = 12$, BFS would need to store up to $10^{12}$ frontier nodes, which is far beyond practical memory. In such scenarios, DFS variants (with depth limits) or **Informed (Heuristic) Search** like A* are preferred. **(7 marks — 1 each for the four properties + 3 for feasibility discussion)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]
**(a)** Explain the **Depth-First Search (DFS)** algorithm in detail. Describe the role of the **stack** in DFS and how backtracking occurs. Trace DFS on the graph from Question A (same graph). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare BFS and DFS across the four key evaluation criteria. Under what kind of problem is DFS preferred over BFS, and what is its main drawback? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle:** DFS explores a search tree by going **as deep as possible** along each branch before backtracking. It uses a **LIFO stack** so that the most recently generated (deepest) node is the next one to be expanded.

**Role of the Stack:** When a node is expanded, its unvisited successors are pushed onto the **top** of the stack. The next iteration pops the topmost element, which is the deepest unexpanded node, thereby enforcing depth-first behavior.

**Backtracking:** When a node is popped and either (i) it is a leaf with no unvisited successors, or (ii) all its successors have been explored, the algorithm effectively "backtracks" to the previous level by popping more nodes off the stack.

**Step-by-Step Trace (DFS on the same graph, stack-top is the rightmost element):**

| Step | Stack (top is right) | Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | [S] | Initialize |
| 1 | [A, B, C] | Pop S → push A, B, C |
| 2 | [A, B, H] | Pop C → push H |
| 3 | [A, B] | Pop H → no successors |
| 4 | [A, F, G] | Pop B → push F, G |
| 5 | [A, F] | Pop G → **GOAL FOUND** |

**Final DFS Path:** S → C → B → G (length 3, *not* the shortest). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | BFS | DFS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data Structure | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack |
| Completeness | Yes (finite $b$) | No (infinite spaces) |
| Optimality | Yes (uniform costs) | No |
| Time | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{m})$ |
| Space | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ |

**When DFS is Preferred:**
- When the search space is **very deep** and the goal is expected to be located in the **deeper levels** of the tree.
- When **memory is limited**, because DFS only needs to store one path plus siblings — $O(bm)$ instead of $O(b^{d})$.
- When the search tree is **very large** but the solution path is reasonably long.
- In problems like **cycle detection, topological sorting, solving mazes, and puzzle solvers (Sudoku, N-Queens)**.

**Main Drawback of DFS:**
- It can get stuck in **infinitely deep branches** or loops.
- It is **not optimal** — it returns the *first* goal it finds, not the shortest path.
- It may explore the **wrong subtree** for a long time before backtracking, wasting computation. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the visited set:** Without a visited set, DFS will cycle infinitely in graphs with cycles. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 2. **Confusing Stack-Top and Queue-Front:** BFS dequeues from the *front*, DFS pops from the *top*. Mixing these up leads to a wrong trace and zero marks for the trace portion.
> 3. **Stating DFS is "always complete":** It is **NOT** complete in infinite-depth state spaces. Use precise language: "DFS is complete in finite-state spaces."
> 4. **Forgetting optimality depends on uniform costs:** BFS is optimal *only* if all step costs are equal. State this condition explicitly.
> 5. **No clear goal node marked:** Always mark the goal node in your diagram and clearly write "GOAL FOUND" at the step it is detected.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU OEERT615 Module 2**

- **Uninformed Search** = Blind search; no heuristic information used.
- **BFS** uses a **FIFO Queue**; expands **shallowest** node first; **complete** and **optimal** (for uniform costs); complexity **$O(b^{d})$** in both time and space.
- **DFS** uses a **LIFO Stack**; expands **deepest** node first; **not complete** (in infinite spaces) and **not optimal**; time **$O(b^{m})$** but space only **$O(b \cdot m)$**.
- **$b$** = branching factor, **$d$** = depth of shallowest goal, **$m$** = maximum depth of the state space.
- BFS finds the **shortest path** in an unweighted graph — useful for GPS, social networks, broadcasting.
- DFS uses **much less memory** and is preferred for **deep-narrow trees** and puzzle-solving (mazes, Sudoku, N-Queens).
- **Always include a visited/explored set** to prevent infinite loops in graphs containing cycles.
- The **frontier** is the set of nodes generated but not yet expanded; BFS/DFS differ only in *how* this frontier is managed (queue vs stack).
- In KTU exam answers, **always** (i) state the algorithm's principle, (ii) show the data structure used, (iii) draw a clear trace table, (iv) state all four properties, and (v) mark the goal-found step explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Informed Search Strategies - Greedy Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Informed Search Strategies - Greedy Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)** is an *informed search algorithm* that expands the node which the evaluation function estimates to be **closest to the goal state**, based purely on a heuristic estimate $h(n)$ without considering the cost incurred so far to reach that node.

Formally, the evaluation function for Greedy Best-First Search is defined as:

$$f(n) = h(n)$$

where:
- $h(n)$ = heuristic function estimating the cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal state.
- $f(n)$ = the ordering criterion used by the priority queue (open list) to select the next node for expansion.

Unlike **Uniform Cost Search (UCS)** which uses $f(n) = g(n)$ (path cost so far) or **A* Search** which uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, Greedy Search ignores $g(n)$ completely and makes decisions *purely* on perceived proximity to the goal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Mountain Climber**
> Imagine you are lost in a dense fog on a mountainside and need to reach the summit (the **goal**). You can only see a short distance ahead, but you have an altimeter. At every fork in the trail, you pick the path that **steeply ascends the most**, because your heuristic (altitude gain visible) *appears* to lead you closer to the peak. You do not look back to check how far you have walked. This is Greedy Search — always moving toward what *looks* closest to the goal, ignoring how far you have already come.

The result? You might reach the peak quickly if lucky, or you might scale a smaller foothill, become stranded on a dead-end cliff, or even walk in circles if the terrain loops back. This perfectly captures the trade-off of Greedy Search: **fast but unreliable**.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics at a Glance

| Property | Standard Greedy Best-First Search |
|---|---|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = h(n)$ |
| Uses path cost $g(n)$? | **No** |
| Uses heuristic $h(n)$? | **Yes** |
| Data structure | **Priority queue** (ordered by $h(n)$) |
| Backtracking supported? | **Yes** (via open/closed lists) |
| Informed? | **Yes** (uses domain knowledge) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Greedy Best-First Search algorithm operates on two fundamental lists:

- **OPEN List (Frontier)**: A priority queue of nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded. Sorted in ascending order of $h(n)$ (lowest heuristic first).
- **CLOSED List (Explored)**: A set of nodes that have already been expanded, kept to avoid revisiting.

### Algorithm Steps

1. **Initialize**: Place the start node $S$ into the OPEN list. Set $h(S)$ to its estimated cost to goal. Initialize CLOSED list as empty.
2. **Termination Check**: If OPEN list is empty → return **FAILURE** (no solution exists).
3. **Select Node**: Remove the node $n$ from OPEN list having the **smallest $h(n)$** value.
4. **Goal Test**: If $n$ is a goal node → return **SUCCESS** with the path traced via parent pointers.
5. **Mark Expanded**: Move $n$ to the CLOSED list.
6. **Generate Successors**: For each successor (child) $n_i$ of $n$:
   - If $n_i$ is not in OPEN or CLOSED → compute $h(n_i)$, set parent pointer to $n$, and add to OPEN list.
   - If $n_i$ is already in OPEN with a higher $h$ → re-evaluate and update parent pointer if heuristic via current $n$ is better.
   - If $n_i$ is already in CLOSED with a higher $h$ → propagate the better heuristic value back to its descendants (reopening).
7. **Loop** back to Step 2.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Greedy Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Philosophy**
> Greedy Search is a **depth-first style** exploration but guided by heuristic information. Its key insight is that **the closer a node appears to the goal, the sooner we should explore it**, hoping to find a goal state quickly. This trades off **optimality for speed**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal | Cost units (problem-dependent) |
| $f(n)$ | Evaluation function for Greedy | $f(n) = h(n)$ |
| $g(n)$ | Actual cost from start to $n$ | **Not used** by Greedy |
| $d$ | Depth of shallowest goal | Nodes |
| $m$ | Maximum depth of search space | Nodes |
| $b$ | Effective branching factor | Edges per node |
| $C^*$ | Optimal path cost | Cost units |
| $h^*(n)$ | True (unknown) cost from $n$ to goal | Lower bound on $h$ |
| $O(b^m)$ | Time complexity (worst case) | Same as worst-case DFS |
| $O(b^m)$ | Space complexity | Keeps all nodes in memory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Admissible Heuristic** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$: A heuristic that **never overestimates** the true cost. Greedy Search does not *require* admissibility to function, but admissibility is what guarantees optimality in A* (not in Greedy!).

## 2.4 Properties of Greedy Best-First Search

| Property | Greedy Search Result | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete** | **No** | Can get stuck in dead ends or infinite loops if heuristic is poorly designed. |
| **Optimal** | **No** | Returns the first goal found, not the cheapest path. |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^m)$ | Worst case, where $m$ is max search depth. Best case $O(bd)$ with perfect heuristic. |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b^m)$ | All nodes must be kept in memory (OPEN + CLOSED lists). |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> Greedy Search forms the foundation for many practical AI systems:
> - **Route Navigation** (e.g., Google Maps' "fastest route" pre-processing): Pick the road that *seems* closest to destination.
> - **Job Scheduling**: Always execute the shortest job first (minimizes immediate waiting time).
> - **Huffman Coding**: Builds optimal prefix codes by greedily merging least-frequent nodes.
> - **Minimum Spanning Tree (Prim's & Kruskal's)**: Classical greedy graph algorithms.
> - **Robot Path Planning**: Quick local navigation toward a visible target.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Greedy Search on a Graph

Consider the following search graph where the start node is **S** and the goal is **G**. The heuristic values $h(n)$ are given alongside each node. Edge weights are the step costs.

**Graph Definition:**

- Nodes: $S, A, B, C, D, E, G$
- Heuristic estimates: $h(S)=7, h(A)=6, h(B)=4, h(C)=5, h(D)=3, h(E)=2, h(G)=0$
- Edges (with costs):
  - $S \rightarrow A$ (cost 3)
  - $S \rightarrow B$ (cost 2)
  - $A \rightarrow C$ (cost 4)
  - $A \rightarrow D$ (cost 1)
  - $B \rightarrow D$ (cost 5)
  - $B \rightarrow E$ (cost 3)
  - $C \rightarrow G$ (cost 2)
  - $D \rightarrow G$ (cost 6)
  - $E \rightarrow G$ (cost 1)

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace

### Step 1: Initialization

$$\text{OPEN} = [S(7)] \quad ; \quad \text{CLOSED} = []$$

### Step 2: Expand S (smallest h=7)

Remove $S$ from OPEN. $S$ is not goal. Generate successors:

- $A$: $h(A)=6$, parent = $S$, cost so far $g(A) = 3$
- $B$: $h(B)=4$, parent = $S$, cost so far $g(B) = 2$

$$\text{OPEN} = [B(4), A(6)] \quad ; \quad \text{CLOSED} = [S]$$

### Step 3: Expand B (smallest h=4)

Remove $B$ from OPEN. $B$ is not goal. Generate successors:

- $D$: $h(D)=3$, parent = $B$, not in any list → add to OPEN.
- $E$: $h(E)=2$, parent = $B$, not in any list → add to OPEN.

$$\text{OPEN} = [E(2), D(3), A(6)] \quad ; \quad \text{CLOSED} = [S, B]$$

### Step 4: Expand E (smallest h=2)

Remove $E$ from OPEN. $E$ is not goal. Generate successor:

- $G$: $h(G)=0$, parent = $E$, not in any list → add to OPEN.

$$\text{OPEN} = [G(0), D(3), A(6)] \quad ; \quad \text{CLOSED} = [S, B, E]$$

### Step 5: Expand G (smallest h=0)

Remove $G$ from OPEN. **$G$ is the goal state!**

$$\textbf{GOAL REACHED} \Rightarrow \text{Return path: } S \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow G$$

### 3.3 Final Path Cost Analysis

The path returned by Greedy Search: $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow G$

$$g_{\text{greedy}} = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6$$

However, the **optimal path** is: $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$ with cost

$$g_{\text{optimal}} = 3 + 1 + 6 = 10 \text{ ???}$$

Wait — let us recompute carefully:

$$S \rightarrow A = 3, \quad A \rightarrow D = 1, \quad D \rightarrow G = 6 \Rightarrow g = 10$$

$$S \rightarrow B = 2, \quad B \rightarrow E = 3, \quad E \rightarrow G = 1 \Rightarrow g = 6$$

In this case, the Greedy path $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow G$ with $g = 6$ is actually the **optimal** path. However, Greedy Search is **not guaranteed** to be optimal in general; it happened to succeed here. A different heuristic could have led it to a sub-optimal path.

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification of Complexity

Let the search tree have branching factor $b$ and maximum depth $m$. In the **worst case**, Greedy Search behaves like depth-first search because the heuristic provides no useful guidance (e.g., $h(n) = \text{constant}$).

$$\text{Time Complexity} = T(n) = b \cdot T(n-1) + O(1)$$

Solving this recurrence using expansion:

$$T(m) = b^m + b^{m-1} + \cdots + b + 1 = \sum_{i=0}^{m} b^i$$

Applying the geometric series formula:

$$T(m) = \frac{b^{m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{for } b > 1$$

$$T(m) \approx O(b^m) \quad \text{as } m \to \infty$$

For the **space complexity**, every generated node is stored in either OPEN or CLOSED:

$$\text{Space Complexity} = \text{Max OPEN} + \text{Max CLOSED} \approx O(b^m)$$

## 3.5 Why Greedy Search Fails — Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead End** | Heuristic leads toward a node with no successors or no path to goal. | Algorithm returns FAILURE despite a solution existing elsewhere. |
| **Sub-optimality** | Returns the *first* goal found, which may not be the cheapest. | In a 2D maze, finds *a* exit, not the *shortest* exit. |
| **Infinite Loop** | With a cyclic graph and non-consistent heuristic, revisits same node with lower $h$. | Algorithm cycles between two nodes. |
| **Heuristic Misguidance** | Inaccurate heuristic sends search in wrong direction. | Air-distance heuristic ignores walls/obstacles. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Greedy Search — Control Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete control flow of the Greedy Best-First Search algorithm, including OPEN/CLOSED list management.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["START"]
    node2["Initialize OPEN with Start Node S"]
    node3["Initialize CLOSED as Empty"]
    node4{"Is OPEN Empty?"}
    node5["Return FAILURE - No Solution"]
    node6["Select Node n with Smallest h n from OPEN"]
    node7{"Is n the Goal?"}
    node8["Return SUCCESS with Path"]
    node9["Move n to CLOSED List"]
    node10["Generate Successors of n"]
    node11{"Successor n_i in OPEN or CLOSED?"}
    node12["Compute h n_i - Add to OPEN with Parent n"]
    node13["Update Parent - Keep Best Heuristic"]
    node14["Back to Open Empty Check"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 -- Yes --> node5
    node4 -- No --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 -- Yes --> node8
    node7 -- No --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 -- No --> node12
    node11 -- Yes --> node13
    node12 --> node14
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node4
```

## 4.2 Search Tree Expansion Order (for the Worked Example)

The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes the order in which Greedy Search expands nodes from the example in Section 3, illustrating that lower heuristic values are explored first.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootS["S h=7 - Expanded 1st"]
    childA["A h=6 - Generated"]
    childB["B h=4 - Expanded 2nd"]
    childC["C h=5 - Generated"]
    childD["D h=3 - Generated"]
    childE["E h=2 - Expanded 3rd"]
    goalG["G h=0 - GOAL Expanded 4th"]

    rootS --- childA
    rootS --- childB
    childA --- childC
    childA --- childD
    childB --- childD
    childB --- childE
    childC --- goalG
    childD --- goalG
    childE --- goalG

    style rootS fill:#FFD700
    style childB fill:#FFA500
    style childE fill:#FF6347
    style goalG fill:#32CD32
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The colour-coded nodes (gold → orange → tomato → green) represent the order of expansion. Note how Greedy Search descends quickly along the path of decreasing heuristics: $S(7) \rightarrow B(4) \rightarrow E(2) \rightarrow G(0)$, ignoring the entire $A$-branch.

## 4.3 Greedy Search vs Other Informed Strategies — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    family["Informed Search Family"]
    greedy["Greedy Best-First f n = h n"]
    ucs["Uniform Cost Search f n = g n"]
    astar["A Star Search f n = g n + h n"]
    ida["Iterative Deepening A Star"]

    family --> greedy
    family --> ucs
    family --> astar
    family --> ida

    greedy --- greedyProp["Complete: No, Optimal: No, Fast, Memory Heavy"]
    ucs --- ucsProp["Complete: Yes, Optimal: Yes, Slow, Memory Heavy"]
    astar --- astarProp["Complete: Yes, Optimal: Yes with admissible h, Memory Heavy"]
    ida --- idaProp["Complete: Yes, Optimal: Yes with admissible h, Memory Efficient"]
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    heuristicModule["Heuristic Function h n"]
    priorityQ["OPEN List Priority Queue"]
    closedSet["CLOSED List Set"]
    selector["Node Selector Min h"]
    expander["Node Expander"]
    goalTest["Goal Test Module"]
    resultPath["Result Path Constructor"]

    heuristicModule --> priorityQ
    priorityQ --> selector
    selector --> goalTest
    goalTest -- Goal Found --> resultPath
    goalTest -- Not Goal --> expander
    expander --> priorityQ
    expander --> closedSet
    closedSet --> expander
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define the evaluation function used by Greedy Best-First Search. Why is it called "greedy"?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The evaluation function for Greedy Best-First Search is $f(n) = h(n)$, where $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of the cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal state. The algorithm always expands the node with the **lowest $h(n)$** value first.
- **Why "Greedy" (1 mark):** It is called "greedy" because it makes the locally optimal choice at each step — always picking the node that *appears* closest to the goal — without considering the global path cost or whether this choice might lead to a dead end.

---

### Question A2
**Q: Is Greedy Best-First Search complete and optimal? Justify briefly.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Completeness: No (1 mark).** Greedy Best-First Search is not complete because a poorly designed heuristic can lead the search into infinite loops or dead ends, and the algorithm returns failure if the OPEN list is exhausted before reaching a goal.
- **Optimality: No (1 mark).** It is not optimal because it returns the **first goal found**, not the one with the lowest total path cost. A sub-optimal goal may be discovered first along the path of steepest heuristic descent.
- **Justification (1 mark):** Example — In a graph where node $A$ has $h(A)=1$ but leads to a very costly goal, while node $B$ has $h(B)=3$ but leads to the cheapest goal, Greedy will commit to $A$ and miss the optimal path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q: Explain the Greedy Best-First Search algorithm in detail. Apply it on a 7-node graph with the start node $S$ and goal $G$, where the heuristics are $h(S)=7$, $h(A)=6$, $h(B)=4$, $h(C)=5$, $h(D)=3$, $h(E)=2$, $h(G)=0$, and edges $S \rightarrow A$ (3), $S \rightarrow B$ (2), $A \rightarrow C$ (4), $A \rightarrow D$ (1), $B \rightarrow D$ (5), $B \rightarrow E$ (3), $C \rightarrow G$ (2), $D \rightarrow G$ (6), $E \rightarrow G$ (1). Show the OPEN and CLOSED list after each step. Also discuss the completeness and optimality of the algorithm.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — Algorithm Description [7 marks]

**Answer:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Greedy Best-First Search is an informed search strategy that uses a heuristic function $h(n)$ to estimate the cost from a node $n$ to the goal, and expands the node with the lowest $h(n)$ at each step.

2. **Evaluation Function (1 mark):** $f(n) = h(n)$. Unlike UCS ($f(n) = g(n)$) or A* ($f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$), Greedy completely ignores the cost incurred so far.

3. **Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Initialize OPEN with the start node $S$, CLOSED as empty.
   - **Step 2:** If OPEN is empty, return FAILURE.
   - **Step 3:** Select node $n$ from OPEN with the smallest $h(n)$.
   - **Step 4:** If $n$ is the goal, return SUCCESS with the path.
   - **Step 5:** Move $n$ to CLOSED. Generate successors, add new ones to OPEN (sorted by $h$).
   - **Step 6:** Go to Step 2.

4. **Data Structures (1 mark):** Uses a **priority queue** (OPEN) and a **set/list** (CLOSED) to keep track of explored nodes and avoid cycles.

5. **Key Insight (1 mark):** The heuristic acts as a "compass" guiding the search toward what *appears* to be the goal, akin to a depth-first search with a sense of direction.

#### Part (b) — Worked Example and Properties [7 marks]

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Action | OPEN (sorted by h) | CLOSED |
|------|--------|-------------------|--------|
| 0 | Initialize | $[S(7)]$ | $[\,]$ |
| 1 | Expand $S$ → add $A(6), B(4)$ | $[B(4), A(6)]$ | $[S]$ |
| 2 | Expand $B$ → add $D(3), E(2)$ | $[E(2), D(3), A(6)]$ | $[S, B]$ |
| 3 | Expand $E$ → add $G(0)$ | $[G(0), D(3), A(6)]$ | $[S, B, E]$ |
| 4 | Expand $G$ → GOAL! | $[\,]$ | $[S, B, E, G]$ |

**Final Path:** $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow G$

**Path Cost Calculation:**
$$g_{\text{path}} = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6$$

**Properties Discussion (3 marks):**
- **Completeness: NO** — If the heuristic misleads the algorithm into a dead-end region with no goals, the OPEN list will eventually be exhausted and the algorithm will return failure, even if a goal exists elsewhere. (1.5 marks)
- **Optimality: NO** — Greedy returns the *first* goal it encounters along the heuristic-guided path. In this example, the goal $G$ is reached via the heuristic-cheapest route, but in general a lower-$h$ path may lead to a sub-optimal solution. (1.5 marks)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q: What is a heuristic function? Explain Greedy Best-First Search algorithm with a suitable example. Compare Greedy Search with A* Search in terms of completeness, optimality, and time/space complexity.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Heuristic Functions and Greedy Search [7 marks]

**Answer:**

1. **Heuristic Function Definition (2 marks):**
   A heuristic function $h(n)$ is a function that **estimates the cost of the cheapest path from node $n$ to a goal state**. It is a domain-specific piece of knowledge that guides the search algorithm toward promising regions of the search space.

   > **Examples of heuristics:**
   > - In route-finding: straight-line distance to destination.
   > - In 8-puzzle: number of misplaced tiles (hamming distance).
   > - In chess: material value advantage.

2. **Greedy Best-First Search (3 marks):**
   Greedy Best-First Search uses only the heuristic to make decisions: $f(n) = h(n)$. It expands the node that **appears** closest to the goal first. It uses a priority queue sorted in ascending $h(n)$ order, with OPEN/CLOSED lists for cycle avoidance. Its core strength is **speed** — it dives quickly toward the goal — but it is neither complete nor optimal.

3. **Worked Example (2 marks):** Use the same 7-node graph as in Question A. After expansion, Greedy Search selects the path $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow G$ because of the descending heuristic values $7 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 0$, reaching the goal in 4 expansions.

#### Part (b) — Comparison with A* Search [7 marks]

| Property | Greedy Best-First Search | A* Search |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluation Function | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Uses path cost $g(n)$? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Uses heuristic $h(n)$? | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Complete? | **No** (can get stuck in dead ends) | **Yes** (if $h$ is admissible and branching factor is finite) |
| Optimal? | **No** (returns first goal) | **Yes** (if $h$ is admissible; or consistent for graph search) |
| Time Complexity | $O(b^m)$ worst case | $O(b^d)$ where $d$ is depth of optimal solution |
| Space Complexity | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ (must keep all generated nodes) |
| Behavior | Depth-first-like, fast but unreliable | Best-first with cost awareness, slower but reliable |
| Heuristic requirement | None specific | Admissible ($h \leq h^*$) or consistent |

**Key Difference (2 marks):**
A* Search combines the *benefits* of UCS (cost-awareness via $g(n)$) and Greedy Search (goal-direction via $h(n)$). This makes A* **optimally efficient** among all algorithms using the same heuristic, but at the cost of more memory and time. Greedy Search sacrifices optimality for speed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Greedy with A* (Major Pitfall):** Students often write $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ for Greedy Search. This is the A* formula, NOT Greedy. Greedy uses **only** $f(n) = h(n)$. Deduct 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting OPEN/CLOSED Lists:** In step-by-step trace questions, students frequently skip the explicit maintenance of the OPEN and CLOSED lists. KTU examiners award marks for *each* list state. Missing them costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Claiming Greedy is Complete or Optimal:** Both are **false**. Writing "Greedy Best-First Search is complete" will fetch negative marking or zero for that sub-question.
>
> 4. **Not Backtracking in Tree Diagrams:** When drawing the search tree, students often forget to show that nodes already in OPEN/CLOSED are NOT regenerated. Always annotate this check.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Heuristic Properties:** When asked about admissibility, remember: Greedy Search does NOT require admissibility, but if the heuristic is admissible, Greedy Search will find **a** goal (not necessarily optimal). Mixing these up loses marks.
>
> 6. **Wrong Node Selection Order:** Always select the node with the **smallest** $h(n)$ value. Students sometimes select the largest, or the most recently added. Read the algorithm carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Evaluation function:** $f(n) = h(n)$ — heuristic only, **no path cost**.
- [ ] **Strategy:** Always expand the node with the **lowest $h(n)$** from the OPEN list.
- [ ] **Data structures:** OPEN (priority queue, ascending $h$) and CLOSED (set of explored nodes).
- [ ] **Complete?** **NO** — can fail on graphs with dead ends or infinite paths.
- [ ] **Optimal?** **NO** — returns the first goal found, not the cheapest.
- [ ] **Time complexity:** $O(b^m)$ worst case, $O(bd)$ best case with a perfect heuristic.
- [ ] **Space complexity:** $O(b^m)$ — keeps all generated nodes in memory.
- [ ] **Informed?** Yes — uses domain knowledge via the heuristic function.
- [ ] **Heuristic requirement:** No admissibility needed to function, but admissibility affects result quality.
- [ ] **Key difference from A\*:** Greedy = $h(n)$ only; A* = $g(n) + h(n)$.
- [ ] **Key difference from UCS:** Greedy = $h(n)$ only; UCS = $g(n)$ only.
- [ ] **Analogy:** Mountain climber with limited visibility, always heading toward perceived highest point.
- [ ] **Failure modes:** Dead ends, infinite loops, sub-optimal solutions, heuristic misguidance.
- [ ] **KTU trick:** If a question says "fastest but not optimal" — the answer is **Greedy Search**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
