topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** A* Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔍 Module 2 — A\* Search: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A\* (A-Star) Search** is a **best-first, informed search algorithm** that finds the least-cost path from a start node to a goal node in a weighted graph. It evaluates each node **n** using the evaluation function:
>
> $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$
>
> where **g(n)** is the actual path cost from the start node to **n**, and **h(n)** is a **heuristic estimate** of the cost from **n** to the nearest goal. A\* is **complete** and **optimal** when the heuristic is **admissible** (and **consistent** for graph search).

The algorithm was first described by **Peter Hart, Nils Nilsson, and Bertram Raphael** in 1968 as an extension of **Dijkstra's algorithm** that uses heuristics to guide the search toward the goal more efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (OEERT615 — Module 2: Informed Search):**
> A\* Search is the **central pillar** of informed (heuristic) search techniques. It is the **most frequently asked topic** in University exams, Part B (14 marks), and is mandatory for solving path-planning problems in the AI laboratory.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🗺️

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Bengaluru** and you have two pieces of information on your GPS:

1. **How far you have already driven** (this is **g(n)** — the *actual* cost incurred so far).
2. **The straight-line ("as-the-crow-flies") distance remaining to Bengaluru** (this is **h(n)** — the *estimated* cost left).

A\* is exactly like a smart GPS: it doesn't just look at how far you've come, and it doesn't just look at how far remains — it **sums both** (f(n) = g + h) and **always drives the road that minimizes this combined number first**.

- If you only minimize "distance driven so far" → that's **Uniform-Cost Search** (Dijkstra).
- If you only minimize "distance remaining" → that's **Greedy Best-First Search**.
- **A\* combines both**, which is why it is both **efficient and optimal**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"A\* = Dijkstra's caution (g) + Greedy's speed (h)."*

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Answers

| Term | Meaning | Typical Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Start Node** | The initial state where search begins | **S** |
| **Goal Node** | The target state to reach | **G** |
| **Path Cost** | Sum of edge weights from S to current node | **g(n)** |
| **Heuristic** | Estimated cost from n to nearest goal | **h(n)** |
| **Evaluation Function** | Combined cost guiding A\*'s expansion | **f(n) = g(n) + h(n)** |
| **OPEN List** | Frontier of nodes discovered but not yet expanded | **Priority Queue** |
| **CLOSED List** | Set of already-expanded nodes | **Hash Set** |
| **Successor** | A neighboring node reachable in one step | — |
| **Admissible Heuristic** | h(n) ≤ true cost to goal (never overestimates) | — |
| **Consistent Heuristic** | h(n) ≤ c(n,n') + h(n') (triangle inequality) | — |

## 1.4 Classification of A\* in the Search Family Tree

A\* belongs to a hierarchy of search algorithms based on the heuristic function used:

- When **h(n) = 0** for all n → A\* reduces to **Uniform-Cost Search**.
- When **g(n) = 0** for all n → A\* reduces to **Greedy Best-First Search**.
- When **h(n) = h*(n)** (perfect heuristic) → A\* expands only nodes on the optimal path.
- When **h(n) > h*(n)** (overestimates) → A\* may become non-optimal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evaluation Function — Heart of A\*

The fundamental equation that drives every decision in A\* is:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

| Component | Meaning | Computed Using |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **g(n)** | Cost from **start** to node **n** along the best-known path | Sum of edge weights |
| **h(n)** | Estimated cost from **n** to the nearest **goal** | Heuristic function |
| **f(n)** | Estimated cost of the **cheapest solution passing through n** | g(n) + h(n) |

> [!NOTE]
> A\* always selects the node from the OPEN list with the **lowest f-value** for expansion. This priority-queue behavior is what gives A\* its optimality guarantee.

## 2.2 The A\* Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The A\* algorithm operates by maintaining two lists and repeatedly selecting the most promising node:

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
- Create **OPEN list** containing only the start node **S** with f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + h(S) = h(S).
- Create an empty **CLOSED list**.
- Set g(S) = 0.

**Step 2 — Termination Check:**
- If OPEN is empty → **failure** (no path exists).
- Otherwise, select node **n** from OPEN with the **minimum f(n)**.

**Step 3 — Goal Test:**
- If **n is the goal node** → **return the reconstructed path** and total cost g(n). SUCCESS.

**Step 4 — Expand n:**
- Move **n** from OPEN to CLOSED.
- For each **successor n'** of n:
  - Compute tentative g(n') = g(n) + cost(n, n').
  - Compute f(n') = g(n') + h(n').
  - If n' is already in CLOSED with a lower g → skip.
  - If n' is not in OPEN, or this path has lower g(n') → **update its g and f values**, set its parent to n, and add to OPEN.

**Step 5 — Loop:**
- Go back to **Step 2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why A\* is Optimal (Intuitive Proof Sketch):**
> Any node **n** on the OPEN list satisfies **f(n) ≥ C\***, where **C\*** is the optimal solution cost. A\* always expands the node with the lowest f, so it cannot "skip past" the goal by exploring a suboptimal branch first. Formally, this is proven using the **admissibility** of the heuristic.

## 2.3 Admissibility vs. Consistency — The Two Critical Properties

### 2.3.1 Admissibility

A heuristic **h(n)** is **admissible** if it **never overestimates** the true cost to the nearest goal:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \text{for every node } n$$

where **h\*(n)** is the **true (actual) minimum cost** from n to a goal.

- **Admissible example:** Straight-line distance in road navigation (always ≤ road distance).
- **Non-admissible example:** Straight-line distance × 1.5 (overestimates).

### 2.3.2 Consistency (Monotonicity)

A heuristic is **consistent** (or **monotone**) if, for every node **n** and every successor **n'** of n:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$$

where **c(n, n')** is the actual cost of the edge from n to n'.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** Every **consistent** heuristic is also **admissible**, but the converse is **not always true**. Consistency guarantees that **f-values are non-decreasing along any path**, which prevents A\* from re-expanding already-closed nodes — a huge efficiency gain.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — At a Glance 📋

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Variable Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A\* Evaluation Function** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | f: estimated total cost via n |
| **Path Cost Component** | $g(n) = \sum \text{edges from S to n}$ | g: actual cost so far |
| **Heuristic Component** | $h(n) \approx \text{cost from n to G}$ | h: estimated cost remaining |
| **Admissibility** | $h(n) \leq h^{\*}(n)$ | never overestimates true cost |
| **Consistency** | $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ | triangle inequality over graph |
| **Optimal Path Cost** | $C^{\*} = f(\text{goal})$ | cost returned by A\* |
| **Time Complexity (worst)** | $O(b^{d})$ | b = branching factor, d = depth |
| **Space Complexity (worst)** | $O(b^{d})$ | all generated nodes stored |
| **Effective Branching Factor** | $b^{\*} \approx N^{1/d}$ | measure of heuristic quality |
| **Dominated Heuristic** | $h_{1}(n) \geq h_{2}(n)$ for all n | h₂ dominates h₁ → fewer nodes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse "consistency" with "admissibility" in answers. If a question asks *"Is the heuristic admissible?"*, the answer depends only on whether **h(n) ≤ h\*(n)**. Consistency is a **stricter, separate** condition.

## 2.5 Common Heuristic Functions

| Heuristic | Use Case | Admissible? | Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Straight-Line (Euclidean) Distance** | Geographic routing | Yes (in 2D) | $h = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^{2} + (y_2 - y_1)^{2}}$ |
| **Manhattan Distance** | Grid world (4-connected) | Yes (if movement is cardinal) | $h = \vert x_2 - x_1 \vert + \vert y_2 - y_1 \vert$ |
| **Chebyshev Distance** | Grid world (8-connected) | Yes (if diagonal = 1) | $h = \max(\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert, \vert y_2 - y_1 \vert)$ |
| **Zero Heuristic** | Degenerate case → UCS | Trivially yes | $h(n) = 0$ |
| **Misplaced Tiles** | 8-puzzle | Yes | $h =$ number of tiles not in goal position |
| **Manhattan for 8-puzzle** | 8-puzzle | Yes (dominates misplaced) | $h = \sum \text{Manhattan distance of each tile}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **For 8-puzzle problems** in KTU exams, the **Manhattan distance heuristic dominates** the **misplaced-tile heuristic** because it gives a **tighter** (larger) lower bound while still being admissible. A heuristic that dominates another will always expand **fewer or equal** nodes.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

A\* is the workhorse algorithm behind:

- **Google Maps / OpenStreetMap** route planning (with road-network heuristics).
- **Game AI pathfinding** (NPC navigation in RPGs, real-time strategy games).
- **Robotics motion planning** (ROS Navigation Stack uses A\* / its variants).
- **Network routing protocols** (OSPF, Cisco's path computation).
- **Logistics and supply-chain optimization** (warehouse robot routing at Amazon).
- **Maze-solving microcontrollers** and embedded robotic vacuum cleaners.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Complete Worked Example — KTU-Style Trace

**Problem Statement:**
Consider the following graph. Find the shortest path from **S** to **G** using A\* Search. The number on each edge is the actual step cost; the number next to each node (in brackets) is the heuristic value **h(n)**.

```
        S
       / \
    1 /   \ 4
     /     \
    A ──2── B
    |       |\
  5 |       | \5
    |       |  \
    C ──2── D   \
    |           |
  3 |           | 7
    |           |
    G ──────────┘
       (direct 12 from A to G)
```

**Heuristic values:**

| Node | S | A | B | C | D | G |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **h(n)** | 7 | 4 | 5 | 2 | 6 | 0 |

**Edges and Costs:**
- S → A : 1
- S → B : 4
- A → B : 2
- A → C : 5
- A → G : 12
- B → C : 2
- B → D : 5
- C → D : 2
- C → G : 3
- D → G : 7

### Step 1 — Initialize

- **OPEN** = { S } with f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + 7 = **7**
- **CLOSED** = { }

### Step 2 — Expand S (lowest f = 7)

Successors of S:
- A: g(A) = g(S) + cost(S,A) = 0 + 1 = 1; f(A) = 1 + 4 = **5**
- B: g(B) = g(S) + cost(S,B) = 0 + 4 = 4; f(B) = 4 + 5 = **9**

- **OPEN** = { A(5), B(9) }
- **CLOSED** = { S }

### Step 3 — Expand A (lowest f = 5)

Successors of A:
- B (via A): g(B) = g(A) + cost(A,B) = 1 + 2 = 3; f(B) = 3 + 5 = **8**
  - B is already in OPEN with f = 9 → **update** to f = 8 (better path found).
- C: g(C) = g(A) + cost(A,C) = 1 + 5 = 6; f(C) = 6 + 2 = **8**
- G (via A): g(G) = g(A) + cost(A,G) = 1 + 12 = 13; f(G) = 13 + 0 = **13**

- **OPEN** = { B(8), C(8), G(13) }
- **CLOSED** = { S, A }

### Step 4 — Expand B (tied lowest f = 8 — break tie by lower g, so B first with g=3 vs C with g=6)

Successors of B:
- C (via B): g(C) = g(B) + cost(B,C) = 3 + 2 = 5; f(C) = 5 + 2 = **7**
  - C is in OPEN with f = 8 → **update** to f = 7 (better path).
- D: g(D) = g(B) + cost(B,D) = 3 + 5 = 8; f(D) = 8 + 6 = **14**

- **OPEN** = { C(7), C(8 stale), G(13), D(14) } → { C(7), G(13), D(14) }
- **CLOSED** = { S, A, B }

### Step 5 — Expand C (lowest f = 7)

Successors of C:
- D (via C): g(D) = g(C) + cost(C,D) = 5 + 2 = 7; f(D) = 7 + 6 = **13**
  - D is in OPEN with f = 14 → **update** to f = 13.
- G (via C): g(G) = g(C) + cost(C,G) = 5 + 3 = 8; f(G) = 8 + 0 = **8**
  - G is in OPEN with f = 13 → **update** to f = 8 (much better path!).

- **OPEN** = { G(8), D(13) }
- **CLOSED** = { S, A, B, C }

### Step 6 — Expand G (lowest f = 8) — GOAL TEST PASSES ✅

- **Goal reached!**
- Reconstruct path using parent pointers: **S → A → B → C → G**
- **Total cost** = g(G) = 8

### 3.2 Final Result & Summary Table

| Iteration | Node Expanded | f(n) | g(n) | h(n) | OPEN after expansion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | S | 7 | 0 | 7 | A(5), B(9) |
| 2 | A | 5 | 1 | 4 | B(8), C(8), G(13) |
| 3 | B | 8 | 3 | 5 | C(7), G(13), D(14) |
| 4 | C | 7 | 5 | 2 | G(8), D(13) |
| 5 | G | 8 | 8 | 0 | GOAL — STOP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Path Returned:** **S → A → B → C → G** with total cost = **8**
>
> **Optimality Verified:** A direct path S → A → G would cost 1 + 12 = 13. The A\* algorithm correctly avoided this suboptimal shortcut because its heuristic-guided exploration found a cheaper route through B and C.

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification of Optimality

To prove A\* returns the optimal solution, we use two lemmas:

**Lemma 1 — Lower Bound Property:**
For any node **n** that has not been expanded and is on the optimal path, we have:

$$f(n) \leq C^*$$

where **C\*** is the optimal solution cost.

**Proof outline:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f(n) &= g(n) + h(n) \\
&\leq g(n) + h^*(n) \quad \text{(by admissibility, since } h(n) \leq h^*(n)\text{)} \\
&= g^*(n) \quad \text{(if } n \text{ is on the optimal path, } g(n) = g^*(n)\text{)} \\
&\leq C^* \quad \text{(optimal path cost is the maximum } g \text{ along it)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Lemma 2 — No Overestimation:**
A\* will not expand a node **n** with **f(n) > C\*** before reaching the goal.

**Combined Conclusion:** Since OPEN is sorted by f, and every expanded node has f ≤ C\*, the moment a goal node is dequeued its g-value equals C\* and A\* terminates with an optimal path.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Tip:** For 14-mark derivation questions, this **two-lemma proof** is the gold-standard structure. Sketch both lemmas, show the inequality chain, and conclude with the line *"Hence A\* is optimal under admissible heuristics."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 A\* Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start A Star Search]
    initA[Initialize OPEN with Start Node S]
    setGS[Set g of S equals 0 and f of S equals h of S]
    initB[Initialize empty CLOSED list]
    checkOpenA{Is OPEN list empty?}
    returnFailA[Return FAILURE - No path exists]
    selectMinF[Select node n from OPEN with minimum f n]
    goalTestA{Is n the Goal node G?}
    returnPathA[Return reconstructed path and cost]
    moveClosedA[Move n from OPEN to CLOSED]
    genSuccA[Generate all successors n prime of n]
    compG[Compute new g of n prime equals g of n plus cost n to n prime]
    compF[Compute new f of n prime equals g of n prime plus h of n prime]
    checkClosedA{n prime already in CLOSED with lower g?}
    skipA[Skip this successor]
    checkOpenB{n prime in OPEN with lower g?}
    skipB[Skip this successor]
    updateA[Update g f and parent of n prime]
    addOpenA[Add or update n prime in OPEN]
    loopBackA[Loop back to check OPEN empty]
    stopA[Stop A Star Search]

    startA --> initA --> setGS --> initB --> checkOpenA
    checkOpenA -- Yes --> returnFailA --> stopA
    checkOpenA -- No --> selectMinF --> goalTestA
    goalTestA -- Yes --> returnPathA --> stopA
    goalTestA -- No --> moveClosedA --> genSuccA --> compG --> compF --> checkClosedA
    checkClosedA -- Yes --> skipA --> genSuccA
    checkClosedA -- No --> checkOpenB
    checkOpenB -- Yes --> skipB --> genSuccA
    checkOpenB -- No --> updateA --> addOpenA --> loopBackA --> checkOpenA
```

## 4.2 A\* Search Tree for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    level0["S  g equals 0  f equals 7  h equals 7"]
    level1A["A  g equals 1  f equals 5  h equals 4"]
    level1B["B  g equals 4  f equals 9  h equals 5"]
    level2BviaA["B  g equals 3  f equals 8  h equals 5"]
    level2CviaA["C  g equals 6  f equals 8  h equals 2"]
    level2GviaA["G  g equals 13  f equals 13  h equals 0"]
    level3CviaB["C  g equals 5  f equals 7  h equals 2"]
    level3DviaB["D  g equals 8  f equals 14  h equals 6"]
    level4DviaC["D  g equals 7  f equals 13  h equals 6"]
    level4GviaC["G  g equals 8  f equals 8  h equals 0  GOAL"]

    level0 --> level1A
    level0 --> level1B
    level1A --> level2BviaA
    level1A --> level2CviaA
    level1A --> level2GviaA
    level2BviaA --> level3CviaB
    level2BviaA --> level3DviaB
    level3CviaB --> level4DviaC
    level3CviaB --> level4GviaC
```

## 4.3 Search Algorithm Family Tree (Conceptual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Informed Search Algorithms]
    uninformed[Uninformed Search - no heuristic]
    informed[Informed Search - uses heuristic h of n]
    ucs[Uniform Cost Search - h equals 0]
    bfs[Breadth First Search - unit costs]
    dfs[Depth First Search]
    gbfs[Greedy Best First - g equals 0]
    aStar[A Star Search - f equals g plus h]
    idaStar[IDA Star - iterative deepening variant]
    smaStar[SMA Star - bounded memory variant]

    root --> uninformed
    root --> informed
    uninformed --> ucs
    uninformed --> bfs
    uninformed --> dfs
    informed --> gbfs
    informed --> aStar
    informed --> idaStar
    informed --> smaStar
    aStar -. reduces to .-> ucs
    aStar -. reduces to .-> gbfs
```

## 4.4 Heuristic Property Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hDef[Heuristic h of n given]
    checkAdmiss[Does h of n never exceed true cost h star of n for all n?]
    admissYes[Yes - Admissible Heuristic]
    admissNo[No - Non Admissible]
    checkCons[Does triangle inequality hold h of n less or equal c of n n prime plus h of n prime?]
    consYes[Yes - Consistent Heuristic]
    consNo[No - Inconsistent but Admissible]
    optA[Optimality Guaranteed for Tree Search]
    optB[Optimality Guaranteed for Graph Search Too]
    optC[May not be optimal without re expansion check]

    hDef --> checkAdmiss
    checkAdmiss -- Yes --> admissYes --> checkCons
    checkAdmiss -- No --> admissNo
    checkCons -- Yes --> consYes --> optB
    checkCons -- No --> consNo --> optC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define the A\* search algorithm. Write its evaluation function and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

A\* Search is an **informed best-first search algorithm** that finds the least-cost path from a start state to a goal state. It uses an **evaluation function**:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- **g(n)** = the actual cost of the path from the start node to node **n**.
- **h(n)** = the heuristic estimated cost from node **n** to the nearest goal node.
- **f(n)** = the estimated total cost of the cheapest solution that passes through node **n**.

A\* maintains an **OPEN list** (frontier, implemented as a priority queue) and a **CLOSED list** (already expanded). It always expands the node with the lowest f-value. It is **complete** and **optimal** if the heuristic is **admissible** (h(n) ≤ h\*(n)).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**What is an admissible heuristic? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A heuristic function **h(n)** is called **admissible** if it **never overestimates** the true cost to reach the nearest goal from node **n**. Formally:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \text{for every node } n$$

where **h\*(n)** is the actual minimum cost from n to a goal.

**Example — Manhattan Distance in 8-puzzle:**
The Manhattan distance (sum of horizontal and vertical displacements of each tile from its goal position) is admissible because the minimum number of moves required to bring each tile to its goal position cannot be less than its Manhattan distance.

> [!NOTE]
> If a heuristic is admissible, A\* is guaranteed to find an **optimal** path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has internal choice. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR one main 14-mark problem.

---

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the A\* search algorithm with a neat flowchart. State the conditions for A\* to be optimal and complete.

**b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between A\* and Uniform-Cost Search (UCS). Under what conditions does A\* reduce to UCS?

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**A\* Algorithm:**

1. **Initialize** OPEN with the start node S; set f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = h(S). CLOSED = empty.
2. **If OPEN is empty** → return failure.
3. **Select** n = node in OPEN with minimum f(n). If n is goal → return path and cost.
4. **Move** n from OPEN to CLOSED.
5. **For each successor n' of n:**
   - Compute new_g = g(n) + cost(n, n').
   - Compute new_f = new_g + h(n').
   - If n' not in OPEN, or new_g < g(n') → update parent, g, f, and add to OPEN.
6. **Go to step 2.**

**Conditions for A\* Optimality and Completeness:**

- **Completeness:** A\* is complete in any finite graph if all step costs are ≥ ε > 0 (no zero-cost cycles).
- **Optimality:** A\* is optimal if the heuristic **h(n) is admissible** (never overestimates true cost).
- **Strong Optimality:** A\* expands **fewer nodes** than any other optimal search using the same heuristic (if consistent).

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Property | Uniform-Cost Search (UCS) | A\* Search |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Evaluation Function** | f(n) = g(n) | f(n) = g(n) + h(n) |
| **Uses Heuristic?** | No (h = 0) | Yes |
| **Expansion Order** | Lowest g(n) first | Lowest f(n) = g(n) + h(n) first |
| **Optimality** | Yes (if costs ≥ ε) | Yes (if heuristic is admissible) |
| **Completeness** | Yes (finite graph, positive costs) | Yes (same conditions) |
| **Efficiency** | Explores in all directions | Goal-directed (uses heuristic) |
| **Space Usage** | Stores all reached nodes | Stores all reached nodes |
| **Reduces to UCS when** | h(n) = 0 for all n | Same condition makes A\* = UCS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **"optimal"** with **"efficient"**. A\* is *optimal* (finds the best path) but not necessarily *efficient* in space (still exponential worst case). Use the phrase **"admissibility → optimality, consistency → efficiency."**

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the properties of heuristic functions — admissibility, consistency, and dominance. Show how consistency implies admissibility.

**b) [7 Marks]** Consider the graph below. Each edge is labeled with its cost. The heuristic values are h(S) = 7, h(A) = 4, h(B) = 5, h(C) = 2, h(D) = 6, h(G) = 0. Use A\* search to find the shortest path from **S** to **G**. Show the OPEN and CLOSED lists at each step.

```
Edges:
S-A: 1,   S-B: 4
A-B: 2,   A-C: 5,   A-G: 12
B-C: 2,   B-D: 5
C-D: 2,   C-G: 3
D-G: 7
```

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**1. Admissibility:**
A heuristic h(n) is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n$$

**2. Consistency (Monotonicity):**
A heuristic h(n) is **consistent** if for every node n and every successor n':

$$h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$$

**3. Dominance:**
A heuristic **h₂** dominates **h₁** if h₂(n) ≥ h₁(n) for all n, while both are admissible. The dominating heuristic expands **fewer** nodes.

**Proof that Consistency ⟹ Admissibility:**

We prove by induction on path length that h(n) ≤ h\*(n).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Base case: } & \text{For a goal node } G, \; h(G) = 0 = h^*(G). \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Inductive step: } & \text{Assume for } n', \; h(n') \leq h^*(n'). \\
& \text{For its parent } n \text{ with edge cost } c(n, n'): \\
& h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n') \quad \text{(by consistency)} \\
& \leq c(n, n') + h^*(n') \quad \text{(by inductive hypothesis)} \\
& = h^*(n) \quad \text{(by definition of true cost)}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, **consistency implies admissibility**. ∎

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Step | Expand | OPEN (before) → (after) | CLOSED (after) | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **S** (f=7) | {S(7)} → {A(5), B(9)} | {S} | g(S)=0, f(S)=0+7=7 |
| 2 | **A** (f=5) | {A(5), B(9)} → {B(8), C(8), G(13)} | {S, A} | g(A)=1, f(A)=1+4=5 |
| 3 | **B** (f=8) | {B(8), C(8), G(13)} → {C(7), G(13), D(14)} | {S, A, B} | g(B)=3 via A, f(B)=3+5=8 |
| 4 | **C** (f=7) | {C(7), G(13), D(14)} → {G(8), D(13)} | {S, A, B, C} | g(C)=5 via A→B, f(C)=5+2=7 |
| 5 | **G** (f=8) | {G(8), D(13)} → **GOAL** | {S, A, B, C, G} | g(G)=8 via C, f(G)=8+0=8 |

**Optimal Path Returned:** **S → A → B → C → G**
**Total Cost:** 1 + 2 + 2 + 3 = **8**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** When updating a node already in OPEN, students frequently **forget to compare the new g-value with the old g-value**. The rule is: only update if **new_g < old_g**. Showing this in the table earns full marks for path-update logic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist 🏁

- ✅ **A\* = f(n) = g(n) + h(n)** is the central equation — memorize it verbatim.
- ✅ **g(n)** = cost from start; **h(n)** = estimated cost to goal; **f(n)** = estimated total.
- ✅ A\* uses a **priority queue (OPEN list)** sorted by f-value.
- ✅ **Admissibility** (h(n) ≤ h\*(n)) guarantees **optimality**.
- ✅ **Consistency** (h(n) ≤ c(n, n') + h(n')) guarantees **efficiency in graph search** (no re-expansions).
- ✅ **Consistency ⟹ Admissibility** (proved by induction on path length).
- ✅ **A\* with h(n) = 0** reduces to **Uniform-Cost Search**.
- ✅ **A\* with g(n) = 0** reduces to **Greedy Best-First Search**.
- ✅ A\* is **complete** for finite graphs with positive step costs.
- ✅ **Dominating heuristics** are better — they expand fewer nodes while staying admissible.
- ✅ **Manhattan distance** is admissible for grid worlds and 8-puzzle; **Euclidean** is admissible for 2D plane.
- ✅ For 8-puzzle, **Manhattan distance** dominates **misplaced-tile count** → prefer Manhattan.
- ✅ A\* in the worst case is **O(b^d)** in both time and space — exponential memory is its main weakness.
- ✅ Variants to know: **IDA\*** (iterative deepening A\*, low memory), **SMA\*** (simplified memory-bounded A\*).
- ✅ Common exam trick: ask to **trace A\* on a small graph** and produce an **OPEN/CLOSED table**.
- ✅ Always **reconstruct the path** in the final answer, not just the cost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AO* Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AO* Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AO\* (And-Or Star) Search** is a heuristic best-first search algorithm that operates over **AND-OR graphs** to find an **optimal solution graph** (the cheapest set of paths from the start node to a set of goal nodes). It is the natural extension of the A\* algorithm into a structured space where problems are either **decomposed into sub-problems (AND nodes)** or have **alternative solution paths (OR nodes)**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, AO\* is formally defined as:
> *"A goal-directed search procedure that uses an admissible heuristic to expand the most promising AND-OR node frontier, propagates revised cost estimates upward through a 'solution graph' (marked 'solved' from the frontier to the start), and terminates when the start node is marked solved."*

The two fundamental node/arc types in this representation are:

| Element | Semantics | Edge Cost Aggregation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **OR node** | A problem that can be solved by solving *any one* of its successors | $h(n) = \min_{n'\in\text{successors}(n)}\left[c(n,n') + h(n')\right]$ |
| **AND node** | A problem that can be solved only by solving *all* of its successors | $h(n) = \sum_{n'\in\text{successors}(n)}\left[c(n,n') + h(n')\right]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of OEERT615 treats AO\* as a *heuristic variant* of the uninformed AND-OR search. You must explicitly mention the role of the heuristic $h(n)$ and the **solution graph** marking — these are the two markers examiners use to differentiate AO\* from plain AND-OR search.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam Preparation" Intuition

Imagine you are a KTU student preparing for the **B.Tech S8 ESE exam** in AI.

- The **goal** is to "pass the exam with highest marks."
- The top-level problem can be approached in **two ways (OR)**:
  - *Way A:* Score well in the descriptive part.
  - *Way B:* Score well in the analytical part.
- However, scoring well in the descriptive part (a sub-problem) **itself requires (AND)** studying *both* Module 1 *and* Module 2 *and* Module 3.
- Each individual module study is an OR sub-problem (either attend class, watch video, or read textbook).

You, the student, are like the AO\* algorithm:
1. You start with the goal (top node).
2. You check which path or sub-problem set looks **cheapest / most promising** (using a heuristic, e.g., "marks achievable per hour of study").
3. You **commit** to a plan: this committed subgraph is the "**solution graph**."
4. As you discover new information, you **revise** your estimated effort and may change your commitment.

The **cost** represents study hours, and the **heuristic** represents your optimistic estimate of remaining hours to master a topic. AO\* ensures you never waste time on a sub-plan that has been superseded by a cheaper one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** *AO\* = A\* + AND edges.* Whenever you see "AND," think **"All must succeed"** (like ALL clauses in a database query). Whenever you see "OR," think **"Any one will do"** (like OR clauses).

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

- **Solution Graph:** The marked subgraph of nodes that AO\* commits to as the optimal path. All nodes in it are eventually marked **SOLVED**.
- **Marking:** The act of attaching a cost estimate $f(n)$ or $h(n)$ to a node, and tagging it as **SOLVED** (when a goal descendant is reached on a marked path) or **UNSOLVED**.
- **Future / Contingent Node:** An OR node whose successor choice depends on an agent's percept (rarely tested in KTU, but mentioned for completeness).
- **Admissible Heuristic:** A heuristic $h(n)$ that never overestimates the true cost to a goal. AO\* requires admissibility to guarantee an optimal solution graph.
- **Cost of a Node:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ where $g(n)$ is the known cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the estimated cost from $n$ to the nearest goal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of AO\* — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The AO\* algorithm, as specified in the classic Nilsson-Nilsson framework adopted by KTU textbooks, follows this **closed-loop iterative procedure**:

1. **Initialization**
   - Construct a graph $G$ containing only the **start node** $S$.
   - Compute $f(S) = h(S)$ using the heuristic.
   - Set a list `OPEN` containing the start node.

2. **Frontier Selection**
   - Traverse the marked solution graph from $S$ downward.
   - Identify the **leftmost (or topmost) UNSOLVED node** with unexpanded successors. This is the *frontier node* $n_{\text{front}}$.

3. **Expansion**
   - Expand $n_{\text{front}}$: generate all its successors $\{n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k\}$.
   - For each newly generated successor $n_i$ that is not in $G$, compute $f(n_i) = h(n_i)$.
   - If $n_i$ is a **goal node**, mark it as **SOLVED**.

4. **Cost Revision (Bottom-Up Backpropagation)**
   - For each ancestor of $n_{\text{front}}$ in the marked solution graph, **update the cost** using:
     - For an **OR node** $p$: $f(p) = \min_{i}\left[c(p, n_i) + f(n_i)\right]$
     - For an **AND node** $p$: $f(p) = \sum_{i}\left[c(p, n_i) + f(n_i)\right]$
   - If a newly cheaper alternative path exists for an OR node, **re-mark** the solution graph along the new minimum-cost branch.

5. **Termination Check**
   - If the start node $S$ is marked **SOLVED**, return the solution graph and **halt**.
   - Else, go to step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Best-First" within AND-OR space?** AO\* never expands a node whose parent OR-branch is already dominated by a cheaper, already-expanded sibling. This is the **admissibility guarantee** that distinguishes it from blind AND-OR search.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Applies To |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Estimated total cost of cheapest solution through $n$ | All nodes |
| 2 | $h_{\text{OR}}(n) = \min_{i}\left[c(n, n_i) + h(n_i)\right]$ | Cost of cheapest OR successor | OR nodes |
| 3 | $h_{\text{AND}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\left[c(n, n_i) + h(n_i)\right]$ | Sum of all AND-successor costs | AND nodes |
| 4 | $g(n)$ | Cost from start node to $n$ along marked path | All nodes |
| 5 | $h^*(n)$ | True optimal cost from $n$ to goal (unknown) | Reference |
| 6 | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Admissibility condition | Heuristic design |
| 7 | **SOLVED** label | A node whose descendants include a goal on marked graph | Termination |
| 8 | **Solution graph** | Subgraph $G'$ containing optimal path(s) from $S$ to goals | Output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Convention for KTU scripts:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value bars (e.g., $h(n) = \min \vert c(n, n_i) + h(n_i) \vert$) when writing inside markdown tables, never a raw $\vert$ character which can break the table parser.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of AO\*

In modern engineering systems, AO\*-style search is the conceptual backbone of:

- **Automated Planning & Scheduling (STRIPS, PDDL planners):** Goal decomposition into sub-goals.
- **Robotic Task Planning:** "Pick and place" actions decomposed into AND-OR sub-tasks.
- **Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) Planning:** Industrial robotics and drone missions.
- **Medical Diagnosis Expert Systems:** A disease (OR) can be confirmed by *either* a blood test *or* an imaging study (AND = both must show specific markers for the differential).
- **Compiler Code Generation:** Expression trees decomposed into AND-OR subtrees for register allocation and instruction selection.

The **advantage over flat A\*** is **exponential**: a problem of branching factor $b$ and depth $d$ that decomposes into $k$ independent AND-sub-problems reduces the search cost from $O(b^d)$ to roughly $O(k \cdot b^{d/k})$ in the best case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Cost Propagation in an AND-OR Graph

Consider the following AND-OR graph where:
- $S$ is the start (OR node).
- $A$, $B$ are OR successors of $S$.
- $C$, $D$ are AND successors of $B$ (i.e., $B$ is an AND node from $S$'s perspective).
- $E$, $F$, $G$, $H$ are leaf goal nodes with known heuristic estimates.
- Edge costs are written on the arcs.

**Given heuristic estimates for leaf nodes:**

$$
h(E) = 2, \quad h(F) = 3, \quad h(G) = 1, \quad h(H) = 4
$$

**Edge costs from $B$ and $A$:**

$$
c(B, C) = 2, \quad c(B, D) = 1, \quad c(A, E) = 3, \quad c(A, F) = 1
$$

**Edge cost from $S$ to its successors:**

$$
c(S, A) = 2, \quad c(S, B) = 1
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $f$ for the AND node $B$:**

The two successors $C$ and $D$ themselves have successors $G$ and $H$. Treating $C$ and $D$ as OR nodes (the cheapest single successor wins):

$$
h(C) = c(C, G) + h(G) = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

$$
h(D) = \min\Big\{c(D, H) + h(H)\Big\} = 1 + 4 = 5
$$

Now $B$ is an **AND node** whose successors $C$ and $D$ *both* must be solved:

$$
f(B) = \big[c(B, C) + h(C)\big] + \big[c(B, D) + h(D)\big]
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
f(B) = (2 + 2) + (1 + 5) = 4 + 6 = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f$ for the OR node $A$:**

$A$ is an OR node; we pick the cheapest single successor:

$$
f(A) = \min\Big\{c(A, E) + h(E),\ \ c(A, F) + h(F)\Big\}
$$

$$
f(A) = \min\{(3 + 2),\ (1 + 3)\} = \min\{5,\ 4\} = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $f$ for the start OR node $S$:**

$S$ is an OR node (pick one successor — either $A$ or $B$):

$$
f(S) = \min\Big\{c(S, A) + f(A),\ \ c(S, B) + f(B)\Big\}
$$

$$
f(S) = \min\{(2 + 4),\ (1 + 10)\} = \min\{6,\ 11\} = 6
$$

**Step 4 — Mark the Solution Graph:**

The minimum-cost branch is $S \to A \to F$ with total cost **6**. The marked solution graph is:

$$
S \xrightarrow{\text{OR, cost } 2} A \xrightarrow{\text{OR, cost } 1} F
$$

All three nodes are marked **SOLVED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style derivation tip:** Always show the substitution step explicitly. Examiners award 2 marks out of 7 for the **correct formula identification**, 2 for **substitution**, and 3 for the **final evaluation and solution-graph marking**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: A\* vs. AO\*

| Dimension | A\* Search | AO\* Search |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Graph type | Standard directed graph | AND-OR graph |
| Successor semantics | Pick **one** best successor | Pick **cheapest set** (mixed AND/OR) |
| Node evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with AND-sum / OR-min propagation |
| Optimality | Admissible $h \Rightarrow$ optimal path | Admissible $h \Rightarrow$ optimal **solution graph** |
| Backtracking | Yes (on dead-end) | Implicit (via re-marking) |
| Computational complexity | $O(b^d)$ worst case | $O(b^{d/k})$ for $k$-way decomposition |
| Termination | Goal node reached | Start node marked SOLVED |
| Use case | Path-finding, route planning | Planning, diagnosis, game-tree search |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *cost of a node* $f(n)$ with the *cost of an edge* $c(n, n')$. In AO\*, edge cost is *added* to the successor's $f$-value; it is **not** stored at the parent.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal-Solution-Graph Property

To prove that AO\* returns an *optimal* solution graph when $h$ is admissible, we proceed by induction on the iteration count:

**Base Case (Iteration 0):** The graph contains only $S$. The estimated cost $f(S) = h(S) \leq h^*(S)$ by admissibility.

**Inductive Hypothesis:** After $k$ iterations, every node $n$ on the marked solution graph satisfies $f(n) \leq h^*(n)$.

**Inductive Step (Iteration $k+1$):** A frontier node $n_{\text{front}}$ is expanded. For each OR node $p$ ancestor, the new $f(p) = \min_i [c(p, n_i) + f(n_i)]$. Since each $f(n_i) \leq h^*(n_i)$ (by hypothesis) and $c(p, n_i)$ is exact:

$$
f(p) = \min_i \big[c(p, n_i) + f(n_i)\big] \leq \min_i \big[c(p, n_i) + h^*(n_i)\big] = h^*(p)
$$

Similarly, for AND nodes, the sum is $\leq$ the sum of true optimal costs, so the inequality is preserved. The marked solution graph is therefore never over-estimated. When $S$ is marked SOLVED, $f(S) = h^*(S)$, i.e., **optimality is achieved**.

$$\boxed{\text{Optimality Theorem: } h \text{ admissible} \implies f(S) = h^*(S) \text{ at termination.}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AND-OR Graph Visual Representation

The diagram below illustrates a generic AND-OR search space. **OR nodes** are connected by single arcs, and **AND nodes** are depicted using a small horizontal "merge arc" joining the two successor edges — the standard Nilsson notation used in KTU textbooks.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode((S - Start OR Node))
    nodeA((A - OR Successor 1))
    nodeB((B - OR Successor 2))
    nodeC((C - AND Successor Left))
    nodeD((D - AND Successor Right))
    leafE((E - Goal h=2))
    leafF((F - Goal h=3))
    leafG((G - Goal h=1))
    leafH((H - Goal h=4))

    startNode -- c S A equals 2 --> nodeA
    startNode -- c S B equals 1 --> nodeB
    nodeA -- c A E equals 3 --> leafE
    nodeA -- c A F equals 1 --> leafF
    nodeB -- c B C equals 2 --> nodeC
    nodeB -- c B D equals 1 --> nodeD
    nodeC -- c C G equals 1 --> leafG
    nodeD -- c D H equals 1 --> leafH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** From $S$, the algorithm must choose EITHER $A$ OR $B$ (OR semantics). If $B$ is chosen, the algorithm is **forced** to also solve both $C$ AND $D$ (AND semantics). The merged arc between $B \to C$ and $B \to D$ is the visual signature of an AND node.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the AO\* Algorithm

The following flowchart captures the iterative control logic of AO\* — equivalent to a Mermaid `flowchart` block, but rendered as a hierarchical block diagram to make the *data flow* explicit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initBlock["INITIALIZATION BLOCK\nConstruct graph G with start node S\nSet f S = h S\nMark S as frontier"]
    frontierBlock["FRONTIER SELECTION BLOCK\nTraverse marked solution graph\nPick leftmost UNSOLVED node n"]
    expandBlock["EXPANSION BLOCK\nGenerate successors of n\nCompute f for each new node\nMark GOAL nodes as SOLVED"]
    reviseBlock["COST REVISION BLOCK\nFor each ancestor p of n\nIF p is OR: f p = min over successors\nIF p is AND: f p = sum over successors\nRE-MARK if cheaper branch exists"]
    checkBlock{"TERMINATION CHECK BLOCK\nIs start node S marked SOLVED?"}
    doneBlock["TERMINATION BLOCK\nReturn marked solution graph\nHalt algorithm"]
    loopArrow["LOOP BACK\nSend control to FRONTIER SELECTION"]

    initBlock --> frontierBlock
    frontierBlock --> expandBlock
    expandBlock --> reviseBlock
    reviseBlock --> checkBlock
    checkBlock -- NO --> loopArrow
    loopArrow --> frontierBlock
    checkBlock -- YES --> doneBlock
```

## 4.3 Cost Propagation Trace Diagram

The diagram below shows the state of the solution graph *after each iteration* for the worked example from Section 3.1, illustrating the upward cost propagation:

```mermaid
graph TD
    step0["ITERATION 0\nG contains only S\nf S = h S = 6 estimated"]
    step1["ITERATION 1\nExpand S\nGenerate A and B\nf A = 4   f B = 10\nS marks A as cheaper branch"]
    step2["ITERATION 2\nExpand A\nGenerate E and F\nf E = 2   f F = 3 via successor\nA marks F as cheaper branch"]
    step3["ITERATION 3\nF is GOAL\nMark F SOLVED\nBackpropagate: A SOLVED, S SOLVED\nReturn solution graph S A F with cost 6"]

    step0 --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Answers:** When drawing AND-OR graphs in the exam, always include (a) clear node labels, (b) edge costs, (c) heuristic values at leaves, and (d) a **bold or dashed** outline for the *final solution graph*. Examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for a clearly drawn solution-graph distinction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define AO\* search. How does it differ from a standard A\* search?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
AO\* search is a heuristic best-first search algorithm that finds the **optimal solution graph** in an **AND-OR graph** by combining A\*-style cost estimates with AND/OR semantics. It differs from A\* in three ways: (i) it operates on **AND-OR graphs** rather than standard graphs, allowing problem decomposition, (ii) it uses **min (for OR) and sum (for AND)** aggregation of successor costs when propagating estimates upward, and (iii) it terminates when the **start node is marked SOLVED**, not when any single goal node is reached. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is a "solution graph" in the context of AO\* search? State the admissibility condition on the heuristic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **solution graph** is the marked subgraph of the AND-OR graph containing those nodes and arcs that AO\* has committed to as constituting the cheapest path (or set of paths) from the start node to a set of goal nodes. All nodes in this graph are tagged **SOLVED**. The **admissibility condition** requires that the heuristic $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for every node $n$, i.e., the heuristic must **never overestimate** the true optimal cost to a goal. This guarantees that AO\* returns an optimal solution graph. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** The two most common reasons students lose marks on AO\* problems are: **(1)** Forgetting to **re-mark** the solution graph when a cheaper alternative OR-branch is found during cost revision, and **(2)** Using a **sum** for an OR node or a **min** for an AND node — the aggregation rule is node-type dependent. Always re-draw the marked solution graph *after* every cost-revision step. A missing re-marking step costs a full **2-mark penalty** in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

> Consider the AND-OR graph shown below. $S$ is the start node (OR). $A$ and $B$ are OR-successors of $S$. $C$ and $D$ are AND-successors of $B$. $E$ and $F$ are OR-successors of $A$. $G$, $H$, $I$, $J$ are leaf goal nodes.
>
> Edge costs: $c(S,A)=3$, $c(S,B)=2$, $c(A,E)=2$, $c(A,F)=1$, $c(B,C)=1$, $c(B,D)=2$, $c(C,G)=2$, $c(C,H)=3$, $c(D,I)=1$, $c(D,J)=4$.
>
> Heuristic values at leaves: $h(G)=2$, $h(H)=1$, $h(I)=3$, $h(J)=2$, $h(E)=4$, $h(F)=2$.
>
> **(a)** Compute the cost $f$ of the solution graph and identify the marked path. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain, with a neat diagram, how AO\* would terminate after the second iteration if $F$ were replaced by a goal node. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A

**Part (a) — Cost computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute $f$ at leaves via their parent OR successors.

For $E$ (a direct leaf): $f(E) = h(E) = 4$.

For $F$ (a direct leaf): $f(F) = h(F) = 2$.

For $G$ and $H$ (children of OR node $C$):

$$
f(C) = \min\big[c(C,G) + h(G),\ c(C,H) + h(H)\big] = \min\{2+2,\ 3+1\} = \min\{4, 4\} = 4
$$

For $I$ and $J$ (children of OR node $D$):

$$
f(D) = \min\big[c(D,I) + h(I),\ c(D,J) + h(J)\big] = \min\{1+3,\ 4+2\} = \min\{4, 6\} = 4
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $f(B)$ — $B$ is an **AND node** (both $C$ and $D$ required):

$$
f(B) = \big[c(B,C) + f(C)\big] + \big[c(B,D) + f(D)\big] = (1+4) + (2+4) = 5 + 6 = 11
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f(A)$ — $A$ is an **OR node**:

$$
f(A) = \min\big[c(A,E) + f(E),\ c(A,F) + f(F)\big] = \min\{2+4,\ 1+2\} = \min\{6, 3\} = 3
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $f(S)$ — $S$ is an **OR node**:

$$
f(S) = \min\big[c(S,A) + f(A),\ c(S,B) + f(B)\big] = \min\{3+3,\ 2+11\} = \min\{6, 13\} = 6
$$

**Solution Graph:** The minimum-cost branch is $S \to A \to F$ with total cost **6**. All three nodes are marked SOLVED.

> **Mark Allocation:** Formula identification (2) + substitution (2) + final cost + solution graph marking (3) = **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — AO\* termination after Iteration 2 (7 marks):**

If $F$ is a **goal node**, then the algorithm proceeds as follows:

- **Iteration 1:** Expand $S$. Generate $A$ and $B$. Compute heuristic-based costs. Since $A$ has lower estimated cost, mark the OR-branch $S \to A$ in the solution graph.
- **Iteration 2:** Expand $A$. Generate $E$ and $F$. **Since $F$ is a goal node, it is immediately tagged SOLVED.** The cost revision propagates upward: $f(A) = 1 + 0 = 1$ (the cost of traversing $A \to F$ is $c(A,F) = 1$ and $h(F) = 0$ for a goal). This is cheaper than $A \to E$ (cost $2+4=6$), so the solution graph is **re-marked** to $S \to A \to F$.
- **Termination:** Backpropagation marks $A$ SOLVED (it has a SOLVED successor $F$). Backpropagation marks $S$ SOLVED (it has a SOLVED successor $A$). The algorithm **halts** with the final solution graph $S \to A \to F$ of cost $\mathbf{1 + 1 = 2}$.

The diagram for the solution graph is:

```mermaid
graph TD
    solS((S - SOLVED))
    solA((A - SOLVED))
    solF((F - SOLVED GOAL))

    solS -- c S A equals 1 --> solA
    solA -- c A F equals 1 --> solF
```

> **Mark Allocation:** Iteration 1 description (2) + Iteration 2 expansion & SOLVED tagging (3) + Final solution graph drawing (2) = **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

> An intelligent agent must navigate a building. The top-level task is **OR-decomposed** into two sub-tasks: "Reach the office" ($A$) OR "Reach the lab" ($B$). Reaching the office ($A$) further **AND-decomposes** into two independent sub-tasks: "Open the door" ($C$) AND "Walk through" ($D$). Reaching the lab ($B$) is an OR-decomposition into "Take the lift" ($E$) OR "Take the stairs" ($F$).
>
> Edge costs: $c(S,A)=1$, $c(S,B)=1$, $c(A,C)=1$, $c(A,D)=2$, $c(B,E)=1$, $c(B,F)=3$.
>
> Heuristic values: $h(C)=1$, $h(D)=1$, $h(E)=2$, $h(F)=0$ ($F$ is a goal node).
>
> **(a)** Apply the AO\* algorithm step by step and identify the optimal solution graph with its total cost. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare AO\* search with A\* search in terms of (i) graph structure, (ii) cost aggregation, and (iii) optimality conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B

**Part (a) — AO\* execution (7 marks):**

**Iteration 1 — Expand $S$:**
Compute $f(A)$ and $f(B)$ using their successors (deferred for now since $A$ and $B$ are not yet expanded; we use $f(A) = c(A, ?) + \text{min/sum of successors}$ after their expansion).

Place $A$ and $B$ in the solution graph as alternative OR-branches. **Provisional costs** (using only the direct edge to $A$/$B$ plus $h$ of the cheaper "unexpanded" sub-tree, conservatively zero):

For KTU rigor, the *correct* AO\* approach expands the *unexpanded* frontier node first. Suppose we expand $A$ first (leftmost rule).

**Iteration 2 — Expand $A$ (AND node):**
Generate $C$ and $D$. Both are leaves.

$$
f(C) = h(C) = 1
$$

$$
f(D) = h(D) = 1
$$

Since $A$ is an **AND node**, both $C$ and $D$ must be solved:

$$
f(A) = \big[c(A,C) + f(C)\big] + \big[c(A,D) + f(D)\big] = (1+1) + (2+1) = 2 + 3 = 5
$$

**Iteration 3 — Expand $B$ (OR node):**
Generate $E$ and $F$.

$$
f(E) = h(E) = 2
$$

Since $F$ is a goal: $f(F) = 0$.

$$
f(B) = \min\big[c(B,E) + f(E),\ c(B,F) + f(F)\big] = \min\{1+2,\ 3+0\} = \min\{3, 3\} = 3
$$

**Iteration 4 — Cost revision at $S$:**

$$
f(S) = \min\big[c(S,A) + f(A),\ c(S,B) + f(B)\big] = \min\{1+5,\ 1+3\} = \min\{6, 4\} = 4
$$

**Optimal Solution Graph:** $S \to B \to F$ with total cost $\mathbf{1 + 3 + 0 = 4}$.

> **Mark Allocation:** Iteration-by-iteration trace (3) + AND/OR aggregation formulas applied correctly (2) + Final solution graph + cost (2) = **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Dimension | A\* Search | AO\* Search |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Graph structure** | Standard directed graph with single-type nodes | AND-OR graph with OR and AND arc types |
| **(ii) Cost aggregation** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ (uniform) | $f(n) = \min$ (OR) or $\sum$ (AND) over successors |
| **(iii) Optimality condition** | Admissible $h$ ensures optimal *path* | Admissible $h$ ensures optimal *solution graph* |

> **Mark Allocation:** Three dimensions × ~2 marks each (1 for statement + 1 for correct technical detail) + overall coherence (1) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 / AO\* Search**

- ✅ **AO\* operates on AND-OR graphs**, not standard graphs — this is the single most important distinguishing feature.
- ✅ **OR node**: pick the *minimum*-cost successor branch.
- ✅ **AND node**: pick the *sum* of all successor branches.
- ✅ **Solution graph** = the marked subgraph AO\* commits to; all its nodes are eventually **SOLVED**.
- ✅ **Admissibility condition**: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$ — never overestimate the true remaining cost.
- ✅ **Termination condition**: Start node $S$ is marked **SOLVED**, not when the first goal is reached.
- ✅ **Cost propagation** is **bottom-up**: revise ancestors *after* expanding a frontier node.
- ✅ **Re-marking** is mandatory when a cheaper OR-branch is discovered during cost revision.
- ✅ **Computational advantage**: Decomposition into $k$ AND-sub-problems reduces worst-case complexity from $O(b^d)$ to roughly $O(k \cdot b^{d/k})$.
- ✅ **Heuristic must be consistent** (monotone) for efficient AO\*; otherwise re-expansion may be required.
- ✅ **Applications**: Planning, robotics task trees, medical diagnosis, compiler code generation.
- ✅ **Common exam trap**: Students often write $f(n) = \min$ for an AND node — **deduct 2 marks**.
- ✅ **Edge cost** is *added* to successor cost, never stored at the parent alone.
- ✅ **Solution graph must be re-drawn** at the end of every iteration for full marks in the KTU answer script.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hill Climbing Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hill Climbing Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hill Climbing** is an **iterative, heuristic-based local search algorithm** belonging to the family of *informed search* strategies. It continuously moves *in the direction of increasing evaluation* (heuristic value, cost, or objective function) to find the **best possible state** — specifically, the **peak of a hill (global maximum)** — by exploring only the immediate neighbors of the current state in the state-space graph.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Hill Climbing is positioned as the **simplest and most fundamental local search algorithm** used in solving *constraint satisfaction problems*, *optimization problems*, and forms the conceptual backbone for advanced algorithms like *Simulated Annealing* and *Genetic Algorithms*.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **State Space** | The graph of all possible states reachable from the initial state. |
| **Objective Function $f(n)$** | A numeric function that evaluates how *good* a state is. Higher $f(n) \rightarrow$ closer to the goal. |
| **Current State** | The state where the algorithm currently resides. |
| **Neighbor State** | Any state reachable by a *single legal move* from the current state. |
| **Heuristic $h(n)$** | An *estimate* of the distance/cost from a state $n$ to the goal. |
| **Peak / Global Maximum** | The optimal state with the highest objective value. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Mountain Climber"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a blindfolded mountaineer standing somewhere on a foggy, hilly terrain.** The climber cannot see the entire mountain range. The only feedback they receive is the *direction of steepest ascent under their feet*. At every step, they:
> 1. Feel which neighboring direction goes *uphill*.
> 2. Take **one step** toward the highest adjacent point.
> 3. **Stop** when every neighboring step is *downhill* — they assume they have reached the peak.

This is precisely how the **Hill Climbing Algorithm** operates:
- It has **no global map** of the search space.
- It makes **locally optimal choices** at every step.
- It uses **greedy evaluation** (best neighbor) to navigate.

**The crucial catch:** The blind climber may reach a *small foothill* and assume it is Mount Everest — this is the famous **Local Maxima** problem of Hill Climbing.

---

## 1.3 Why Hill Climbing Matters in AI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hill Climbing is the **prototype of informed local search**. It is computationally cheap ($O(b)$ per step where $b$ is branching factor), memory efficient (stores only current state), and forms the foundation for:
> - **Heuristic search optimization** (e.g., A* with limited memory).
> - **Neural network weight optimization** (basic gradient ascent).
> - **Robotics path planning** (real-time reactive navigation).
> - **Game playing AI** (move selection in Chess/Checkers).
> - **NP-hard problem solving** (TSP, N-Queens, scheduling).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The Hill Climbing algorithm follows a strict **generate-and-test loop**. Every iteration consists of:

1. **Evaluate** the current state using the heuristic function $h$.
2. **Generate** all neighbor states reachable via a single legal move.
3. **Compare** each neighbor's heuristic value against the current state.
4. **Select** the neighbor with the steepest improvement (or any improvement, depending on variant).
5. **Move** to the selected neighbor if it improves; else **terminate**.
6. **Loop** until no improvement is possible or a maximum iteration count is reached.

---

## 2.2 Variants of Hill Climbing (KTU High-Yield Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **three main variants**. Understand each carefully.

### 2.2.1 Simple Hill Climbing (also called *next-ascent* or *basic*)

- Inspects **only one neighbor** at a time (randomly or sequentially).
- Moves if that single neighbor is better than the current state.
- **Fastest**, but **least thorough** — may miss the global optimum.

### 2.2.2 Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing (also called *variant 2* or *maximization search*)

- **Exhaustively evaluates ALL neighbors** of the current state.
- Moves to the neighbor with the **largest improvement** $\Delta h$.
- **Most thorough**, but **computationally expensive** per step.
- This is the **most commonly tested variant in KTU exams**.

### 2.2.3 Stochastic Hill Climbing

- Selects neighbors **randomly** with a probability proportional to their heuristic.
- Avoids deterministic traps, but does not guarantee convergence.

---

## 2.3 State Space Diagram — Peak Topologies

The Hill Climbing algorithm's behavior is best understood through the **state-space landscape**. There are four critical topologies that KTU exams repeatedly emphasize:

| Topology | Visual Description | Hill Climbing's Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Maximum** | A peak that is *higher than all neighbors* but *not the highest* in the space. | Algorithm **terminates prematurely**, mistaking the foothill for the true peak. |
| **Global Maximum** | The **highest peak** in the entire state space. | The *ideal* outcome — reachable only under favorable initial conditions. |
| **Plateau (Flat Region)** | A region where multiple states share the **same heuristic value**. | Algorithm **stalls**, as no neighbor appears "better". |
| **Ridge** | A high-elevation sequence of local maxima connected like a **mountain ridge**. | Algorithm may oscillate between slopes and struggle to ascend along the ridge. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ridge Problem Analogy:** Imagine walking on the roof of a house. You can step sideways (staying at roof-height) but every forward/backward step drops you to the ground. Hill Climbing keeps falling off the roof because it greedily picks the highest neighbor, which may be one step sideways instead of staying on top.

---

## 2.4 Mathematical Formulation

Let the current state be $s_c$ with objective value $f(s_c)$. A neighbor $s_n \in N(s_c)$ is **better** if:

$$
f(s_n) > f(s_c)
$$

The **steepest ascent update rule** is:

$$
s_{c}^{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{s_n \in N(s_c)} f(s_n)
$$

where:
- $s_c$ = current state.
- $N(s_c)$ = set of all neighbors of $s_c$.
- $\arg\max$ = the neighbor that yields the **maximum** value of $f$.

The **termination condition** is:

$$
\forall \, s_n \in N(s_c) : \quad f(s_n) \le f(s_c)
$$

i.e., **no neighbor strictly improves** upon the current state — the algorithm halts at a local optimum.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Equation / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $f(s)$ | Objective function at state $s$ | Higher is better (maximization) |
| $h(s)$ | Heuristic estimate at state $s$ | $h(s) \approx$ distance to goal |
| $N(s_c)$ | Neighbor set of $s_c$ | All states one move away |
| $\Delta f$ | Improvement offered by a neighbor | $\Delta f = f(s_n) - f(s_c)$ |
| Steepest ascent rule | Choose best neighbor | $s_c^{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{s_n \in N(s_c)} f(s_n)$ |
| Termination | No improvement possible | $\max_{s_n \in N(s_c)} f(s_n) \le f(s_c)$ |
| Time complexity per step | $O(b)$ for simple, $O(b)$ for steepest | $b$ = branching factor |
| Space complexity | $O(1)$ (only current state stored) | Constant memory |
| Total worst-case steps | Unbounded (may loop on plateaus) | — |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Telecommunications:** Dynamic channel allocation by hill climbing on signal-to-noise ratio surfaces.
> - **VLSI Design:** Wire routing using hill climbing on congestion heuristics.
> - **Logistics:** Vehicle routing in delivery networks.
> - **Machine Learning:** Basic weight tuning in shallow perceptrons (precursor to gradient descent).
> - **Robotics:** Real-time local trajectory planning when global maps are unavailable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Classic KTU Problem — 8-Puzzle (Heuristic Hill Climbing)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> Use **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** with the heuristic $h(n) =$ *number of tiles out of place* to solve the 8-puzzle starting from the initial state below.

**Initial State $S_0$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 3 & \_ \\
4 & 2 & 5 \\
7 & 8 & 6
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Goal State $S_g$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 3 \\
4 & 5 & 6 \\
7 & 8 & \_ \\
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Heuristic:** $h(n)$ = number of tiles not in their goal position (blank excluded).

For the **Initial State $S_0$**:
- Tile 2 is at position $(2,2)$ instead of $(1,2)$ $\rightarrow$ wrong.
- Tile 5 is at $(2,3)$ instead of $(2,2)$ $\rightarrow$ wrong.
- Tile 6 is at $(3,3)$ instead of $(2,3)$ $\rightarrow$ wrong.

$$
h(S_0) = 3
$$

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Generate All Neighbor States of $S_0$

The blank `\_` at position $(1,3)$ can slide in **four directions**: Up, Down, Left, Right. However, only **three** are valid (Up and Right are outside the grid, so we consider **Left** and **Down** moves, plus **Right** if we consider wrap — here standard 8-puzzle only allows Down and Left).

**Move A — Slide blank LEFT** (tile 3 slides right):

$$
S_A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \_ & 3 \\ 4 & 2 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- Tiles out of place: 2 (at $(2,2)$), 3 (at $(1,3)$), 5 (at $(2,3)$), 6 (at $(3,3)$).

$$
h(S_A) = 4
$$

**Move B — Slide blank DOWN** (tile 5 slides up):

$$
S_B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 2 & \_ \\ 7 & 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- Tiles out of place: 2, 5, 6 (same three).

$$
h(S_B) = 3
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Neighbor | $h$ value | $\Delta h = h(S_n) - h(S_0)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $S_A$ | 4 | $+1$ (worse) |
| $S_B$ | 3 | $0$ (no improvement) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Hill Climbing is STUCK!** Both neighbors give $h \le 3$. The algorithm **terminates prematurely** at $S_0$, even though the goal is reachable. This is the classic **Plateau problem**.

---

## 3.3 Demonstration — Working Case (Modified Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **To illustrate successful ascent, consider starting state $S_1$:**

$$
S_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & \_ & 5 \\ 7 & 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_1) = 3
$$

**Neighbors of $S_1$:**

**$S_{1A}$** — blank moves LEFT (tile 2 slides right):

$$
S_{1A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 4 \\ \_ & 2 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_{1A}) = 5
$$

**$S_{1B}$** — blank moves UP (tile 3 slides down):

$$
S_{1B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \_ & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_{1B}) = 4
$$

**$S_{1C}$** — blank moves RIGHT (tile 5 slides left):

$$
S_{1C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 & \_ \\ 7 & 8 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_{1C}) = 2
$$

**Selection using steepest ascent:**

$$
S_c^{\text{new}} = \arg\min \{ h(S_{1A}), h(S_{1B}), h(S_{1C}) \} = S_{1C} \quad (\text{minimum } h = 2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU textbooks:** Many AI textbooks (including the prescribed KTU reference) define $h$ as *tiles out of place*, which must be **minimized**. Hence steepest ascent on $f$ corresponds to steepest descent on $h$. The selection rule remains $\arg\max f = \arg\min h$.

The algorithm moves to $S_{1C}$ and **continues climbing** until the goal $S_g$ with $h(S_g) = 0$ is reached.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Proof of Termination

Let the objective function $f$ be bounded:

$$
f_{\min} \le f(s) \le f_{\max} \quad \forall \, s \in \text{State Space}
$$

Since the algorithm only **increases** $f$ at every step:

$$
f(s_0) \le f(s_1) \le f(s_2) \le \dots
$$

and the state space is **finite** with cardinality $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert < \infty$:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} f(s_k) = f^{*}
$$

where $f^*$ is some local maximum. Thus, the algorithm **must terminate** in finite steps. However, $f^*$ is **not guaranteed** to equal the global maximum $f_{\max}$.

---

## 3.5 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Mathematical, Language-Agnostic)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the pseudocode for **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** in pure mathematical notation. (KTU does not mandate code; the steps are what is assessed.)

**Algorithm:** `STEEPEST_ASCENT_HILL_CLIMBING(problem)`

```
1.  current  ← MAKE_NODE(problem.INITIAL_STATE)
2.  loop:
3.      neighbor ← a highest-valued successor of current
4.      if VALUE(neighbor) ≤ VALUE(current) then
5.          return current  // Local Maximum reached
6.      current ← neighbor
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hill Climbing — Functional Block Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below models the complete **functional architecture** of the Hill Climbing algorithm. Each block represents a distinct computation phase and the data flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Initial State S0]) --> B[Current State Sc]
    B --> C[Heuristic Evaluator f Sc]
    C --> D[Neighbor Generator N Sc]
    D --> E[Compute f Sn for all neighbors]
    E --> F{Any neighbor better than Sc?}
    F -- Yes --> G[argmax over neighbors Sn]
    G --> H[Update Sc to Snew]
    H --> I[Increment iteration counter]
    I --> J{Max iterations reached?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K([Return current as local optimum])
    F -- No --> K
    K --> L([End])
```

**Block-by-Block Description:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| `Heuristic Evaluator` | Computes $f(s_c)$ for the current state. |
| `Neighbor Generator` | Enumerates all legal successor states $N(s_c)$. |
| `Compute f Sn` | Evaluates heuristic for each generated neighbor. |
| `argmax Selector` | Picks the neighbor with maximum $f$ (steepest ascent). |
| `Termination Check` | Verifies iteration budget and convergence. |

---

## 4.2 State Space Landscape — Hill Climbing Failure Modes

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram visualizes the **four topological pitfalls** of Hill Climbing in a single integrated schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Topology[State Space Topology and Failure Modes]
        direction TB
        T1[Global Maximum Peak]:::peak
        T2[Local Maximum Foothill]:::foothill
        T3[Plateau Flat Region]:::plateau
        T4[Ridge Zigzag Path]:::ridge
    end

    S([Start S0]) -- greedy ascent --> T2
    T2 -. premature halt .-> H([Algorithm Halted at Foothill])
    S -. alternative path .-> T4
    T4 -. oscillations .-> H2([Algorithm Trapped on Ridge])
    S -. via side path .-> T3
    T3 -. no improvement .-> H3([Algorithm Stalled on Plateau])
    T1:::peak

    classDef peak fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef foothill fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    classDef plateau fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
    classDef ridge fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Algorithmic Flow — Simple vs. Steepest Ascent Hill Climbing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph V1[Simple Hill Climbing]
        direction TB
        V1S([Start]) --> V1A[Pick ONE random neighbor]
        V1A --> V1B{Is neighbor better?}
        V1B -- Yes --> V1C[Move to neighbor]
        V1C --> V1A
        V1B -- No --> V1E([Halt])
    end

    subgraph V2[Steepest Ascent Hill Climbing]
        direction TB
        V2S([Start]) --> V2A[Generate ALL neighbors]
        V2A --> V2B[Evaluate f Sn for each]
        V2B --> V2C{Any neighbor best?}
        V2C -- Yes --> V2D[Move to BEST neighbor]
        V2D --> V2A
        V2C -- No --> V2F([Halt at peak])
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
