# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Hill Climbing search. List any two of its limitations.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> Hill Climbing is a **local search algorithm** that iteratively moves from the current state to the *neighbor state with the highest heuristic value*, terminating when no neighbor offers an improvement. It uses a **greedy evaluation** of the immediate neighborhood to find the peak of an objective function.
>
> **Limitations (any 2, ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Local Maxima trap:** The algorithm halts at a suboptimal peak.
> 2. **Plateau problem:** No direction of ascent exists in flat regions.
> 3. **Ridge problem:** Greedy moves cause oscillation across the ridge.
> 4. **No backtracking:** Cannot escape once a poor move is made.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Simple Hill Climbing and Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Simple Hill Climbing | Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Neighbors examined per step** | Only **one** (random or sequential) | **All** neighbors |
> | **Selection criterion** | First better neighbor found | Neighbor with **maximum** $f(s_n)$ |
> | **Time per step** | $O(1)$ | $O(b)$ where $b$ = branching factor |
> | **Solution quality** | Lower (may settle quickly) | Higher (more thorough) |
> | **Best use case** | Time-critical applications | Quality-critical applications |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Both options below are equivalent in difficulty.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hill Climbing algorithm** with its general procedure. Discuss the **state-space landscape** and how local maxima, plateaus, and ridges affect the search. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Algorithm Definition (2 marks):**
Hill Climbing is an **informed local search algorithm**. Starting from an initial state, it iteratively:
- Examines all neighboring states.
- Moves to the neighbor with the highest heuristic value $f$.
- Terminates when no neighbor is better.

The **update rule** is:

$$
s_{c}^{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{s_n \in N(s_c)} f(s_n)
$$

**(ii) General Procedure (2 marks):**
The procedural flow is:
1. Initialize $s_c$ = initial state.
2. Evaluate $f(s_c)$.
3. Generate $N(s_c)$.
4. Compute $f(s_n)$ for all $s_n \in N(s_c)$.
5. If $\max f(s_n) \le f(s_c)$ → **terminate**.
6. Else set $s_c \leftarrow \arg\max f(s_n)$ and return to step 3.

**(iii) State-Space Landscape (3 marks):**

| Feature | Effect on Hill Climbing |
|---|---|
| **Local Maxima** | Algorithm halts prematurely; *the foothill looks like Mount Everest*. Solution: **Random restart** from a different initial state. |
| **Plateau** | All neighbors have equal $f$; no direction is "uphill". Solution: Apply **random walk** or **limited sideways moves**. |
| **Ridge** | Greedy descent on both sides causes oscillation; the algorithm cannot climb *along* the ridge. Solution: Use **bidirectional search** or **modified heuristic** that combines two criteria. |

---

**(b)** Solve the following 8-puzzle instance using **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** with $h(n) =$ *number of misplaced tiles*. Show all neighbor states and their heuristic values at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Initial State $S_0$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 4 \\ 7 & \_ & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Goal State $S_g$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & \_ & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Evaluate $h(S_0)$.**
Misplaced tiles (excluding blank): 2, 8, 3, 1, 6, 4, 7, 5.
Compared to goal: 2 (wrong), 8 (wrong), 1 (wrong), 6 (wrong).

$$
h(S_0) = 4
$$

**Step 2: Generate neighbors of $S_0$** (blank at $(3,2)$ → can move Left, Up, Right):

**Neighbor A — Blank moves LEFT** (tile 7 slides right):

$$
S_A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 4 \\ \_ & 7 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_A) = 5
$$

**Neighbor B — Blank moves UP** (tile 6 slides down):

$$
S_B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & \_ & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_B) = 5
$$

**Neighbor C — Blank moves RIGHT** (tile 5 slides left):

$$
S_C = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 4 \\ 7 & 5 & \_ \end{bmatrix}, \quad h(S_C) = 5
$$

**Step 3: Apply termination rule.**
All neighbors have $h = 5 > h(S_0) = 4$ in terms of "misplaced count" being *higher*, which is **worse** for our minimization objective. So:

$$
\min \{ h(S_A), h(S_B), h(S_C) \} = 5 > h(S_0) = 4
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **No neighbor improves the heuristic.** Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing **terminates at $S_0$** with $h = 4$. The goal is *unreachable* from this start without auxiliary techniques like random restart.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **three variants of Hill Climbing**: Simple, Steepest-Ascent, and Stochastic. Provide a clear tabular comparison along with appropriate use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Hill Climbing has three principal variants that differ in how they select the next state from the available neighbors.

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Simple Hill Climbing | Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing | Stochastic Hill Climbing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Neighbor selection** | First better neighbor (deterministic) | Neighbor with maximum $f(s_n)$ | Random neighbor, weighted by $f$ |
| **Computation per step** | $O(1)$ | $O(b)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Stochastic |
| **Quality of result** | Moderate | Highest (per step) | Variable |
| **Tendency to get stuck** | High | High | Lower (randomness helps) |
| **Memory** | $O(1)$ | $O(b)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Use case** | Fast prototyping, real-time systems | Optimization when quality matters | Noisy/uncertain landscapes |

**Use Cases (2 marks):**
- **Simple:** Real-time game AI where speed dominates.
- **Steepest-Ascent:** Combinatorial optimization (TSP, scheduling) where near-optimal solutions are critical.
- **Stochastic:** Non-deterministic environments, robotics with sensor noise, simulated annealing precursors.

---

**(b)** What are the **limitations of Hill Climbing**? Explain any **two techniques** used to overcome these limitations, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Local Maxima:** Algorithm halts at a suboptimal peak that appears optimal locally.
2. **Plateau:** A flat region provides no heuristic gradient; the algorithm stalls indefinitely.
3. **Ridge:** Greedy lateral moves cause oscillation between ridges instead of climbing along the ridge.
4. **No backtracking:** Once a move is made, it cannot be undone — leading to irreversible traps.

**Overcoming Techniques (4 marks — 2 marks each):**

**Technique 1: Random Restart Hill Climbing**
- *Concept:* Conduct Hill Climbing from **multiple randomly chosen initial states** and return the best result across all runs.
- *Example:* Solving N-Queens — restart from a new random board configuration if a local minimum (conflicts > 0) is reached. With enough restarts, the probability of finding the global optimum approaches **1**.

**Technique 2: Simulated Annealing (Probabilistic Escape)**
- *Concept:* Allow the algorithm to occasionally accept **worse moves** with a probability that **decreases over time** (controlled by a "temperature" parameter $T$).

$$
P(\text{accept worse move}) = e^{-\Delta f / T}
$$

- *Example:* In VLSI chip layout, accept a temporary increase in wire-length to escape a local minimum, hoping to reach a globally shorter layout later.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently:
> - **Confuse minimization vs. maximization** of $h$ in numerical problems (1 mark lost).
> - **Forget to list neighbors** at each step, jumping straight to "we move here" (2 marks lost).
> - **State limitations without giving remedies** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests remediation (2 marks lost).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hill Climbing Algorithm**

- [ ] Hill Climbing is a **local search** algorithm — it does **not** maintain a search tree or path.
- [ ] It uses **greedy evaluation** of the immediate neighbor set $N(s_c)$.
- [ ] **Three variants:** Simple, Steepest-Ascent, Stochastic.
- [ ] **Update rule:** $s_c^{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{s_n \in N(s_c)} f(s_n)$.
- [ ] **Termination:** When $\max f(s_n) \le f(s_c)$, or when iteration budget exhausted.
- [ ] **Time per step:** $O(b)$ for steepest, $O(1)$ for simple.
- [ ] **Space:** $O(1)$ — only the current state is stored.
- [ ] **Local Maxima** is the most critical failure mode.
- [ ] **Plateau** → use random walk or sideways moves.
- [ ] **Ridge** → use bidirectional or heuristic-combination approaches.
- [ ] **Remedies:** Random Restart, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search.
- [ ] The algorithm **always halts** in finite time (bounded state space, monotonic $f$).
- [ ] Result is **not guaranteed** to be globally optimal.
- [ ] Suitable for **NP-hard optimization** when approximation is acceptable.
- [ ] Forms the **conceptual foundation** for gradient ascent in continuous optimization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristic Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heuristic Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Function ($h(n)$):** A heuristic function is a domain-specific **estimation function** that maps any node $n$ in a search space to a non-negative real number representing an estimated cost of the cheapest path from node $n$ to a **goal state**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, heuristic functions are the cornerstone of **Informed Search Strategies**, also called *heuristic search*. They inject *problem-specific knowledge* into general search algorithms, transforming blind, exponential-time exploration into directed, polynomial-time traversal.

Mathematically, for any node $n$ in state space:

$$h(n) = \text{estimated cost of cheapest path from } n \text{ to a goal}$$

The "true" cost (unknown to the algorithm during search) is denoted $h^*(n)$. Thus, the fundamental relationship explored in KTU board exams is:

$$0 \leq h(n) \approx h^*(n)$$

The goal of heuristic design is to make $h(n)$ as close to $h^*(n)$ as possible while remaining computationally cheap.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Mountain Climber's Estimate**
>
> Imagine you are standing somewhere in a vast mountain range, blindfolded, and need to reach the lowest valley (the goal). You cannot see the entire terrain, but you have a **barometric altimeter** that tells you your current altitude. The straight-line distance to the valley based on your current elevation is your *heuristic estimate* — it is not the actual walking distance (which could be longer due to ridges, cliffs, or winding paths), but it gives you a *reasonable guess* about which direction is "downhill-ish."
>
> - A **good heuristic** (like the altimeter) closely approximates the true difficulty without requiring exhaustive exploration.
> - A **bad heuristic** might tell you "0 cost" everywhere (useless) or "infinity" everywhere (overly pessimistic).
> - An **overestimating heuristic** tells you a path is shorter than it really is — this is *dangerous* because it can mislead the search into dead ends.

This intuition directly motivates the formal properties — *admissibility* and *consistency* — discussed in Section 2.

---

### 1.3 Standard Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Standard Usage |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate from node $n$ to goal | Always non-negative |
| $h^*(n)$ | True cost from $n$ to nearest goal | Oracle value, used only in analysis |
| $g(n)$ | Path cost from start to $n$ | Used in A* as part of $f(n)$ |
| $f(n)$ | Evaluation function $g(n)+h(n)$ | Used by A* search |
| $c(n,a,n')$ | Cost of action $a$ from $n$ to $n'$ | Step cost in state space |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, heuristic functions are evaluated under **Course Outcome CO2 (Apply intelligent search techniques)** and are central to questions on **Best-First Search, A\*, and AOA (Admissibility/Optimality Analysis)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Foundation: How Heuristics Power Informed Search

Informed search algorithms select the next node to expand based on an evaluation function. The structure of this function determines the algorithm's behavior:

**1. Greedy Best-First Search**

$$f(n) = h(n)$$

- Expands the node that *appears* closest to the goal.
- **Fast but suboptimal** — can be misled by overestimated heuristics.
- Does not revisit the past cost $g(n)$.

**2. A\* Search (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael — 1968)**

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

- Combines *past cost* (path so far) with *future estimate* (heuristic).
- **Optimal IF** $h(n)$ is *admissible* (tree search) or *consistent* (graph search).
- This is the gold-standard KTU board question.

**3. Uniform-Cost Search (for comparison)**

$$f(n) = g(n)$$

- Blind to future; degenerates into Dijkstra's algorithm.

---

### 2.2 The Two Critical Properties of Heuristic Functions

#### 2.2.1 Admissibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Admissibility):** A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost to reach a goal. Formally:
> $$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall \, n$$
> An admissible heuristic is therefore **optimistic** — it believes the goal is closer (or equal in cost) than it actually is.

**Consequence:** A\* using an admissible heuristic on a tree search is guaranteed to find an *optimal* (minimum-cost) solution.

#### 2.2.2 Consistency (Monotonicity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Consistency):** A heuristic $h$ is **consistent** (or **monotone**) if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ of $n$ generated by action $a$ with step cost $c(n,a,n') \geq 0$:
> $$h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

**Consequence:** Consistency implies admissibility (provided $h(\text{goal}) = 0$). A\* with a consistent heuristic:
- Never re-expands a node.
- Finds an optimal path in graph search without a closed list gymnastics.

**Triangular interpretation:** Consistency is essentially the *triangle inequality* applied to heuristic estimates — the estimated cost from $n$ cannot exceed the step cost to $n'$ plus the estimate from $n'$.

---

### 2.3 Dominance Between Heuristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Dominance Rule:** Given two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$, if
> $$h_2(n) \geq h_1(n) \quad \forall \, n$$
> then $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$, and any search algorithm using $h_2$ will expand **no more nodes** than the same algorithm using $h_1$.

Domination is the theoretical foundation for **inventing better heuristics** — the closer $h$ is to $h^*$ (without exceeding it), the more efficient the search.

---

### 2.4 The Effective Branching Factor ($b^*$)

This is the KTU-preferred metric for *empirically* comparing heuristic quality without inspecting the entire search tree:

$$N + 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (b^*)^i$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of nodes expanded by A\* in finding a solution
- $d$ = depth of the solution
- $b^*$ = effective branching factor (the rate at which nodes actually grow)

For a well-designed heuristic, $b^*$ is close to **1** (very efficient). For uniform-cost search, $b^*$ equals the actual branching factor (inefficient).

---

### 2.5 Methods for Constructing Heuristic Functions

| Method | KTU Textbook Reference | Description |
|--------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Relaxed Problems** | Russell & Norvig, Ch. 3 | Drop constraints of the original problem to get an easier subproblem whose optimal cost is admissible. |
| **Pattern Databases** | Culberson & Schaeffer (1996) | Store exact subproblem costs in a lookup table (hash map). |
| **Landmarks** | Richter & Westphal (2010) | Use must-pass waypoints; estimate cost by summing landmark distances. |
| **Disjoint Pattern DBs** | Korf (1997) | Combine pattern DBs whose patterns are non-overlapping. |
| **Weighted Heuristic** | $f(n) = g(n) + W \cdot h(n), W > 1$ | Trades optimality for speed; used in *Anytime Reparing A\** (ARA\*). |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Equation / Concept | Formal Statement | Practical Use |
|---|--------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Heuristic Definition | $h(n) \geq 0$, $h(\text{goal}) = 0$ | Standard boundary conditions. |
| 2 | Admissibility | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Guarantees A* optimality (tree search). |
| 3 | Consistency | $h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ | Guarantees A* optimality (graph search). |
| 4 | A* Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Node selection criterion. |
| 5 | Greedy Evaluation | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Fast, non-optimal search. |
| 6 | Effective Branching Factor | $N + 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (b^*)^i$ | Empirical heuristic-quality metric. |
| 7 | Dominance | $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n) \Rightarrow$ fewer expansions | Comparative heuristic analysis. |
| 8 | Relaxed Problem Cost | $h_{\text{relax}}(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Admissibility justification. |
| 9 | Heuristic Combination (Max) | $h(n) = \max(h_1(n), h_2(n), \dots, h_k(n))$ | Both admissible $\Rightarrow$ combination admissible. |
| 10 | Manhattan Distance (8-puzzle) | $h(n) = \sum_i \vert x_i - x_i^{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_i - y_i^{\text{goal}} \vert$ | Classic admissible heuristic. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pitfall Avoided:** In the row above, the Manhattan distance uses LaTeX `\vert` (rendered as $\vert$) instead of raw $\vert$ symbols, preventing markdown table-breaking.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

Heuristic functions are not academic curiosities — they power:

- **GPS Route Planning** (Google Maps, HERE): $h(n)$ = straight-line geodesic distance.
- **Robotic Motion Planning (RRT\*, PRM\*)**: $h(n)$ = Euclidean distance in configuration space.
- **Game Playing Engines (Chess, Go)**: $h(n)$ = material/positional evaluation function.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain (OR-Tools, SAP)**: $h(n)$ = crow-fly distance between warehouses.
- **Automated Theorem Proving**: $h(n)$ = number of unproven subgoals remaining.
- **Network Routing (OSPF, BGP)**: Distance-vector heuristics for next-hop selection.

In each case, the heuristic encodes *geometric* or *semantic* knowledge that a blind searcher lacks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Consistency ⇒ Admissibility

This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question. The examiner expects a fully written-out proof by mathematical induction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that if $h$ is consistent (i.e., $h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$) and $h(\text{goal}) = 0$, then $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$.

**Proof by induction on path length $k$ from $n$ to goal.**

---

**Base Case ($k = 0$):** Node $n$ is itself a goal.

By boundary condition:

$$h(n) = h(\text{goal}) = 0 = h^*(n)$$

Thus $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ holds with equality. ✓

---

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume the result holds for all nodes at distance $\leq k$ from any goal. That is, for any node $m$ with a shortest path of length $\leq k$ to the goal:

$$h(m) \leq h^*(m)$$

---

**Inductive Step ($k \rightarrow k+1$):** Consider a node $n$ whose shortest path to a goal has length $k+1$. Let the first step be action $a$, leading to successor $n'$ with step cost $c(n,a,n')$. The remainder of the shortest path from $n'$ to the goal has length $k$, so by the inductive hypothesis:

$$h(n') \leq h^*(n')$$

The total optimal cost from $n$ satisfies:

$$h^*(n) = c(n,a,n') + h^*(n')$$

Applying the *consistency condition*:

$$h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$$

By the inductive hypothesis, $h(n') \leq h^*(n')$, and since $c(n,a,n') \geq 0$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n') \leq c(n,a,n') + h^*(n') = h^*(n)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{h(n) \leq h^*(n)}$$

which establishes **admissibility**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: A* on the 8-Puzzle

This is the canonical KTU example. Consider the start state $S$ and goal $G$:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 4 \\ 8 & 6 & 2 \\ 7 & \text{\_} & 5 \end{bmatrix} \qquad G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & \text{\_} & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Heuristic Used:** $h_1(n)$ = number of misplaced tiles (excluding blank). Let's compute for the start $S$:
- Tile 1: correct ✓ (cost 0)
- Tile 2: at position 6, goal position 6 — actually correct ✓ (cost 0)
- Tile 3: at position 2, goal position 3 → misplaced
- Tile 4: at position 3, goal position 4 → misplaced
- Tile 5: at position 9, goal position 9 → correct ✓
- Tile 6: at position 5, goal position 8 → misplaced
- Tile 7: at position 7, goal position 7 → correct ✓
- Tile 8: at position 4, goal position 5 → misplaced

Total misplaced tiles $h_1(S) = 4$.

For the *root* node $S$, $g(S) = 0$, so $f(S) = 0 + 4 = 4$.

**Heuristic Comparison Table:**

| State | $g(n)$ | $h_1(n)$ (misplaced) | $h_2(n)$ (Manhattan) | $f_1 = g+h_1$ | $f_2 = g+h_2$ |
|-------|--------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------|
| $S$   | 0      | 4                    | 6                    | 4             | 6             |
| $S_1$ | 1      | 3                    | 5                    | 4             | 6             |
| $S_2$ | 1      | 4                    | 7                    | 5             | 8             |
| $S_3$ | 1      | 5                    | 8                    | 6             | 9             |

**Observation:** $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for every state. Hence **$h_2$ dominates $h_1$**, and A* using $h_2$ will never expand more nodes than A* using $h_1$.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Computation: Effective Branching Factor

Suppose A* with heuristic $h$ expands $N = 52$ nodes to find a solution at depth $d = 6$. Compute $b^*$.

The defining equation:

$$N + 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (b^*)^i$$

With $N = 52$ and $d = 6$:

$$53 = (b^*) + (b^*)^2 + (b^*)^3 + (b^*)^4 + (b^*)^5 + (b^*)^6$$

We solve iteratively. **Try $b^* = 1.5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.5^1 &= 1.500 \\
1.5^2 &= 2.250 \\
1.5^3 &= 3.375 \\
1.5^4 &= 5.063 \\
1.5^5 &= 7.594 \\
1.5^6 &= 11.391 \\
\text{Sum} &= 31.172
\end{aligned}
$$

Too low. **Try $b^* = 1.7$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.7^1 &= 1.700 \\
1.7^2 &= 2.890 \\
1.7^3 &= 4.913 \\
1.7^4 &= 8.352 \\
1.7^5 &= 14.198 \\
1.7^6 &= 24.137 \\
\text{Sum} &= 56.190
\end{aligned}
$$

Slightly too high. **Try $b^* = 1.68$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.68^1 &= 1.680 \\
1.68^2 &= 2.822 \\
1.68^3 &= 4.741 \\
1.68^4 &= 7.965 \\
1.68^5 &= 13.381 \\
1.68^6 &= 22.480 \\
\text{Sum} &= 53.070
\end{aligned}
$$

Very close to 53. **Refine: $b^* \approx 1.679$.**

For KTU exams, showing one iteration step-by-step and a final rounded answer ($b^* \approx 1.68$) is sufficient.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Heuristic from a Relaxed Problem

Consider the 8-puzzle with the move rule "a tile can slide into the blank." The relaxed problem allowing "a tile can move anywhere" is essentially the *15-puzzle without adjacency constraint.* The optimal cost of this relaxed problem is exactly the **Manhattan distance**:

$$h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert x_i - x_i^{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_i - y_i^{\text{goal}} \vert$$

Since relaxing the problem *removes constraints*, the optimal cost of the relaxed problem is $\leq$ the optimal cost of the original problem:

$$h_{\text{relax}}(n) \leq h^*_{\text{original}}(n)$$

This is the **admissibility proof via relaxation**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: KTU Frequently Confused Pairs

| Concept A | Concept B | Distinguishing Test |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Admissible | Consistent | Consistency $\Rightarrow$ Admissibility, but not vice versa (in general). |
| Greedy Best-First | A* | Greedy uses only $h(n)$; A* uses $g+h$. |
| Tree Search | Graph Search | Tree may re-expand; graph needs consistency to avoid re-expansion. |
| $h_1$ vs $h_2$ domination | Tie on admissibility | Dominance is **pointwise**: $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for *all* $n$. |
| Effective branching factor | Actual branching factor | $b^*$ is empirically measured; $b$ is structurally defined. |
| Pattern DB | Landmark heuristic | Pattern DB uses exact subproblem costs; landmarks use intermediate waypoints. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following block diagram classifies the family of heuristic functions studied in KTU Module 2.

flowchart TD

### 4.2 A* Search Operational Flow with Heuristic Injection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Start Node n0"] --> INIT["Initialize OPEN with start CLOSED empty"]
    INIT --> LOOP{"OPEN empty"}
    LOOP -- "Yes" --> FAIL["Return FAILURE"]
    LOOP -- "No" --> POP["Pop node n with min f of n equals g plus h"]
    POP --> GOAL{"n is GOAL"}
    GOAL -- "Yes" --> SUCC["Return Solution Path"]
    GOAL -- "No" --> EXPAND["Generate Successors n prime"]
    EXPAND --> COMP["For each n prime compute g of n prime equals g of n plus c"]
    COMP --> EVAL["Compute h of n prime using heuristic function"]
    EVAL --> UPDATE["Insert or update in OPEN if f value improves"]
    UPDATE --> ADD["Add n to CLOSED"]
    ADD --> LOOP

    EVAL -.Heuristic Input.-> HEUR["h of n from Relaxed Problem or Pattern DB or Manhattan Distance"]

    class S,INIT,POP,EXPAND,COMP,EVAL,UPDATE,ADD,SUCC,FAIL io

```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heuristic Pipeline in a Solver

This block diagram maps the *end-to-end* flow of how a heuristic is constructed, stored, queried, and consumed during an A* run — useful for engineering-style questions about real-world AI systems.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a heuristic function. State the admissibility condition for a heuristic to be used with A* search.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ is an estimate of the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal node. It encodes problem-specific knowledge to guide informed search.

For use with A* search (tree-search variant), the heuristic must be **admissible**:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall \, n$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost. An admissible heuristic is *optimistic* — it never overestimates the remaining cost. This property ensures A* returns an optimal solution in tree search. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between Greedy Best-First Search and A* Search in terms of the evaluation function used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Greedy Best-First Search | A* Search |
|---------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Considers past cost? | No | Yes |
| Optimality | Not guaranteed | Optimal if $h$ is admissible/consistent |
| Speed | Faster | Slightly slower but accurate |

A* balances *path cost so far* ($g$) with *estimated cost to goal* ($h$), whereas Greedy Best-First uses only the heuristic estimate. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the concept of heuristic functions in informed search. Discuss admissibility and consistency with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the 8-puzzle problem, the start state $S$ and goal state $G$ are given. Compute:
- (i) The number of misplaced tiles heuristic $h_1(S)$
- (ii) The Manhattan distance heuristic $h_2(S)$
- (iii) Which heuristic dominates? Justify. **(7 marks)**

States:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 4 \\ 7 & \text{\_} & 5 \end{bmatrix} \quad G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & \text{\_} & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Heuristic Function — Definition (2 marks):**
A heuristic function $h(n)$ estimates the cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal. It is a *rule of thumb* derived from domain knowledge. For the 8-puzzle, common heuristics include $h_1$ = misplaced tiles and $h_2$ = Manhattan distance.

**Admissibility (2.5 marks):**
A heuristic is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall \, n$$

*Example:* In route-finding, the straight-line distance (Euclidean) is admissible because the actual driving distance is always $\geq$ the straight-line distance.

**Consistency (2.5 marks):**
A heuristic is **consistent** (monotone) if for every edge $(n, n')$ with step cost $c$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$$

*Example:* The Manhattan distance on the 8-puzzle is consistent because moving one tile by one square changes $h_2$ by exactly $\pm 1$, satisfying the triangle inequality.

Consistency implies admissibility; both guarantee A* optimality.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Misplaced Tiles $h_1(S)$ (2 marks):**

Comparing $S$ and $G$ position-by-position:

| Position | Tile in S | Tile in G | Correct? |
|----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| (1,1) | 2 | 1 | ✗ |
| (1,2) | 8 | 2 | ✗ |
| (1,3) | 3 | 3 | ✓ |
| (2,1) | 1 | 8 | ✗ |
| (2,2) | 6 | \_ | (blank, skip) |
| (2,3) | 4 | 4 | ✓ |
| (3,1) | 7 | 7 | ✓ |
| (3,2) | \_ | 6 | (blank, skip) |
| (3,3) | 5 | 5 | ✓ |

Misplaced non-blank tiles: 2, 8, 1 → **$h_1(S) = 3$**.

**(ii) Manhattan Distance $h_2(S)$ (3 marks):**

For each misplaced tile, compute $\vert x - x_{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y - y_{\text{goal}} \vert$:

- **Tile 1:** in S at (2,1), in G at (1,1) → $|2-1| + |1-1| = 1$
- **Tile 2:** in S at (1,1), in G at (1,2) → $|1-1| + |1-2| = 1$
- **Tile 8:** in S at (1,2), in G at (2,1) → $|1-2| + |2-1| = 2$
- **Tile 6:** in S at (2,2), in G at (3,2) → $|2-3| + |2-2| = 1$
- **Tile \_ (blank):** in S at (3,2), in G at (2,2) → $|3-2| + |2-2| = 1$

(Note: KTU convention counts blank only if required; using non-blank Manhattan sum is more common.)

Summing tile distances: $1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 5$ → **$h_2(S) = 5$**.

**(iii) Dominance (2 marks):**

Since for every state $n$ in the 8-puzzle space, $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$, the **Manhattan distance dominates the misplaced-tile heuristic**. A* using $h_2$ will expand *no more nodes* than A* using $h_1$, making it strictly more efficient.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme Style]

**(a)** Define the effective branching factor $b^*$. A* with heuristic $h$ expands $N = 100$ nodes to find a solution at depth $d = 5$. Calculate $b^*$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **relaxed problem** approach to designing heuristic functions. Prove that the heuristic obtained from a relaxed problem is always admissible. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition of $b^*$ (3 marks):**
The **effective branching factor** $b^*$ is the branching factor that a uniform tree of depth $d$ would need to have in order to contain $N+1$ nodes — the same number expanded by A* to find a depth-$d$ solution. It satisfies:

$$N + 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (b^*)^i$$

A $b^*$ close to 1 indicates an excellent heuristic; a $b^*$ close to the actual branching factor indicates a weak heuristic.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Given $N = 100$, $d = 5$:

$$101 = b^* + (b^*)^2 + (b^*)^3 + (b^*)^4 + (b^*)^5$$

**Try $b^* = 2.0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 + 4 + 8 + 16 + 32 &= 62 \quad \text{(too low)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Try $b^* = 2.5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.5 + 6.25 + 15.625 + 39.0625 + 97.656 &= 161.04 \quad \text{(too high)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Try $b^* = 2.2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.2 + 4.84 + 10.648 + 23.426 + 51.537 &= 92.65 \quad \text{(slightly low)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Try $b^* = 2.25$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.25 + 5.0625 + 11.391 + 25.629 + 57.665 &= 101.997 \quad \text{(≈ 101)} \quad ✓
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{b^* \approx 2.25}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Relaxed Problem Approach (3.5 marks):**

A **relaxed problem** is a simplified version of the original problem with one or more constraints *removed*. For example:
- *Original 8-puzzle:* a tile can move only if the adjacent square is empty.
- *Relaxed 8-puzzle:* a tile can move to any adjacent square regardless of occupancy.
- *Further relaxation:* a tile can move to any square at all.

Since constraints are removed, the relaxed problem is **easier** to solve and its optimal solution cost provides an estimate of the original cost.

**Admissibility Proof (3.5 marks):**

Let $P$ be the original problem with optimal cost $h^*_P(n)$, and let $P'$ be a relaxation with optimal cost $h^*_{P'}(n)$.

Because $P'$ has fewer constraints, every solution to $P$ is also a valid solution to $P'$. Hence the *optimal* solution of $P'$ is at least as good as the optimal solution of $P$:

$$h^*_{P'}(n) \leq h^*_P(n)$$

If we set $h(n) = h^*_{P'}(n)$, then:

$$h(n) = h^*_{P'}(n) \leq h^*_P(n) = h^*(n)$$

Therefore $h$ is admissible. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $h(\text{goal}) = 0$** when proving admissibility from consistency — this is the **boundary condition** that KTU evaluators explicitly check. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing admissibility with consistency** in definitions. Consistency is a *stronger* property involving edge costs; admissibility is pointwise. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting to skip the blank tile** when computing $h_1$ or $h_2$ for the 8-puzzle. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Showing one step only** in the effective branching factor calculation. Examiners expect at least **two trial values** and a concluding statement. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Misapplying dominance** — saying "$h_2$ dominates $h_1$" without checking pointwise inequality $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for *all* $n$. (-1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Heuristic Functions**

- [x] **Heuristic $h(n)$** = estimated cost from $n$ to goal; **always non-negative**, with $h(\text{goal}) = 0$.
- [x] **Admissibility**: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — optimistic, never overestimates.
- [x] **Consistency**: $h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ — triangle inequality on estimates.
- [x] **Consistency $\Rightarrow$ Admissibility**, but the converse is false in general.
- [x] **A\* optimality** is guaranteed in tree search if $h$ is admissible; in graph search if $h$ is consistent.
- [x] **Greedy Best-First**: $f(n) = h(n)$ — fast but suboptimal.
- [x] **A\***: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — optimal when admissible/consistent.
- [x] **Dominance**: $h_2 \geq h_1$ pointwise $\Rightarrow$ $h_2$ expands $\leq$ nodes than $h_1$.
- [x] **Effective branching factor** $b^*$ from $N+1 = \sum_{i=1}^{d}(b^*)^i$ — empirical heuristic-quality metric; closer to 1 is better.
- [x] **Relaxed problem** heuristic: drop a constraint $\Rightarrow$ easier subproblem $\Rightarrow$ admissible estimate.
- [x] **Pattern databases** store exact subproblem costs in a hash table; admissible by construction.
- [x] **Manhattan distance** dominates **misplaced tiles** on the 8-puzzle.
- [x] **Heuristic combination**: $h(n) = \max(h_1(n), h_2(n), \dots, h_k(n))$ preserves admissibility if each $h_i$ is admissible.
- [x] **Real-world examples**: straight-line distance (GPS), material count (chess), configuration-space distance (robotics).
- [x] **Common KTU exam tags**: admissibility proof, 8-puzzle computation, $b^*$ calculation, dominance justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Games
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Games # 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Artificial Intelligence, **Games** refer to deterministic, two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information adversarial search problems in which an intelligent agent must select optimal actions by anticipating the rational counter-moves of an opponent. Formally, a game is defined as a **search problem with an adversary**, characterized by: - An **initial state** (board configuration / position). - A set of legal operators / **successor function** (legal moves). - A **terminal test** (game-over condition: win, lose, or draw). - A **utility / payoff function** (numerical outcome: e.g., +1 win, -1 lose, 0 draw). - A **turn-based adversarial player** (MAX vs. MIN). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Games):** > The syllabus mandates coverage of: (i) **Game Trees**, (ii) **Minimax Algorithm**, (iii) **Alpha-Beta Pruning**, (iv) **Heuristic Evaluation Functions**, and (v) the analysis of **Perfect vs. Imperfect Information** games. ## 1.2 The Zero-Sum Assumption A game is called **zero-sum** if the total utility awarded to all players at the end of the game is constant (typically zero). Therefore, *whatever one player gains, the other loses in equal measure*. Chess, Tic-Tac-Toe, and Checkers are canonical examples. $$U_{MAX}(s) + U_{MIN}(s) = 0$$ ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Chess Master & The Mirror > [!NOTE] > **🍎 Intuition for Students:** > Imagine you are playing chess against a mirror-image version of yourself — an opponent *as smart as you are*. Every move you make, the opponent makes the move that is **worst for you but best for them**. Your job is not merely to find a "good" move, but to find the move that works **even under the assumption that your opponent will respond optimally**. This is the essence of adversarial search — you are not searching in a vacuum; you are searching through the *mind* of a rational adversary. ## 1.4 The "Game" as a Search Problem — Key Differences from Single-Agent Search | Property | Single-Agent Search (Module 1/2) | Adversarial Game Search | |---|---|---| | Goal | Find a goal state | Find a strategy (a contingent plan) | | Opponent | None (static environment) | An intelligent adversary | | Solution | A path from start to goal | A *policy* specifying a move for every possible opponent reply | | Optimality | Path cost minimization | Maximin / Minimax optimality | | Evaluation | Heuristic at each node | Heuristic at *leaf* nodes only | ## 1.5 Classification of Games (Taxonomy) > [!TIP] > **Why is this classification important?** > The complexity of an AI algorithm for a game depends entirely on which category the game falls into. KTU examiners love asking **2-mark / 3-mark questions** on this taxonomy. | Game Property | Examples | AI Technique Used | |---|---|---| | Deterministic, Perfect Information | Chess, Go, Checkers, Tic-Tac-Toe | Minimax, Alpha-Beta, Monte Carlo Tree Search | | Stochastic, Perfect Information | Backgammon, Monopoly | Expectiminimax | | Deterministic, Imperfect Information | Poker (closed cards), Stratego | Partially Observable Search, CFR | | Stochastic, Imperfect Information | Bridge, Scrabble (hidden tiles) | POMDPs, Probabilistic Reasoning | # 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Game Tree A **game tree** is a directed graph in which: - **Root** = current state. - **Internal nodes** = game states reachable by a sequence of moves. - **Edges** = legal moves (actions). - **Leaves (terminal nodes)** = states where the game ends. - **Levels alternate**: MAX's turn → MIN's turn → MAX's turn ... > [!IMPORTANT] > The **branching factor `b`** is the average number of legal moves available at any state, and the **depth `d`** is the average number of plies (half-moves) to termination. The total number of leaf nodes in a fully expanded game tree is `b^d`. ### 2.1.1 Concrete Numbers — Why Brute Force Fails | Game | Avg. Branching Factor `b` | Avg. Depth `d` | Total Leaf Nodes `b^d` | Time (1 node/μs) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tic-Tac-Toe | ~5 | ~9 | ~1.96 × 10⁶ | ~2 seconds | | Checkers | ~8 | ~70 | ~10²⁰ | ~3,170 years | | Chess | ~35 | ~80 | ~10¹²⁴ | ~10¹⁸ years (Heat death of universe!) | | Go (19×19) | ~250 | ~150 | ~10³⁶⁰ | Astronomically impossible | > [!WARNING] > This is the **central motivation** for Alpha-Beta Pruning. Brute-force Minimax is computationally infeasible for real games. **KTU frequently tests the numbers above.** ## 2.2 The Minimax Algorithm — Theory **Minimax** is a recursive, depth-limited (or terminal) backtracking algorithm that computes the optimal move for MAX under the assumption that MIN plays perfectly. ### 2.2.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Generate the game tree** to a chosen depth `d` (either to terminal states or to a cut-off depth). 2. **Evaluate leaf nodes** using the heuristic/utility function. 3. **Propagate values upward**: - At a **MAX level**, choose the child with the **maximum** value. - At a **MIN level**, choose the child with the **minimum** value. 4. **At the root**, MAX selects the move that leads to the child with the highest backed-up value. ### 2.2.2 The Minimax Recurrence $$\text{Minimax}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(s) & \text{if } s \text{ is terminal} \\ \max_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s,a)) & \text{if it is MAX's turn} \\ \min_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s,a)) & \text{if it is MIN's turn} \end{cases}$$ ## 2.3 Alpha-Beta Pruning — Theory **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an *exact* optimization of Minimax — it returns the same move as plain Minimax but prunes (ignores) subtrees that cannot possibly influence the final decision. ### 2.3.1 The Two Bounds (α and β) - **α (alpha)** = the value of the **best (highest-value) choice** we have found so far **along the path for MAX**. Initially `α = -∞`. - **β (beta)** = the value of the **best (lowest-value) choice** we have found so far **along the path for MIN**. Initially `β = +∞`. ### 2.3.2 The Pruning Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pruning Condition:** Prune (stop exploring) a branch as soon as `α ≥ β`. > - When a MAX node's value `≥ β`, MIN will never allow this branch → **β-cutoff**. > - When a MIN node's value `≤ α`, MAX will never allow this branch → **α-cutoff**. ## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Notes | |---|---|---| | `b` | Branching factor | Avg. legal moves per state | | `d` | Search depth in plies | Half-moves | | `b^d` | Worst-case leaves of full tree | Brute force cost | | `b^{d/2}` | Best-case leaves with Alpha-Beta (perfect ordering) | **Square root** of brute force | | `b^{2d/3}` | Average-case leaves with Alpha-Beta (random ordering) | Practical estimate | | `α` | Best value for MAX so far | Starts at `-∞` | | `β` | Best value for MIN so far | Starts at `+∞` | | `α ≥ β` | **Pruning condition** | Cut-off the branch | | `f(n) = w₁·Material + w₂·Mobility + w₃·KingSafety + ...` | Heuristic evaluation | Weighted sum of features | | `Effective Branching Factor (EBF)` | `≈ \sqrt[\leftroot{-1}\uproot{2} d]{\text{Leaves explored}}` | Lower = better pruning | | `time complexity` | `O(b^m)` where `m` is effective depth | With Alpha-Beta, `m ≈ d/2` | | `space complexity` | `O(b·m)` | Recursion depth × sibling stack | ## 2.5 Heuristic Evaluation Functions — Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Use in Chess Engines:** Modern engines (Stockfish, Komodo) use a **piece-weighted evaluation**: Pawn=1, Knight=3, Bishop=3, Rook=5, Queen=9, plus positional bonuses (center control, king safety, pawn structure). This same idea generalizes to **portfolio optimization, supply-chain adversarial bidding, and game-theoretic pricing** in engineering. A typical evaluation function: $$f(n) = w_1 \cdot (\text{Material}_{MAX} - \text{Material}_{MIN}) + w_2 \cdot M(n) + w_3 \cdot K(n)$$ where: - `M(n)` = mobility (number of legal moves). - `K(n)` = king safety score. - `w_i` = empirically tuned weights. ## 2.6 Why Alpha-Beta Works — The Intuition > [!TIP] > **💡 The "Stop Reading a Bad Book" Analogy:** > Imagine you are MAX reading a book review. If you have already found a book rated 9/10, and the next chapter of a competing book starts with a 3/10, you stop reading — you already have a better option. Alpha-Beta does the same for game trees: as soon as MIN sees that MAX has a better option elsewhere, MIN stops exploring the current branch. This can **halve the effective depth** of the search (square-root the branching factor) without losing optimality. # 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Optimal Move via Minimax (Worked Example) Consider the following simple game tree where MAX is at the root. `A`, `B`, `C`, `D`, `E`, `F` are leaf values. ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ A=3 B=12 C=2 D ``` Wait, the standard KTU example tree: ``` MAX (root) / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ MAX MAX MAX MAX / \ / \ / \ / \ 3 12 2 ? ... ... ``` Let us use a **fully worked 2-ply (4-level) tree** which is the standard KTU textbook example: ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ A B C D ``` Where `A=3`, `B=12`, `C=2`, leaf `D=...` etc. **Full Sample Tree** (used in Russell & Norvig textbook example): ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ MAX MAX MAX MAX / \ / \ / \ / \ 3 12 2 ... ``` Let us use the **Russell & Norvig standard example** (very commonly asked in KTU): ``` (MAX) / \ (MIN) (MIN) / \ / \ (B) (C) (D) (E) /\ /\ /\ /\ 3 12 2 ... etc ``` For rigorous correctness, let me construct a **clean KTU-style 4-level tree** with explicit values: **Level 3 (Leaves)**: 8 leaves, all values given below. **Level 2 (MAX nodes)**: 4 nodes, each backed up. **Level 1 (MIN nodes)**: 2 nodes, each backed up. **Level 0 (Root MAX)**: Final value. ### Sample Tree (Numerical): ``` [MAX, Root] / \ [MIN, Left] [MIN, Right] / \ / \ [MAX, L1] [MAX, L2] [MAX, R1] [MAX, R2] / \ / \ / \ / \ 3 12 2 ? 14 5 2 20 ``` Wait, let me use the **canonical Russell & Norvig tree** that KTU follows: **Canonical Tree (B, C, D, E as MAX-nodes with 2 leaves each, leaves: 3, 12, 2, ?, 14, 5, 2, 20):** ``` (MAX) / \ (MIN) (MIN) / \ / \ (B) (C) (D) (E) /\ /\ /\ /\ 3 12 2 ? 14 5 2 20 ``` ### Step-by-Step Minimax Backup **Step 1:** Evaluate the leaves (terminal utility values): - `B`'s leaves: `3` and `12`. - `C`'s leaves: `2` and a missing value (we'll use a known value). Let us use a **fully numeric** version, replacing unknowns with explicit numbers — this is the rigorous way KTU expects it: ``` MAX / \ MIN MIN / \ / \ MAX MAX MAX MAX /\ /\ /\ /\ 3 12 2 15 14 5 2 20 ``` **Step 1 (Level 3 → Level 2, MAX-nodes B, C, D, E):** For each MAX node, pick the **maximum** of its children: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Value}(B) &= \max(3, 12) = 12 \\ \text{Value}(C) &= \max(2, 15) = 15 \\ \text{Value}(D) &= \max(14, 5) = 14 \\ \text{Value}(E) &= \max(2, 20) = 20 \end{aligned}$$ > Each MAX node **maximizes** — it picks the move giving the highest utility for itself. **Step 2 (Level 2 → Level 1, MIN-nodes Left and Right):** For each MIN node, pick the **minimum** of its children: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Value}(\text{Left MIN}) &= \min(12, 15) = 12 \\ \text{Value}(\text{Right MIN}) &= \min(14, 20) = 14 \end{aligned}$$ > Each MIN node **minimizes** — it picks the move that is worst for MAX (and hence best for MIN). **Step 3 (Level 1 → Level 0, Root MAX):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Value}(\text{Root}) &= \max(12, 14) = 14 \end{aligned}$$ > The root picks the branch giving `14`, i.e., the **Right MIN** branch, leading to **sub-tree D**. ### Final Answer: **MAX's optimal first move is towards the Right MIN branch (sub-tree D)**, with a backed-up utility of **14**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning on the Same Tree (with full trace) Reuse the same tree, but now apply Alpha-Beta with the **left-to-right ordering**: ``` MAX (α=-∞, β=+∞) / \ MIN (α=-∞, β=+∞) MIN (α=12, β=+∞) / \ / \ MAX MAX MAX MAX (α=-∞,β=+∞)(α=-∞,β=12) (α=12,β=+∞) (α=12,β=14) / \ / \ / \ / \ 3 12 2 15 14 5 2 20 ``` ### Step-by-Step Trace: **Step 1 — Visit leaf `3` (left-most):** - At MAX node B, `α = max(-∞, 3) = 3`. No pruning (`3 < +∞`). - Return `3` to parent Left MIN. **Step 2 — Visit leaf `12`:** - At MAX node B, `α = max(3, 12) = 12`. Return `12` to Left MIN. - Left MIN: `β = min(+∞, 12) = 12`. - B's value is now `12`. Return `12` to Root MAX. - Root MAX: `α = max(-∞, 12) = 12`. **Step 3 — Visit leaf `2` (under C):** - Enter Left MIN's other child (C) with `α = 12` (inherited from root) and `β = +∞`. - At MAX node C, `α = max(-∞, 2) = 2`. - Check pruning: Is `α ≥ β`? `2 ≥ +∞`? **No** → continue. - Visit leaf `15`. **Step 4 — Visit leaf `15` (under C):** - At MAX node C, `α = max(2, 15) = 15`. Return `15` to Left MIN. - Left MIN: `β = min(12, 15) = 12` (no change). - Left MIN returns `12` to Root. - Root: `α = max(12, 12) = 12`. **Step 5 — Now Root decides on Right MIN branch:** - Right MIN inherits `α = 12, β = +∞`. - Enter Right MIN's first child (D) with `α = 12, β = +∞`. **Step 6 — Visit leaf `14` (under D):** - At MAX node D, `α = max(-∞, 14) = 14`. - Check pruning: `α ≥ β`? `14 ≥ +∞`? **No** → continue. - Return `14` to Right MIN. Right MIN: `β = min(+∞, 14) = 14`. - Return `14` to Root. Root: `α = max(12, 14) = 14`. **Step 7 — Visit leaf `5` (under D):** - Enter D's second child with `α = 12, β = 14`. - At MAX node D, leaf = 5. `α = max(12, 5) = 12` (no change). - **Check pruning:** Is `α ≥ β`? `12 ≥ 14`? **No** → continue. - Return `5` to Right MIN. Right MIN: `β = min(14, 5) = 5`. - D's value = `5`. Return `5` to Right MIN's parent (D itself returns max=14 already? No — actually, the MAX node D returns `max(14, 5) = 14`.) > [!NOTE] > **The order of operations matters!** The MAX-node D's *own* return value is computed from its children. The α-β bounds inherited by MAX-node D are `(α=12, β=14)`. The leaf 5 doesn't trigger a cutoff, so D returns `14` to Right MIN. **Step 8 — Right MIN computes its value so far:** - `β = min(14, 14) = 14`. So far Right MIN has seen a value of `14` from D. - Return `14` to Root. Root: `α = max(14, 14) = 14`. **Step 9 — Enter Right MIN's second child (E) with `α = 14, β = +∞`:** - At MAX node E, `α = max(-∞, 2) = 2`. - Check pruning: `α ≥ β`? `2 ≥ +∞`? **No** → continue. - Visit leaf `20`. **Step 10 — Visit leaf `20` (under E):** - At MAX node E, `α = max(2, 20) = 20`. Return `20` to Right MIN. - Right MIN: `β = min(14, 20) = 14` (no change). Return `14`. **Final:** Root MAX's value is `14`, achieved via the **Right MIN → D** branch. ### Pruning Analysis — What Got Cut? In this particular ordering, **no branches were pruned**, because the move ordering was *bad* (sub-optimal leaves came first). This illustrates a key KTU point: > [!WARNING] > **KTU Trap Question:** "Did alpha-beta always reduce work?" > **Answer:** No. Without **good move ordering** (best moves first), alpha-beta can degenerate to `O(b^d)` — no better than plain Minimax. With **perfect ordering**, it is `O(b^{d/2})` — i.e., you can search **twice as deep** in the same time. ## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Heuristic Evaluation Function (Chess) Consider a simple chess position. The evaluation function is: $$f(s) = w_1 \cdot \Delta P + w_2 \cdot M(s) + w_3 \cdot K(s)$$ **Sample calculation** with `w₁ = 1, w₂ = 0.1, w₃ = 0.5`: - `ΔP` (material): MAX has Q+R+2P = `9+5+2 = 16`; MIN has R+B+N = `5+3+3 = 11`. So `ΔP = 5`. - `M(s)` (mobility, # of legal moves): MAX = `24`, MIN = `18`. So `M = 6`. - `K(s)` (king safety, 0-10 scale): MAX = `8`, MIN = `5`. So `K = 3`. Then: $$\begin{aligned} f(s) &= (1)(5) + (0.1)(6) + (0.5)(3) \\ &= 5 + 0.6 + 1.5 \\ &= 7.1 \end{aligned}$$ A **positive score of +7.1** indicates that the position favors MAX. ## 3.4 Derivation #4 — The Effective Branching Factor (EBF) Suppose a chess engine using Alpha-Beta searches a tree of depth `d = 8` and evaluates `T = 1,000,000` leaves. The EBF is: $$\begin{aligned} \text{EBF} &= \sqrt[\leftroot{-1}\uproot{2} d]{T} \\ &= \sqrt[8]{1{,}000{,}000} \\ &= 1{,}000{,}000^{1/8} \\ &\approx 10^{6/8} = 10^{0.75} \approx 5.62 \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** An EBF close to the ideal `b^{1/2}` (= `√35 ≈ 5.92` for chess) indicates excellent move ordering. The closer the EBF is to `√b`, the more efficient the pruning. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Algorithms | Algorithm | Time Complexity (Worst) | Time Complexity (Best) | Returns Optimal Move? | Requires Heuristic? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Plain Minimax | `O(b^d)` | `O(b^d)` | ✅ Yes (if depth covers all leaves) | Only at leaves | | Alpha-Beta (random order) | `O(b^d)` | `O(b^{d/2})` | ✅ Yes | Only at leaves | | Alpha-Beta (perfect order) | `O(b^{d/2})` | `O(b^{d/2})` | ✅ Yes | Only at leaves | | Depth-limited Alpha-Beta + Heuristic | `O(b^m)` where `m` = depth limit | — | ❌ Only near-optimal | **Yes**, at every leaf reached | | Iterative Deepening + Alpha-Beta | `O(b^m)` | — | ❌ Near-optimal | Yes | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram #1 — The Game Tree Backed-Up Values ```mermaid graph TD rootA["MAX Root
