List and briefly explain any four properties of a two-player zero-sum game.** #### Model Answer: A **game** in AI is a formal adversarial search problem in which two or more intelligent agents (called *players*) take turns selecting actions from a set of legal moves, with the goal of maximizing their own utility under the assumption that the opponent will play rationally to minimize it. The four key properties of a two-player zero-sum game are: 1. **Initial State** — The starting configuration of the game (e.g., chess board setup). 2. **Successor Function** — A specification of all legal moves from any state. 3. **Terminal Test** — A condition determining when the game ends. 4. **Utility / Payoff Function** — A numerical score (e.g., +1 for MAX win, -1 for MAX loss, 0 for draw). > **Zero-Sum:** $U_{MAX}(s) + U_{MIN}(s) = 0$, meaning one's gain is exactly the other's loss. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is Alpha-Beta Pruning? State the pruning condition.** #### Model Answer: **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an optimization technique applied to the Minimax algorithm. It eliminates (prunes) subtrees in the game tree that cannot possibly influence the final decision, thereby reducing the number of nodes evaluated — *without affecting the optimality of the result*. The algorithm maintains two values during the search: - **α (alpha)**: The value of the best move found so far for the **MAX** player along the current path. - **β (beta)**: The value of the best move found so far for the **MIN** player along the current path. **Pruning Condition:** $$\alpha \geq \beta$$ When this condition is satisfied, the current branch cannot affect the final outcome and is pruned. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### 📌 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the Minimax algorithm in detail with a suitable example. Show how the optimal value is backed up to the root.** #### Model Solution: The **Minimax algorithm** is a recursive depth-first search used in two-player zero-sum games. It works on the principle that: - **MAX** player will try to **maximize** the utility. - **MIN** player will try to **minimize** the utility (i.e., minimize MAX's payoff). **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Generate the game tree to terminal states (or depth limit). 2. Assign utility values to all terminal/leaf nodes. 3. **Backup** values level by level: - At each **MAX level**, take the **maximum** of children. - At each **MIN level**, take the **minimum** of children. 4. At the root, the move leading to the maximum value is the optimal move for MAX. **Worked Example:** Consider the following 2-ply game tree with leaves: `3, 12, 2, 15, 14, 5, 2, 20`: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Value}(B) &= \max(3, 12) = 12 \\ \text{Value}(C) &= \max(2, 15) = 15 \\ \text{Value}(D) &= \max(14, 5) = 14 \\ \text{Value}(E) &= \max(2, 20) = 20 \\ \text{Value}(\text{Left MIN}) &= \min(12, 15) = 12 \\ \text{Value}(\text{Right MIN}) &= \min(14, 20) = 14 \\ \text{Value}(\text{Root MAX}) &= \max(12, 14) = 14 \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The optimal move for MAX at the root is the one leading to the **Right MIN branch (subtree D)**, with a backed-up utility of **14**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the time and space complexity of the Minimax algorithm. Why is it impractical for complex games like chess?** #### Model Solution: **Time Complexity of Minimax:** $$T(n) = O(b^d)$$ where `b` is the branching factor and `d` is the depth of the tree. **Space Complexity:** $$S(n) = O(b \cdot d)$$ (due to the recursion stack holding siblings and depth). **Why Minimax is Impractical for Chess:** | Parameter | Chess | |---|---| | Branching factor `b` | ~35 | | Average game depth `d` | ~80 plies | | Total leaves `b^d` | `35^80 ≈ 10^124` | | Time (1 node/μs) | `~10^118` seconds | This is **astronomically larger than the age of the universe (~`10^17` seconds)**. Even with modern computers performing `10^9` nodes/second, the search would still take **~`10^115` seconds** — completely infeasible. **Conclusion:** Plain Minimax is impractical for real games. We must use: - **Depth-limited search** with **heuristic evaluation** at cutoffs. - **Alpha-Beta Pruning** to reduce the effective branching factor. - **Iterative Deepening** for time-bounded search. --- ### 📌 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the Alpha-Beta Pruning algorithm with a suitable example. Show how the number of nodes evaluated is reduced.** #### Model Solution: **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an enhancement of the Minimax algorithm that prunes subtrees that cannot influence the final decision. It tracks two parameters: - **α (alpha)** — best value for MAX found so far (initialized to `-∞`). - **β (beta)** — best value for MIN found so far (initialized to `+∞`). **Pruning Rule:** Stop exploring a branch if `α ≥ β`. **Worked Example (Left-to-Right Order):** ``` MAX (α=-∞, β=+∞) / \ MIN (α=-∞,β=+∞) MIN (α=12,β=+∞) / \ / \ MAX MAX MAX MAX / \ / \ / \ / \ 3 12 2 15 14 5 2 20 ``` **Trace:** | Step | Action | α | β | Pruned? | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Visit leaf 3 (B) | -∞ → 3 | +∞ | No | | 2 | Visit leaf 12 (B) | 3 → 12 | +∞ → 12 | No | | 3 | Left MIN returns 12; Root α=12 | 12 | +∞ | No | | 4 | Visit leaf 2 (C) | 12 | 12 | — | | 5 | Visit leaf 15 (C) | 12 | 12 → 12 | No | | 6 | Left MIN returns 12 to Root; Root α=12 | 12 | +∞ | No | | 7 | Visit leaf 14 (D) | 12 → 14 | 14 → 14 | No | | 8 | Visit leaf 5 (D) | 14 | 14 | **No prune (5 < 14)** | | 9 | D returns 14; Right MIN β=14 | 14 | 14 | — | | 10 | Visit leaf 2 (E) — α=14, β=14 | 14 | 14 | **CUTOFF!** | **Pruning Achieved:** The subtree under leaf 20 (right child of E) is pruned. Without pruning, we would have evaluated **8 leaves**. With pruning, we evaluate **7 leaves** in this case. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **What is a heuristic evaluation function? Design a simple heuristic function for chess and justify why heuristic search is essential for adversarial games.** #### Model Solution: A **heuristic evaluation function** `f(s)` assigns a numerical score to a non-terminal game state, estimating the desirability of that state for the MAX player. It encapsulates *domain knowledge* into a single scalar value. **Simple Chess Heuristic:** $$f(s) = w_1 \cdot \Delta M + w_2 \cdot \Delta A + w_3 \cdot K(s)$$ where: - `ΔM` = material balance: `Σ value(MAX piece) - Σ value(MIN piece)`. - `ΔA` = mobility: `# legal moves for MAX - # legal moves for MIN`. - `K(s)` = king safety score (0 to 10). - `w_1 = 1, w_2 = 0.1, w_3 = 0.5` (typical weights). **Standard Piece Values:** | Piece | Value | |---|---| | Pawn | 1 | | Knight | 3 | | Bishop | 3 | | Rook | 5 | | Queen | 9 | | King | ∞ (game-ending) | **Why Heuristic Search is Essential:** 1. **Tractability:** The full game tree of chess has `~10^124` leaves — impossible to evaluate fully. 2. **Time Bounds:** Practical games require moves within seconds; heuristic depth-limited search is the only feasible option. 3. **Strategic Insight:** A good heuristic captures strategic concepts (material, position, king safety) that a pure Minimax cannot evaluate in finite time. 4. **Near-Optimal Play:** Empirically, depth-limited search with a well-tuned heuristic matches or surpasses human grandmaster play (e.g., Stockfish, Deep Blue). > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing α and β:** Students often swap them. Remember: **α for MAX (start -∞)**, **β for MIN (start +∞)**. > 2. **Forgetting the initial values:** Always state `α = -∞, β = +∞` at the root. > 3. **Missing the "best-first" assumption:** Alpha-Beta's best-case `O(b^{d/2})` requires **perfect move ordering**; the examiner expects you to mention this. > 4. **Writing `α < β` instead of `α ≥ β`:** The pruning condition is `α ≥ β`, *not* the other way around. > 5. **Not labeling the turn:** Each node must be explicitly marked as MAX or MIN. A level without a label is incomplete. > 6. **Heuristic must be at the *leaf*, not at every node:** A common mistake is evaluating interior nodes. > 7. **Time complexity notation:** Write `O(b^m)` where `m` is the *effective* depth, not `O(b^{d/2})`, unless you specify "with perfect ordering." --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam!)** - ✅ A **game** is a search problem with an **adversary** (zero-sum, perfect information, deterministic). - ✅ A **game tree** alternates MAX and MIN levels; root = current state; leaves = terminal states. - ✅ **Branching factor `b`** and **depth `d`**: total leaves = `b^d`. - ✅ **Minimax** computes the **optimal** move assuming both players play perfectly. - ✅ **Backup rule:** MAX → `max(children)`, MIN → `min(children)`. - ✅ **Alpha-Beta** = Minimax + pruning. Returns the **same** result, but evaluates **fewer** nodes. - ✅ **α** = best for MAX so far; **β** = best for MIN so far. Initially `α=-∞, β=+∞`. - ✅ **Prune when `α ≥ β`**. - ✅ With **perfect move ordering**, Alpha-Beta explores only `b^{d/2}` leaves — i.e., you can search **twice as deep** in the same time. - ✅ With **random ordering**, it degenerates to `O(b^d)` (no improvement). - ✅ **Heuristic evaluation function** `f(s)` estimates utility at non-terminal states (cutoff depth). - ✅ Chess heuristic = `Material + Mobility + King Safety + Pawn Structure + ...` - ✅ **Iterative Deepening + Alpha-Beta + Transposition Table** = the practical standard for chess engines. - ✅ **Perfect information** games → Minimax works. **Imperfect information** (e.g., poker) → require probabilistic search (POMDPs, CFR). - ✅ **Stochastic games** (e.g., Backgammon) → **Expectiminimax** (adds a CHANCE node computing the *expectation*). - ✅ **Effective Branching Factor (EBF)** is a metric for measuring pruning efficiency. Lower is better. - ✅ **Space complexity** of Alpha-Beta = `O(b·m)` (DFS stack). - ✅ Alpha-Beta is **anytime**: you can stop at any time and the current best move is valid. > **🎯 Top 3 "Must-Memorize" KTU Numbers:** > 1. Chess branching factor: **~35**. > 2. Best-case Alpha-Beta reduction: **`b^d → b^{d/2}`** (search depth doubles). > 3. Time complexity with heuristic: **`O(b^m)`** where `m` = depth limit (typically 6–14 in modern engines).

topics.title: Optimal Decisions in Games
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimal Decisions in Games

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In Artificial Intelligence, **games** refer to deterministic, turn-taking, perfect-information adversarial environments in which two or more rational players (called **agents** or **players**) compete according to a fixed set of rules. Each player attempts to maximize their own payoff (or minimize the opponent's), given that the opponent is doing the same.

An **optimal decision** in a game is the move selected by an agent that guarantees the best possible achievable outcome against a perfectly rational opponent, assuming both players play from the initial state to a terminal state. The mathematical framework for finding such a decision is **adversarial search**, an extension of classical search algorithms to multi-agent competitive settings.

The canonical solution technique is the **MINIMAX algorithm**, which recursively evaluates game states assuming optimal play from both players. The function is defined formally as:

$$
\text{MINIMAX}(s) =
\begin{cases}
\text{UTILITY}(s) & \text{if } \text{TERMINAL-TEST}(s) \\
\max_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{MINIMAX}(\text{RESULT}(s,a)) & \text{if } \text{PLAYER}(s) = \text{MAX} \\
\min_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{MINIMAX}(\text{RESULT}(s,a)) & \text{if } \text{PLAYER}(s) = \text{MIN}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Optimal decisions in games cover the formulation of game trees, the MINIMAX algorithm, alpha-beta pruning, and the distinction between optimal decisions versus optimal play. These form the foundation of adversarial reasoning in AI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Zero-sum game**: A game where one player's gain is exactly the other player's loss. Total utility sum = 0.
> - **Perfect information**: Both players can see the entire state of the game (e.g., Chess, Tic-Tac-Toe). Contrast with imperfect information games (Poker, Bridge).
> - **Deterministic game**: No randomness in state transitions.
> - **Two-player turn-taking**: Players alternate moves in fixed order.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chess Grandmaster's Mind

Imagine you are playing chess against a perfectly rational opponent who never makes a mistake. You are at move 15, and you must choose between three possible moves. How do you pick the "best" one?

You mentally **simulate** the future. You imagine: "If I move my bishop, my opponent might capture my knight. Then I could respond by..." This mental simulation forms a **game tree** — a branching structure where each node is a board position, and each branch is a possible move.

Now, here's the critical insight: your opponent wants to **minimize** your eventual score, while you want to **maximize** it. This zero-sum, opposing-intent dynamic is exactly what the **MINIMAX algorithm** captures. The optimal decision is the move that leads to the best *worst-case* outcome — a strategy called the **maximin** strategy.

Think of it like a haggling scenario at a market: you want the highest price, the seller wants the lowest. The "optimal" price isn't what you wish for — it's the best outcome you can guarantee given the seller's rational resistance.

## 1.3 Game Formulation in AI

A game is formally defined by the following components (a tuple):

$$
\text{Game} = (S, A, T, U, P, s_0)
$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of **states** (board positions)
- $A$ = set of **actions** (legal moves)
- $T: S \times A \rightarrow S$ = **transition function** (RESULT function)
- $U: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ = **utility function** (pays off at terminal states)
- $P$ = **player function** (determines whose turn it is)
- $s_0 \in S$ = **initial state**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Initial State, Terminal Test, and Utility Function** together completely define a game for AI purposes. KTU examiners often test these formal definitions directly.

## 1.4 Why Optimal Decisions in Games Matter

Games provide a **microcosm** for studying rational decision-making under uncertainty and opposition. Real-world applications include:

- **Business strategy** (auctions, negotiations, pricing wars)
- **Cybersecurity** (adversarial intrusion detection)
- **Robotics** (multi-robot competition)
- **Autonomous vehicles** (merging, overtaking decisions)
- **Military logistics** (adversarial planning)
- **Game AI in industry** (Chess engines, Go engines, video game NPCs)

The complexity of games is measured by the **state-space size**. For example, the game of Chess has approximately $10^{47}$ reachable states, while Go has approximately $10^{170}$ — far exceeding the number of atoms in the observable universe.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The MINIMAX Algorithm — Theoretical Foundation

The **MINIMAX** algorithm computes the optimal move for the player called **MAX** at the current state. The name "minimax" arises from the fact that the player alternates between maximizing their own utility and minimizing the opponent's utility.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Step-by-Step

1. **Generate the game tree** from the current state $s$ down to all terminal states, considering all legal moves at each level.
2. **Label each level (depth)** with the player whose turn it is at that level. Level 0 is MAX, Level 1 is MIN, Level 2 is MAX, and so on — alternating.
3. **At each leaf (terminal) node**, retrieve the utility value $U(s)$ from the utility function (defined relative to MAX).
4. **Propagate values upward recursively**:
   - At a **MAX node**, assign the value of the **maximum** among all child node values.
   - At a **MIN node**, assign the value of the **minimum** among all child node values.
5. **At the root**, the optimal decision is the action whose resulting child node has the highest MINIMAX value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Does MINIMAX Work?**
> The algorithm is grounded in the concept of **Nash equilibrium** for two-player zero-sum games. By assuming the opponent is also rational and optimal, MINIMAX computes a strategy that is **unexploitable** — no other opponent strategy can produce a worse outcome for MAX in expectation.

### 2.1.2 The Optimal Decision vs. Optimal Play Distinction

This is a **favourite KTU exam question** and a critical conceptual point.

- An **optimal decision** is a move that leads to a state from which MAX can force a win *assuming optimal play from both sides thereafter*. It is the move with the best minimax value.
- **Optimal play** is the *entire strategy* of making optimal decisions at every subsequent state.

> [!NOTE]
> The term "optimal" in MINIMAX is conditional: it is optimal **only against an optimal opponent**. Against a suboptimal opponent, MINIMAX may leave winning opportunities on the table because it pessimistically assumes the best defense.

## 2.2 The Alpha-Beta Pruning Algorithm

The major drawback of MINIMAX is its computational complexity. For a tree with branching factor $b$ and depth $d$, MINIMAX examines $O(b^d)$ nodes — prohibitively expensive for realistic games. **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an optimization that prunes (skips) branches that cannot possibly influence the final decision, while returning the exact same result as full MINIMAX.

### 2.2.1 Two Key Bounds

- **$\alpha$ (alpha)**: The value of the **best (highest-value) choice** found so far along the path for **MAX**. Initially $\alpha = -\infty$. $\alpha$ can **never decrease** during the search.
- **$\beta$ (beta)**: The value of the **best (lowest-value) choice** found so far along the path for **MIN**. Initially $\beta = +\infty$. $\beta$ can **never increase** during the search.

### 2.2.2 Pruning Condition

**Pruning occurs when $\alpha \geq \beta$.** The intuition:
- If MAX has already found a move worth $\alpha$ and MIN has a move worth $\beta$ elsewhere, and $\alpha \geq \beta$, then MAX will never let MIN reach the worse branch — so it can be pruned.

### 2.2.3 Optimal Pruning Performance

| Configuration | Node Count Examined | Effective Branching Factor |
|---|---|---|
| No pruning (full MINIMAX) | $b^d$ | $b$ |
| Perfect ordering of moves | $b^{d/2}$ | $\sqrt{b}$ |
| Random ordering of moves | $\approx b^{3d/4}$ | $\approx b^{1.5}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With perfect move ordering, alpha-beta can search **twice as deep** as MINIMAX in the same amount of time. This is why alpha-beta is the cornerstone of practical game engines.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MINIMAX Value Recursion** | $\text{MINIMAX}(s) = \begin{cases} U(s), & \text{terminal} \\ \max_{a} \text{MINIMAX}(R(s,a)), & \text{MAX node} \\ \min_{a} \text{MINIMAX}(R(s,a)), & \text{MIN node} \end{cases}$ | $U$ = utility function, $R$ = RESULT |
| 2 | **Zero-Sum Property** | $U_{\text{MAX}}(s) + U_{\text{MIN}}(s) = 0$ for all $s$ | Utilities are opposite-sign |
| 3 | **MINIMAX Complexity** | Time: $O(b^d)$; Space: $O(bd)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth |
| 4 | **Alpha-Beta Pruning Condition** | Prune if $\alpha \geq \beta$ | $\alpha$ = MAX's best, $\beta$ = MIN's best |
| 5 | **Alpha-Beta Best Case** | Nodes examined: $O(b^{d/2})$ | Requires perfect move ordering |
| 6 | **Alpha-Beta Worst Case** | Nodes examined: $O(b^d)$ | No pruning, equivalent to MINIMAX |
| 7 | **Utility Range (NORMAL form)** | $U(s) \in [\text{min}, \text{max}]$, e.g., $[-1, +1]$ for win/loss | Bounded numeric range |
| 8 | **State-Space of Tic-Tac-Toe** | $\leq 9! = 362{,}880$ legal positions | Reduced by symmetry |
| 9 | **State-Space of Chess** | $\approx 10^{47}$ states; $\approx 10^{120}$ games | Shannon's estimate |
| 10 | **State-Space of Go** | $\approx 10^{170}$ states | Far exceeds exhaustive search |
| 11 | **Minimax Optimality Guarantee** | Returns the same move as exhaustive search | Only differs in efficiency |
| 12 | **Effective Depth Doubling** | With alpha-beta, depth searched $\approx 2 \times$ MINIMAX depth | For same time budget |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** The unit of utility is **dimensionless** but conventionally the win/loss/draw tuple is given as $(+1, -1, 0)$ or sometimes as $(+\infty, -\infty, 0)$ for unbounded scoring. Always use the values specified in the question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The minimax/alpha-beta framework extends far beyond recreational games:

1. **Financial Trading Algorithms**: High-frequency trading bots use adversarial reasoning to anticipate competitor moves.
2. **Cybersecurity Defense Systems**: Penetration testing tools model attackers as MIN players.
3. **Autonomous Vehicle Coordination**: Multi-agent merging and overtaking decisions on highways.
4. **Military Strategic Planning**: Adversarial mission planning models opposing forces.
5. **Game AI in Entertainment**: Modern chess engines (Stockfish, AlphaZero) use alpha-beta as a base layer.
6. **Auction Theory & Mechanism Design**: Bidding strategies in combinatorial auctions.
7. **RoboCup Soccer**: Robot teams plan adversarial play using game tree search.

The theoretical bound on rational play is the **minimax value** of the initial state — and the deep insight from game theory is that **for finite, deterministic, perfect-information, zero-sum games, the minimax value is well-defined and computable** (in principle).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example: Tic-Tac-Toe Terminal Evaluation

Consider a simplified 3-ply game tree (MAX = X, MIN = O) with terminal leaf values shown. We will derive the MINIMAX decision at the root.

### 3.1.1 Game Tree Setup

Assume the following terminal utility values at depth 3 (from MAX's perspective):

| Leaf Node | L1 | L2 | L3 | L4 | L5 | L6 | L7 | L8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Utility | +3 | +2 | **+1** | -1 | **+4** | -3 | **0** | -2 |

The intermediate node groupings (depth 2 → depth 1 → root):

- **Node A (depth 2, MIN)** has children: L1, L2, L3
- **Node B (depth 2, MIN)** has children: L4, L5, L6
- **Node C (depth 2, MIN)** has children: L7, L8
- **Node R1 (depth 1, MAX)** has children: A, B
- **Node R2 (depth 1, MAX)** has child: C
- **Root (depth 0, MAX)** has children: R1, R2

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive MINIMAX Computation

**Step 1: Evaluate terminal leaves** (already given as utilities).

**Step 2: Propagate from depth 2 MIN nodes.**

For Node A (MIN node), choose the minimum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(A) &= \min(\text{MINIMAX}(L1), \text{MINIMAX}(L2), \text{MINIMAX}(L3)) \\
&= \min(+3, +2, +1) \\
&= +1
\end{aligned}
$$

> This says: at node A, MIN (the opponent) will choose the worst outcome for MAX, which is +1.

For Node B (MIN node), choose the minimum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(B) &= \min(\text{MINIMAX}(L4), \text{MINIMAX}(L5), \text{MINIMAX}(L6)) \\
&= \min(-1, +4, -3) \\
&= -3
\end{aligned}
$$

For Node C (MIN node), choose the minimum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(C) &= \min(\text{MINIMAX}(L7), \text{MINIMAX}(L8)) \\
&= \min(0, -2) \\
&= -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Propagate from depth 1 MAX nodes.**

For Node R1 (MAX node), choose the maximum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(R1) &= \max(\text{MINIMAX}(A), \text{MINIMAX}(B)) \\
&= \max(+1, -3) \\
&= +1
\end{aligned}
$$

For Node R2 (MAX node), choose the maximum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(R2) &= \max(\text{MINIMAX}(C)) \\
&= \max(-2) \\
&= -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final decision at the root (MAX node).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(\text{Root}) &= \max(\text{MINIMAX}(R1), \text{MINIMAX}(R2)) \\
&= \max(+1, -2) \\
&= +1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The optimal decision from the root is the action leading to R1 (since +1 > -2). The minimax value of the root is **+1**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Alpha-Beta Pruning

Using the same tree as Section 3.1, we now apply alpha-beta pruning. Assume the leftmost children are evaluated first (the standard depth-first order).

### 3.2.1 Search Trace Table

| Step | Node | Type | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | Action / Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Root | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Initialize |
| 2 | R1 | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Descend |
| 3 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Descend |
| 4 | L1 | Leaf | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Return $+3$ |
| 5 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+3$ | Update $\beta = +3$ |
| 6 | L2 | Leaf | $-\infty$ | $+3$ | Return $+2$ |
| 7 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+2$ | Update $\beta = +2$ |
| 8 | L3 | Leaf | $-\infty$ | $+2$ | Return $+1$ |
| 9 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+1$ | Update $\beta = +1$; return $+1$ |
| 10 | R1 | MAX | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Update $\alpha = +1$ |
| 11 | B | MIN | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Descend |
| 12 | L4 | Leaf | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Return $-1$ |
| 13 | B | MIN | $+1$ | $-1$ | Update $\beta = -1$ |
| 14 | — | — | — | — | **PRUNE!** $\alpha = +1 \geq \beta = -1$ |
| 15 | B | MIN | $+1$ | $-1$ | Return $-1$ (skipped children L5, L6) |
| 16 | R1 | MAX | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | $\max(+1, -1) = +1$; return $+1$ |
| 17 | R2 | MAX | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Descend |
| 18 | C | MIN | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Descend |
| 19 | L7 | Leaf | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Return $0$ |
| 20 | C | MIN | $+1$ | $0$ | Update $\beta = 0$ |
| 21 | — | — | — | — | **PRUNE!** $\alpha = +1 \geq \beta = 0$ |
| 22 | C | MIN | $+1$ | $0$ | Return $0$ (skipped L8) |
| 23 | R2 | MAX | $+1$ | $+\infty$ | Return $-2$ |
| 24 | Root | MAX | — | — | $\max(+1, -2) = +1$ — **Optimal decision: R1** |

### 3.2.2 Pruning Analysis

**Total nodes examined: 7 of 8** (L5 and L8 were pruned, with the rest at R1 leading to L1, L2, L3, L4, then R2's C with L7, plus root/MAX nodes).

Wait — let me recount. We examined L1, L2, L3, L4, L7 plus all internal nodes: Root, R1, R2, A, B, C. That's 5 leaves + 6 internal = 11 nodes examined out of (8 leaves + 6 internal) = 14 total. We pruned 1 leaf (L5, L6 effectively via parent B once $\alpha \geq \beta$ — actually only L5 was reached in some traces; here we pruned at the parent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Alpha-beta pruning never prunes a node that would change the minimax value. It is **sound** (preserves correctness) and **complete** (finds an answer if one exists).

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Alpha-Beta Returns the Same Answer as MINIMAX

We prove by induction on tree depth that the alpha-beta algorithm returns $\text{MINIMAX}(s)$ for every node $s$.

**Base Case (depth 0 — terminal nodes):** Both algorithms return $U(s)$ directly. Trivially equivalent.

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Suppose alpha-beta returns the correct MINIMAX value for all nodes at depth $\leq k$.

**Inductive Step (depth $k+1$):** Consider a node $s$ at depth $k+1$. There are two cases:

**Case 1: $s$ is a MAX node.** Alpha-beta maintains $\alpha = $ best value found for MAX in the subtree so far. When evaluating child $c$, if $\text{MINIMAX}(c) \leq \alpha$, then MAX already has a better option elsewhere and will not select $c$. So the unevaluated children cannot affect the root decision. This is exactly what MINIMAX would compute (which is $\max_{c} \text{MINIMAX}(c)$). The pruning is safe.

**Case 2: $s$ is a MIN node.** Symmetric argument with $\beta$.

**Conclusion:** Alpha-beta returns $\text{MINIMAX}(s)$ for all $s$ and never prunes a value-affecting branch. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Multi-Player Game Extension

For $n > 2$ players, the utility function becomes a **vector**:

$$
U(s) = (u_1(s), u_2(s), \ldots, u_n(s))
$$

Where $u_i$ is the utility of player $i$. The recursion becomes:

$$
\text{MINIMAX}(s) = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n) \text{ where } v_i = \text{best outcome for player } i
$$

A complication arises: with more than two players, **alliances** may form dynamically during play, and a player may prefer a move that helps a co-player if it hurts a common enemy. This means the zero-sum assumption breaks down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** While the formal syllabus focuses on two-player zero-sum games, KTU examiners occasionally ask about multi-player extensions. Be ready to describe the vector-valued utility and the alliance problem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 MINIMAX Game Tree Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1["Root State s0 — MAX to move"]
    a1["State A — MIN to move"]
    a2["State B — MIN to move"]
    a3["State C — MIN to move"]
    l1["Leaf L1: U = +3"]
    l2["Leaf L2: U = +2"]
    l3["Leaf L3: U = +1"]
    l4["Leaf L4: U = -1"]
    l5["Leaf L5: U = +4"]
    l6["Leaf L6: U = -3"]
    l7["Leaf L7: U = 0"]
    l8["Leaf L8: U = -2"]
    a1val["MINIMAX A = min of children"]
    a2val["MINIMAX B = min of children"]
    a3val["MINIMAX C = min of children"]
    r1val["MAX value = max A, B"]
    r2val["MAX value = max C"]
    bestaction["Best Action: Path to R1 — value +1"]
    root1 --> a1
    root1 --> a2
    root1 --> a3
    a1 --> l1
    a1 --> l2
    a1 --> l3
    a2 --> l4
    a2 --> l5
    a2 --> l6
    a3 --> l7
    a3 --> l8
    a1 --> a1val
    a2 --> a2val
    a3 --> a3val
    a1val --> r1val
    a2val --> r1val
    a3val --> r2val
    r1val --> bestaction
    r2val --> bestaction
```

## 4.2 Alpha-Beta Pruning Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["ALPHA-BETA Search — initial call alpha minus infinity, beta plus infinity"]
    test["Is this node a terminal state?"]
    returnU["Return utility U of node"]
    ismax["Is player at node MAX?"]
    updateA["Set alpha equals max of current alpha and child value"]
    cutoffA["Prune if alpha greater than or equal beta — return beta"]
    updateB["Set beta equals min of current beta and child value"]
    cutoffB["Prune if alpha greater than or equal beta — return alpha"]
    recurseMax["Recurse on child with current alpha, beta"]
    recurseMin["Recurse on child with current alpha, beta"]
    returnMax["Return alpha — best for MAX"]
    returnMin["Return beta — best for MIN"]
    start --> test
    test -- yes --> returnU
    test -- no --> ismax
    ismax -- yes --> recurseMax
    recurseMax --> updateA
    updateA --> cutoffA
    cutoffA -- no prune --> recurseMax
    cutoffA -- prune --> returnMax
    ismax -- no --> recurseMin
    recurseMin --> updateB
    updateB --> cutoffB
    cutoffB -- no prune --> recurseMin
    cutoffB -- prune --> returnMin
    returnMax --> end1["Result: MINIMAX value with pruning"]
    returnMin --> end1
    returnU --> end1
```

## 4.3 Function-Level Architecture: Game Engine with MINIMAX + Alpha-Beta

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input["Input Layer"]
        gamestate["Current Game State s0"]
        depthlimit["Depth Limit d"]
        player["Current Player MAX or MIN"]
    end
    subgraph core["Search Engine Core"]
        gentreegen["Game Tree Generator"]
        alphabeta["Alpha-Beta Pruning Engine"]
        evalheur["Heuristic Evaluation Function"]
        terminalcheck["Terminal State Detector"]
    end
    subgraph output["Output Layer"]
        bestmove["Best Action a star"]
        valuev["MINIMAX Value V of s0"]
        path["Decision Path Trace"]
    end
    gamestate --> terminalcheck
    gamestate --> gentreegen
    depthlimit --> gentreegen
    player --> alphabeta
    gentreegen --> alphabeta
    terminalcheck --> evalheur
    evalheur --> alphabeta
    alphabeta --> bestmove
    alphabeta --> valuev
    alphabeta --> path
```

## 4.4 Decision Comparison: Optimal Decision vs. Optimal Play

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    game["Game Begins at Initial State s0"]
    decision1["Make Optimal Decision at s0"]
    play1["Play Optimally at s1, s2, ... sN"]
    decision2["Make Optimal Decision at s1"]
    play2["Play Optimally at s2, s3, ... sN"]
    win["Reach Terminal State — Win Draw or Loss"]
    decision1 -- "The single best move" --> play1
    decision1 -- "contingent on opponent" --> play2
    decision2 -- "second move" --> play2
    play1 --> win
    play2 --> win
    game --> decision1
    decision2 --> play2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** An "optimal decision" is just the first move in a complete "optimal play" strategy. The full strategy requires re-evaluating at every state encountered.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]
**Define adversarial search and explain why the MINIMAX algorithm is suitable for game-playing AI. Mention the role of the utility function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adversarial search is a type of search technique used in multi-agent competitive environments where the goal is not just to find a path to a goal state, but to find a strategy that performs optimally against an opponent trying to minimize the agent's success. It is used in two-player, turn-based, zero-sum, perfect-information games like chess, tic-tac-toe, and checkers.

The **MINIMAX algorithm** is suitable because it explicitly models the opposing player's optimal behavior by alternating between maximizing and minimizing the utility at successive levels of the game tree. The algorithm guarantees that the chosen move is optimal against a rational opponent.

The **utility function** assigns a numeric score to terminal states from the perspective of the MAX player — for example, $+1$ for a win, $-1$ for a loss, $0$ for a draw. The recursive evaluation propagates these utilities upward to determine the value of non-terminal states, and the move leading to the highest MINIMAX value is selected as the optimal decision. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Distinguish between optimal decision and optimal play in the context of two-player zero-sum games.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **optimal decision** is a single best move selected from the current state, given that both players will play optimally thereafter. It is the action whose resulting child has the highest MINIMAX value at the root of the game tree.

**Optimal play** is the entire strategy consisting of optimal decisions made at every state that the game might pass through.

The distinction matters because a player making only one optimal decision without a strategy for the rest of the game may end up in losing positions if the opponent exploits their non-strategic play. Optimal play requires re-running MINIMAX (or following a pre-computed strategy) at every move, and the minimax value of the game represents the *guaranteed* outcome of optimal play from the initial state. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the MINIMAX algorithm for two-player zero-sum games with a suitable example. Derive the MINIMAX value at the root for a game tree of depth 3 where MAX is at the root, MIN at depth 1, MAX at depth 2, and terminal utilities at depth 3 are as follows:
- Left subtree of root: children with values $(+3, +2, +1)$, $(-1, +4, -3)$
- Right subtree of root: child with values $(0, -2)$

State the optimal move and its minimax value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply alpha-beta pruning to the same game tree as in part (a) using the standard left-to-right depth-first ordering. Show all intermediate values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ at each node. Identify all pruned nodes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — MINIMAX Evaluation (7 marks):**

Let us label the depth-1 MIN nodes as $M_1$ (left) and $M_2$ (right), and the depth-2 MAX children of $M_1$ as $X_1$ and $X_2$.

**Step 1: Terminal leaves evaluation** — utility values are given.

**Step 2: Evaluate depth-2 MAX node $X_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(X_1) &= \max(+3, +2, +1) \\
&= +3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate depth-2 MAX node $X_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(X_2) &= \max(-1, +4, -3) \\
&= +4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate depth-1 MIN node $M_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(M_1) &= \min(\text{MINIMAX}(X_1), \text{MINIMAX}(X_2)) \\
&= \min(+3, +4) \\
&= +3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate depth-2 MAX node $X_3$ (right subtree of $M_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(X_3) &= \max(0, -2) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Evaluate depth-1 MIN node $M_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(M_2) &= \min(\text{MINIMAX}(X_3)) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Evaluate root MAX node:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MINIMAX}(\text{Root}) &= \max(\text{MINIMAX}(M_1), \text{MINIMAX}(M_2)) \\
&= \max(+3, 0) \\
&= +3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Optimal Move:** The action leading to $M_1$ (left subtree) with **MINIMAX value = +3**. **(7 marks — full marks for showing all 7 propagation steps explicitly)**

**Part (b) — Alpha-Beta Pruning (7 marks):**

We use the standard left-to-right depth-first ordering. Initially $\alpha = -\infty$ and $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 1: Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Descend into $M_1$.

**Step 2: $M_1$ (MIN)** — $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Descend into $X_1$.

**Step 3: $X_1$ (MAX)** — evaluate children: $+3 \rightarrow \alpha = +3$; $+2 \rightarrow \alpha = +3$; $+1 \rightarrow \alpha = +3$. Return $\alpha = +3$.

**Step 4: Back at $M_1$** — $\beta = \min(+\infty, +3) = +3$. Descend into $X_2$.

**Step 5: $X_2$ (MAX)** — evaluate first child: $-1 \rightarrow \alpha = -1$. But $X_2$ was called with $\alpha = -\infty$ (passed from $M_1$). Now $\alpha = -1$, $\beta = +3$. Since $\alpha \geq \beta$ is false, continue. Second child: $+4 \rightarrow \alpha = +4$. Third child: $-3 \rightarrow \alpha = +4$. Return $\alpha = +4$.

**Step 6: Back at $M_1$** — $\beta = \min(+3, +4) = +3$. Return $\beta = +3$.

**Step 7: Back at Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = \max(-\infty, +3) = +3$. Descend into $M_2$ with $\alpha = +3$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 8: $M_2$ (MIN)** — descend into $X_3$ with $\alpha = +3$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 9: $X_3$ (MAX)** — evaluate first child: $0 \rightarrow \alpha = 0$. Check: $\alpha = 0 \geq \beta = +\infty$? No. Second child: $-2 \rightarrow \alpha = 0$. Return $\alpha = 0$.

**Step 10: Back at $M_2$** — $\beta = \min(+\infty, 0) = 0$. Return $\beta = 0$.

**Step 11: Back at Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = \max(+3, 0) = +3$. **No pruning occurred** in this particular tree because the terminal values were not ordered to allow cutoffs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pruning Observation:** In this specific example, no nodes were pruned. This illustrates that alpha-beta pruning effectiveness depends on the **ordering of moves**. With the given left-to-right order, all branches had to be examined. If we had examined $+4$ first in $X_2$, the pruning would have been more aggressive. The same final MINIMAX value of **+3** is returned, confirming alpha-beta's correctness.

> **Alternative ordering to demonstrate pruning:** If the leaves of $X_2$ had been ordered as $(+4, -1, -3)$, then at $X_2$ after seeing $+4$, $\alpha = +4$ and $\beta = +3$ (inherited from $M_1$). Since $\alpha \geq \beta$ at the very first child, the remaining leaves of $X_2$ would be pruned, demonstrating the power of good move ordering. **(7 marks — awarded for explicit trace, alpha/beta values at each step, identification of pruning (or lack thereof), and final result)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What is the time complexity of the MINIMAX algorithm? Explain how alpha-beta pruning reduces the effective branching factor and state the condition for pruning. Derive the best-case and worst-case node counts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a chess-like game where MAX has 3 legal moves at the root, leading to states $S_1, S_2, S_3$. From each $S_i$, MIN has 2 responses, leading to states $S_{ij}$. From each $S_{ij}$, MAX has 2 moves, reaching leaves with the following utilities (rows = $j$, columns = $i$):

| | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Response 1 | $+2$ | $-1$ | $+3$ |
| Response 2 | $-3$ | $+4$ | $-2$ |

Compute the MINIMAX value and the optimal decision using alpha-beta pruning, assuming left-to-right evaluation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Complexity Analysis (7 marks):**

The **time complexity of MINIMAX** is:

$$
T_{\text{MINIMAX}} = O(b^d)
$$

Where $b$ is the branching factor (average number of legal moves per state) and $d$ is the search depth. This is because every node in the game tree must be expanded to determine the value at the root.

The **space complexity** is $O(bd)$ for a depth-first implementation, since only one path is kept in memory at a time.

**Alpha-beta pruning** reduces the effective branching factor by skipping branches that cannot affect the final decision. The pruning condition is:

$$
\alpha \geq \beta
$$

Where $\alpha$ is the best (highest) value MAX can guarantee so far, and $\beta$ is the best (lowest) value MIN can guarantee so far.

**Best-case complexity** (with perfect move ordering — best moves examined first):

$$
T_{\text{Alpha-Beta, best}} = O(b^{d/2})
$$

This is equivalent to a tree of branching factor $\sqrt{b}$. The intuition: at each level, only the "best" child needs full exploration; the others can be cut off after seeing just one grandchild.

**Worst-case complexity** (with adversarial move ordering — worst moves examined first):

$$
T_{\text{Alpha-Beta, worst}} = O(b^d)
$$

In the absolute worst case, no pruning occurs and alpha-beta degenerates to full MINIMAX. **(7 marks — full marks for explicit formulas, definitions, and explanations)**

**Part (b) — Alpha-Beta Computation (7 marks):**

The tree is: Root (MAX) → $S_1, S_2, S_3$ (MIN) → 2 responses each → 2 leaves per response.

**Trace:**

**Step 1: Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Visit $S_1$.

**Step 2: $S_1$ (MIN)** — $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Visit first response.

**Step 3: Response 1 of $S_1$ (MAX)** — first leaf: $+2$. $\alpha = +2$. Second leaf: $-3$. $\alpha = +2$. Return $+2$.

**Step 4: Back at $S_1$ (MIN)** — $\beta = \min(+\infty, +2) = +2$. Visit second response.

**Step 5: Response 2 of $S_1$ (MAX)** — first leaf: $-3$. Check: $\alpha = -3 \geq \beta = +2$? No. Continue. Second leaf: $+4$. $\alpha = +4$. Return $+4$.

**Step 6: Back at $S_1$ (MIN)** — $\beta = \min(+2, +4) = +2$. Return $\beta = +2$.

**Step 7: Back at Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = \max(-\infty, +2) = +2$. Visit $S_2$.

**Step 8: $S_2$ (MIN)** — descend with $\alpha = +2$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 9: Response 1 of $S_2$ (MAX)** — first leaf: $-1$. Check: $\alpha = -1 \geq \beta = +\infty$? No. $\alpha = -1$. Second leaf: $+4$. $\alpha = +4$. Return $+4$.

**Step 10: Back at $S_2$ (MIN)** — $\beta = \min(+\infty, +4) = +4$. Visit second response.

**Step 11: Response 2 of $S_2$ (MAX)** — first leaf: $+2$. $\alpha = +2$. **Check: $\alpha = +2 \geq \beta = +4$? No.** Continue. Second leaf: $-2$. $\alpha = +2$. Return $+2$.

**Step 12: Back at $S_2$ (MIN)** — $\beta = \min(+4, +2) = +2$. Return $\beta = +2$.

**Step 13: Back at Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = \max(+2, +2) = +2$. Visit $S_3$ with $\alpha = +2$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 14: $S_3$ (MIN)** — descend.

**Step 15: Response 1 of $S_3$ (MAX)** — first leaf: $+3$. Check: $\alpha = +3 \geq \beta = +\infty$? No. $\alpha = +3$. Second leaf: $-2$. $\alpha = +3$. Return $+3$.

**Step 16: Back at $S_3$ (MIN)** — $\beta = \min(+\infty, +3) = +3$. Visit second response.

**Step 17: Response 2 of $S_3$ (MAX)** — first leaf: $-1$. Check: $\alpha = -1 \geq \beta = +3$? No. $\alpha = -1$. Second leaf: $-2$. $\alpha = -1$. Return $-1$.

**Step 18: Back at $S_3$ (MIN)** — $\beta = \min(+3, -1) = -1$. Return $\beta = -1$.

**Step 19: Back at Root (MAX)** — $\alpha = \max(+2, -1) = +2$.

> **Final MINIMAX value = +2**. **Optimal decision:** Any of the actions leading to $S_1$ or $S_2$ (both yield $+2$). Conventionally, the first (leftmost) is selected.

> **Nodes examined:** All 12 leaves were examined in this trace. No pruning occurred. The result is the same as full MINIMAX. **(7 marks — awarded for correct propagation, alpha/beta updates at each level, and final answer)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update $\alpha$ and $\beta$ correctly** at every level — always remember: $\alpha$ updates at MAX nodes, $\beta$ updates at MIN nodes.
> 2. **Confusing the pruning condition** — pruning is $\alpha \geq \beta$, not $\alpha > \beta$ (equality is sufficient, though it may not always be reached in integer-valued trees).
> 3. **Incorrect player identification at each level** — always alternate MAX and MIN as you go deeper; the player function depends on whose turn it is.
> 4. **Sign errors in zero-sum games** — utilities are from MAX's perspective; MIN's preference is the negation.
> 5. **Not stating the optimal move explicitly** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for clearly identifying the optimal action in addition to the minimax value.
> 6. **Confusing optimal decision with optimal play** — these are distinct concepts; the question will specify which one is being asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Optimal Decisions in Games:**

- [ ] **Adversarial search** extends classical search to multi-agent competitive settings where opponents have opposing objectives.
- [ ] **Games in AI** are characterized by initial state, terminal test, utility function, actions, and result function.
- [ ] **Two-player zero-sum perfect-information deterministic games** (e.g., chess, tic-tac-toe) are the canonical setting.
- [ ] **MINIMAX** algorithm recursively propagates values: MAX picks max, MIN picks min.
- [ ] **Time complexity** of MINIMAX: $O(b^d)$; space: $O(bd)$.
- [ ] **Optimal decision** = single best move at current state; **optimal play** = full strategy of optimal decisions.
- [ ] **Alpha-beta pruning** is the standard optimization that prunes branches where $\alpha \geq \beta$.
- [ ] **Best case** of alpha-beta: $O(b^{d/2})$ — equivalent to a tree of branching factor $\sqrt{b}$ with perfect move ordering.
- [ ] **Worst case** of alpha-beta: $O(b^d)$ — no pruning occurs.
- [ ] **Practical benefit**: alpha-beta can search **twice as deep** as MINIMAX in the same time, with perfect ordering.
- [ ] **$\alpha$** = best (highest) value MAX can guarantee so far; initialized to $-\infty$, only increases.
- [ ] **$\beta$** = best (lowest) value MIN can guarantee so far; initialized to $+\infty$, only decreases.
- [ ] **Multi-player games** use **vector-valued utilities** $(u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n)$ — alliances can form dynamically.
- [ ] **Heuristic evaluation functions** are used when trees are too deep for full expansion.
- [ ] **Game complexity scale**: Tic-Tac-Toe $\sim 10^5$ states, Chess $\sim 10^{47}$ states, Go $\sim 10^{170}$ states.
- [ ] **Soundness and completeness**: alpha-beta returns the same minimax value as exhaustive search — it never prunes a decision-affecting branch.
- [ ] **Zero-sum property**: $U_{\text{MAX}}(s) = -U_{\text{MIN}}(s)$ for all states $s$.
- [ ] **Real-world uses**: financial trading bots, cybersecurity, autonomous vehicles, military planning, game engines (Stockfish, AlphaZero).

> **End of Notes — Optimal Decisions in Games | Module 3 | OEERT615**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimax Algorithm (KTU 2024 Definition)**  
> The **Minimax Algorithm** is a *backward-induction* based adversarial search strategy used in **two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games**. It computes the *optimal move* for the **MAX player** by recursively exploring the entire game tree, assuming that the opponent (**MIN player**) always selects the move that *minimises* MAX's utility. The algorithm operates on the principle: **MAX chooses the move with the highest minimax value, while MIN chooses the move with the lowest minimax value.**

The algorithm was formalised in the context of **Game Theory** by **John von Neumann (1928)** and later refined for AI search by **Shannon (1950)** and **Russell & Norvig (2010)**. It is the foundational search technique for classical game-playing AI systems such as *Tic-Tac-Toe*, *Connect-4*, *Checkers*, and the early *Chess* engines.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Game Tree** | A directed graph whose nodes represent game states and edges represent legal moves. |
| **Root Node** | The current state of the game from which MAX must choose a move. |
| **MAX Player** | The player trying to *maximise* the utility (often the AI agent). |
| **MIN Player** | The opponent trying to *minimise* MAX's utility. |
| **Terminal State** | A leaf node where the game ends (win, loss, or draw). |
| **Utility Function** | $U(s)$ — a numeric score assigned to a terminal state from MAX's perspective. |
| **Ply / Depth ($m$)** | One level of the game tree (one half-move). |
| **Branching Factor ($b$)** | The average number of legal moves from any non-terminal state. |
| **Optimal Play** | Both players selecting moves that maximise/minimise the long-term expected utility. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯

> [!TIP]
> **"The Chess-Player's Crystal Ball" Analogy**  
> Imagine you are playing chess against a grandmaster. Before making **your move**, you mentally simulate *every possible future board position* — not just one or two moves ahead, but all the way to the end of the game. For each final position, you assign a score: **+10 if you win, -10 if you lose, 0 if it is a draw**.  
>  
> Then, you assume the grandmaster will *always* choose the move that is **worst for you**. Working *backwards* from the leaves, you label each of your own positions with the **maximum** of its children's scores (because you will pick the best one), and each of the opponent's positions with the **minimum** of its children's scores (because they will pick the worst one for you). The move at the root that leads to the highest score is your **optimal move**.  
>  
> This backward reasoning is exactly what the **Minimax algorithm** does — it is the *crystal ball* that lets the AI "see" the consequences of every possible game play-out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Zero-Sum" Matters**  
> A game is *zero-sum* if one player's gain is exactly the other's loss — a property essential to Minimax, because it allows the algorithm to use a **single utility function** to evaluate positions for both players. If a game is not zero-sum (e.g., *Poker*, *Diplomacy*), the algorithm must be extended into a *multi-utility* or *general-sum* framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

The **Minimax value** of a state $s$ is defined recursively as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimax}(s) &=
\begin{cases}
U(s), & \text{if } s \text{ is a TERMINAL state} \\
\displaystyle\max_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s,a)), & \text{if } s \text{ is a MAX node} \\
\displaystyle\min_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s,a)), & \text{if } s \text{ is a MIN node}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $U(s)$ is the **utility** of a terminal state $s$
- $\text{Actions}(s)$ is the set of legal moves from state $s$
- $\text{Result}(s, a)$ is the state reached by applying action $a$ in state $s$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Phases of the Minimax Algorithm (KTU 2024 Module 3)**

1. **Tree Generation (Forward Phase):**  
   Starting from the *current state* (root), recursively expand all legal moves of MAX and MIN players alternately, until either a terminal state is reached or the **depth limit $d$** is hit.

2. **Utility Assignment (Base Case):**  
   Each *leaf node* (terminal or depth-limited) is assigned a numeric utility $U(s)$. In real-world games where the tree is too deep, an **evaluation function** $Eval(s)$ approximates the utility of non-terminal states.

3. **Backward Induction (Recursive Phase):**  
   Propagate the utilities from leaves to root:  
   - At a **MAX node**: assign the *maximum* of its children's minimax values.  
   - At a **MIN node**: assign the *minimum* of its children's minimax values.

4. **Action Selection (Decision Phase):**  
   At the root, MAX selects the action that leads to the child with the **highest minimax value**. This action is the **optimal move**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU past paper on adversarial search.**

| Property / Metric | Formula / Expression | Typical Value (Chess) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Nodes in Full Tree** | $N = 1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^m = \dfrac{b^{m+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | $\approx 10^{154}$ (infeasible) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^m)$ | $O(35^{100})$ — impossible |
| **Space Complexity (DFS impl.)** | $O(bm)$ | $O(3500)$ — feasible |
| **Branching Factor $b$ (Tic-Tac-Toe)** | $b \approx 5$ on average | — |
| **Branching Factor $b$ (Chess)** | $b \approx 35$ | — |
| **Branching Factor $b$ (Go)** | $b \approx 250$ | — |
| **Game Tree Depth $m$ (Chess)** | $m \approx 100$ plies (50 moves per side) | — |
| **Best-Case Nodes (Perfect Ordering)** | $O(b^{m/2})$ | $O(35^{50})$ — still too large |
| **Worst-Case Nodes (Bad Ordering)** | $O(b^m)$ | $O(35^{100})$ |
| **Completeness** | Yes (finite game tree) | — |
| **Optimality** | Yes (vs. optimal opponent) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **💡 Mnemonic for Complexity:**  
> *"**B**ig **B**ranching factor × **M**assive depth = **B**e **M**inimaxed — but slowly!"*  
> The space is only $O(bm)$ because a *depth-first traversal* recurses and backtracks; it does not store the entire tree at once.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Game AI Engines:** Stockfish (chess), Alpha-Beta search in Connect-4, Pluribus (poker — extended versions).
- **Robotic Adversarial Planning:** Two-robot competition scenarios, RoboCup soccer.
- **Cybersecurity:** Adversarial attack trees, red-team vs blue-team penetration testing.
- **Economics & Auctions:** Bidding strategies in combinatorial auctions, sealed-bid first-price auctions.
- **Decision Theory:** General-purpose *decision-making under worst-case adversary* assumptions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Standard Minimax Pseudocode (Depth-First Recursion)

> [!NOTE]
> The following algorithm is the **canonical textbook implementation** (Russell & Norvig style) as prescribed by the KTU 2024 AI syllabus. Memorise this structure.

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{function } & \text{MINIMAX}(s, \text{depth}, \text{maximisingPlayer}) \\
\textbf{returns} & \text{: a utility value} \\
1.\quad & \textbf{if } \text{depth} = 0 \textbf{ or } \text{IsTerminal}(s) \textbf{ then} \\
2.\quad & \qquad \textbf{return } U(s) \\
3.\quad & \textbf{if } \text{maximisingPlayer} \textbf{ then} \\
4.\quad & \qquad \text{best} \leftarrow -\infty \\
5.\quad & \qquad \textbf{for each } a \in \text{Actions}(s) \textbf{ do} \\
6.\quad & \qquad\qquad v \leftarrow \text{MINIMAX}(\text{Result}(s,a),\, \text{depth}-1,\, \text{FALSE}) \\
7.\quad & \qquad\qquad \text{best} \leftarrow \max(\text{best},\, v) \\
8.\quad & \qquad \textbf{return } \text{best} \\
9.\quad & \textbf{else} \\
10.\quad & \qquad \text{best} \leftarrow +\infty \\
11.\quad & \qquad \textbf{for each } a \in \text{Actions}(s) \textbf{ do} \\
12.\quad & \qquad\qquad v \leftarrow \text{MINIMAX}(\text{Result}(s,a),\, \text{depth}-1,\, \text{TRUE}) \\
13.\quad & \qquad\qquad \text{best} \leftarrow \min(\text{best},\, v) \\
14.\quad & \qquad \textbf{return } \text{best}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Line-by-line decoding (for KTU theory exam):**  
> - **Lines 1–2:** *Base case* — terminate recursion at depth $0$ or at terminal states.  
> - **Lines 3–8:** *MAX recursion* — explore all actions, return the **largest** child value.  
> - **Lines 9–14:** *MIN recursion* — explore all actions, return the **smallest** child value.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — "The 3-Ply Game Tree" 📊

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-asked Minimax problem in KTU university exams**. The question typically reads: *"For the following game tree, determine the optimal move for MAX at the root using the Minimax algorithm."*

### Given Game Tree

```
                         (Root, MAX)
                        /          \
                  (B, MIN)         (C, MIN)
                  /      \          /      \
            (D, MAX)  (E, MAX)  (F, MAX)  (G, MAX)
            /  \      /  \      /  \       /  \
          [3] [5]  [6] [9]   [1] [2]    [0] [-1]
```

The bracketed values are the **terminal utilities**. We will now propagate values from the leaves back to the root.

### Step 1 — Evaluate Level 2 (MAX Nodes)

At each MAX node, the player **maximises** the value of its children.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimax}(D) &= \max(3,\, 5) = 5 \\
\text{Minimax}(E) &= \max(6,\, 9) = 9 \\
\text{Minimax}(F) &= \max(1,\, 2) = 2 \\
\text{Minimax}(G) &= \max(0,\, -1) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of step:**  
Each node at Level 2 represents a turn where MAX plays. The rational MAX player will *always* select the move that yields the *highest* terminal utility among its children. For example, at node $D$ the children have utilities $3$ and $5$; MAX will pick the move leading to $5$, so the minimax value of $D$ is $5$.

### Step 2 — Evaluate Level 1 (MIN Nodes)

At each MIN node, the player **minimises** the value of its children.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimax}(B) &= \min(5,\, 9) = 5 \\
\text{Minimax}(C) &= \min(2,\, 0) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of step:**  
Each node at Level 1 represents a turn where MIN plays. MIN is the *adversary* of MAX, so it will *always* select the move that yields the *lowest* minimax value among its children. For example, at node $B$, the children have values $5$ (from $D$) and $9$ (from $E$); MIN will choose the move leading to $5$, so the minimax value of $B$ is $5$.

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Root (MAX Node)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimax}(\text{Root}) &= \max(5,\, 0) = 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of step:**  
The root is a MAX node, so MAX picks the higher of the two children: $5$ (from $B$) and $0$ (from $C$). Since $5 > 0$, MAX selects the **left branch** (the action leading to MIN node $B$).

### Step 4 — State the Optimal Move

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:**  
> The **optimal first move for MAX** is to go to node **$B$** (the left subtree), which guarantees a utility of **$5$** under the assumption of *optimal play* by MIN. The corresponding optimal continuation at $B$ is the move leading to MAX node $D$, with the eventual leaf of $5$ (rather than 3).

### 3.3 The Analytical Matrix (Propagation Table)

The following table is the **valuation key** examiners expect KTU students to write in the exam:

| Node | Type | Children's Values | Operation | Minimax Value |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $D$ | MAX | $3,\ 5$ | $\max(3,5)$ | **$5$** |
| $E$ | MAX | $6,\ 9$ | $\max(6,9)$ | **$9$** |
| $F$ | MAX | $1,\ 2$ | $\max(1,2)$ | **$2$** |
| $G$ | MAX | $0,\ -1$ | $\max(0,-1)$ | **$0$** |
| $B$ | MIN | $5,\ 9$ | $\min(5,9)$ | **$5$** |
| $C$ | MIN | $2,\ 0$ | $\min(2,0)$ | **$0$** |
| Root | MAX | $5,\ 0$ | $\max(5,0)$ | **$5$** |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Exams:** Always annotate the **operation** (max or min) *next to* the arrow connecting a node to its parent. Examiners award partial marks for correctly identifying the operation even if the final numeric value is wrong.

---

## 3.4 Properties of the Minimax Algorithm (Proof Sketch)

### Property 1: Completeness
> A search algorithm is *complete* if it is guaranteed to find a solution (here, a move) when one exists.

The full Minimax algorithm is **complete** because it explores the *entire* game tree up to the terminal states. Since the game tree of a finite two-player game is itself finite, the search must terminate with a move.

### Property 2: Optimality
> An algorithm is *optimal* if it finds the *best possible* solution.

Minimax is **optimal against an optimal opponent** because:
1. The recursion is *exhaustive* — it considers every possible continuation.
2. The MAX/MIN alternation *perfectly models* the worst-case adversarial behaviour.
3. The backward induction produces a *Nash equilibrium* in pure strategies for the two-player zero-sum game.

### Property 3: Time & Space Complexity Derivation

Let the branching factor be $b$ and the depth (ply) be $m$.

**Time:** Every node at every level is visited exactly once. The number of nodes in a complete $m$-level tree is:

$$N = \sum_{i=0}^{m} b^{i} = \frac{b^{m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \approx O(b^{m})$$

Thus, **Time Complexity** $= O(b^{m})$.

**Space:** Using depth-first recursion, we only store nodes along the *current path* from the root to a leaf, plus one sibling at each level. Hence:

$$\text{Space} = O(b \cdot m)$$

---

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Control Flow of the Algorithm)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode["START: Receive State s"]
    checkTerm["Is Terminal? OR depth = 0?"]
    evalLeaf["RETURN U(s) or Eval(s)"]
    checkPlayer["Is MAX turn?"]
    initMax["Set best = -INFINITY"]
    initMin["Set best = +INFINITY"]
    loopChild["FOR each child c of s"]
    recurseCall["v = MINIMAX child at depth-1, opposite player"]
    updateMax["best = MAX best, v"]
    updateMin["best = MIN best, v"]
    returnVal["RETURN best value"]
    endNode["END: Optimal move identified at root"]

    startNode --> checkTerm
    checkTerm -- YES --> evalLeaf
    checkTerm -- NO --> checkPlayer
    checkPlayer -- YES --> initMax
    checkPlayer -- NO --> initMin
    initMax --> loopChild
    initMin --> loopChild
    loopChild --> recurseCall
    recurseCall --> updateMax
    recurseCall --> updateMin
    updateMax --> loopChild
    updateMin --> loopChild
    loopChild -- "DONE iterating" --> returnVal
    evalLeaf --> returnVal
    returnVal --> endNode

    style startNode fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style checkTerm fill:#e377c2,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style evalLeaf fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style checkPlayer fill:#e377c2,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style initMax fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style initMin fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style loopChild fill:#17becf,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style recurseCall fill:#17becf,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style updateMax fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style updateMin fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style returnVal fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style endNode fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the architecture:**  
> - **Purple blocks** = entry/exit points.  
> - **Pink blocks** = decision diamonds.  
> - **Blue block** = MAX-branch initialisation.  
> - **Orange block** = MIN-branch initialisation.  
> - **Cyan blocks** = recursive loop body.  
> - **Green block** = base case (leaf evaluation).  
> - **Brown block** = return of the optimal value.
