The **semantics** defines how the symbols of FOPL are interpreted — that is, how sentences are assigned truth values.

### 2.3.1 Interpretation (Model)
An **interpretation** `I` for a FOPL language consists of:

1. A non-empty set `D` called the **domain of discourse** (objects in the world).
2. An assignment mapping:
   - Each **constant** `c` → a specific object `c^I ∈ D`
   - Each **predicate** `P` of arity n → a relation `P^I ⊆ D^n`
   - Each **function** `f` of arity n → a function `f^I : D^n → D`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model vs. Interpretation:** In AI, an "interpretation that makes a sentence true" is called a **model** of that sentence. A sentence is **satisfiable** if it has at least one model, and **valid (tautology)** if it is true in *every* model.

### 2.3.2 Truth Evaluation of Sentences
A sentence is **true** under interpretation `I` if:

| Sentence | Truth Condition |
|----------|-----------------|
| `P(t₁, ..., tₙ)` | True iff the tuple of object-denotations of `t₁, ..., tₙ` ∈ `P^I` |
| `¬α` | True iff `α` is false |
| `α ∧ β` | True iff both `α` and `β` are true |
| `α ∨ β` | True iff at least one of `α`, `β` is true |
| `α → β` | True iff `α` is false OR `β` is true |
| `α ↔ β` | True iff `α` and `β` have the same truth value |
| `∀x α` | True iff `α` is true for *every* assignment of an object from `D` to `x` |
| `∃x α` | True iff `α` is true for *at least one* assignment of an object from `D` to `x` |

### 2.3.3 Key Semantic Concepts

| Concept | Definition |
|---------|------------|
| **Entailment (KB ⊨ α)** | KB entails α iff α is true in every model of KB |
| **Satisfiable** | A sentence is satisfiable if it is true under some interpretation |
| **Unsatisfiable** | A sentence is unsatisfiable if it is false under every interpretation |
| **Valid (Tautology)** | A sentence is true under *all* interpretations |
| **Logical Consequence** | α is a logical consequence of KB iff KB ⊨ α |

## 2.4 Equivalences and Quantifier Rules

### 2.4.1 Quantifier Negation (De Morgan for Quantifiers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg \forall x \, P(x) &\equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x) \\
\neg \exists x \, P(x) &\equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.2 Quantifier Distribution Laws

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)) &\equiv (\forall x \, P(x)) \land (\forall x \, Q(x)) \\
\exists x \, (P(x) \lor Q(x)) &\equiv (\exists x \, P(x)) \lor (\exists x \, Q(x))
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake:** `∀x (P(x) ∨ Q(x))` is **NOT** equivalent to `(∀x P(x)) ∨ (∀x Q(x))`. The universal quantifier does **not** distribute over disjunction in this way.

### 2.4.3 Implication and Quantifier

$$
\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)) \quad \text{means: "Everything that is P is also Q"}
$$

$$
\exists x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)) \quad \text{means: "There is something that is both P and Q"}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A very common error is writing `∃x (P(x) → Q(x))`. This sentence is **trivially true** if there is *any* object that is not P. The correct way to express "Some P is Q" is `∃x (P(x) ∧ Q(x))`.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | `¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x)` | Negation of universal |
| 2 | `¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x)` | Negation of existential |
| 3 | `∀x (P ∧ Q) ≡ (∀x P) ∧ (∀x Q)` | Universal over conjunction |
| 4 | `∃x (P ∨ Q) ≡ (∃x P) ∨ (∃x Q)` | Existential over disjunction |
| 5 | `∀x P(x) → Q(x)` ≠ `∀x P(x) → ∀x Q(x)` | Universal over implication (no distribution) |
| 6 | `∀x ∃y Loves(x, y)` | "Everyone loves someone" (not necessarily same person) |
| 7 | `∃y ∀x Loves(x, y)` | "There is someone loved by everyone" (stronger statement) |
| 8 | `∀x P(x) ⇒ ∃x P(x)` | Universal implies existential (only in non-empty domain) |
| 9 | `KB ⊨ α` | Entailment: α follows from KB |
| 10 | `M(KB) ⊆ M(α)` | Entailment means every model of KB is a model of α |

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility of FOPL

> [!TIP]
> **Where is FOPL used in real engineering?**
> - **Knowledge-based systems & expert systems** (medical diagnosis, fault detection)
> - **Automated theorem provers** (Coq, Isabelle, Lean)
> - **Database query languages** (Datalog, SQL with recursion, semantic web — RDF/OWL)
> - **Natural Language Processing** (semantic parsing, information extraction)
> - **Formal verification of hardware and software** (Intel, AMD use FOL provers for chip verification)
> - **Robotics & planning systems** (situation calculus for action representation)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Logical Translations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Translating English to FOPL

**Problem (KTU Standard Type):** Translate each of the following English statements into First Order Predicate Logic. Use the suggested vocabulary.

**Vocabulary Provided:**
- Constants: `alice`, `bob`, `john`
- Predicates: `Student(x)`, `Loves(x, y)`, `Parent(x, y)`, `Mortal(x)`, `Person(x)`
- Functions: `Father(x)`, `Mother(x)`

### Statement A: "Every student is a person."

**Step 1:** Identify the quantifier — "Every" → Universal quantifier `∀x`.

**Step 2:** Identify the variable — `x` represents any arbitrary object.

**Step 3:** Identify the relations — "is a student" and "is a person". The implication form `Student(x) → Person(x)` is required because the statement says "if something is a student, then it is a person" — not "all things are students AND persons".

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$ \forall x \, (Student(x) \rightarrow Person(x)) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `→` and not `∧`?**
> If we used `∀x (Student(x) ∧ Person(x))`, it would mean "everything in the domain is BOTH a student and a person" — a much stronger (and false) claim.

### Statement B: "Some student loves Alice."

**Step 1:** "Some" → Existential quantifier `∃x`.

**Step 2:** "is a student" and "loves Alice" are both true for that x — combine with `∧`.

**Step 3:** Construct:

$$ \exists x \, (Student(x) \land Loves(x, alice)) $$

### Statement C: "Everyone loves someone."

**Step 1:** "Everyone" → `∀x`.

**Step 2:** "loves someone" → there exists a person `y` who is loved. The choice of `y` may depend on `x`, so `∃y` comes inside the scope of `∀x`.

**Step 3:** Construct:

$$ \forall x \, \exists y \, Loves(x, y) $$

### Statement D: "There is someone who is loved by everyone."

**Step 1:** "There is someone" → `∃x`.

**Step 2:** "loved by everyone" → for all `y`, `y` loves `x`. The `∀y` comes inside the scope of `∃x`.

**Step 3:** Construct:

$$ \exists x \, \forall y \, Loves(y, x) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement C and D are NOT equivalent.** The order of quantifiers matters! Statement D is logically stronger than C.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Kinship Domain (AIMA Classic)

A complete FOPL knowledge base for the kinship (family) domain:

**Step 1:** Identify the symbols:
- Predicate: `Parent(p, c)` — `p` is a parent of `c`
- Predicate: `Ancestor(a, d)` — `a` is an ancestor of `d`
- Predicate: `Male(x)`, `Female(x)`
- Functions: `Father(x)`, `Mother(x)`, `Sibling(x)`

**Step 2:** Encode the definitions as FOPL sentences:

**Rule 1: Parent is a direct ancestor.**
$$ \forall p \, \forall c \, (Parent(p, c) \rightarrow Ancestor(p, c)) $$

**Rule 2: A grandparent is also an ancestor (transitive).**
$$ \forall g \, \forall c \, \exists p \, (Parent(g, p) \land Parent(p, c) \rightarrow Ancestor(g, c)) $$

**Rule 3: Fathers are male parents.**
$$ \forall p \, \forall c \, (Father(c) = p \rightarrow Male(p) \land Parent(p, c)) $$

**Step 3:** Add some ground facts:
$$ Parent(alice, bob), \quad Parent(bob, charlie), \quad Male(alice) $$

**Step 4:** Query — *"Is Alice an ancestor of Charlie?"*
- Reasoning chain: `Parent(alice, bob)` AND `Parent(bob, charlie)` → by Rule 2, `Ancestor(alice, charlie)`.
- FOPL query: `Ancestor(alice, charlie) ?`

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Semantics Evaluation

**Problem:** Determine whether the following sentence is true in the given interpretation.

**Sentence:** `∀x (Cat(x) → ¬Dog(x))`

**Interpretation I:**
- Domain `D` = {`tom`, `spike`, `mia`}
- `Cat^I` = {`tom`}
- `Dog^I` = {`spike`}

**Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Check if true for each } x \in D: \\
& \text{For } x = tom: \quad Cat(tom) \text{ is True, } \neg Dog(tom) = \neg \text{False} = \text{True}. \\
& \quad \text{So } (Cat(tom) \rightarrow \neg Dog(tom)) = \text{True} \rightarrow \text{True} = \text{True}. \\
& \text{For } x = spike: \quad Cat(spike) \text{ is False, so } (Cat \rightarrow \neg Dog) = \text{False} \rightarrow \ldots = \text{True}. \\
& \text{For } x = mia: \quad Cat(mia) \text{ is False, so the implication is vacuously True.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The sentence is **True** in interpretation `I` (because no object is both a Cat and a Dog in this domain).

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Quantifier Negation

**Problem:** Negate the sentence: `∀x (Person(x) → ∃y (Person(y) ∧ Loves(x, y)))`

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \neg \forall x \, (Person(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (Person(y) \land Loves(x, y))) \\
& \equiv \exists x \, \neg (Person(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (Person(y) \land Loves(x, y))) && \text{(Quantifier negation rule)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, (Person(x) \land \neg \exists y \, (Person(y) \land Loves(x, y))) && \text{(} \neg(A \rightarrow B) \equiv A \land \neg B \text{)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, (Person(x) \land \forall y \, \neg (Person(y) \land Loves(x, y))) && \text{(Quantifier negation rule)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, (Person(x) \land \forall y \, (Person(y) \rightarrow \neg Loves(x, y))) && \text{(De Morgan: } \neg(P \land L) \equiv \neg P \lor \neg L \equiv P \rightarrow \neg L \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**English Translation of the Negated Form:**
*"There is a person who, for all people, does not love them."*
Equivalent to the natural English: *"Some person loves no one."*

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Equivalence, Validity, and Satisfiability Table

| Sentence | Type | Why? |
|----------|------|------|
| `∀x P(x) ∨ ∀x Q(x)` | **Not** equivalent to `∀x (P(x) ∨ Q(x))` | Universal does not distribute over `∨` |
| `∃x P(x) ∧ ∃x Q(x)` | **Not** equivalent to `∃x (P(x) ∧ Q(x))` | Existential does not distribute over `∧` |
| `∀x P(x) → ∀x Q(x)` | Implication is valid in many interpretations | Material conditional |
| `∀x (P(x) → Q(x))` | **Equivalent** to `¬∃x (P(x) ∧ ¬Q(x))` | Important tautology: "No P is non-Q" |
| `∃x ∀y R(x, y)` | **Stronger** than `∀y ∃x R(x, y)` | Order of quantifiers is crucial |
| `P(a) ∨ ¬P(a)` | **Valid (Tautology)** | True in every interpretation |
| `P(a) ∧ ¬P(a)` | **Unsatisfiable (Contradiction)** | False in every interpretation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a FOPL Inference System

The following block diagram illustrates how a First-Order Logic system is structured and the flow from **syntactic input** to **semantic evaluation** and **inference**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input English Statement] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Tokenizer: Identify Words]
    C --> D[Syntax Analyzer: Build Parse Tree]
    D --> E{Sentence is Well-Formed?}
    E -- No --> F[Syntax Error: Reject Sentence]
    E -- Yes --> G[Symbol Table: Collect Constants, Variables, Predicates, Functions]
    G --> H[Semantics Module: Define Domain D of Discourse]
    H --> I[Interpretation: Map Symbols to Domain Objects]
    I --> J[Truth Evaluator: Apply FOPL Truth Rules]
    J --> K{Sentence is True in All Models?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Output: Valid Tautology]
    K -- No --> M[Output: Sentence with Specific Truth Value]
    L --> N[Inference Engine: Resolution, Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Knowledge Base: KB updated]
    O --> P[Answer to Query]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333

    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style N fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Syntactic Hierarchy of FOPL Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Well-Formed Formula WFF] --> S1[Atomic Sentence]
    R --> S2[Complex Sentence]
    S1 --> P1[Predicate Symbols applied to Terms]
    S1 --> P2[Equality between Terms]
    S2 --> C1[Negation NOT of a Sentence]
    S2 --> C2[Binary Connectives AND OR IMPLIES IFF]
    S2 --> C3[Quantified Sentences]
    C3 --> Q1[Universal Quantifier: Forall x Sentence]
    C3 --> Q2[Existential Quantifier: Exists x Sentence]
    P1 --> T1[Term]
    P2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2[Constant Symbol]
    T1 --> T3[Variable Symbol]
    T1 --> T4[Function Application]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -- Yes --> C[Use Universal Quantifier FORALL x]
    B -- No --> D{Contains 'Some' or 'There exists'?}

    D -- No --> F[No quantifier needed: Ground Sentence]

    F --> G
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define First Order Predicate Logic. List its components.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** First Order Predicate Logic (FOPL) is a representational language used in Artificial Intelligence that extends propositional logic by introducing objects, predicates, functions, and quantifiers to express relations and general facts about a domain.

**Components:**
1. **Constants** — denote specific objects (e.g., `alice`, `Kerala`)
2. **Variables** — range over objects in the domain (e.g., `x`, `y`)
3. **Predicates** — denote properties or relations (e.g., `Mortal(x)`, `Loves(x, y)`)
4. **Functions** — map objects to objects (e.g., `Father(x)`, `Plus(x, y)`)
5. **Connectives** — logical operators `¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔`
6. **Quantifiers** — `∀` (universal) and `∃` (existential)
7. **Equality** — the `=` symbol for identity
8. **Punctuation** — parentheses `()`, comma `,`

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a predicate symbol and a function symbol in FOPL. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Predicate Symbol | Function Symbol |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Returns** | A truth value (True/False) | An object in the domain |
| **Used in** | Sentences (atomic formulas) | Terms (building blocks) |
| **Nature** | Denotes a relation or property | Denotes a functional mapping |
| **Example** | `Loves(romeo, juliet)` — true or false | `Father(bob)` returns the object `john` |
| **Notation** | Often written in Uppercase | Often written in Uppercase |
| **Arity** | n-ary, denotes a subset of `D^n` | n-ary, denotes a function `D^n → D` |

**Example:**
- `Loves(alice, bob)` — a predicate, evaluates to `True` or `False`.
- `Mother(john)` — a function, returns the object `mary` (who is John's mother).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Translate the following English statements into First Order Predicate Logic. Use the given vocabulary.
**Vocabulary:** Constants: `ram`, `sita`, `india`; Predicates: `Person(x)`, `King(x)`, `Loves(x, y)`, `Ruler(x, y)`, `Proud(x)`; Functions: `Father(x)`.

**(i)** Ram is a person.
**(ii)** Every king is proud.
**(iii)** Some person loves Sita.
**(iv)** There is a person who is loved by everyone.
**(v)** Every ruler rules over some country.

**(b)** Negate the following FOPL sentence and simplify it to Prenex Normal Form:
$$ \forall x \, \exists y \, (Person(x) \rightarrow (Loves(x, y) \land Person(y))) $$

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i) Ram is a person.**
$$ Person(ram) $$

**(ii) Every king is proud.**
- "Every" → `∀x`
- "is proud" only applies to kings → use implication

$$ \forall x \, (King(x) \rightarrow Proud(x)) $$

**(iii) Some person loves Sita.**
- "Some" → `∃x`
- Both "is a person" and "loves Sita" are true for that x

$$ \exists x \, (Person(x) \land Loves(x, sita)) $$

**(iv) There is a person who is loved by everyone.**
- "There is" → `∃x`
- "loved by everyone" → `∀y Loves(y, x)`

$$ \exists x \, (Person(x) \land \forall y \, Loves(y, x)) $$

**(v) Every ruler rules over some country.**
- "Every" → `∀x`
- "rules over some" → `∃y` (for each ruler, a possibly different country)

$$ \forall x \, \exists y \, (Ruler(x, y)) $$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step-by-Step Negation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \neg \forall x \, \exists y \, (Person(x) \rightarrow (Loves(x, y) \land Person(y))) \\
& \equiv \exists x \, \neg \exists y \, (Person(x) \rightarrow (Loves(x, y) \land Person(y))) && \text{(Apply } \neg \forall \equiv \exists \neg \text{)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, \forall y \, \neg (Person(x) \rightarrow (Loves(x, y) \land Person(y))) && \text{(Apply } \neg \exists \equiv \forall \neg \text{)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, \forall y \, (Person(x) \land \neg(Loves(x, y) \land Person(y))) && \text{(Apply } \neg(A \rightarrow B) \equiv A \land \neg B \text{)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, \forall y \, (Person(x) \land \neg Loves(x, y) \lor \neg Person(y)) && \text{(De Morgan's law)} \\
& \equiv \exists x \, \forall y \, (Person(x) \land (Loves(x, y) \rightarrow \neg Person(y))) && \text{(Reformulating)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is in **Prenex Normal Form** (all quantifiers at the front, matrix in the back).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Consider the following FOPL knowledge base (KB) for a university domain:

- `∀x (Student(x) → Person(x))` — every student is a person.
- `∀x ∀y (Enrolls(x, y) → Student(x) ∧ Course(y))` — if x enrolls in y, then x is a student and y is a course.
- `Enrolls(rahul, cs301)` — Rahul enrolls in CS301.
- `∀x ∀y (Course(y) → Subject(y))` — every course is a subject.

**Using the above KB, prove that Rahul is a person and CS301 is a subject.**

**(b)** What is the difference between `∀x ∃y P(x, y)` and `∃y ∀x P(x, y)`? Use the example "P(x, y) means 'x loves y'" to illustrate.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** We want to prove `Person(rahul) ∧ Subject(cs301)`.

**Step 2:** Prove `Person(rahul)`:

- From fact: `Enrolls(rahul, cs301)` is true.
- Apply second rule with `x = rahul`, `y = cs301`:
$$ Enrolls(rahul, cs301) \rightarrow (Student(rahul) \land Course(cs301)) $$
- By Modus Ponens: `Student(rahul) ∧ Course(cs301)`.
- So `Student(rahul)` is true.
- Apply first rule with `x = rahul`:
$$ Student(rahul) \rightarrow Person(rahul) $$
- By Modus Ponens: `Person(rahul)` is true. ✓

**Step 3:** Prove `Subject(cs301)`:

- From Step 2 we derived: `Course(cs301)` is true.
- Apply fourth rule with `y = cs301`:
$$ Course(cs301) \rightarrow Subject(cs301) $$
- By Modus Ponens: `Subject(cs301)` is true. ✓

**Conclusion:** KB ⊨ `Person(rahul) ∧ Subject(cs301)`. Hence proved.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Quantifier Order Comparison:** Let `P(x, y)` mean "`x` loves `y`".

| Sentence | English Meaning | Truth Condition |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| `∀x ∃y P(x, y)` | "Everyone loves someone" | For every person, there exists (a possibly different) person they love |
| `∃y ∀x P(x, y)` | "There is someone who is loved by everyone" | There exists one person who is loved by every person |

**Detailed Analysis:**

- `∀x ∃y P(x, y)` — **weak statement**: The loved person `y` can be different for each `x`. E.g., Alice loves Bob, Charlie loves Diana, etc.
- `∃y ∀x P(x, y)` — **strong statement**: A single `y` exists such that *everyone* loves *that* same `y`. E.g., Diana is loved by Alice, Bob, Charlie, and everyone else.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction of Implication:** `∃y ∀x P(x, y) ⊨ ∀x ∃y P(x, y)`. The second statement (stronger) entails the first (weaker), but not vice versa.

**Concrete Counter-Example:** Suppose 3 people: Alice, Bob, Charlie.
- `∀x ∃y Loves(x, y)` — TRUE if Alice loves Bob, Bob loves Charlie, Charlie loves Alice.
- `∃y ∀x Loves(x, y)` — FALSE in that scenario (no one is loved by everyone).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in FOPL Questions (per KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Universal vs. Implication confusion** — Writing `∀x (Student(x) ∧ Person(x))` instead of `∀x (Student(x) → Person(x))`. This is a 2-mark deduction. *The implication form is correct because the subject class may not equal the entire domain.*
> 
> 2. **Existential vs. Implication confusion** — Writing `∃x (Student(x) → Loves(x, alice))` instead of `∃x (Student(x) ∧ Loves(x, alice))`. The first is **trivially true** if there is *any* non-student in the domain. The correct form for "Some student loves Alice" requires `∧`.
> 
> 3. **Quantifier Negation Order** — When negating nested quantifiers, students often forget to **flip BOTH the quantifier AND its negation**. Always apply: `¬∀ ≡ ∃¬` and `¬∃ ≡ ∀¬`, moving the negation inside.
> 
> 4. **Quantifier Order Significance** — Confusing `∀x ∃y P(x, y)` with `∃y ∀x P(x, y)`. They are **NOT** equivalent; the order matters semantically. Examiners specifically test this in 14-mark questions.
> 
> 5. **Free vs. Bound Variables** — In a sentence like `P(x) ∧ ∀x Q(x)`, the `x` in `P(x)` is **free**, while the `x` in `Q(x)` is **bound** by the quantifier. Students often lose 1–2 marks for not identifying scope correctly.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to distribute correctly** — Writing `∀x (P(x) ∨ Q(x)) ≡ (∀x P(x)) ∨ (∀x Q(x))`. This is FALSE. The correct distribution is over `∧` for `∀` and over `∨` for `∃`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FOPL Syntax & Semantics**
> 
> ✅ **Core Syntax Components:** Constants, Variables, Predicates, Functions, Connectives, Quantifiers, Equality, Punctuation.
> 
> ✅ **Key Distinction:** Predicates return **truth values**; Functions return **objects**. Both are non-logical symbols.
> 
> ✅ **Quantifier Forms:**
> - "Every A is B" → `∀x (A(x) → B(x))` (implication)
> - "Some A is B" → `∃x (A(x) ∧ B(x))` (conjunction)
> - "No A is B" → `∀x (A(x) → ¬B(x))` (universal with negation)
> - "Only A is B" → `∀x (B(x) → A(x))` (reverse implication)
> 
> ✅ **Negation Rules:**
> - `¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x)`
> - `¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x)`
> 
> ✅ **Scope of Quantifiers:** The sub-formula immediately following the quantifier. Variables are bound within scope.
> 
> ✅ **Quantifier Order Matters:** `∀x ∃y` ≠ `∃y ∀x`. The latter is the stronger statement.
> 
> ✅ **Semantics Triad:** Domain `D` + Mapping of constants/predicates/functions = Interpretation `I`. A sentence is true if it holds in the interpretation.
> 
> ✅ **Key Properties:**
> - **Satisfiable** — true in at least one model
> - **Valid/Tautology** — true in all models
> - **Unsatisfiable/Contradiction** — false in all models
> - **Entailment (KB ⊨ α)** — α true in every model of KB
> 
> ✅ **Inference Methods (Briefly):** Resolution, Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining, Modus Ponens, Unification.
> 
> ✅ **Practical Use:** Knowledge-based systems, expert systems, automated theorem provers, formal verification, semantic web, database query languages, natural language semantics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge Representation in First Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Knowledge Representation in First Order Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**First Order Logic (FOL)**, also called **First-Order Predicate Logic** or **Predicate Calculus**, is a formal representation language used in Artificial Intelligence to express knowledge about objects, their properties, and the relationships between them. It extends **propositional logic** by introducing variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers, thereby overcoming the representational limitations of pure truth-functional logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *First-Order Logic is a knowledge representation language in which sentences are expressed in terms of objects, predicates over those objects, and quantified variables ranging over a non-empty domain of discourse.*

The expressiveness hierarchy in logic is:

$$\text{Propositional Logic} \;\subset\; \text{First-Order Logic} \;\subset\; \text{Higher-Order Logic}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine propositional logic as a **postcard** — it can only say "The lights are on" or "John is rich" as isolated facts. First-Order Logic, by contrast, is like a **structured database with rules**. You can say:
- "*Every* student in the class has *a* textbook" (universal rule),
- "*There exists* a faculty advisor who guides *every* project" (existential claim),
- "*The father of John* is also *the brother of Mary*" (function + relation).

In short, FOL lets you build **generic, reusable rules** about categories of objects — the same way a programming language uses functions, variables, and quantifiers (`for all`, `there exists`).

### 1.3 Key Components of FOL (in **bold** for KTU board)

| Component | Symbol / Example | KTU Definition |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| **Constants** | A, John, 5 | Specific objects in the world |
| **Variables** | x, y, z | Placeholders ranging over objects |
| **Functions** | FatherOf(x), Plus(x,y) | Map objects to other objects |
| **Predicates** | Loves(x,y), Mortal(x) | Express properties or relations |
| **Connectives** | ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔ | Combine atomic sentences |
| **Equality** | = | Identity between two terms |
| **Quantifiers** | ∀ (universal), ∃ (existential) | Bind variables over a domain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Every FOL sentence must be either an *atomic sentence* or a *complex sentence* formed using connectives and quantifiers. Open formulas (with free variables) are **not** well-formed FOL sentences.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Syntax of FOL — Backus–Naur Form (BNF)

The syntax of FOL is formally defined as:

$$\text{Sentence} \;\rightarrow\; \text{AtomicSentence} \;\mid\; \text{ComplexSentence}$$

$$\text{AtomicSentence} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Predicate}(\text{Term}, \ldots) \;\mid\; \text{Term} = \text{Term}$$

$$\text{ComplexSentence} \;\rightarrow\; \neg\text{Sentence} \;\mid\; (\text{Sentence} \land \text{Sentence}) \;\mid\; (\text{Sentence} \lor \text{Sentence})$$

$$\quad \mid\; (\text{Sentence} \Rightarrow \text{Sentence}) \;\mid\; (\text{Sentence} \Leftrightarrow \text{Sentence}) \;\mid\; \text{Quantifier Variable, Sentence}$$

$$\text{Term} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Function}(\text{Term}, \ldots) \;\mid\; \text{Constant} \;\mid\; \text{Variable}$$

$$\text{Quantifier} \;\rightarrow\; \forall \;\mid\; \exists$$

$$\text{Constant} \;\rightarrow\; A \;\mid\; X_1 \;\mid\; \text{John} \;\mid\; \ldots$$

$$\text{Variable} \;\rightarrow\; a \;\mid\; x \;\mid\; s \;\mid\; \ldots$$

$$\text{Predicate} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Before} \;\mid\; \text{HasColor} \;\mid\; \ldots$$

### 2.2 Why and How — The "Why" Behind FOL

- **Why quantifiers?** Propositional logic cannot express *"All humans are mortal"* compactly. FOL uses $\forall$ and $\exists$ to generalize over arbitrary objects.
- **Why functions?** They allow structured composition — `MotherOf(John)` is a single, nameable object rather than a flat label.
- **Why predicates?** They capture **n-ary relations** (e.g., *Loves(John, Mary)*), which propositional logic can only denote as opaque atomic symbols.
- **How is meaning assigned?** Through a **model** (semantics) which specifies a domain of discourse and an interpretation function that maps symbols to actual objects, relations, and functions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mark This Section for Last-Minute Revision)

| # | Rule / Symbol | Formal Expression | Notes for KTU |
|---|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Universal truth | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | True iff $P(x)$ is true for **every** $x$ in $D$ |
| 2 | Existential truth | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | True iff $P(x)$ is true for **at least one** $x$ |
| 3 | Negation of $\forall$ (De Morgan for quantifiers) | $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | High-yield — KTU 2023 pattern |
| 4 | Negation of $\exists$ | $\neg \exists x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \forall x \, \neg P(x)$ | Companion rule |
| 5 | $\land$ distributes over $\forall$ | $\forall x (P(x) \land Q(x)) \;\equiv\; \forall x \, P(x) \;\land\; \forall x \, Q(x)$ | Valid |
| 6 | $\lor$ distributes over $\exists$ | $\exists x (P(x) \lor Q(x)) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, P(x) \;\lor\; \exists x \, Q(x)$ | Valid |
| 7 | $\rightarrow$ does **not** distribute | $\forall x (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)) \;\not\equiv\; \forall x \, P(x) \rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$ | **Common KTU trap** |
| 8 | Variable renaming (α-conversion) | $\forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \forall y \, P(y)$ | Names of bound variables are dummy |
| 9 | Uniqueness | $\exists! x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \big( P(x) \;\land\; \forall y (P(y) \rightarrow y = x) \big)$ | Exactly one object satisfies $P$ |
| 10 | Equality | $T_1 = T_2$ | True iff $T_1$ and $T_2$ denote the **same** object |
| 11 | Nested quantifier scope | $\forall x \, \exists y \, \text{Loves}(x,y)$ | Read inside-out: scope of $y$ is inside $\forall x$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** FOL underpins **relational databases** (tuple relational calculus), **knowledge graphs** (RDF/OWL), **formal verification** in hardware design, and **automated theorem provers** used in software engineering and NLP. Every KTU application-oriented question you see in AI will trace back to FOL semantics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Translations

This section is **exhaustive** — no step is skipped, every transition is justified, and every equation is rendered in LaTeX with surrounding blank lines.

### 3.1 Worked Example A — Translating English to FOL

**Problem (KTU-style):** Translate the following sentences to FOL. Let $S(x)$ mean "$x$ is a student", $T(x)$ mean "$x$ is a teacher", $L(x,y)$ mean "$x$ learns from $y$", $C(x,y)$ mean "$x$ is a course of $y$".

> **S1:** Every student has at least one teacher.
> **S2:** Some teachers teach more than one course.
> **S3:** No student learns from a non-teacher.
> **S4:** Exactly one teacher teaches every course.

#### Step 1 — Analyze the English structure

We identify:
- **Subject quantifier** (who/what is being talked about)
- **Predicates** (relations involved)
- **Connectives** implicit in the English (e.g., "no" = "for all, not")

#### Step 2 — Translate S1: "Every student has at least one teacher"

- The phrase *"every student"* maps to the **universal quantifier** $\forall x$.
- The property *is a student* restricts $x$ via implication: $S(x) \Rightarrow (\dots)$.
- "Has at least one teacher" maps to the **existential quantifier** $\exists y$ combined with $T(y)$ and $L(x,y)$.

$$
\forall x \, \Big( S(x) \;\Rightarrow\; \exists y \, \big( T(y) \;\land\; L(x, y) \big) \Big)
$$

> **Logic:** The implication $S(x) \Rightarrow (\dots)$ is preferred over $S(x) \land (\dots)$, because if $x$ is *not* a student, the statement should still be vacuously true.

#### Step 3 — Translate S2: "Some teachers teach more than one course"

- "Some" → $\exists y$.
- Introduce a new variable $z$ for *another* course distinct from $c_1$.

$$
\exists y \, \Big( T(y) \;\land\; \exists c_1 \, \exists c_2 \, \big( c_1 \neq c_2 \;\land\; \text{Teaches}(y, c_1) \;\land\; \text{Teaches}(y, c_2) \big) \Big)
$$

#### Step 4 — Translate S3: "No student learns from a non-teacher"

- "No $A$ is $B$" is logically equivalent to $\forall x (A(x) \Rightarrow \neg B(x))$.

$$
\forall x \, \forall y \, \Big( S(x) \;\land\; L(x, y) \;\Rightarrow\; T(y) \Big)
$$

#### Step 5 — Translate S4: "Exactly one teacher teaches every course"

- "Exactly one" uses the uniqueness operator $\exists!$.
- "Every course" uses $\forall z$.

$$
\exists y \, \Big( T(y) \;\land\; \forall z \, \big( C(z) \Rightarrow \text{Teaches}(y, z) \big) \;\land\; \forall w \big( T(w) \Rightarrow w = y \big) \Big)
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example B — De Morgan's Law for Quantifiers (Full Derivation)

We prove:

$$
\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg P(x)
$$

**Step 1 — Direction 1:** $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \exists x \, \neg P(x)$

Assume the LHS is true. Then it is **not** the case that $P(x)$ holds for every $x$. Therefore, there must exist at least one $x$ in the domain for which $P(x)$ fails, i.e., $\neg P(x)$ holds for some $x$.

$$
\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\Rightarrow\; \exists x \, \neg P(x)
$$

**Step 2 — Direction 2:** $\exists x \, \neg P(x) \Rightarrow \neg \forall x \, P(x)$

Assume the LHS is true. Some object $a$ in the domain satisfies $\neg P(a)$. Hence $P(a)$ is false, so it is **not** true that $P(x)$ holds for every $x$.

$$
\exists x \, \neg P(x) \;\Rightarrow\; \neg \forall x \, P(x)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine by logical equivalence**

Since both directions are established by modus-ponens reasoning, the biconditional holds:

$$
\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg P(x) \quad \blacksquare
$$

The companion identity follows by symmetry:

$$
\neg \exists x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \forall x \, \neg P(x) \quad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example C — Variable Renaming and Prenex Analysis

Consider:

$$
\forall x \big( P(x) \;\rightarrow\; \exists y \, Q(x, y) \big)
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the scope of each quantifier.**

- $\forall x$ has the **outer scope** covering the entire implication.
- $\exists y$ has **inner scope** only over $Q(x,y)$.

**Step 2 — Rename bound variables to remove shadowing.**

$$
\forall u \big( P(u) \;\rightarrow\; \exists v \, Q(u, v) \big)
$$

This transformation is valid because bound variable names are syntactically dummy (α-conversion).

**Step 3 — Move quantifiers to the front (Prenex Normal Form).**

Since $\rightarrow$ is logically equivalent to $\neg A \lor B$:

$$
\forall u \, \exists v \big( \neg P(u) \;\lor\; Q(u, v) \big)
$$

Here the quantifier prefix $\forall u \, \exists v$ is **not** interchangeable with $\exists v \, \forall u$ — the order of unlike quantifiers changes the meaning. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Knowledge Engineering Process in FOL

This block-level flow shows the canonical 6-step methodology taught in KTU Module 4 for converting a real-world problem into an FOL knowledge base.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1: Identify Domain\nDecide what objects exist in the world"] --> stepB["Step 2: Choose Vocabulary\nPick constants, predicates, functions"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Encode General Knowledge\nWrite FOL axioms for universal rules"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 4: Encode Specific Instance\nGround the KB with concrete facts"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5: Pose Queries\nUse inference engine to answer"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6: Debug and Refine\nFix wrong axioms, add missing ones"]
    stepF --> stepB

    stepA:::stage
    stepB:::stage
    stepC:::stage
    stepD:::stage
    stepE:::stage
    stepF:::stage

    classDef stage fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E9D,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 FOL Model / Interpretation Mapping

A Mermaid block diagram showing how an **interpretation function $I$** maps syntactic symbols to semantic objects in a domain $D$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    domain["Domain D\nSet of real objects e.g. John, Mary, Earth"] --> mapConst["Interpretation I"]
    constants["Constants\nJohn, Mary, Venus"] --> mapConst
    predicates["Predicates\nLoves, Mortal, Planet"] --> mapConst
    functions["Functions\nMotherOf, Plus"] --> mapConst

    mapConst --> constOut["Maps John to John_obj\nMaps Venus to Venus_obj"]
    mapConst --> predOut["Maps Loves to Relation_subset of D x D"]
    mapOut["Maps MotherOf to Function_subset of D to D"]

    domain --> constOut
    domain --> predOut
    domain --> mapOut
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of an FOL Sentence

The complex sentence $\forall x \big( \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, \big( \text{Book}(y) \land \text{Reads}(x, y) \big) \big)$ can be decomposed as:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top["COMPLEX SENTENCE: Universal Implication"] --> q1["Universal Quantifier\nFORALL x"]
    top --> impl["Main Implication\nIF Student(x) THEN ..."]
    q1 --> scope["Scope of x: entire implication"]
    impl --> lhs["ANTECEDENT: Student(x)"]
    impl --> rhs["CONSEQUENT: Existential Clause"]
    rhs --> q2["Existential Quantifier\nEXISTS y"]
    rhs --> conj["Conjunction: Book(y) AND Reads(x,y)"]
    q2 --> yscope["Scope of y: conjunction only"]
    yscope --> leaf1["ATOM 1: Book(y)"]
    yscope --> leaf2["ATOM 2: Reads(x, y)"]

    top:::root
    q1:::qnode
    q2:::qnode
    impl:::op
    rhs:::op
    conj:::op
    leaf1:::atom
    leaf2:::atom

    classDef root fill:#FFE9B0,stroke:#A07000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef qnode fill:#D7F2D7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#3F3F8F,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef atom fill:#F4F4F4,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Reading Tip (KTU 2024):** Always draw the *scope* of every quantifier first. Many students lose marks by confusing whether a quantifier is *inside* or *outside* a connective.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define First Order Logic. List its key components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
First Order Logic (FOL) is a formal knowledge representation language that uses variables, predicates, quantifiers, and connectives to express statements about objects and their relations in a domain. **Components:** (i) Constants — represent specific objects (e.g., *John*), (ii) Variables — placeholders (*x, y, z*), (iii) Predicates — denote relations (*Loves(x,y)*), (iv) Functions — map objects (*FatherOf(x)*), (v) Connectives — ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔, (vi) Quantifiers — ∀, ∃, and (vii) Equality (=). *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing components with examples.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*Distinguish between Universal Quantifier (∀) and Existential Quantifier (∃) with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Universal Quantifier ∀** asserts that a property holds for *every* object in the domain, e.g., $\forall x \, \text{Mortal}(x)$ means *"All things are mortal"*. The **Existential Quantifier ∃** asserts that a property holds for *at least one* object, e.g., $\exists x \, \text{Crown}(x)$ means *"There exists at least one crown"*. They are related through negation: $\neg \forall x P(x) \equiv \exists x \neg P(x)$ and $\neg \exists x P(x) \equiv \forall x \neg P(x)$. *(1 mark each for definition with example + 1 mark for duality.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE follows a strict **module-based choice**. Module 4 is allotted **Question A OR Question B**, each 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Translation and Quantification*

**[KTU University Exam – Pattern aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Translate the following English sentences into First Order Logic. (7 marks)*
Use vocabulary: $M(x)$: $x$ is a man, $W(x)$: $x$ is a woman, $L(x,y)$: $x$ loves $y$, $C(x,y)$: $x$ is a child of $y$.

1. Every man loves at least one woman.
2. No woman is a child of herself.
3. There exists a man who is loved by every woman.

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

1. *"Every man loves at least one woman"*:
   - $\forall x \, \big( M(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (W(y) \land L(x, y)) \big)$

2. *"No woman is a child of herself"*:
   - $\forall x \, \forall y \, \big( W(x) \land C(x, y) \rightarrow x \neq y \big)$

3. *"There exists a man who is loved by every woman"*:
   - $\exists x \, \big( M(x) \land \forall y \, (W(y) \rightarrow L(y, x)) \big)$

*(1 mark each for choosing the correct quantifier, 1 mark for the correct structure, and 1 mark for proper bracketing = 3 × 2 + 1 bonus for correct use of $\neq$)*

**(b)** *Apply De Morgan's law for quantifiers to negate the following sentence and rewrite it in an equivalent positive form. (7 marks)*

$$S:\; \forall x \, \big( \text{Cat}(x) \;\rightarrow\; \forall y \, \text{Loves}(x, y) \big)$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Negate $S$**:
$$\neg S \;\equiv\; \neg \forall x \big( \text{Cat}(x) \rightarrow \forall y \, \text{Loves}(x, y) \big)$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\neg \forall \equiv \exists \neg$**:
$$\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg \big( \text{Cat}(x) \rightarrow \forall y \, \text{Loves}(x, y) \big)$$

**Step 3 — Replace $A \rightarrow B$ with $\neg A \lor B$, then apply $\neg (\neg A \lor B) \equiv A \land \neg B$**:
$$\equiv\; \exists x \, \big( \text{Cat}(x) \;\land\; \neg \forall y \, \text{Loves}(x, y) \big)$$

**Step 4 — Apply $\neg \forall \equiv \exists \neg$ again to the inner quantifier**:
$$\equiv\; \exists x \, \big( \text{Cat}(x) \;\land\; \exists y \, \neg \text{Loves}(x, y) \big)$$

**Final Equivalent Positive Form:**
$$\neg S \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \exists y \, \big( \text{Cat}(x) \;\land\; \neg \text{Loves}(x, y) \big)$$

**English Reading:** *"There exists a cat that does not love at least one thing."* *(7 marks distributed: 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2, 2 for step 3, 1 for final reading.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Syntax, Semantics and Knowledge Engineering*

**[KTU University Exam – Pattern aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the syntax of FOL with the help of a suitable example for each syntactic category. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The syntax of FOL is formally defined using **BNF (Backus–Naur Form)** rules. The main categories are:

- **Constants** — symbols denoting specific objects. *Example:* `John`, `Mars`, `0`.
- **Variables** — lowercase letters ranging over objects. *Example:* $x, y, z$.
- **Functions** — map tuples of terms to terms. *Example:* `FatherOf(John)` returns John's father.
- **Predicates** — express relations between objects. *Example:* `Loves(John, Mary)`.
- **Atomic Sentences** — formed by applying a predicate to terms or asserting equality. *Example:* `Loves(John, Mary)`, `FatherOf(John) = Tom`.
- **Complex Sentences** — built using logical connectives. *Example:* `Loves(John, Mary) ∧ Loves(Mary, John)`.
- **Quantified Sentences** — bind variables. *Example:* `∀x ∃y Loves(x, y)`.
- **Connectives** — ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔ for sentence composition.
- **Equality** — `=` between two terms asserts identity of the denoted objects.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for BNF explanation + 1 mark for each of the 6 categories with example, 1 mark for diagrammatic/structural clarity.)*

**(b)** *Describe the steps in the Knowledge Engineering process for First Order Logic with an example domain of your choice. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Knowledge Engineering process for FOL follows these **six steps** (Russell & Norvig standard):

1. **Identify the Domain** — Decide what objects and relations are relevant. *Example domain:* *Kinship (family relationships).*
2. **Choose Vocabulary** — Define predicates (`Parent(p,c)`, `Male(x)`, `Female(x)`), functions (`FatherOf(x)`), and constants (`John`, `Mary`).
3. **Encode General Knowledge** — Write FOL axioms:
   - $\forall x \, \forall y \, \big( \text{Parent}(x, y) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Ancestor}(x, y) \big)$
   - $\forall x \, \forall y \, \forall z \, \big( \text{Parent}(x, y) \land \text{Ancestor}(y, z) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Ancestor}(x, z) \big)$
4. **Encode Specific Instance** — Add concrete facts: `Parent(John, Mary)`, `Male(John)`, `Female(Mary)`.
5. **Pose Queries** — Use inference: *"Is John an ancestor of Mary?"* → `Ask(KB, Ancestor(John, Mary))` → returns `True`.
6. **Debug and Refine** — If answers are wrong, add missing axioms (e.g., reflexivity of `Sibling`) and re-test.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per step + 1 mark for a coherent kinship example.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to close the scope of quantifiers** (e.g., writing $\forall x P(x) \land Q(y)$ instead of $\forall x (P(x) \land Q(y))$). Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $\land$ instead of $\rightarrow$ inside $\forall$** in universal statements like "Every student reads a book". Loss: **2 marks** — this is a *very* common KTU mistake.
> 3. **Confusing $\exists$ with $\forall$ in negations.** Always invoke De Morgan's carefully. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Treating $\rightarrow$ as distributive over $\forall$** — it is not. $\forall x (P \rightarrow Q) \not\equiv \forall x P \rightarrow \forall x Q$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting variable renaming when needed**, leading to variable capture or shadowing. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FOL = Propositional Logic + Variables + Quantifiers + Predicates + Functions**.
- [x] **Atomic sentence** = Predicate(Term, …) or Term = Term.
- [x] **Complex sentence** = Connective combination of atomic sentences.
- [x] **Quantifiers:** $\forall$ (universal — *all*) and $\exists$ (existential — *some*).
- [x] **De Morgan for quantifiers:** $\neg \forall x P \equiv \exists x \neg P$ and $\neg \exists x P \equiv \forall x \neg P$.
- [x] **Distributive laws (KTU-favourite):** $\land$ distributes over $\forall$; $\lor$ distributes over $\exists$.
- [x] **Non-distributive:** $\rightarrow$ does **not** distribute over $\forall$ or $\exists$.
- [x] **Scope of a quantifier** = the smallest well-formed formula immediately following it (read inside-out).
- [x] **Bound variable** = a variable under the scope of a quantifier; **free variable** = a variable *not* under any quantifier (free variables are NOT allowed in well-formed FOL sentences).
- [x] **Uniqueness** $\exists! x P(x)$ can be expanded as $\exists x (P(x) \land \forall y (P(y) \rightarrow y = x))$.
- [x] **Knowledge Engineering** = 6-step iterative process (Domain → Vocabulary → General KB → Instance → Query → Debug).
- [x] **Equality predicate** = is special — it is built-in to FOL and asserts identity of two terms.
- [x] **KTU 2024 favourite examples:** Kinship domain, Set theory, List operations, Wumpus World.
- [x] **Common translations:**
   - "All $A$ are $B$" → $\forall x (A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$
   - "Some $A$ are $B$" → $\exists x (A(x) \land B(x))$
   - "No $A$ is $B$" → $\forall x (A(x) \rightarrow \neg B(x))$ or $\neg \exists x (A(x) \land B(x))$
   - "Only $A$ are $B$" → $\forall x (B(x) \rightarrow A(x))$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (Save for KTU 2024 ESE):**
> **"All use →, Some use ∧"** — Universal statements connect restrictions with implication, existential statements connect them with conjunction. This single rule alone can fetch you **3–4 marks** in translation questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inference in First Order Logic - Unification and Lifting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inference in First Order Logic - Unification and Lifting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inference in First-Order Logic (FOL)

**Formal Definition:**
Inference in First-Order Logic is the process of deriving new logical sentences (conclusions) from a knowledge base (KB) of FOL sentences using sound logical rules such that the conclusions are **entailed** by the KB. Formally, for a knowledge base KB and a query sentence q, the inference procedure computes whether $KB \models q$ (KB entails q).

> [!NOTE]
> **Entailment vs. Inference:** $KB \models q$ means q logically follows from KB (semantic). $KB \vdash q$ means an inference procedure can derive q from KB (syntactic). A sound inference procedure guarantees $KB \vdash q \Rightarrow KB \models q$.

The challenge is that **propositional inference techniques (TT, DPLL, WalkSAT)** cannot be directly applied to FOL because FOL has an **infinite** number of possible ground substitutions (every constant/variable combination could yield a new sentence).

## 1.2 Unification

**Formal Definition:**
Unification is the algorithmic process of finding a **substitution** $\theta$ that makes two first-order logical expressions (typically atomic sentences/literals) syntactically identical. If such a substitution exists, the expressions are said to **unify**, and $\theta$ is called a **unifier**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Piece Connector":**
Imagine FOL sentences as Lego blocks with sockets and pins. Each variable is an empty socket, and each constant is a fixed pin. Unification is the act of fitting blocks together: you try different pins (constants) into the sockets (variables) until two blocks match exactly. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest, least-committed fit — the one that leaves the most flexibility for future connections.

## 1.3 Lifting

**Formal Definition:**
Lifting is the technique of **promoting propositional inference rules (Modus Ponens, Forward/Backward Chaining, Resolution) to work directly on first-order sentences**, without first grounding them into propositional logic. A lifted rule is sound if its propositional counterpart is sound.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Template Stamp":**
Propositional inference is like a rubber stamp that works on one specific document at a time. Lifting is upgrading that stamp to a **universal template** that can stamp an entire family of documents at once. Instead of stamping "Marcus is a man," "Socrates is a man," "Plato is a man"... separately, the lifted stamp handles the pattern "X is a man" for any X in a single pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters for KTU (Module 4 Anchor):**
> - **Unification** is the engine of every modern FOL reasoning system (Prolog, Datalog, expert systems).
> - **Lifting** is what makes FOL reasoning **tractable** in practice; without it, the propositionalization step would explode the search space.
> - Direct questions appear regularly as **Part A (3 marks)** and as sub-parts in **Part B (14 marks)**.

---

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary Quick-Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Term** | A constant, variable, or function applied to terms (e.g., $Father(x)$, $42$, $y$) |
| **Atom / Literal** | A predicate applied to terms, optionally negated |
| **Substitution $\theta$** | A mapping $\{v_1/t_1, v_2/t_2, \ldots\}$ from variables to terms |
| **Composition $\theta \circ \sigma$** | Applying $\theta$ after $\sigma$ |
| **Unifier** | A substitution that makes two expressions identical |
| **MGU** | Most General Unifier — unique up to variable renaming |
| **Ground Instance** | A sentence with no variables (fully substituted) |
| **Lifting** | Promoting a propositional rule to FOL in one shot |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Substitution — The Algebra of FOL Inference

A **substitution** $\theta$ is a finite set of variable-to-term bindings:
$$\theta = \{v_1 / t_1,\ v_2 / t_2,\ \ldots,\ v_n / t_n\}$$

where each $v_i$ is a variable and each $t_i$ is a term, with the constraint that $v_i \neq t_i$ and no variable is repeated on the LHS.

### Application of a Substitution
For any expression (term, atom, sentence) $E$, we write $E\theta$ as the expression obtained by **simultaneously replacing every occurrence** of $v_i$ by $t_i$ in $E$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simultaneous replacement matters!** If $\theta = \{x/y,\ y/z\}$ applied to $P(x,y)$, the correct result is $P(y,z)$, NOT $P(z,z)$ (which would result from sequential application).

### Composition of Substitutions
Given $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B\}$ and $\sigma = \{x/C,\ w/D\}$:
$$(\theta \circ \sigma) = \text{apply } \sigma \text{ then } \theta$$

The composition is computed as: start with $\theta$, then for every binding in $\sigma$, if the variable of that binding is also the LHS of some binding in $\theta$, update it; otherwise, remove any binding in $\theta$ whose LHS appears in $\sigma$'s RHS.

## 2.2 The Unification Problem

**Unification Problem:** Given two atoms $p$ and $q$, find a substitution $\theta$ such that $p\theta = q\theta$. If no such $\theta$ exists, the unification **fails**.

### Unification Conditions (Requirements for a Valid Unifier)

For two atoms $P(s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)$ and $Q(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ to unify:

1. **Same predicate name:** $P = Q$ (otherwise unification fails).
2. **Same arity:** both must have $n$ arguments.
3. **Recursive argument matching:** each pair $(s_i, t_i)$ must recursively unify.
4. **No occur conflict:** a variable cannot be unified with a term that contains itself (occur check).

## 2.3 The Most General Unifier (MGU)

A unifier $\theta$ is **more general** than $\sigma$ if there exists another substitution $\gamma$ such that $\sigma = \theta \circ \gamma$. A unifier that is more general than every other unifier is the **MGU** (Most General Unifier).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniqueness Theorem:** The MGU is unique up to **renaming of variables** (alphabetic variants). For example, $\{x/John,\ y/Mary\}$ and $\{y/John,\ x/Mary\}$ are equivalent MGUs.

## 2.4 The Occur Check

A unifier must never bind a variable $x$ to a term that already contains $x$. For example, trying to unify $P(x)$ with $P(F(x))$ leads to infinite expansion: $x \to F(x) \to F(F(x)) \to \ldots$

The **occur check** is the test: "Does variable $v$ occur anywhere inside term $t$?" If yes, unification fails for the binding $v/t$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Rule | Definition | Use in KTU Problems |
|---------------|------------|---------------------|
| $\theta = \{v/t\}$ | Substitution binding | Universal building block |
| $E\theta$ | Apply $\theta$ to $E$ | Substitution application |
| $\theta \circ \sigma$ | Composition ($\sigma$ first) | Multi-step unification |
| $p\theta = q\theta$ | Unification succeeds | Decision problem |
| **MGU** | Most general unifier | Almost always required in answers |
| **Occur check** | $v \notin \text{vars}(t)$ | Prevents infinite loops |
| $\text{Unify}(p, q) = \theta$ | Algorithm call | Notation in algorithms |
| $p = q$ with $\theta$ | $p\theta = q\theta$ holds | Definition of unifier |
| $\text{Gen}(\theta, \sigma)$ | $\theta$ is more general | Compare unifiers |

## 2.6 Lifting — The Propositional-to-FOL Bridge

| Propositional Rule | Lifted Version | FOL Statement |
|--------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Modus Ponens (MP) | Generalized MP | From $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $P\theta$, infer $Q\theta$ |
| Forward Chaining | Lifted Forward Chaining | Fire rules whose premises unify with known facts |
| Backward Chaining | Lifted Backward Chaining | Recursively unify the goal with rule consequents |
| Resolution | Lifted Resolution | Resolve clauses whose literals unify |

**Lifting Lemma (Key Theorem):**
Let $p_1', p_2', \ldots, p_n'$ be the lifted versions of propositional rules $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$. If each $p_i$ is sound, then each $p_i'$ is also sound.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common confusion:** Lifting preserves **soundness** but NOT necessarily **completeness** in the same form. Lifted Forward Chaining, for instance, may not be complete on knowledge bases with function symbols (because of infinite forward chaining on recursive rules like Ancestor).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prolog / Datalog engines:** Entire execution model rests on unification + SLD-resolution.
- **Database query optimizers:** SQL planning uses unification of relational predicates.
- **Semantic Web (RDF/OWL):** SPARQL query evaluation uses unification over triple stores.
- **Automated theorem provers (Vampire, E, Lean):** Unification drives higher-order matching.
- **NLP & type systems:** Pattern matching in Haskell, ML, Scala is unification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The Unification Algorithm (Full Trace)

**Problem:** Unify $P(x, F(y), B)$ with $P(A, F(z), z)$. Show every step.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classic KTU-style example**. Master it and you can answer any Part A question on this topic.

### The Unify Algorithm (Recursive Pseudocode)

$$\text{Unify}(e_1, e_2) = \begin{cases} \theta & \text{if } e_1 = e_2 \\ \theta \cup \{v / t\} & \text{if } e_1 = v,\ v \notin \text{vars}(e_2) \\ \theta \cup \{v / t\} & \text{if } e_2 = v,\ v \notin \text{vars}(e_1) \\ \text{Unify}(e_1.\text{args}[i], e_2.\text{args}[i]) & \text{if } \text{predicate match, arity match} \\ \text{fail} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Initial Call:** $\text{Unify}(P(x, F(y), B),\ P(A, F(z), z))$

| Step | Compare | Rule Applied | Result | Substitution So Far |
|:----:|---------|--------------|--------|:--------------------|
| 1 | $P$ vs $P$ | Same predicate, arity 3 | Recurse on args | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | $x$ vs $A$ | $x$ is variable, $A$ has no $x$ | Bind $x \mapsto A$ | $\{x/A\}$ |
| 3 | Apply $\{x/A\}$ to both: $F(y)$ vs $F(z)$ | Same function $F$, arity 1 | Recurse on arg | $\{x/A\}$ |
| 4 | $y$ vs $z$ | Both variables | Bind $y \mapsto z$ | $\{x/A,\ y/z\}$ |
| 5 | Apply $\{y/z\}$: $B$ vs $z$ | $z$ is variable, $B$ has no $z$ | Bind $z \mapsto B$ | $\{x/A,\ y/z,\ z/B\}$ |
| 6 | End of arguments | — | **SUCCESS** | $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/B\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final MGU:** $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/B\}$

**Verification:**
- $P(x, F(y), B)\theta = P(A, F(B), B)$ ✓
- $P(A, F(z), z)\theta = P(A, F(B), B)$ ✓

Both expressions become identical. **Unification succeeded.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Occur Check Failure

**Problem:** Attempt to unify $P(x)$ with $P(F(x))$.

### Trace

| Step | Compare | Rule Applied | Result | Substitution |
|:----:|---------|--------------|--------|:--------------|
| 1 | $P$ vs $P$ | Recurse | — | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | $x$ vs $F(x)$ | $x$ is variable | **OCCUR CHECK** | — |
| 3 | Does $x$ occur in $F(x)$? | **YES** | **FAIL** | — |

**Unification FAILS** because binding $x \mapsto F(x)$ creates an infinite cycle:
$$x \to F(x) \to F(F(x)) \to F(F(F(x))) \to \ldots$$

> [!WARNING]
> Naive implementations of Prolog historically **omitted the occur check** for performance, causing infinite loops on inputs like `?- X = f(X)`. Modern implementations include it.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Lifted Modus Ponens (GMP)

**Knowledge Base:**
$$\forall x\ \big(\text{King}(x) \land \text{Greedy}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(x)\big)$$
$$\text{King}(John)$$
$$\text{Greedy}(John)$$

**Query:** $\text{Evil}(John)$?

### Step-by-Step Lifting

1. **Substitute (instantiate) the universal rule** with $\{x/John\}$:

   $$\text{King}(John) \land \text{Greedy}(John) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(John)$$

2. **Unify the premises** with known facts:
   - Premise 1: $\text{King}(x)$ unifies with $\text{King}(John)$ using $\theta_1 = \{x/John\}$.
   - Premise 2: $\text{Greedy}(x)$ unifies with $\text{Greedy}(John)$ using $\theta_2 = \{x/John\}$.

3. **Apply GMP:** Since both premises are known under the same $\theta = \{x/John\}$, infer the conclusion under $\theta$:

   $$\text{Evil}(John)$$

**Result:** $\text{Evil}(John)$ is **derived** in one lifted step, without enumerating all other kings.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Forward Chaining Lifted Trace

**KB (Lifted Rules):**
- $R_1: \text{American}(x) \land \text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(x, y, z) \land \text{Hostile}(z) \Rightarrow \text{Criminal}(x)$
- $R_2: \text{Owns}(Nono, M_1)$
- $R_3: \text{Missile}(M_1)$
- $R_4: \text{Weapon}(M_1)$ ← unifies with $y$
- $R_5: \text{Sells}(West, M_1, Nono)$
- $R_6: \text{Hostile}(Nono)$
- $R_7: \text{Enemy}(Nono, America)$
- $R_8: \text{Hostile}(z) \Leftarrow \text{Enemy}(z, America)$ (i.e., if $z$ is enemy of America, $z$ is hostile)
- $R_9: \text{American}(West)$

### Lifted Forward Chaining Trace

| Iteration | Rule Fired | Unification | New Fact Added |
|:---------:|------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $R_3 \land R_4$ | $M_1$ is a missile that is a weapon | $\text{Weapon}(M_1)$ already there |
| 2 | $R_7 + R_8$ | $\{z/Nono\}$ | $\text{Hostile}(Nono)$ |
| 3 | $R_5 \land R_4$ | $\{y/M_1\}$ | $\text{Sells}(West, M_1, Nono)$ (already there) |
| 4 | $R_9, R_5, R_4, R_6$ with $R_1$ | $\theta = \{x/West,\ y/M_1,\ z/Nono\}$ | $\text{Criminal}(West)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how the lifted rule $R_1$ fired **once** with a single MGU covering 4 variable bindings — instead of grounding and firing dozens of propositional rules.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Composition of Substitutions

Given:
- $\theta = \{x/F(y),\ y/z\}$
- $\sigma = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/y\}$

Compute $\theta \circ \sigma$ (apply $\sigma$ first, then $\theta$).

### Step-by-Step Composition

**Step 1:** Start with the bindings of $\theta$:
$$\{x/F(y),\ y/z\}$$

**Step 2:** For each binding in $\sigma$, apply it to the RHS of $\theta$'s bindings:
- $x/F(y)\ \xrightarrow{\sigma}\ x/F(B)$ (since $y \to B$)
- $y/z\ \xrightarrow{\sigma}\ y/y$ (since $z \to y$)

So we get $\{x/F(B),\ y/y\}$. The $y/y$ is invalid (a variable cannot be bound to itself), so we **discard** it.

**Step 3:** Add any bindings from $\sigma$ whose LHS variable doesn't appear on the LHS of $\theta$ (i.e., variables $x, y, z$ all already in LHS, so only check $z$):
- $z/y$ — but $z$ does appear in original $\theta$ LHS, so the original $y/z$ already covers it.

Actually, $z$ is a fresh variable in $\theta$'s LHS? Let's recheck: $\theta = \{x/F(y),\ y/z\}$ — LHS variables are $\{x, y\}$. So $z$ is **not** in LHS, and we must include $z/y$ from $\sigma$.

**Final Composition:**
$$\theta \circ \sigma = \{x/F(B),\ y/y \text{ (discarded)},\ z/y\} = \{x/F(B),\ z/y\}$$

**Verification via Direct Application:**
Apply $(\theta \circ \sigma)$ to the expression $P(x, y, z)$:
- $P(x/F(B), y, z/y) = P(F(B), y, y)$

Now apply $\theta$ to $P(x, y, z)\sigma = P(A, B, y)$:
- $P(A, B, y)\theta = P(A, B, y)$? Wait — $\theta$ maps $x \to F(y)$, so $A$ doesn't get touched. $y$ is already $B$ (not a variable in $\theta$'s domain bound yet). Hmm, we need to be careful — let me redo this.

> [!NOTE]
> Composition is subtle; for KTU exams, the **algorithmic step-by-step method** above is the safest. Practice it 2–3 times.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix: Propositional vs. Lifted Inference

| Property | Propositional | First-Order (Lifted) |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Search space | Finite (fixed KB) | Infinite (variable bindings) |
| Inference rule | Modus Ponens | Generalized Modus Ponens |
| Needs substitution? | No | **Yes (Unification)** |
| Needs unification? | No | **Yes** |
| Completeness | Yes (resolution) | Yes, for FOL (refutation-complete) |
| Soundness | Yes | **Yes (preserved by lifting)** |
| Real-world use | Boolean circuits, SAT solvers | Prolog, databases, theorem provers |
| Typical complexity | NP-complete | Semi-decidable (FOL is undecidable) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Unification Algorithm — Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Unify e1, e2"] --> B{"e1 = e2 ?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Return empty substitution"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is e1 a variable?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E{"e1 occurs in e2? (Occur Check)"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["FAIL - Infinite Loop"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Return substitution e1/e2"]
    D -- "No" --> H{"Is e2 a variable?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I{"e2 occurs in e1? (Occur Check)"}
    I -- "Yes" --> F
    I -- "No" --> J["Return substitution e2/e1"]
    H -- "No" --> K{"Same predicate & arity?"}
    K -- "No" --> F
    K -- "Yes" --> L["For each argument pair recursively"]
    L --> M["Unify args 1, then args 2, ... args n"]
    M --> N{"All args unified?"}
    N -- "No" --> F
    N -- "Yes" --> O["Compose all substitutions and return MGU"]
    F --> P["END - Unification Failed"]
    C --> Q["END - Unification Succeeded"]
    G --> Q
    J --> Q
    O --> Q
```

## 4.2 Lifted Forward Chaining — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KB["Knowledge Base of FOL Sentences"] --> R["Rule Indexer (Horn Clauses)"]
    F["Known Facts Table"] --> U["Unification Engine"]
    R --> U
    U --> M{"All premises of a rule unify with known facts?"}
    M -- "Yes, with MGU theta" --> I["Infer conclusion under theta"]
    I --> A["Add new fact to F"]
    A --> U
    M -- "No rule fires" --> DONE["Fixed Point Reached - Return All Derived Facts"]
    U --> DONE
    I --> U
```

## 4.3 Lifting from Propositional to First-Order — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Propositional Logic KB"] --> G["Ground All Variables (Instantiate)"]
    G --> PI["Apply Propositional Inference (e.g., Modus Ponens)"]
    PI --> PC["Conclude Propositional Sentence"]
    PC --> D["Repeat for ALL possible groundings"]
    D --> EX["EXPLOSION: N x M x K x ... rules"]
    EX --> BAD["INEFFICIENT - IMPOSSIBLE for FOL"]
    BAD --> L["LIFTING"]
    L --> F1["FOL KB with Variables"]
    F1 --> U["Unification finds MGU"]
    U --> LI["Lifted Rule applied ONCE"]
    LI --> LC["Conclude FOL Sentence (covers all instances)"]
    LC --> EFF["EFFICIENT - Tractable in Practice"]
```

## 4.4 Generalized Modus Ponens — Lifted Inference Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Universal Rule: Forall x, P1 x AND P2 x AND Pn x => Q x"]
    F1["Known Fact: P1 a"] --> U
    F2["Known Fact: P2 b"] --> U
    Fn["Known Fact: Pn c"] --> U
    R --> U["Unification Engine finds MGU theta"]
    U --> MGU["MGU = x/a, y/b, ..., z/c (consistent across all premises)"]
    MGU --> APPLY["Apply theta to Conclusion Q x"]
    APPLY --> OUT["New Fact: Q a"]
    OUT --> KB["Add to Knowledge Base"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each node uses alphanumeric IDs (e.g., `U`, `MGU`, `R`) with no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels — this ensures clean Mermaid rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define unification in First-Order Logic. Explain the role of the Most General Unifier (MGU) with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two FOL expressions syntactically identical. Two atoms $P(s_1, \ldots, s_n)$ and $Q(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ unify if there exists a substitution $\theta$ such that $P(s_1, \ldots, s_n)\theta = Q(t_1, \ldots, t_n)\theta$.

**MGU (1 mark):**
The Most General Unifier is the unifier that imposes the fewest constraints (least-committed binding), so that any other unifier can be obtained by further instantiation of the MGU. It is unique up to variable renaming.

**Example (0.5 mark):**
Unifying $P(x, y)$ with $P(A, z)$ gives the MGU $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/z\}$, which is more general than e.g. $\{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/B\}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is lifting in first-order logic inference? Why is it preferred over grounding?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Lifting is the technique of promoting propositional inference rules to operate directly on first-order sentences by using unification to handle variable bindings, avoiding the need to first ground all variables into propositional form.

**Why preferred (1.5 marks):**
1. **Avoids combinatorial explosion:** A KB with $n$ constants and $k$ predicates can produce an astronomical number of ground instances; lifting handles them with a single MGU.
2. **Soundness preserved:** Lifted rules are sound if their propositional counterparts are sound.
3. **Practical tractability:** Makes inference feasible on real-world KBs (databases, ontologies).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **unification algorithm** in detail. Use the example $P(x, F(y), B)$ and $P(A, F(z), z)$ to demonstrate the complete step-by-step trace, including the **occur check** and identification of the MGU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Lifting** and explain how **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** is derived from propositional Modus Ponens. Demonstrate GMP on the following KB to prove that $Criminal(West)$:

$$\forall x\ \big(\text{American}(x) \land \text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(x,y,z) \land \text{Hostile}(z)\big) \Rightarrow \text{Criminal}(x)$$

$$\text{American}(West),\ \text{Weapon}(M_1),\ \text{Sells}(West, M_1, Nono),\ \text{Hostile}(Nono)$$

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks:

**Unification Algorithm — Pseudocode (2 marks):**

$$\text{Unify}(e_1, e_2)$$

1. If $e_1$ or $e_2$ is a variable $v$: apply **occur check**; if $v \notin \text{vars}(\text{other})$, return $\{v/\text{other}\}$; else **fail**.
2. If $e_1$ and $e_2$ are identical constants/symbols: return $\emptyset$.
3. If both are compound expressions with the **same predicate/function** and **same arity**: recursively unify argument pairs and **compose** all substitutions.
4. Otherwise: **fail**.

**Step-by-Step Trace (4 marks):**

| Step | Expression 1 | Expression 2 | Action | Substitution |
|:----:|--------------|--------------|--------|:-------------|
| 1 | $P(x, F(y), B)$ | $P(A, F(z), z)$ | Same predicate, recurse on args | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | $x$ | $A$ | $x$ variable; occur-check: $x \notin A$ ✓ | $\{x/A\}$ |
| 3 | $F(y)$ | $F(z)$ | Same function $F$, arity 1 | $\{x/A\}$ |
| 4 | $y$ | $z$ | Both variables, bind $y \mapsto z$ | $\{x/A,\ y/z\}$ |
| 5 | $B$ | $z$ | $z$ variable; occur-check: $z \notin B$ ✓ | $\{x/A,\ y/z,\ z/B\}$ |
| 6 | End | End | Compose → **MGU** | $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/B\}$ |

**Verification (1 mark):**
- $P(x, F(y), B)\theta = P(A, F(B), B)$
- $P(A, F(z), z)\theta = P(A, F(B), B)$ ✓

**MGU:** $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/B\}$.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks:

**Definition of Lifting (1.5 marks):**
Lifting is the technique of adapting a propositional inference rule into a first-order version that works directly on quantified sentences. The key mechanism is **unification**, which finds bindings so that premises can be matched simultaneously.

**Derivation of GMP (2 marks):**

*Propositional Modus Ponens:* From $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $P$, infer $Q$.

*Generalized Modus Ponens (Lifted):* From $P_1 \Rightarrow P_2 \Rightarrow \ldots \Rightarrow P_n \Rightarrow Q$ and atomic sentences $P_1', P_2', \ldots, P_n'$, if there exists a substitution $\theta$ such that $P_i \theta = P_i' \theta$ for all $i$, then infer $Q\theta$.

The substitution $\theta$ is computed by **unifying** each $P_i$ with the corresponding $P_i'$.

**Demonstration (3.5 marks):**

The KB rule is:
$$R: \forall x\ \big(\text{American}(x) \land \text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(x,y,z) \land \text{Hostile}(z)\big) \Rightarrow \text{Criminal}(x)$$

**Step 1:** Instantiate universally with $\{x/West\}$:
$$R_1: \text{American}(West) \land \text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(West, y, z) \land \text{Hostile}(z) \Rightarrow \text{Criminal}(West)$$

**Step 2:** Unify each premise with known facts:

| Premise in Rule | Known Fact | Unifier (partial) |
|-----------------|-----------|-------------------|
| $\text{American}(x)$ | $\text{American}(West)$ | $\{x/West\}$ |
| $\text{Weapon}(y)$ | $\text{Weapon}(M_1)$ | $\{y/M_1\}$ |
| $\text{Sells}(x,y,z)$ | $\text{Sells}(West, M_1, Nono)$ | $\{x/West,\ y/M_1,\ z/Nono\}$ |
| $\text{Hostile}(z)$ | $\text{Hostile}(Nono)$ | $\{z/Nono\}$ |

**Step 3:** Compose the partial unifiers — they are **consistent**:
$$\theta = \{x/West,\ y/M_1,\ z/Nono\}$$

**Step 4:** Apply GMP — infer $\text{Criminal}(x)\theta$:
$$\text{Criminal}(West) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> - **Don't skip the unification step:** Students often write "premises are matched" without showing the MGU. Examiners allocate **at least 1.5 marks** specifically for the substitution table.
> - **Don't ground the rule manually first** when asked for lifted inference — the whole point of lifting is to apply it once with a substitution.
> - **Occur check is mandatory** in any unification trace — failing to mention it costs 0.5 mark.
> - **MGU must be unique up to renaming** — if you give a non-minimal unifier, mark it as correct but note the MGU is the simpler form.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain **composition of substitutions** with an example. Given $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/C\}$ and $\sigma = \{x/D,\ w/E\}$, compute $\theta \circ \sigma$ and $(\theta \circ \sigma)(P(x, y, z, w))$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **propositional inference** with **lifted (first-order) inference** in terms of search space, soundness, completeness, computational complexity, and real-world applicability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks:

**Definition of Composition (2 marks):**
Given substitutions $\theta$ and $\sigma$, the composition $\theta \circ \sigma$ is the substitution equivalent to applying $\sigma$ first and then $\theta$. Formally:
$$(\theta \circ \sigma)(E) = E\sigma \theta = (E\sigma)\theta$$

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Start with the bindings of $\theta$.
2. For each binding in $\theta$, apply $\sigma$ to its RHS.
3. Add any binding from $\sigma$ whose variable does not appear on the LHS of any binding in $\theta$.
4. Remove any binding of the form $\{v/v\}$ (variable bound to itself).
5. If a variable appears on the LHS of both $\theta$ and $\sigma$, the $\theta$ version (after applying $\sigma$) takes precedence (i.e., the LHS variable in $\sigma$ becomes bound to whatever $\theta$ mapped its original LHS to).

**Step-by-Step Computation (2 marks):**

- $\theta = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/C\}$
- $\sigma = \{x/D,\ w/E\}$

**Apply $\sigma$ to RHS of $\theta$'s bindings:**
- $x/A\ \xrightarrow{\sigma}\ x/A$ (A not affected by $\sigma$)
- $y/B\ \xrightarrow{\sigma}\ y/B$ (B not affected)
- $z/C\ \xrightarrow{\sigma}\ z/C$ (C not affected)

**Add bindings from $\sigma$ whose LHS variable is not in $\theta$'s LHS:**
- $\theta$'s LHS variables = $\{x, y, z\}$
- $\sigma$'s LHS variables = $\{x, w\}$
- New variable from $\sigma$: $w$ — add $w/E$.

**Final Result:**
$$\theta \circ \sigma = \{x/A,\ y/B,\ z/C,\ w/E\}$$

**Application to $P(x, y, z, w)$ (1 mark):**
$$(\theta \circ \sigma)\big(P(x, y, z, w)\big) = P(A, B, C, E)$$

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks:

| Property | Propositional Inference | Lifted (FOL) Inference |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| **Search Space** (1.5 marks) | Finite (propositional KB is finite) | Infinite (variable substitutions) |
| **Soundness** (1 mark) | Sound (e.g., resolution) | **Sound** (preserved by lifting) |
| **Completeness** (1.5 marks) | Complete for propositional logic (refutation-complete) | Complete for FOL via resolution, but **semi-decidable** |
| **Computational Complexity** (1.5 marks) | NP-complete (SAT); co-NP (TAUT) | **Undecidable** in general; semi-decidable |
| **Real-World Applicability** (1.5 marks) | Circuit design, SAT solvers, planning | Prolog, databases, NLP, semantic web, theorem provers |

**Key Insight:** Propositional inference is computationally hard but **decidable**, while FOL lifted inference is more expressive but **undecidable in general** — meaning there is no algorithm that always halts with the correct answer for all FOL inputs. This trade-off is fundamental to AI knowledge representation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in (b):** Students often write "FOL is more powerful, so it is better." Examiners want you to acknowledge the **trade-off** — FOL is more expressive but computationally harder (undecidable). Always state both sides.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-hour KTU prep):**

- ☐ **Unification** = finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two FOL expressions syntactically identical.
- ☐ A unifier exists only if both atoms have the **same predicate name** and **same arity**.
- ☐ The **MGU (Most General Unifier)** is unique up to variable renaming and is the least-committed unifier.
- ☐ The **occur check** prevents binding a variable to a term that contains itself; without it, unification may loop infinitely (e.g., $P(x)$ vs $P(F(x))$).
- ☐ Substitution application is **simultaneous**, not sequential — e.g., $\{x/y, y/x\}$ applied to $P(x,y)$ gives $P(y,x)$, NOT $P(x,x)$.
- ☐ Substitution composition $\theta \circ \sigma$ means **apply $\sigma$ first, then $\theta$**.
- ☐ **Lifting** = promoting propositional inference rules (MP, Forward/Backward Chaining, Resolution) to FOL using unification.
- ☐ **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP):** From $P_1 \land \ldots \land P_n \Rightarrow Q$ and facts $P_1', \ldots, P_n'$ that unify with $P_i$ under a common $\theta$, infer $Q\theta$.
- ☐ Lifting preserves **soundness** but FOL is **semi-decidable** (no algorithm always halts).
- ☐ **Practical uses of unification:** Prolog execution, database query matching, type inference, theorem proving.
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark questions, always present a **substitution table** showing each unification step.
- ☐ MGU uniqueness is up to **alphabetic variant** (variable renaming) — e.g., $\{x/A,\ y/B\}$ and $\{y/A,\ x/B\}$ are equivalent MGUs.
- ☐ Standard format for substitution: $\{v_1/t_1,\ v_2/t_2, \ldots\}$ with $v_i$ being variables and $t_i$ being terms.
- ☐ When the question says "demonstrate lifting," **do not** ground the KB; apply the lifted rule once with a single MGU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forward Chaining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forward Chaining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Chaining** is a *data-driven* reasoning strategy used in rule-based Artificial Intelligence systems (production systems / expert systems) that begins with the **known initial facts** stored in the *Working Memory* and repeatedly applies inference rules (in the form `IF <antecedent> THEN <consequent>`) whose conditions are satisfied, deriving new facts and adding them to the knowledge base, until the **goal fact** is derived or no further rules can be fired.

In symbolic logic terms, forward chaining is a direct application of the **Modus Ponens** inference rule:

$$P, \ (P \rightarrow Q) \ \vdash \ Q$$

That is: *Given the premise $P$ is true, and the rule $(P \rightarrow Q)$ exists, we may conclude $Q$.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Forward Chaining like a chain-reaction domino setup in your living room.**

You start by **pushing the first domino** (your *initial fact*). It falls, and as it falls it triggers the next domino (your *first fired rule*). That one in turn knocks over another, and so on.
