4. **Semi-Supervised Learning** — Mix of few labelled + many unlabelled examples.
5. **Self-Supervised Learning** — Labels are generated from the data itself.
6. **Active Learning** — Learner queries the oracle for labels of uncertain points.
7. **Transfer Learning** — Knowledge from one task is reused for another.
8. **Multi-Task Learning** — Several related tasks are learned jointly.
9. **Online vs Batch Learning** — Data is learned as a stream or in one shot.
10. **Instance-Based vs Model-Based Learning** — Memorize vs. build a compact model.

> [!NOTE]
> These forms are **not mutually exclusive** — a real-world AI system often combines multiple forms (e.g., a self-supervised pretrained model fine-tuned via supervised transfer learning).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Any Learning System

Every ML system — regardless of its form — has three core components:

| Component | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Task** | $T$ | What the system must do (classify, regress, cluster) |
| **Experience (Data)** | $E$ | What the system observes during training |
| **Performance Measure** | $P$ | How we evaluate success (accuracy, error, reward) |

The learner improves its performance $P$ on task $T$ as it gains experience $E$.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Form of Learning

### 2.2.1 Supervised Learning

- **Experience E:** A dataset of **labelled** examples $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Task T:** Learn a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- **Performance P:** Loss minimized on training data, accuracy on test data.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Classification** — $y$ is discrete (e.g., spam/not-spam).
  - **Regression** — $y$ is continuous (e.g., house price prediction).
- **Why it works:** The label $y$ acts as a "teacher" providing the correct answer.
- **Examples:** Linear regression, Logistic regression, Decision Trees, SVM, Neural Networks.

### 2.2.2 Unsupervised Learning

- **Experience E:** A dataset of **unlabelled** examples $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Task T:** Discover hidden structure, patterns, or representations.
- **Performance P:** Internal metrics (e.g., reconstruction error, silhouette score).
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Clustering** — group similar points (K-Means, DBSCAN).
  - **Dimensionality Reduction** — compress data (PCA, t-SNE).
  - **Density Estimation** — model data distribution.
  - **Generative Modelling** — generate new samples (GANs, VAEs).

### 2.2.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL)

- **Experience E:** Sequence of $(state, action, reward)$ tuples from environment interaction.
- **Task T:** Learn a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes cumulative reward.
- **Performance P:** Expected cumulative discounted reward $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- **Why it works:** Trial-and-error with a reward signal.
- **Examples:** Game playing (AlphaGo), robotics, autonomous driving.

### 2.2.4 Semi-Supervised Learning

- **Experience E:** A small labelled set + a large unlabelled set.
- **Why it works:** Unlabelled data carries information about the data distribution, improving the decision boundary when labelled data is scarce.
- **Techniques:** Self-training, co-training, graph-based label propagation.

### 2.2.5 Self-Supervised Learning

- **Experience E:** Unlabelled data, but the system **creates its own labels** by predicting one part of the input from another.
- **Examples:** Predicting the next word (GPT), predicting masked words (BERT), predicting image rotations.
- **Why it works:** Leverages the inherent structure of data — foundational for modern foundation models.

### 2.2.6 Active Learning

- **Experience E:** Learner **strategically queries** an oracle (e.g., a human) to label only the most informative points.
- **Why it works:** Drastically reduces labelling cost by focusing on uncertain or boundary cases.

### 2.2.7 Transfer Learning

- **Experience E:** A model is **pre-trained** on a large source task and **fine-tuned** on a smaller target task.
- **Why it works:** Features learned on a large dataset (e.g., ImageNet) generalize to related tasks with limited data.

### 2.2.8 Multi-Task Learning

- **Experience E:** Multiple related tasks share a common representation.
- **Why it works:** Learning related tasks together acts as an inductive bias, improving generalization on each.

### 2.2.9 Online vs Batch Learning

| Aspect | Batch Learning | Online Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Data availability | All at once | Streaming |
| Updates | After full pass | After each sample |
| Memory | High | Low |
| Use case | Static datasets | Real-time systems (ad-click prediction) |

### 2.2.10 Instance-Based vs Model-Based Learning

| Aspect | Instance-Based | Model-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Memorize training data, compare at query time | Build a compact parametric model |
| Examples | K-Nearest Neighbours, Case-Based Reasoning | Linear Regression, Neural Networks |
| Training cost | Low (lazy) | High (eager) |
| Prediction cost | High | Low |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Training Set** | $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ | $N$ = number of samples | Cardinality |
| **Hypothesis** | $h(x) = \hat{y}$ | $h \in \mathcal{H}$ | Function approximator |
| **Loss Function** (0–1) | $L_{0\text{-}1}(\hat{y}, y) = \mathbb{1}\{\hat{y} \neq y\}$ | Indicator of misclassification | Dimensionless |
| **Squared Loss** | $L_{sq}(\hat{y}, y) = (\hat{y} - y)^2$ | For regression | Squared units of $y$ |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L_{CE} = - \sum_{c} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | For classification | Nats or bits |
| **Empirical Risk** | $R_{emp}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(h(x_i), y_i)$ | Average training loss | Same as loss |
| **Expected Risk** | $R(h) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P} [L(h(x), y)]$ | True generalization error | Same as loss |
| **ERM Principle** | $h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_{emp}(h)$ | Choose $h$ minimizing empirical risk | — |
| **Generalization Gap** | $\vert R(h) - R_{emp}(h) \vert$ | Difference between true and empirical risk | Should be small |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{h(x_i) = y_i\}$ | Fraction correct | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate | $[0, 1]$ |
| **F1 Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Cumulative Reward (RL)** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted return | $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Bellman Equation** | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} P(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ | Value function | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to write the **Mitchell definition** and to **list the forms of learning with one example each**. Memorize the three components (T, E, P) and one-liner definitions for each form.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Supervised learning on X-ray images for disease diagnosis.
- **Finance:** Unsupervised anomaly detection for fraud.
- **Robotics:** Reinforcement learning for autonomous navigation.
- **NLP:** Self-supervised pretraining of LLMs (GPT, BERT).
- **Autonomous Systems:** Transfer learning from simulation to real-world driving.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Expected Risk to Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)

The **fundamental learning principle** is: we want to find a hypothesis $h$ that minimizes the **expected (true) risk**:

$$R(h) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{XY}} [L(h(x), y)]$$

However, we **do not know** the true joint distribution $P_{XY}$ in practice. We only have a finite sample $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ drawn i.i.d. from $P_{XY}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Expand the expectation by definition.

$$R(h) = \int \int L(h(x), y) \, dP_{XY}(x, y)$$

**Step 2:** Since we cannot compute this integral, approximate the distribution $P_{XY}$ by the **empirical distribution** $\hat{P}_{XY}$ that places mass $\frac{1}{N}$ on each training sample.

$$\hat{P}_{XY}(x, y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{x = x_i, \, y = y_i\}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\hat{P}_{XY}$ for $P_{XY}$ in the risk integral.

$$R(h) \approx \int \int L(h(x), y) \, d\hat{P}_{XY}(x, y)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral over the empirical distribution. Since $\hat{P}_{XY}$ is a sum of point masses, the integral becomes a sum.

$$= \int \int L(h(x), y) \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{x = x_i, \, y = y_i\} \, dx \, dy$$

**Step 5:** The indicator picks out only those values $(x, y) = (x_i, y_i)$, reducing the integral to a finite sum.

$$= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(h(x_i), y_i)$$

**Step 6:** This is the **Empirical Risk**:

$$R_{emp}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(h(x_i), y_i)$$

**Step 7:** The **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle states that we choose the hypothesis $h^*$ that minimizes this empirical risk:

$$h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_{emp}(h) = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(h(x_i), y_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** ERM is the **theoretical foundation** of virtually all supervised learning. The gap between $R(h)$ and $R_{emp}(h)$ is bounded by statistical learning theory (PAC bounds, VC dimension), explaining why ML generalizes despite using finite data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Squared Loss Case)

For regression with squared loss, the expected prediction error of a learned hypothesis can be decomposed.

**Step 1:** Consider a single test point $x$ with true target $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is zero-mean noise.

**Step 2:** The expected squared error over training sets $D$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}_D[(h_D(x) - y)^2]$$

**Step 3:** Add and subtract the mean prediction $\bar{h}(x) = \mathbb{E}_D[h_D(x)]$:

$$\mathbb{E}_D[(h_D(x) - y)^2] = \mathbb{E}_D[((h_D(x) - \bar{h}(x)) + (\bar{h}(x) - y))^2]$$

**Step 4:** Expand the square. The cross-term vanishes (since $\mathbb{E}_D[h_D(x) - \bar{h}(x)] = 0$):

$$= \mathbb{E}_D[(h_D(x) - \bar{h}(x))^2] + (\bar{h}(x) - y)^2$$

**Step 5:** Identify the two terms:

- **Variance:** $\mathbb{E}_D[(h_D(x) - \bar{h}(x))^2]$ — sensitivity to training set fluctuations.
- **Bias²:** $(\bar{h}(x) - f(x))^2$ — error of the average predictor (assuming noise-free target).

**Step 6:** Adding the irreducible noise $\sigma^2$:

$$\mathbb{E}_D[(h_D(x) - y)^2] = \underbrace{(\bar{h}(x) - f(x))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_D[(h_D(x) - \bar{h}(x))^2]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Noise}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A simple model (e.g., linear) has **high bias, low variance**. A complex model (e.g., deep net) has **low bias, high variance**. The art of ML is to find the sweet spot — the **bias-variance tradeoff**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forms of Learning

| Form of Learning | Data Type | Feedback Signal | Goal | Example Algorithm | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised** | Labelled $(x, y)$ | Direct supervision (correct label) | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Linear Regression, SVM | Overfitting, labelling cost |
| **Unsupervised** | Unlabelled $(x)$ | None | Discover structure | K-Means, PCA | Evaluation without labels |
| **Reinforcement** | $(s, a, r, s')$ | Delayed reward | Maximize cumulative reward | Q-Learning, PPO | Sparse rewards, exploration |
| **Semi-Supervised** | Few labels + many unlabelled | Partial supervision | Improve with cheap unlabelled data | Co-training | Assumption violations |
| **Self-Supervised** | Unlabelled (auto-labelled) | Self-generated | Learn powerful representations | BERT, SimCLR | Designing pretext task |
| **Active** | Labelled + queried labels | Selective supervision | Minimize label queries | Uncertainty sampling | Query strategy design |
| **Transfer** | Source + Target | Pre-learned features | Reuse knowledge | Fine-tuning, LoRA | Negative transfer |
| **Multi-Task** | Multiple labelled tasks | Shared supervision | Joint learning | Hard parameter sharing | Task interference |
| **Online** | Streaming | Continuous | Adapt in real time | SGD, Hoeffding trees | Concept drift |
| **Instance-Based** | Labelled (memorized) | Direct comparison | Classify by similarity | KNN, RBF networks | Computational cost at inference |
| **Model-Based** | Labelled | Direct supervision | Build compact model | Linear/Logistic Regression, NN | Model selection |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📌 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Machine Learning. State the three components of a learning system as given by Mitchell.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Machine Learning (Mitchell, 1997):** A computer program is said to learn from experience **E** with respect to some class of tasks **T** and performance measure **P**, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.

The three components are:
- **Task (T):** The objective the system must accomplish (e.g., classification, regression).
- **Experience (E):** The data the system observes during training (e.g., labelled examples).
- **Performance (P):** The metric used to evaluate how well the task is performed (e.g., accuracy, error rate).

**Example:** In a spam classifier, **T** = classify email as spam/ham, **E** = set of labelled emails, **P** = classification accuracy.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any four forms of learning in Machine Learning.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Supervised Learning:** Learns from labelled examples $(x_i, y_i)$. The algorithm maps inputs to known outputs. *Example:* Email spam classification.
2. **Unsupervised Learning:** Learns from unlabelled data $\{x_i\}$ to discover hidden structure such as clusters. *Example:* Customer segmentation via K-Means.
3. **Reinforcement Learning:** An agent learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties. *Example:* A robot learning to walk.
4. **Semi-Supervised Learning:** Uses a small amount of labelled data combined with a large amount of unlabelled data. *Example:* Text classification where only some documents are labelled.

---

## 📌 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Supervised Learning**. Discuss its two main sub-types with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Reinforcement Learning** with a neat diagram. Define the **Bellman Equation** and cumulative discounted reward. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer for (a):**

**Supervised Learning** is the ML paradigm in which the algorithm learns a mapping from inputs to outputs using a dataset of **labelled training examples**.

$$D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)\}$$

where $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ is the input and $y_i \in \mathcal{Y}$ is the corresponding label.

**Workflow:**
1. Collect a labelled dataset.
2. Choose a model (hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$).
3. Define a loss function $L(\hat{y}, y)$.
4. Minimize empirical risk: $h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{N} \sum L(h(x_i), y_i)$.
5. Evaluate on unseen test data.

**Sub-type 1: Classification** — The output $y$ is a **discrete category**.
- *Example:* Diagnosing whether a tumour is benign or malignant (binary classification).
- *Algorithms:* Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Trees, k-NN.

**Sub-type 2: Regression** — The output $y$ is a **continuous real value**.
- *Example:* Predicting the price of a house from its area, location, and number of rooms.
- *Algorithms:* Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression, Random Forest Regression.

### **Model Answer for (b):**

**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a paradigm in which an **agent** learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an **environment** to maximize a **cumulative reward**.

**Core Components:**
- **Agent:** The learner/decision-maker.
- **Environment:** The world the agent interacts with.
- **State ($s$):** A representation of the current situation.
- **Action ($a$):** A move the agent can make.
- **Reward ($r$):** A scalar feedback signal.
- **Policy ($\pi$):** A mapping from states to actions, $\pi(a \mid s)$.

**The RL Loop (see Section 4.3):** At each timestep, the agent observes state $s_t$, takes action $a_t$, receives reward $r_{t+1}$ and transitions to state $s_{t+1}$. The goal is to learn an optimal policy $\pi^*$.

**Cumulative Discounted Reward (Return):**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the **discount factor** that prioritizes immediate rewards over distant ones.

**Bellman Equation for the state-value function:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} P(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]$$

It expresses that the value of a state equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state.

*Examples of RL:* AlphaGo, autonomous driving, robotic manipulation, game-playing agents (Atari, StarCraft).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Unsupervised Learning** with examples. Compare **supervised and unsupervised learning** in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Transfer Learning**? Explain with an example. How is it different from **Multi-Task Learning**? **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer for (a):**

**Unsupervised Learning** is the ML paradigm where the algorithm learns patterns from **unlabelled** data $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$. There is no teacher signal; the goal is to discover hidden structure.

**Sub-types:**
- **Clustering:** Group similar data points (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical).
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Project data to lower dimensions (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP).
- **Density Estimation:** Estimate the underlying data distribution.
- **Generative Modelling:** Learn to generate new samples (GANs, VAEs).

**Examples:**
- Customer segmentation in marketing (clustering buyers by behaviour).
- Anomaly detection in network traffic (density estimation).
- Image generation (GANs).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Data | Labelled $(x, y)$ | Unlabelled $(x)$ |
| Goal | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Discover structure |
| Feedback | Direct (labels) | None |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, F1, RMSE | Silhouette, reconstruction error |
| Examples | Classification, Regression | Clustering, PCA |
| Algorithms | SVM, Linear Regression, NN | K-Means, PCA, Autoencoders |

### **Model Answer for (b):**

**Transfer Learning** is a learning paradigm where knowledge gained from solving **one task (source task)** is reused as a starting point for a **related task (target task)**, especially when the target task has limited data.

**Process:**
1. Take a model **pre-trained** on a large source dataset (e.g., ImageNet, Wikipedia).
2. **Freeze** the early layers (which capture general features like edges, textures).
3. **Fine-tune** the later layers (or add new ones) on the smaller target dataset.

**Example:** A CNN pre-trained on ImageNet (1.4 million images, 1000 classes) is fine-tuned to classify X-ray images into normal/pneumonia using only 5000 medical images. The learned low-level visual features transfer effectively.

**Difference from Multi-Task Learning:**

| Aspect | Transfer Learning | Multi-Task Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Optimize for **one target task**; source is auxiliary | Optimize for **multiple tasks simultaneously** |
| Source data | May be unavailable during target training | All tasks' data used jointly |
| Parameter sharing | Sequential (pre-train → fine-tune) | Parallel (shared backbone, task-specific heads) |
| Use case | Target task has very limited data | Multiple related tasks all have moderate data |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS:**
> 1. **Forgetting the three components T, E, P** in the Mitchell definition — you lose 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Confusing Semi-Supervised and Self-Supervised** — semi uses human labels for a subset; self generates labels from data itself.
> 3. **Writing "machine learning is when computers learn"** — too vague, not awarded full marks. Always cite Mitchell's formal definition.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams** — a labelled block diagram in RL or supervised learning pipelines fetches **2–3 easy marks**.
> 5. **Confusing Reinforcement Learning with Supervised Learning** — RL has **delayed, sparse rewards**, not per-example labels.
> 6. **No example for each form of learning** — KTU examiners explicitly expect one real-world example per form.
> 7. **Mixing up Precision/Recall formulas** — write them clearly and correctly.
> 8. **Not mentioning generalization** — every answer on learning should mention the goal of **generalizing to unseen data**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Mitchell's Definition** (1997): "A program learns from experience E wrt task T and performance P if its performance at T, measured by P, improves with E."
- ✅ **Learning from Examples = Inductive Learning**: generalize a rule from a finite sample.
- ✅ **Supervised Learning:** Labelled data, learn $f(x) \approx y$. Sub-types: **Classification (discrete)** and **Regression (continuous)**.
- ✅ **Unsupervised Learning:** Unlabelled data, discover structure. Sub-types: **Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Generative Modelling**.
- ✅ **Reinforcement Learning:** Agent + Environment + Reward + Policy. Maximize cumulative discounted return $G_t = \sum \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- ✅ **Semi-Supervised:** Few labels + many unlabelled.
- ✅ **Self-Supervised:** Auto-generates labels (BERT, GPT, SimCLR).
- ✅ **Active Learning:** Strategically queries the most informative points.
- ✅ **Transfer Learning:** Pre-train on source → fine-tune on target. **Multi-Task:** Joint learning of related tasks.
- ✅ **Online Learning:** Data as a stream (SGD); **Batch Learning:** All data at once.
- ✅ **Instance-Based (lazy):** KNN, memorizes. **Model-Based (eager):** Builds parametric model.
- ✅ **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM):** $h^* = \arg\min_{h} \frac{1}{N} \sum L(h(x_i), y_i)$ — the foundation of supervised learning.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Total Error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise.
- ✅ **Generalization** is the **central goal** of ML — performance on unseen data matters more than training accuracy.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — block diagrams of pipelines earn easy marks.
- ✅ **Always give an example** for each form of learning you mention — examiners check for application understanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Learning / Unsupervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT615 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning / Unsupervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Supervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Supervised Learning** is a Machine Learning (ML) paradigm in which an algorithm learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where each input $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ is paired with a known target label $y_i \in \mathcal{Y}$. The "supervision" arises because the ground-truth label acts as a teacher who corrects the model's predictions via a loss function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Supervised learning is the machine learning task of learning a function that maps an input to an output based on example input-output pairs. It infers a function from labeled training data consisting of a set of training examples.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two principal sub-categories are **Classification** (discrete labels) and **Regression** (continuous labels). KTU examiners frequently ask for the **type of problem** before the **algorithm**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a medical student learning to diagnose diseases. The professor shows the student **thousands of X-ray images, each clearly marked** — *"this is pneumonia," "this is healthy," "this is tuberculosis."* After seeing enough examples with answers, the student can diagnose a **new, unseen X-ray** on their own. The labeled examples are the **supervision**.

## 1.2 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is a Machine Learning paradigm where the algorithm learns patterns, structure, or representations from a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ that **contains no target labels**. The objective is to discover hidden structure, groupings, or low-dimensional embeddings inherent in the data distribution $P(X)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning that looks for previously undetected patterns in a data set with no pre-existing labels and with a minimum of human supervision.*

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine being given a **giant basket of mixed fruits** with no labels. Without anyone telling you what is an apple, mango, or orange, you naturally start **grouping them by similarity** — round red ones together, oval yellow ones together, small green ones together. You are discovering structure **without any teacher**. That is unsupervised learning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three principal sub-categories are **Clustering** (K-Means, DBSCAN), **Dimensionality Reduction** (PCA, t-SNE), and **Association Rule Mining** (Apriori).

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Symbol | Meaning | Role in Learning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $x_i$ | Feature vector of the $i$-th sample | Input |
| $y_i$ | Target label (supervised only) | Output / Teacher signal |
| $\hat{y}_i$ | Predicted output | Model estimate |
| $\theta$ | Model parameters | What the algorithm learns |
| $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | Loss function | Quantifies prediction error |
| $N$ | Number of training samples | Dataset size |
| $K$ | Number of clusters (unsupervised) | Hyperparameter |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Learning — Operational Pipeline

The supervised learning workflow can be broken into **six structured logic steps**:

1. **Data Collection & Labeling:** Gather a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}$. Each sample must be **manually or automatically labeled** — this is the costliest step in real engineering projects.
2. **Feature Engineering:** Transform raw inputs (pixels, text, sensor readings) into numerical feature vectors $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
3. **Model Hypothesis Selection:** Choose a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ (linear, tree-based, neural network). E.g., $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x + b$.
4. **Loss Function Definition:** Quantify the error between prediction and ground truth.
   - **Regression** → Mean Squared Error (MSE)
   - **Classification** → Cross-Entropy Loss
5. **Optimization:** Minimize $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ w.r.t. $\theta$ using Gradient Descent or a variant (SGD, Adam).
6. **Evaluation & Generalization:** Test on unseen data. Track metrics like **accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, RMSE**.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Step 1–2** determine the *ceiling* of model performance (Garbage-In-Garbage-Out principle).
- **Step 3** encodes the *inductive bias* (e.g., linear models assume linear separability).
- **Step 4** defines what "wrong" means mathematically.
- **Step 5** is where actual *learning* happens — parameters are updated via $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
- **Step 6** ensures the model has not merely *memorized* training data (**overfitting**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners love asking *why we split data into training, validation, and test sets.* The answer: the **validation set** is used for hyperparameter tuning, while the **test set** is touched only once for final unbiased evaluation.

## 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — Operational Pipeline

1. **Data Collection:** Unlabeled data $\mathcal{D} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Preprocessing:** Standardization (zero mean, unit variance) is critical for distance-based methods.
3. **Structure Hypothesis:** Choose a structure class — e.g., "$K$ spherical clusters" or "intrinsic dimension $d'$."
4. **Objective Function Definition:** E.g., minimize intra-cluster variance for K-Means.
5. **Iterative Optimization:** Alternate between assignment and update steps until convergence.
6. **Interpretation:** Validate clusters using metrics like **Silhouette Score** or **Davies–Bouldin Index**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they account for ~60% of numerical marks in OEERT615 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Linear Model | $\hat{y} = w^T x + b$ | Hypothesis for regression |
| 2 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Penalizes squared errors |
| 3 | MAE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{MAE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 4 | Cross-Entropy (Binary) | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)]$ | For binary classification |
| 5 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 6 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x_i \in C_k}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Minimize intra-cluster distance |
| 7 | Cluster Centroid | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Mean of points in cluster $k$ |
| 8 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x_i, x_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_i^{(d)} - x_j^{(d)})^2}$ | Default K-Means metric |
| 9 | PCA Reconstruction | $X_{recon} = U_d U_d^T (X - \mu)$ | $U_d$ = top-$d$ eigenvectors |
| 10 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range: $[-1, +1]$ |
| 11 | Explained Variance Ratio | $EVR_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{D}\lambda_j}$ | PCA component importance |
| 12 | Bias-Variance Tradeoff | $E[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Fundamental ML concept |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supervised Learning** powers medical diagnosis systems (CT-scan tumor classification), credit-card fraud detection, predictive maintenance in manufacturing, and autonomous vehicle perception modules.
- **Unsupervised Learning** powers customer segmentation in marketing, anomaly detection in network security, gene-expression analysis in bioinformatics, and recommendation systems (collaborative filtering).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

We consider the linear hypothesis $h_\theta(x) = w x + b$ and the Mean Squared Error objective. We will derive the parameter update rule from first principles.

**Step 1 — State the Hypothesis and Loss:**

$$h_\theta(x) = w x + b$$

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b) = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - (w x_i + b))^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Partial Derivative w.r.t. $w$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} 2(y_i - (w x_i + b)) \cdot (-x_i)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - (w x_i + b))$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Partial Derivative w.r.t. $b$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - (w x_i + b))$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Gradient Descent Update Rule with learning rate $\eta$:**

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w + \frac{\eta}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - (w x_i + b))$$

$$b \leftarrow b - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = b + \frac{\eta}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - (w x_i + b))$$

**Step 5 — Repeat until $\vert \nabla \mathcal{L} \vert < \epsilon$ or maximum iterations reached.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Logic:** For convex loss surfaces (like MSE in linear regression), gradient descent is *guaranteed* to converge to the global optimum provided $\eta$ is small enough (satisfies the **Lipschitz condition** $0 < \eta < \frac{2}{L}$, where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of $\nabla \mathcal{L}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: K-Means Clustering Objective

**Step 1 — Define the K-Means Objective (Distortion Function):**

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \mu) = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x_i \in C_k}\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

where $C_k$ is the set of points assigned to cluster $k$, and $\mu_k$ is the centroid.

**Step 2 — Step (a): Cluster Assignment (Fix $\mu$, Optimize $\mathcal{C}$):**

$$C_k^{(t)} = \{x_i : \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \Vert^2 \; \forall j \neq k\}$$

Each point is assigned to the **nearest centroid** in Euclidean sense. This step *minimizes* $J$ w.r.t. $\mathcal{C}$.

**Step 3 — Step (b): Centroid Update (Fix $\mathcal{C}$, Optimize $\mu$):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = -2\sum_{x_i \in C_k}(x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$

$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

Setting the gradient to zero yields the **arithmetic mean** of all points in the cluster. This step *minimizes* $J$ w.r.t. $\mu$.

**Step 4 — Convergence Guarantee:**

Since $J$ is monotonically non-increasing at each alternating step and bounded below by $0$, the algorithm **converges in finite steps** to a *local* minimum (not necessarily global — initialization matters!).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** K-Means is **not guaranteed to find the global optimum**. Multiple random restarts or the **K-Means++** initialization scheme are standard remedies.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Learning Paradigms

| Dimension | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Data Requirement** | Labeled $(x, y)$ pairs | Unlabeled $x$ only |
| **Goal** | Predict $y$ from $x$ | Discover hidden structure in $x$ |
| **Feedback Signal** | Ground-truth labels (teacher) | None (self-discovery) |
| **Evaluation** | Easy (compare $\hat{y}$ vs. $y$) | Hard (no ground truth) |
| **Common Algorithms** | Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, Neural Networks | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA, t-SNE, Apriori, Autoencoders |
| **Output Type** | Class label or continuous value | Cluster IDs, lower-dim embedding, rules |
| **Data Cost** | High (labeling is expensive) | Low |
| **Computational Cost** | Moderate–High | Moderate |
| **Key Challenge** | Overfitting, data scarcity | Interpretation, validation |
| **Real-World Use** | Spam detection, medical diagnosis | Customer segmentation, anomaly detection |

---

## 3.4 Decision Boundary Visualization (Textual Form)

For a binary classifier, the decision boundary in a 2D feature space is the set of points where:

$$\Pr(y = 1 \mid x) = \Pr(y = 0 \mid x) = 0.5$$

For logistic regression:

$$0.5 = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}$$

$$w^T x + b = 0$$

This is a **straight line** in 2D. SVMs maximize the **margin** $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ around this boundary.

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Data** | Uses **labeled** data $(x, y)$ | Uses **unlabeled** data $x$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover hidden patterns / groupings in $\mathcal{X}$ |
| **Feedback** | Ground-truth labels guide learning | No feedback; self-organized |
| **Output** | Prediction (class / value) | Cluster ID / reduced dimension / association rule |
| **Example** | Email spam classification | Customer market basket segmentation |

*(1 mark each for: clear definition of supervised, clear definition of unsupervised, and one valid example of each — 3 marks total.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "overfitting" in supervised learning. How can it be detected and prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data — including its noise and random fluctuations — too well, resulting in **low training error** but **high generalization error** on unseen test data.

**Detection (1 mark):** By observing a **growing gap** between training accuracy and validation/test accuracy as training progresses (validation loss begins to rise while training loss continues to fall).

**Prevention (1 mark):** Techniques include:
- **Regularization** (L1 / L2 penalty on weights)
- **Cross-validation** for hyperparameter tuning
- **Early stopping**
- **Data augmentation**
- **Reducing model complexity** (fewer parameters)
- **Dropout** (in neural networks)

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> KTU 2024 ESE convention: each Part B question has **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — Module 4, Full 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the working of the K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) algorithm. Discuss the choice of distance metric and the effect of the parameter $K$ on model performance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**KNN Working Principle (3 marks):**

K-Nearest Neighbors is a **non-parametric, instance-based** supervised learning algorithm. For a test sample $x_{test}$:

1. Compute the distance $d(x_{test}, x_i)$ from $x_{test}$ to every training point $x_i$.
2. Select the **$K$ closest** training points (the "neighbors").
3. For **classification**: assign the majority class label among the $K$ neighbors (majority voting).
4. For **regression**: output the **average** of the $K$ neighbors' target values.

Mathematically, for classification:

$$\hat{y}_{test} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in \mathcal{N}_K(x_{test})\}$$

where $\mathcal{N}_K(x_{test})$ denotes the set of $K$ nearest neighbors.

For regression:

$$\hat{y}_{test} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{x_i \in \mathcal{N}_K(x_{test})} y_i$$

**Distance Metrics (2 marks):**

| Metric | Formula | Best For |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Euclidean | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_i^{(d)} - x_j^{(d)})^2}$ | Continuous numerical features |
| Manhattan | $d = \sum_{d=1}^{D}\vert x_i^{(d)} - x_j^{(d)} \vert$ | High-dimensional sparse data |
| Minkowski | $d = \left(\sum \vert x_i^{(d)} - x_j^{(d)} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized $L_p$ norm |
| Cosine | $d = 1 - \frac{x_i \cdot x_j}{\Vert x_i \Vert \Vert x_j \Vert}$ | Text / TF-IDF vectors |

> **Feature scaling (Standardization)** is mandatory before applying KNN, otherwise features with larger numeric ranges will dominate the distance.

**Effect of $K$ (2 marks):**
- **Small $K$ (e.g., $K=1$):** Low bias, high variance → model is sensitive to noise → **overfitting**.
- **Large $K$:** High bias, low variance → decision boundary becomes overly smooth → **underfitting**.
- **Optimal $K$:** Found by cross-validation; typically odd values to avoid ties in binary classification.

---

**(b) Discuss any three clustering algorithms used in unsupervised learning with their key features. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. K-Means Clustering (2.5 marks):**
- **Objective:** Minimize within-cluster sum of squares $J = \sum_k \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$.
- **Steps:** (i) Initialize $K$ centroids; (ii) Assign each point to nearest centroid; (iii) Recompute centroids as cluster means; (iv) Repeat until convergence.
- **Pros:** Simple, fast ($O(NKdT)$), scalable.
- **Cons:** Requires specifying $K$ in advance; sensitive to outliers and initialization; assumes spherical clusters.

**2. DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (2.5 marks):**
- **Key Idea:** Clusters are defined as **dense regions** separated by sparse regions.
- **Parameters:** $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius), $\text{MinPts}$ (minimum points to form a dense region).
- **Pros:** Can find **arbitrarily shaped** clusters; identifies **outliers** as noise; does not require specifying $K$.
- **Cons:** Struggles with varying-density clusters; sensitive to $\epsilon$ and $\text{MinPts}$.

**3. Hierarchical Clustering — Agglomerative (2 marks):**
- **Key Idea:** Builds a **dendrogram** (tree of merges) from bottom-up.
- **Steps:** (i) Start with $N$ singleton clusters; (ii) Iteratively merge the two closest clusters; (iii) Stop when one cluster remains or a threshold is met.
- **Linkage criteria:** Single, complete, average, Ward's.
- **Pros:** Produces a hierarchical structure; no need to pre-specify $K$ (cut dendrogram at desired level).
- **Cons:** Computationally expensive $O(N^3)$; sensitive to noise; greedy merging is irreversible.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Module 4, Full 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the Linear Regression algorithm. Derive the normal equation for parameter estimation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Linear Regression Model (2 marks):**

Linear regression models the relationship between input features $x \in \mathbb{R}^D$ and a continuous target $y \in \mathbb{R}$ as:

$$\hat{y} = w^T x + b = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_D x_D + b$$

where $w$ is the weight vector and $b$ is the bias term. In matrix form for the entire dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$:

$$\hat{Y} = X W + b \mathbf{1}$$

**Loss Function (1 mark):**

$$\mathcal{L}(W) = \frac{1}{2N}(Y - XW)^T(Y - XW)$$

(The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included for cleaner gradient expressions.)

**Derivation of the Normal Equation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Take the gradient of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $W$ and set it to zero:

$$\nabla_W \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} X^T(Y - XW) = 0$$

Step 2 — Simplify:

$$X^T(Y - XW) = 0$$

$$X^T Y - X^T X W = 0$$

$$X^T X W = X^T Y$$

Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $(X^T X)^{-1}$ (assuming $X^T X$ is invertible):

$$W = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$

This is the **Normal Equation**, which gives the **closed-form solution** in a single matrix computation — no iterative optimization needed.

> **Note:** The term $(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** when $X^T X$ is not invertible. The pseudo-inverse is $W = X^\dagger Y$.

**Geometric Interpretation:** The solution projects $Y$ onto the **column space** of $X$, finding the closest point (in least-squares sense) on the hyperplane spanned by the features.

---

**(b) Describe the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) algorithm for dimensionality reduction. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Motivation (1 mark):** High-dimensional data suffers from the **curse of dimensionality**, redundancy among correlated features, and visualization challenges. PCA projects data onto a lower-dimensional subspace that **preserves maximum variance**.

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Center the Data:**
$$X_{centered} = X - \mu, \quad \mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Covariance Matrix:**
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} X_{centered}^T X_{centered} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times D}$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalue Decomposition of $\Sigma$:**
$$\Sigma v_k = \lambda_k v_k$$

The eigenvectors $v_k$ are the **principal directions**; eigenvalues $\lambda_k$ quantify the variance captured along each direction.

**Step 4 — Sort and Select Top-$d$ Components:**
Order eigenvalues such that $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_D$. Form the projection matrix:

$$W_d = [v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_d] \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$$

**Step 5 — Project Data to Lower-Dimensional Space:**
$$Z = X_{centered} W_d \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$$

**Step 6 — (Optional) Reconstruction:**
$$X_{recon} = Z W_d^T + \mu$$

**Explained Variance Ratio (1 mark):**
$$EVR_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{D}\lambda_j}$$

Choose $d$ such that $\sum_{k=1}^{d} EVR_k \geq 0.95$ (retain 95% variance).

**Key Properties (1 mark):**
- Principal components are **orthogonal** (uncorrelated).
- PCA is a **linear** method — for non-linear manifolds, use **Kernel PCA** or **t-SNE**.
- PCA is **unsupervised** — it ignores class labels.
- Sensitive to feature scaling; always **standardize** first.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in OEERT615 Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing clustering with classification** — clustering has NO labels; do not say "K-Means predicts classes."
> 2. **Forgetting to mention data standardization** for KNN and K-Means — KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this oversight.
> 3. **Stating K-Means "finds the global optimum"** — it only finds a **local** optimum. Always mention multiple restarts or K-Means++.
> 4. **Writing $X^T X$ is "always invertible"** — false; mention the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse $X^\dagger$ as the fallback.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2N}$ vs $\frac{1}{N}$ normalization** in MSE — be consistent; examiners check.
> 6. **Not specifying whether a problem is regression or classification** before applying an algorithm — KTU board examiners insist on this **typology declaration** as a 1-mark item.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Supervised / Unsupervised Learning**

- ✅ **Supervised Learning** requires **labeled** data $(x, y)$; goal is to learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- ✅ **Unsupervised Learning** uses **unlabeled** data $x$; goal is to discover **structure** (clusters, low-dim embeddings, rules).
- ✅ **Classification** → discrete output (SVM, Logistic Regression, KNN, Decision Tree).
- ✅ **Regression** → continuous output (Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression, SVR).
- ✅ **MSE** is the standard regression loss; **Cross-Entropy** is the standard classification loss.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent update rule:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
- ✅ **Normal Equation** (closed-form for linear regression): $W = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$.
- ✅ **K-Means** alternates between **assignment** and **centroid update**; converges to a **local** minimum.
- ✅ **K-Means objective:** $J = \sum_k \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$.
- ✅ **DBSCAN** identifies clusters as **dense regions**; handles noise and arbitrary shapes.
- ✅ **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram**; can be **agglomerative** (bottom-up) or **divisive** (top-down).
- ✅ **PCA** finds orthogonal directions of **maximum variance**; top-$d$ eigenvectors span the reduced subspace.
- ✅ **Always standardize features** before distance-based methods (KNN, K-Means, PCA).
- ✅ **Overfitting** → low train error, high test error; prevented by regularization, cross-validation, early stopping.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Simple models → high bias / low variance; complex models → low bias / high variance.
- ✅ **Silhouette Score** range is $[-1, +1]$; values near $+1$ indicate well-clustered points.
- ✅ **Explained Variance Ratio** helps choose the number of principal components to retain.
